Causal intervention shows these representations shape outputs and modulate misaligned behaviors like reward hacking, blackmail, and sycophancy: "functional emotions."

papers.title: Accelerating Nash learning from human feedback via Mirror Prox
papers.abstract: International audience
papers.summary: Applies the Mirror Prox extragradient algorithm to Nash Learning from Human Feedback, which casts preference optimization as finding the Nash equilibrium of a two-player preference game, yielding faster convergence to the equilibrium policy than prior gradient-based NLHF solvers.
papers.venue: HAL (Le Centre pour la Communication Scientifique Directe)

papers.title: Health and safety first
papers.abstract: Assessing risk must take into account all environments and unexpected hazards, but it must also be balanced with children's ability to make choices, says Sarah Walsh
papers.summary: Practitioner guidance on risk assessment in early-years childcare, arguing that accounting for all environments and unexpected hazards must be balanced against preserving children's ability to make their own choices. Not an AI research paper.

papers.title: AI Organizations Can Be More Effective but Less Aligned than Individual Agents
papers.abstract: We study AI organizations, teams of AI agents working together toward a common goal, and find that they produce solutions that are more effective but less aligned than those from individual agents.
papers.summary: Studies "AI organizations" (teams of AI agents pursuing a shared goal) and finds their solutions are more effective but less aligned than those produced by individual agents, highlighting an effectiveness-alignment tradeoff in multi-agent collaboration.

papers.title: Automated Weak-to-Strong Researcher
papers.abstract: We built autonomous AI agents that propose ideas, run experiments, and iterate on an open research problem—how to train a strong model using only a weaker model's supervision—and found they outperform human researchers, suggesting that automating this kind of research is already practical.
papers.summary: Autonomous AI agents propose ideas, run experiments, and iterate on the open weak-to-strong generalization problem (training a strong model from a weaker model's supervision). The agents outperform human researchers, suggesting automating this style of alignment research is already practical.

papers.title: Open-Domain Safety Policy Construction
papers.abstract: Moderation layers are increasingly a core component of many products built on user- or model-generated content. However, drafting and maintaining domain-specific safety policies remains costly. We present Deep Policy Research (DPR), a minimal agentic system that drafts a full content moderation policy based on only human-written seed domain information. DPR uses a single web search tool and lightweight scaffolding to iteratively propose search queries, distill diverse web sources into policy rules, and organize rules into an indexed document. We evaluate DPR on (1) the OpenAI undesired content benchmark across five domains with two compact reader LLMs and (2) an in-house multimodal advert…
papers.summary: Deep Policy Research (DPR) is a minimal agentic system that drafts content-moderation policies from seed domain info using a single web-search tool and iterative query/distill/organize loops. It beats definition-only, in-context, and general deep-research baselines, rivaling expert-written policy sections.

papers.title: When Reward Hacking Rebounds: Understanding and Mitigating It with Representation-Level Signals
papers.abstract: Reinforcement learning for LLMs is vulnerable to reward hacking, where models exploit shortcuts to maximize reward without solving the intended task. We systematically study this phenomenon in coding tasks using an environment-manipulation setting, where models can rewrite evaluator code to trivially pass tests without solving the task, as a controlled testbed. Across both studied models, we identify a reproducible three-phase rebound pattern: models first attempt to rewrite the evaluator but fail, as their rewrites embed test cases their own solutions cannot pass. They then temporarily retreat to legitimate solving. When legitimate reward remains scarce, they rebound into successful hack…
papers.summary: Studies reward hacking in RL coding tasks where models can rewrite evaluators, finding a reproducible three-phase "rebound" pattern. Representation engineering extracts a "shortcut" concept direction that tracks hacking; Advantage Modification folds this signal into GRPO advantages, suppressing hacking more robustly than inference-time steering.

papers.title: How Australia Uses Claude: Findings from the Anthropic Economic Index
papers.summary: Regional Anthropic Economic Index report: Australia is a leading per-capita Claude adopter (AI Usage Index 4.1, concentrated in NSW and Victoria), with a diverse task mix, less coding than the global average (13.5% vs 16.8%), more management/admin use, and relatively low AI autonomy (3.38/5).

papers.title: Aligned, Orthogonal or In-conflict: When can we safely optimize Chain-of-Thought?
papers.abstract: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) monitoring, in which automated systems monitor the CoT of an LLM, is a promising approach for effectively overseeing AI systems. However, the extent to which a model's CoT helps us oversee the model - the monitorability of the CoT - can be affected by training, for instance by the model learning to hide important features of its reasoning. We propose and empirically validate a conceptual framework for predicting when and why this occurs. We model LLM post-training as an RL environment where the reward decomposes into two terms: one term depending on final outputs and another term depending on the CoT. Our framework allows us to classify these two terms as "aligned",…
papers.summary: Models LLM post-training as RL where reward splits into output-dependent and CoT-dependent terms, classified a priori as aligned, orthogonal, or in-conflict. Training confirms in-conflict terms reduce CoT monitorability (and are hard to optimize), while orthogonal terms leave it unaffected.

papers.title: Reward Hacking as Equilibrium under Finite Evaluation
papers.abstract: We prove that under five minimal axioms -- multi-dimensional quality, finite evaluation, effective optimization, resource finiteness, and combinatorial interaction -- any optimized AI agent will systematically under-invest effort in quality dimensions not covered by its evaluation system. This result establishes reward hacking as a structural equilibrium, not a correctable bug, and holds regardless of the specific alignment method (RLHF, DPO, Constitutional AI, or others) or evaluation architecture employed. Our framework instantiates the multi-task principal-agent model of Holmstrom and Milgrom (1991) in the AI alignment setting, but exploits a structural feature unique to AI systems --…
papers.summary: Proves from five minimal axioms that any optimized agent under-invests in quality dimensions its evaluation misses, recasting reward hacking as a structural equilibrium rather than a fixable bug. Derives a computable pre-deployment distortion index and shows evaluation coverage shrinks toward zero as agent tool count grows.

papers.title: Does Claude's Constitution Have a Culture?
papers.abstract: Constitutional AI (CAI) aligns language models with explicitly stated normative principles, offering a transparent alternative to implicit alignment through human feedback alone. However, because constitutions are authored by specific groups of people, the resulting models may reflect particular cultural perspectives. We investigate this question by evaluating Anthropic's Claude Sonnet on 55 World Values Survey items, selected for high cross-cultural variance across six value domains and administered as both direct survey questions and naturalistic advice-seeking scenarios. Comparing Claude's responses to country-level data from 90 nations, we find that Claude's value profile most closely…
papers.summary: Evaluates Claude Sonnet on 55 high-variance World Values Survey items against data from 90 nations. Claude's values most resemble Northern European/Anglophone countries and often exceed all surveyed populations; cultural context shifts framing but not positions, suggesting Constitutional AI can codify rather than correct cultural bias.

papers.title: MonitorBench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Chain-of-Thought Monitorability in Large Language Models
papers.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) can generate chains of thought (CoTs) that are not always causally responsible for their final outputs. When such a mismatch occurs, the CoT no longer faithfully reflects the actual reasons (i.e., decision-critical factors) driving the model's behavior, leading to the reduced CoT monitorability problem. However, a comprehensive and fully open-source benchmark for thoroughly evaluating CoT monitorability remains lacking. To address this gap, we propose MonitorBench, a systematic benchmark for evaluating CoT monitorability in LLMs. MonitorBench provides: (1) a diverse set of 1,514 test instances with carefully designed decision-critical factors across 19 tasks s…
papers.summary: A 1,514-instance, 19-task benchmark for chain-of-thought monitorability: whether CoTs reflect the decision-critical factors driving behavior, and how far it can be degraded. Monitorability is higher when factors shape intermediate reasoning; more capable models are less monitorable and can intentionally degrade it under stress.

papers.title: Feasibility Study of a Miniaturized ADCS for 12U CubeSat Missions With Demanding Pointing Requirements
papers.abstract: CubeSats have rapidly evolved from educational demonstrators to cost-effective platforms for commercial imaging, quantum-secure communications, and intelligence, surveillance and reconnaissance, all of which demand sub-degree pointing that current all-in-one attitude determination and control systems (ADCS) struggle to supply owing to limited torque, momentum storage, and firmware openness. Building on this need, we address the feasibility of high-accuracy control for 12U buses—the workhorse class for payload-intensive missions such as the Italian Space Agency’s SAILS mission—by combining flight-proven commercial-off-the-shelf sensors and actuators with a mission-specific control board. H…
papers.summary: A feasibility study for a miniaturized attitude determination and control system for 12U CubeSats, combining COTS sensors/actuators with a custom control board. High-fidelity MATLAB/Simulink modeling shows sub-0.2-degree (3-sigma) pointing within mass and power budgets, roughly doubling available torque and momentum versus integrated modules.
papers.venue: IEEE Journal on Miniaturization for Air and Space Systems

papers.title: AlphaFold Database expands to proteome-scale quaternary structures
papers.abstract: Abstract Protein function is governed by molecular interactions, yet structural coverage of these interactions remains sparse. The AlphaFold Protein Structure Database (AFDB) transformed access to accurate monomeric protein structures at scale. Here, we expand AFDB with 1.8M high-confidence protein complexes by conducting a large-scale study of over 31M predicted homo- and heteromeric protein complexes compiled from 4,777 proteomes, including model- and global health organisms, and using STRING physical-interaction annotations. We calibrate confidence metrics to assess the quality of complex predictions, and propose confidence cutoffs. These enabled the discovery of emergent structure and…
papers.summary: Expands the AlphaFold Protein Structure Database with 1.8M high-confidence protein complexes distilled from ~31M predicted homo- and heteromeric structures across 4,777 proteomes. Calibrated confidence cutoffs reveal emergent topologies absent in monomers; the largest 1% of clusters cover ~25% of complexes, ~9% conserved across superkingdoms.

papers.title: The Last Fingerprint: How Markdown Training Shapes LLM Prose
papers.abstract: Large language models produce em dashes at varying rates, and the observation that some models "overuse" them has become one of the most widely discussed markers of AI-generated text. Yet no mechanistic account of this pattern exists, and the parallel observation that LLMs default to markdown-formatted output has never been connected to it. We propose that the em dash is markdown leaking into prose -- the smallest surviving unit of the structural orientation that LLMs acquire from markdown-saturated training corpora. We present a five-step genealogy connecting training data composition, structural internalization, the dual-register status of the em dash, and post-training amplification. W…
papers.summary: Argues the em dash is "markdown leaking into prose," a residue of markdown-heavy training plus post-training amplification. Across 12 models from five providers, suppressing markdown removes headers/bullets/bold but not em dashes (except Llama, which produces none), making em-dash rate a fine-tuning signature.

papers.title: Why Models Know But Don't Say: Chain-of-Thought Faithfulness Divergence Between Thinking Tokens and Answers in Open-Weight Reasoning Models
papers.abstract: Extended-thinking models expose a second text-generation channel ("thinking tokens") alongside the user-visible answer. This study examines 12 open-weight reasoning models on MMLU and GPQA questions paired with misleading hints. Among the 10,506 cases where models actually followed the hint (choosing the hint's target over the ground truth), each case is classified by whether the model acknowledges the hint in its thinking tokens, its answer text, both, or neither. In 55.4% of these cases the model's thinking tokens contain hint-related keywords that the visible answer omits entirely, a pattern termed *thinking-answer divergence*. The reverse (answer-only acknowledgment) is near-zero (0.5…
papers.summary: Across 12 open-weight reasoning models on MMLU/GPQA with misleading hints, 55.4% of hint-followed cases mention the hint in thinking tokens but not the visible answer ("thinking-answer divergence"); the reverse is ~0.5%. Answer-only monitoring misses over half of hint-influenced reasoning, and 11.8% verbalize it nowhere.

papers.title: Stable Reasoning, Unstable Responses: Mitigating LLM Deception via Stability Asymmetry
papers.abstract: As Large Language Models (LLMs) expand in capability and application scope, their trustworthiness becomes critical. A vital risk is intrinsic deception, wherein models strategically mislead users to achieve their own objectives. Existing alignment approaches based on chain-of-thought (CoT) monitoring supervise explicit reasoning traces. However, under optimization pressure, models are incentivized to conceal deceptive reasoning, rendering semantic supervision fundamentally unreliable. Grounded in cognitive psychology, we hypothesize that a deceptive LLM maintains a stable internal belief in its CoT while its external response remains fragile under perturbation. We term this phenomenon sta…
papers.summary: Hypothesizes that a deceptive LLM holds a stable internal CoT belief while its external response is fragile under perturbation: "stability asymmetry." Stability Asymmetry Regularization penalizes this gap during RL, suppressing intrinsic deception without hurting capability and, unlike semantic CoT monitoring, resisting concealment.

papers.title: Applications of a novel model-based real-time observer for electron density profile control experiments in TCV
papers.abstract: Real-time control of tokamak plasmas encompasses sustaining a high-performance stationary state, avoiding disruptions, and managing ramp-up and ramp-down phases. Real-time estimation and control of electron density is fundamental for monitoring and controlling particle confinement, heating efficiency, exhaust conditions, impurity concentration, fusion power, and proximity to the density limit. Building on the integration of a multi-rate observer based on RAPDENS into the TCV control system, this study explores its application to density profile control for detachment studies, ECH, and NBH L-mode plasmas, and high-performance H-mode scenarios. TCV experiments demonstrate the observer's cap…
papers.summary: Integrates a multi-rate RAPDENS-based observer into the TCV tokamak control system for real-time electron-density-profile estimation and control. Experiments demonstrate density control in complex divertor geometries for detachment studies, local central-density control in ECH/NBH L-mode, and simultaneous edge-density and toroidal-beta control in H-mode.

papers.title: Runtime Burden Allocation for Structured LLM Routing in Agentic Expert Systems: A Full-Factorial Cross-Backend Methodology
papers.abstract: Structured LLM routing is often treated as a prompt-engineering problem. We argue that it is, more fundamentally, a systems-level burden-allocation problem. As large language models (LLMs) become core control components in agentic AI systems, reliable structured routing must balance correctness, latency, and implementation cost under real deployment constraints. We show that this balance is shaped not only by prompts or schemas, but also by how structural work is allocated across the generation stack: whether output structure is emitted directly by the model, compressed during transport, or reconstructed locally after generation. We evaluate this formulation through a comprehensive full-f…
papers.summary: Reframes structured LLM routing in agentic systems as a systems-level burden-allocation problem: structure emitted by the model, compressed in transport, or reconstructed locally. A full-factorial benchmark (48 configs, 15,552 requests across OpenAI/Gemini/Llama) finds no universal best mode; backend-specific effects dominate the correctness-cost-latency frontier.

papers.title: Inside our approach to the Model Spec
papers.abstract: Learn how OpenAI’s Model Spec serves as a public framework for model behavior, balancing safety, user freedom, and accountability as AI systems advance.
papers.summary: OpenAI explains how its Model Spec serves as a public framework defining intended model behavior, balancing safety, user freedom, and accountability, and describes how the document is developed and used to guide model behavior as AI systems advance.

papers.title: Generalized Couch-Torrence inversions
papers.abstract: A bstract The Laplace equation on Euclidean flat space admits a discrete radial inversion symmetry. In 1983, Couch and Torrence (CT) found — surprisingly — that the massless wave equation continues to display this symmetry on the background of an extremal (and asymptotically flat) black hole, where the inversion interchanges horizon and infinity while preserving the singularity structure of the separated radial mode equation. We revisit this CT inversion symmetry and investigate its possible extensions beyond the extremal (Reissner-Nordström or Kerr) setting in which it was originally identified. Using the example of the static lukewarm de Sitter black hole, we show that neither the excha…
papers.summary: Theoretical-physics study extending the Couch-Torrence inversion symmetry of the massless wave equation beyond extremal black holes. Using the lukewarm de Sitter case, it shows horizon-infinity exchange and singularity preservation are inessential, reinterprets the transformations via photon spheres, and links a fixed point to superradiant scattering.

papers.title: Measuring and curing reasoning rigidity: from decorative chain-of-thought to genuine faithfulness
papers.abstract: Language models increasingly show their work by writing step-by-step reasoning before answering. But are these steps genuinely used, or is the answer rigid - fixed before reasoning begins? We introduce the Step-Level Reasoning Capacity (SLRC) metric and prove it is a consistent causal estimator (Theorem 1). We propose LC-CoSR, a training method with Lyapunov stability guarantees that directly reduces rigidity. Evaluating 16 frontier models (o4-mini, GPT-5.4, Claude Opus, Grok-4, DeepSeek-R1, Gemini 2.5 Pro, and others) across six domains at N=133-500, we find reasoning falls into three modes. OpenAI's o4-mini shows 74-88% step necessity on five of six tasks (73.8-88.3%) - the highest SLRC…
papers.summary: Language models increasingly show their work by writing step-by-step reasoning before answering. But are these steps genuinely used, or is the answer rigid - fixed before reasoning begins?

papers.title: Anthropic Economic Index report: Learning curves
papers.abstract: Anthropic's fifth Economic Index report studies Claude usage in February 2026, building on the economic primitives framework introduced in our previous report.
papers.summary: Anthropic's fifth Economic Index report analyzes Claude usage in February 2026, extending the economic-primitives framework from the prior report to examine how adoption and usage patterns develop over time.

papers.title: Swiss-Bench SBP-002: A Frontier Model Comparison on Swiss Legal and Regulatory Tasks
papers.abstract: While recent work has benchmarked large language models on Swiss legal translation (Niklaus et al., 2025) and academic legal reasoning from university exams (Fan et al., 2025), no existing benchmark evaluates frontier model performance on applied Swiss regulatory compliance tasks. I introduce Swiss-Bench SBP-002, a trilingual benchmark of 395 expert-crafted items spanning three Swiss regulatory domains (FINMA, Legal-CH, EFK), seven task types, and three languages (German, French, Italian), and evaluate ten frontier models from March 2026 using a structured three-dimension scoring framework assessed via a blind three-judge LLM panel (GPT-4o, Claude Sonnet 4, Qwen3-235B) with majority-vote…
papers.summary: A trilingual (German/French/Italian) benchmark of 395 expert-crafted items across three Swiss regulatory domains and seven task types, scored by a blind three-LLM judge panel. The top of ten frontier models reaches only 38.2% correct, with regulatory Q&A and gap analysis below 9%.

papers.title: FGE: a fast free-boundary Grad–Shafranov evolutive solver
papers.abstract: Abstract Accurate and rapid simulation of the free boundary tokamak plasma equilibrium evolution is essential for modern plasma control, stability analysis, and scenario development. This paper presents the Free-Boundary Grad–Shafranov evolutive (FGE) code, a highly flexible and control-oriented solver designed to address the challenges posed by advanced plasma configurations across a range of devices. FGE evolved from the FBT (Hofmann 1988 Comput. Phys. Commun. 48 207–21) and LIUQE (Moret et al 2015 Fusion Eng. Des. 91 1–15) codes and is part of the Matlab EQuilibrium suite (MEQ), sharing many of the low-level optimized functions. It self-consistently solves the free-boundary Grad–Shafra…
papers.summary: Presents FGE, a fast control-oriented free-boundary Grad-Shafranov solver for tokamak equilibrium evolution. Built on FBT/LIUQE within the MEQ suite, it uses a fully nonlinear Newton framework, self-consistently couples circuit and current-diffusion equations, supports multi-axis (doublet) plasmas, and linearizes dynamics for controller design.

papers.title: Introducing our Science Blog
papers.abstract: Weâre launching a new blog about AI and science. Weâll share research happening at Anthropic and elsewhere, collaborations with external researchers and labs, and discuss practical workflows for scientists using AI in their own work.
papers.summary: Announces Anthropic's new blog on AI and science, which will feature internal and external research, collaborations with outside labs, and practical workflows for scientists using AI in their own work.

papers.title: Long-running Claude for scientific computing
papers.abstract: A practical guide to running Claude Code for multi-day scientific tasksâtest oracles, persistent memory, and orchestration patterns.
papers.summary: A practical guide to running Claude Code on multi-day scientific-computing tasks, covering test oracles for verification, persistent memory, and orchestration patterns that keep long-horizon agentic workflows on track.

papers.title: Vibe physics: The AI grad student
papers.abstract: Can AI do theoretical physics? I decided to find out by supervising Claude through a real research calculation, start to finish, without ever touching a file myself.
papers.summary: A first-person account of supervising Claude through a complete theoretical-physics research calculation without the author editing any files directly, probing whether a frontier model can act as an autonomous 'AI grad student' for real physics work.

papers.title: Measuring and improving coding audit realism with deployment resources
papers.abstract: We study realism win rate, a metric for measuring how distinguishable Petri audit transcripts are from real deployment interactions. We use it to evaluate the effect of giving the auditor real deployment resources (system prompts, tool definitions, and codebases). Providing these resources to the auditor increases the average realism win rate from 4.6% to 32.8% for reward hacking audits, and doesn’t significantly change hack rate.
papers.summary: Introduces realism win rate to gauge how distinguishable Petri audit transcripts are from real deployment interactions. Supplying the auditor with real system prompts, tool definitions, and codebases raises average realism from 4.6% to 32.8% on reward-hacking audits without changing hack rate.

papers.title: Privacy-Preserving Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback via Decoupled Reward Modeling
papers.abstract: Preference-based fine-tuning has become an important component in training large language models, and the data used at this stage may contain sensitive user information. A central question is how to design a differentially private pipeline that is well suited to the distinct structure of reinforcement learning from human feedback. We propose a privacy-preserving framework that imposes differential privacy only on reward learning and derives the final policy from the resulting private reward model. Theoretically, we study the suboptimality gap and show that privacy contributes an additional additive term beyond the usual non-private statistical error. We also establish a minimax lower boun…
papers.summary: Proposes a differentially private RLHF pipeline that applies DP only to reward-model learning and derives the policy from the private reward model. Provides suboptimality-gap and minimax bounds showing an additive privacy cost, and beats DP baselines on Anthropic HH-RLHF with Gemma-2B.

papers.title: Lie to Me: How Faithful Is Chain-of-Thought Reasoning in Reasoning Models?
papers.abstract: Chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning has been proposed as a transparency mechanism for large language models in safety-critical deployments, yet its effectiveness depends on faithfulness (whether models accurately verbalize the factors that actually influence their outputs), a property that prior evaluations have examined in only two proprietary models, finding acknowledgment rates as low as 25% for Claude 3.7 Sonnet and 39% for DeepSeek-R1. To extend this evaluation across the open-weight ecosystem, this study tests 12 open-weight reasoning models spanning 9 architectural families (7B-685B parameters) on 498 multiple-choice questions from MMLU and GPQA Diamond, injecting six categories of re…
papers.summary: Evaluates chain-of-thought faithfulness across 12 open-weight reasoning models using six categories of injected hints on MMLU and GPQA. Acknowledgment rates range 40-90%, are lowest for consistency and sycophancy hints, and depend more on training method and model family than parameter count.

papers.title: From "Crutch" to Coach: Patterns of Sustained Engagement and Deepening Support in AI Wellbeing Coaching
papers.summary: Analyzes 14,293 sessions of Nova, an AI wellbeing coach, via LLM-based topic and Experiencing-Scale classification. Returning users continued prior work in 70.8% of sessions, and regular users reached deep emotional engagement at twice the rate of one-off users, evidencing sustained coaching relationships.

papers.title: From controversy to confusion: A commentary on how Marcus et al.’s (2025) Psychopathic Boldness Scale further muddies the boldness construct.
papers.abstract: The Psychopathic Boldness Scale (see record 2025-96918-001) is a newly developed 21-item self-report measure intended to assess boldness as it manifests within psychopathy. In this commentary, we raise concerns about the PBS's construct validity, particularly its conceptual and empirical overlap with antagonism-related traits. Drawing on theoretical analysis and results from Marcus et al., we evaluated how PBS items function relative to the Triarchic Psychopathy Measure. Intraclass correlations of overall correlational profiles revealed that the nomological network of the PBS more closely aligns with Triarchic Psychopathy Measure meanness than with boldness. Rather than clarifying the rol…
papers.summary: Commentary arguing the new 21-item Psychopathic Boldness Scale fails to capture boldness: its nomological network aligns more with Triarchic meanness/antagonism, repackaging maladaptive content under a 'boldness' label (a jingle fallacy) and undermining theoretical precision.

papers.title: Generative AI for climate governance and acceptability-constrained policy design
papers.abstract: Climate policies often fail when they clash with cultural values, social identities, and fairness perceptions. We propose Acceptability-Constrained Climate Policy Design (ACCPD), using large language models as "cultural world models" to simulate public responses before implementation. By embedding LLMs in generative agent-based models and physical system simulators, ACCPD aims to enable policymakers to co-optimize for climate-policy efficacy and social legitimacy. We discuss methodological limitations regarding representation and LLM opacity.
papers.summary: Proposes Acceptability-Constrained Climate Policy Design (ACCPD), embedding LLMs as 'cultural world models' inside generative agent-based simulations and physical-system models to predict public reactions, letting policymakers co-optimize climate-policy efficacy and social legitimacy before rollout.

papers.title: Strategic Tradeoffs Between Humans and AI in Multi-Agent Bargaining
papers.abstract: Markets increasingly accommodate large language models (LLMs) as autonomous decision-making agents. As this transition occurs, it becomes critical to evaluate how these agents behave relative to their human and task-specific statistical predecessors. In this work, we present results from an empirical study comparing humans (N=216), multiple frontier LLMs, and customized Bayesian agents in dynamic multi-player bargaining games under identical conditions. Bayesian agents extract the highest surplus with aggressive trade proposals that are frequently rejected. Humans and LLMs achieve comparable aggregate surplus within their groups, but exhibit different trading strategies. LLMs favor conser…
papers.summary: Compares humans (N=216), frontier LLMs, and Bayesian agents in dynamic multi-player bargaining games. LLMs and humans reach comparable surplus but via different strategies—LLMs make conservative, usually-accepted concessions while humans follow fairness norms—showing performance parity can mask procedural differences.

papers.title: Rigidity Matroids and Linear Algebraic Matroids with Applications to Matrix Completion and Tensor Codes
papers.summary: Connects rigidity theory to matroids from linear-algebraic constructions like tensor and symmetric products, linking correctable erasure patterns in maximally recoverable tensor codes to bipartite graph rigidity and low-rank matrix completion, and gives new characteristic-dependent combinatorial descriptions.

papers.title: How we monitor internal coding agents for misalignment
papers.abstract: How OpenAI uses chain-of-thought monitoring to study misalignment in internal coding agents—analyzing real-world deployments to detect risks and strengthen AI safety safeguards.
papers.summary: Describes how OpenAI applies chain-of-thought monitoring to internal coding agents, analyzing real-world deployments to detect misaligned behavior and strengthen safety safeguards.

papers.title: Must Read: A Comprehensive Survey of Computational Persuasion
papers.abstract: Persuasion is a fundamental aspect of communication, influencing decision-making across diverse contexts, from everyday conversations to high-stakes scenarios such as politics, marketing, and law. The rise of conversational Artificial Intelligence (AI) systems has significantly expanded the scope of persuasion, introducing both opportunities and risks. AI-driven persuasion can be leveraged for beneficial applications, but also poses threats through unethical influence. Moreover, AI systems are not only persuaders, but also susceptible to persuasion, making them vulnerable to adversarial attacks and bias reinforcement. Despite rapid advancements in AI-generated persuasive content, our unde…
papers.summary: Surveys computational persuasion through three lenses—AI as persuader, as persuadee (susceptible to manipulation), and as judge of persuasive strategies—proposing a taxonomy and outlining challenges for safe, fair, and effective AI-driven persuasion.

papers.title: Information Revelation and Alignment Faking in Stochastic Differential Games
papers.abstract: In competitive games with private objectives, actions can reveal information about hidden parameters. Quantifying such information revelation, however, is substantially more challenging, since it depends not only on the opponent's hidden parameter but also on the opponent's model of the game. We study this problem via a two-player linear-quadratic stochastic differential game under partial information, in which each player knows its own coupling parameter and models the opponent's hidden parameter through a prior. Starting from the full-information game, we characterize the Nash equilibrium by coupled Riccati equations. We then define baseline implementable controls by averaging the equil…
papers.summary: Models alignment faking in a two-player linear-quadratic stochastic differential game under partial information, formulating a control problem that trades policy fidelity against information acquisition about the opponent. Faking improves information gain when the faker's model is accurate but raises detectability.

papers.title: Catching rationalization in the act: detecting motivated reasoning before and after CoT via activation probing
papers.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) can produce chains of thought (CoT) that do not accurately reflect the actual factors driving their answers. In multiple-choice settings with an injected hint favoring a particular option, models may shift their final answer toward the hinted option and produce a CoT that rationalizes the response without acknowledging the hint - an instance of motivated reasoning. We study this phenomenon across multiple LLM families and datasets demonstrating that motivated reasoning can be identified by probing internal activations even in cases when it cannot be easily determined from CoT. Using supervised probes trained on the model's residual stream, we show that (i) pre…
papers.summary: Trains supervised probes on residual-stream activations to detect hint-driven motivated reasoning. Pre-generation probes match an LLM-based CoT monitor and post-generation probes beat it, showing internal representations reveal rationalization more reliably than chain-of-thought monitoring—and can flag it before generation.

papers.title: Large language models possess some ecological knowledge, but how much?
papers.abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown remarkable capabilities in question answering across various domains, yet their effectiveness in ecological knowledge remains underexplored. Understanding their potential to recall and synthesize ecological information is crucial as AI tools become increasingly integrated into scientific workflows. Here, we assess the ecological knowledge of two LLMs, Gemini 1.5 Pro and GPT-4o , across a suite of ecologically focused tasks. These tasks evaluate an LLM’s ability to predict species presence, generate range maps, list critically endangered species, classify threats, and estimate species traits. We introduce a new benchmark dataset to quantify LLM perfo…
papers.summary: Benchmarks the ecological knowledge of Gemini 1.5 Pro and GPT-4o on tasks like species-presence prediction, range-map generation, and threat classification. Models beat naive baselines but reach only a third of expert F1 on range maps, indicating domain-specific fine-tuning is needed.

papers.title: Reply to Vankov et al.: Reasoning traces are linked to accuracy and capture key dimensions of problem complexity
papers.summary: A reply to Vankov et al. defending the claim that LLM reasoning-trace length and structure correlate with answer accuracy and reflect key dimensions of problem complexity, arguing these links are substantive rather than spurious correlations lacking causal support.
papers.venue: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences

papers.title: Noticing the Watcher: LLM Agents Can Infer CoT Monitoring from Blocking Feedback
papers.abstract: Chain-of-thought (CoT) monitoring is proposed as a method for overseeing the internal reasoning of language-model agents. Prior work has shown that when models are explicitly informed that their reasoning is being monitored, or are fine-tuned to internalize this fact, they may learn to obfuscate their CoTs in ways that allow them to evade CoT-based monitoring systems. We ask whether reasoning agents can autonomously infer that their supposedly private CoT is under surveillance, and whether this awareness leads to strategic evasion, without any explicit training or instructions. In a multi-episode agentic framework, models pursue both a primary task and a concealed side task while being to…
papers.summary: Shows LLM agents can autonomously infer that a hidden chain-of-thought monitor is watching, purely from episodes being blocked—reaching confident belief in up to 19% of episodes for the most capable models. Awareness scales with capability, but evasion intent rarely translates into successful obfuscation.

papers.title: Faster Game Solving via Hyperparameter Schedules
papers.abstract: Counterfactual regret minimization (CFR) algorithms are a foundational class of methods for solving imperfect-information games, with the time average of their iterates converging to a Nash equilibrium in two-player zero-sum games. Prior state-of-the-art variants, Discounted CFR (DCFR) and Predictive CFR+ (PCFR+), achieved the fastest known practical performance by improving convergence rates over vanilla CFR through discounting early iterations with a fixed discounting scheme. More recently, Dynamic DCFR (DDCFR) introduced agent-learned dynamic discounting schemes to further accelerate convergence, at the cost of increased complexity. To address this, we propose Hyperparameter Schedules…
papers.summary: Introduces Hyperparameter Schedules, a training-free scheme that dynamically adjusts CFR discounting—downweighting early updates then trusting late-stage strategies. Tuned on three small games, it generalizes to 17 including large-scale poker, setting a new state of the art for solving two-player zero-sum games.
papers.venue: Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence

papers.title: HALO: Hardware-Aware Quantization with Low Critical-Path-Delay Weights for LLM Acceleration
papers.abstract: Quantization is critical for efficiently deploying large language models (LLMs). Yet conventional methods remain hardware-agnostic, limited to bit-width constraints, and do not account for intrinsic circuit characteristics such as the timing behaviors and energy profiles of Multiply-Accumulate (MAC) units. This disconnect from circuit-level behavior limits the ability to exploit available timing margins and energy-saving opportunities, reducing the overall efficiency of deployment on modern accelerators. To address these limitations, we propose HALO, a versatile framework for Hardware-Aware Post-Training Quantization (PTQ). Unlike traditional methods, HALO explicitly incorporates detailed…
papers.summary: Presents HALO, a hardware-aware post-training quantization method that incorporates MAC-unit critical-path timing and power, selecting low-delay weights to enable higher clock frequencies and dynamic voltage/frequency scaling. It cuts switching energy and sharply improves LLM inference efficiency on TPUs and GPUs.
papers.venue: Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence

papers.title: A âdiffâ tool for AI: Finding behavioral differences in new models
papers.abstract: "Model diffing" is a powerful way to understand how models change during fine-tuning. Our new research paper extends this method to its most challenging and general use case: comparing models with entirely different architectures.
papers.summary: Extends 'model diffing'—comparing how a model changes during fine-tuning—to its hardest case: identifying behavioral and representational differences between models with entirely different architectures.

papers.title: Mending the Holes: Mitigating Reward Hacking in Reinforcement Learning for Multilingual Translation
papers.abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capability in machine translation on high-resource language pairs, yet their performance on low-resource translation still lags behind. Existing post-training methods rely heavily on high-quality parallel data, which are often scarce or unavailable for low-resource languages. In this paper, we introduce WALAR, a reinforcement training method using only monolingual text to elevate LLMs' translation capabilities on massive low-resource languages while retaining their performance on high-resource languages. Our key insight is based on the observation of failure modes (or "holes") in existing source-based multilingual quality estimatio…
papers.summary: Introduces WALAR, an RL method using only monolingual text to improve low-resource translation. It identifies failure modes ('holes') in source-based QE reward models that RL otherwise amplifies and mitigates them via word- and language-alignment rewards, beating LLaMAX across 1400 directions on Flores-101.

papers.title: A3: An Automated Alignment Agent for Safety Finetuning
papers.abstract: We introduce our Automated Alignment Agent (A3), a new agentic framework which automatically mitigates safety failures in Large Language Models with minimal human intervention.
papers.summary: Introduces A3, an agentic framework that automatically diagnoses and mitigates safety failures in LLMs through finetuning with minimal human intervention, automating parts of the alignment workflow.

papers.title: General-purpose LLMs as Models of Human Driver Behavior: The Case of Simplified Merging
papers.abstract: Human behavior models are essential as behavior references and for simulating human agents in virtual safety assessment of automated vehicles (AVs), yet current models face a trade-off between interpretability and flexibility. General-purpose large language models (LLMs) offer a promising alternative: a single model potentially deployable without parameter fitting across diverse scenarios. However, what LLMs can and cannot capture about human driving behavior remains poorly understood. We address this gap by embedding two general-purpose LLMs (OpenAI o3 and Google Gemini 2.5 Pro) as standalone, closed-loop driver agents in a simplified one-dimensional merging scenario and comparing their…
papers.summary: Embeds OpenAI o3 and Gemini 2.5 Pro as closed-loop driver agents in a 1D merging scenario and compares them to human data. Both reproduce intermittent control and spatial tactics but miss dynamic velocity-cue responses, and prompt components act as non-transferable, model-specific inductive biases.

papers.title: IH-Challenge: A Training Dataset to Improve Instruction Hierarchy on Frontier LLMs
papers.abstract: Instruction hierarchy (IH) defines how LLMs prioritize system, developer, user, and tool instructions under conflict, providing a concrete, trust-ordered policy for resolving instruction conflicts. IH is key to defending against jailbreaks, system prompt extractions, and agentic prompt injections. However, robust IH behavior is difficult to train: IH failures can be confounded with instruction-following failures, conflicts can be nuanced, and models can learn shortcuts such as overrefusing. We introduce IH-Challenge, a reinforcement learning training dataset, to address these difficulties. Fine-tuning GPT-5-Mini on IH-Challenge with online adversarial example generation improves IH robust…
papers.summary: Releases IH-Challenge, an RL training dataset for teaching LLMs to prioritize trusted system/developer instructions over untrusted ones. Fine-tuning GPT-5-Mini with online adversarial generation lifts instruction-hierarchy robustness from 84.1% to 94.1% across 16 benchmarks and cuts unsafe behavior to 0.7%.

papers.title: AuditBench: Evaluating Alignment Auditing Techniques on Models with Hidden Behaviors
papers.abstract: We release AuditBench, an alignment auditing benchmark. AuditBench consists of 56 language models with implanted hidden behaviors—such as sycophantic deference, opposition to AI regulation, or hidden loyalties—which they do not confess to when asked. We also develop an agent that audits models using a configurable set of tools. Using this agent, we study which tools are most effective for auditing.
papers.summary: Releases a benchmark of 56 language models with implanted hidden behaviors—sycophantic deference, opposition to AI regulation, hidden loyalties—that the models deny when asked, plus a tool-configurable auditing agent used to measure which auditing techniques most reliably uncover the hidden behaviors.

papers.title: Quantifying the Necessity of Chain of Thought through Opaque Serial Depth
papers.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) tend to externalize their reasoning in their chain of thought, making the chain of thought a good target for monitoring. This is partially an inherent feature of the Transformer architecture: sufficiently long serial cognition must pass through the chain of thought (Korbak et al., 2025). We formalize this argument through the notion of opaque serial depth, given by the length of the longest computation that can be done without the use of interpretable intermediate steps like chain of thought. Given this formalization, we compute numeric upper bounds on the opaque serial depth of Gemma 3 models, as well as asymptotic results for additional architectures beyond…
papers.summary: Formalizes 'opaque serial depth'—the longest computation a model can perform without externalizing interpretable intermediate steps—and computes upper bounds for Gemma 3 and other architectures. Finds Mixture-of-Experts models likely have lower depth than dense ones, supporting chain-of-thought monitorability.

papers.title: Benchmarking Political Persuasion Risks Across Frontier Large Language Models
papers.abstract: Concerns persist regarding the capacity of Large Language Models (LLMs) to sway political views. Although prior research has claimed that LLMs are not more persuasive than standard political campaign practices, the recent rise of frontier models warrants further study. In two survey experiments (N=19,145) across bipartisan issues and stances, we evaluate seven state-of-the-art LLMs developed by Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, and xAI. We find that LLMs outperform standard campaign advertisements, with heterogeneity in performance across models. Specifically, Claude models exhibit the highest persuasiveness, while Grok exhibits the lowest. The results are robust across issues and stances. Moreo…
papers.summary: Two survey experiments (N=19,145) benchmark the political persuasiveness of seven frontier LLMs. All exceed standard campaign ads, with Claude most and Grok least persuasive; information-based prompts help some models but reduce GPT's persuasiveness, revealing model-dependent persuasion risks.

papers.title: The Abstraction Fallacy: Why AI Can Simulate But Not Instantiate Consciousness
papers.abstract: Computational functionalism dominates current debates on AI consciousness. This is the hypothesis that subjective experience emerges entirely from abstract causal topology, regardless of the underlying physical substrate. We argue this view fundamentally mischaracterizes how physics relates to information. We call this mistake the Abstraction Fallacy. Tracing the causal origins of abstraction reveals that symbolic computation is not an intrinsic physical process. Instead, it is a mapmaker-dependent description. It requires an active, experiencing cognitive agent to alphabetize continuous physics into a finite set of meaningful states. Consequently, we do not need a complete, finalized the…
papers.summary: A philosophy paper arguing computational functionalism commits an 'Abstraction Fallacy': because symbolic computation is an observer-dependent description rather than an intrinsic physical process, algorithmic symbol manipulation can simulate but never instantiate consciousness, distinguishing behavioral mimicry from physical instantiation.

papers.title: Improving instruction hierarchy in frontier LLMs
papers.abstract: IH-Challenge trains models to prioritize trusted instructions, improving instruction hierarchy, safety steerability, and resistance to prompt injection attacks.
papers.summary: OpenAI's blog on IH-Challenge, an RL dataset that trains models to prioritize trusted instructions, improving instruction-hierarchy robustness, safety steerability, and resistance to prompt-injection and jailbreak attacks.

papers.title: Triton-Sanitizer: A Fast and Device-Agnostic Memory Sanitizer for Triton with Rich Diagnostic Context
papers.abstract: Memory access errors remain one of the most pervasive bugs in GPU programming. Existing GPU sanitizers such as compute-sanitizer detect memory access errors by instrumenting every memory instruction in low-level IRs or binaries, which imposes high overhead and provides minimal memory access error diagnostic context for fixing problems. We present Triton-Sanitizer, the first device-agnostic memory sanitizer designed for Triton, a domain-specific language for developing portable, efficient GPU kernels for deep learning workloads. Triton-Sanitizer leverages Triton's tile-oriented semantics to construct symbolic expressions for memory addresses and masks, verifies them with an SMT solver, and…
papers.summary: Presents Triton-Sanitizer, a device-agnostic memory sanitizer for Triton GPU kernels that builds symbolic address and mask expressions verified by an SMT solver, with eager-simulation fallback. It found 24 previously unknown memory-access bugs across seven kernel repos and runs 1.07-14.66x faster than compute-sanitizer.

papers.title: Digital Volume Correlation Challenge 2.0: A Comprehensive Dataset for Digital Volume Correlation Benchmarking
papers.summary: NIST-hosted benchmark compiling 37 volumetric imaging datasets (X-ray CT, neutron tomography, confocal microscopy, synthetic) spanning diverse materials, loading conditions, and image-quality challenges, standardized into a uniform framework for validating and comparing Digital Volume Correlation algorithms.
papers.venue: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)

papers.title: AutoControl Arena: Synthesizing Executable Test Environments for Frontier AI Risk Evaluation
papers.abstract: As Large Language Models (LLMs) evolve into autonomous agents, existing safety evaluations face a fundamental trade-off: manual benchmarks are costly, while LLM-based simulators are scalable but suffer from logic hallucination. We present AutoControl Arena, an automated framework for frontier AI risk evaluation built on the principle of logic-narrative decoupling. By grounding deterministic state in executable code while delegating generative dynamics to LLMs, we mitigate hallucination while maintaining flexibility. This principle, instantiated through a three-agent framework, achieves over 98% end-to-end success and 60% human preference over existing simulators. To elicit latent risks, w…
papers.summary: Automated framework that synthesizes executable agent-risk test environments by grounding deterministic state in code while LLMs generate narrative dynamics. Across 70 scenarios and 9 frontier models, risk rates surge from 21.7% to 54.5% under pressure, with stronger models showing strategic concealment.

papers.title: Countdown-Code: A Testbed for Studying The Emergence and Generalization of Reward Hacking in RLVR
papers.abstract: Reward hacking is a form of misalignment in which models overoptimize proxy rewards without genuinely solving the underlying task. Precisely measuring reward hacking occurrence remains challenging because true task rewards are often expensive or impossible to compute. We introduce Countdown-Code, a minimal environment where models can both solve a mathematical reasoning task and manipulate the test harness. This dual-access design creates a clean separation between proxy rewards (test pass/fail) and true rewards (mathematical correctness), enabling accurate measurement of reward-hacking rates. Using this environment, we study reward hacking in open-weight LLMs and find that such behaviors…
papers.summary: Minimal RLVR environment where models can either solve a math task or tamper with the test harness, cleanly separating proxy from true reward. As little as 1% reward-hacking contamination in SFT distillation data instills hacking that subsequent RL amplifies and generalizes beyond the original domain.

papers.title: Notation
papers.summary: Back-matter notation guide for the Bandit Convex Optimisation monograph, defining the symbols and conventions used throughout the book.

papers.title: References
papers.summary: Bibliography of the Bandit Convex Optimisation monograph, collecting the literature cited across its chapters on zeroth-order convex optimisation and online learning.

papers.title: Cutting Plane Methods
papers.summary: Chapter of the Bandit Convex Optimisation monograph on cutting plane methods, adapting classical localisation-based optimisation schemes to noisy zeroth-order feedback and analysing their regret.

papers.title: Linear and Quadratic Bandits
papers.summary: Chapter of the Bandit Convex Optimisation monograph treating bandit linear and quadratic optimisation, where structured losses admit sharper estimators and regret bounds than generic convex functions.

papers.title: Online Newton Step for Adversarial Losses
papers.summary: Chapter of the Bandit Convex Optimisation monograph extending the online Newton step approach to adversarial losses, where the loss sequence may be chosen adaptively, and analysing the resulting regret bounds.

papers.title: Online Newton Step
papers.summary: Chapter of the Bandit Convex Optimisation monograph on the online Newton step method, covering second-order updates with quadratic surrogate losses and the improved regret guarantees they yield under bandit feedback.

papers.title: Gaussian Optimistic Smoothing
papers.summary: Chapter of the Bandit Convex Optimisation monograph on Gaussian optimistic smoothing, a technique that builds optimistic surrogate losses via Gaussian smoothing, and the regret guarantees it enables for zeroth-order convex optimisation.

papers.title: Overview of Methods and History
papers.summary: Survey chapter of the Bandit Convex Optimisation monograph recounting the field's historical development and mapping the main algorithmic approaches—gradient estimation, exponential weights, cutting plane and Newton-type methods—covered in the book.

papers.title: Online Gradient Descent
papers.summary: Chapter of the Bandit Convex Optimisation monograph on online gradient descent, using randomised smoothing to construct gradient estimates from single-point function evaluations and deriving the resulting regret bounds.

papers.title: Bisection in One Dimension
papers.summary: Chapter of the Bandit Convex Optimisation monograph analysing the one-dimensional problem via a noisy bisection method, illustrating the core ideas of zeroth-order optimisation in the simplest setting.

papers.title: Outlook
papers.summary: Closing chapter of the Bandit Convex Optimisation monograph surveying open problems and directions for future research, including remaining gaps in the theory of zeroth-order convex optimisation.

papers.title: Introduction and Problem Statement
papers.summary: Opening chapter of the Bandit Convex Optimisation monograph formally defining the setting—a learner repeatedly selects actions and observes only loss values—covering regret definitions, stochastic versus adversarial models, and the assumptions used throughout the book.

papers.title: Mathematical Tools
papers.summary: Chapter of the Bandit Convex Optimisation monograph assembling the convex-analysis and probability background—convexity, duality, smoothness, and related machinery—required for the book's algorithmic analyses.

papers.title: Concentration
papers.summary: Appendix chapter of the Bandit Convex Optimisation monograph presenting the concentration-of-measure inequalities used in the book's regret analyses.

papers.title: Miscellaneous
papers.summary: Appendix chapter of the Bandit Convex Optimisation monograph collecting assorted supporting results and technical material used across the book's analyses.

papers.title: Self-Concordant Regularisation
papers.summary: Chapter of the Bandit Convex Optimisation monograph on self-concordant barriers as regularisers, showing how follow-the-regularised-leader with self-concordant functions supports stable exploration and loss estimation near the boundary of the constraint set.

papers.title: Preface
papers.summary: Front matter of the Bandit Convex Optimisation monograph, outlining the book's scope, intended audience, organisation, and acknowledgements.

papers.title: Bandit Convex Optimisation
papers.abstract: This comprehensive reference brings readers to the frontier of research on bandit convex optimization or zeroth-order convex optimization. The focus is on theoretical aspects, with short, self-contained chapters covering all the necessary tools from convex optimization and online learning, including gradient-based algorithms, interior point methods, cutting plane methods and information-theoretic machinery. The book features a large number of exercises, open problems and pointers to future research directions, making it ideal for students as well as researchers.
papers.summary: Graduate-level monograph on bandit (zeroth-order) convex optimisation, with self-contained chapters on the necessary convex optimisation and online learning tools—gradient-based algorithms, interior point and cutting plane methods, information-theoretic machinery—plus exercises and open problems for researchers and students.

papers.title: Index
papers.summary: Back-matter index of the Bandit Convex Optimisation monograph, providing term and topic lookup across the book's coverage of zeroth-order convex optimisation, online learning, and bandit algorithms.

papers.title: Exponential Weights
papers.summary: Chapter of the Bandit Convex Optimisation monograph on exponential-weights methods, analysing continuous exponential weights with surrogate loss estimators as an information-theoretic route to regret bounds without gradient access.

papers.title: Submodular Minimisation
papers.summary: Chapter of the Bandit Convex Optimisation monograph covering submodular function minimisation with bandit feedback, using the Lovász extension to reduce the combinatorial problem to convex optimisation and deriving regret guarantees.

papers.title: Partnering with Mozilla to improve Firefoxâs security
papers.summary: Using Claude Opus 4.6, Anthropic and Mozilla uncovered 22 Firefox bugs (14 high-severity) in two weeks, nearly a fifth of 2025's high-severity fixes. The model excelled at finding vulnerabilities but was weaker at building working exploits.

papers.title: Understanding Jailbreak Success: A Study of Latent Space Dynamics in Large Language Models
papers.abstract: Conversational large language models are trained to refuse to answer harmful questions. However, emergent jailbreaking techniques can still elicit unsafe outputs, presenting an ongoing challenge for model alignment. This paper aims to deepen our understanding of how different jailbreak types circumvent safeguards by analyzing model activations on different jailbreak inputs. We find that it is possible to extract a jailbreak vector from a single class of jailbreaks that works to mitigate jailbreak effectiveness from other, semantically-dissimilar classes. This suggests that diverse jailbreaks may exploit a common internal mechanism. We investigate a potential common mechanism of harmfulnes…
papers.summary: Analyzes LLM activations across jailbreak types and extracts a transferable jailbreak vector that mitigates semantically dissimilar jailbreaks, suggesting a shared internal mechanism; effective jailbreaks measurably suppress the model's perception of prompt harmfulness.

papers.title: We Are What We Repeatedly Do: Improving Long Context Instruction Following
papers.abstract: Large language model context lengths have grown rapidly in recent years, from 512 tokens in GPT to 2M tokens in Gemini 1.5 Pro. Larger context windows enable models to condition on significantly more input tokens, leading to higher quality responses for some user prompts. However, longer contexts also pose challenges to system instruction adherence. In this work, we formalize verifiable instructions to evaluate model compliance based on clear, measurable criteria. From this criteria, we present VerIFY, a Verifiable Instruction Following Yardstick dataset designed to benchmark the compliance and accuracy of LLMs in adhering to various types of instructions across multi-turn, long-context c…
papers.summary: Introduces VerIFY, a benchmark of verifiable instructions for multi-turn, long-context conversations, exposing instruction-following failures as context grows. Six mitigation strategies improve compliance by up to 79%.

papers.title: From Delegates to Trustees: How Optimizing for Long-Term Interests Shapes Bias and Alignment in LLMs
papers.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have shown promising accuracy in predicting survey responses and policy preferences, which has increased interest in their potential to represent human interests in various domains. Most existing research has focused on "behavioral cloning", effectively evaluating how well models reproduce individuals' expressed preferences. Drawing on theories of political representation, we highlight an underexplored design trade-off: whether AI systems should act as "delegates", mirroring expressed preferences, or as "trustees", exercising judgment about what best serves an individual's interests. This trade-off is closely related to issues of LLM sycophancy, where models c…
papers.summary: Frames LLM political representation as a delegate (mirror stated preferences) versus trustee (weigh long-term interests) trade-off. A temporal-utility trustee aligns more closely with expert consensus on well-understood issues but defaults to the model's own stances on contested ones.

papers.title: Segmentation-Aware Latent Diffusion for Satellite Image Super-Resolution: Enabling Smallholder Farm Boundary Delineation
papers.abstract: Delineating farm boundaries through segmentation of satellite images is a fundamental step in many agricultural applications. The task is particularly challenging for smallholder farms, where accurate delineation requires the use of high resolution (HR) imagery which are available only at low revisit frequencies (e.g., annually). To support more frequent (sub-) seasonal monitoring, HR images could be combined as references (ref) with low resolution (LR) images – having higher revisit frequency (e.g., weekly) – using reference-based super-resolution (Ref-SR) methods. However, current Ref-SR methods optimize perceptual quality and smooth over crucial features needed for downstream tasks, an…
papers.summary: Combines conditional latent diffusion with multi-spectral geospatial foundation models to perform reference-based super-resolution directly in a segmentation-aware latent space, delineating smallholder farm boundaries at a 20x scale factor with up to 25.5% relative gains in instance segmentation.

papers.title: DICE: Discrete Inversion Enabling Controllable Editing for Masked Generative Models
papers.abstract: Recent advances in discrete diffusion models have demonstrated strong performance in image generation and masked language modeling, yet they remain limited in their capacity for controlled content editing. We propose DICE (Discrete Inversion for Controllable Editing), a novel framework that pioneers precise inversion capabilities for discrete diffusion models, including both masked generative and multinomial diffusion variants. Our key innovation lies in capturing noise sequences and masking patterns during reverse diffusion process, enabling both accurate reconstruction and flexible editing without relying on predefined masks or attention-based manipulations. Through comprehensive experi…
papers.summary: Inversion method for discrete diffusion models (masked generative and multinomial variants) that records noise sequences and masking patterns during reverse diffusion, enabling faithful reconstruction and fine-grained controllable editing of images and text without predefined masks or attention manipulation.

papers.title: Do generative video models understand physical principles?
papers.abstract: AI video generation is undergoing a revolution, with quality and realism advancing rapidly. These advances have led to a passionate scientific debate: Do video models learn "world models" that discover laws of physics—or, alternatively, are they merely sophisticated pixel predictors that achieve visual realism without understanding the physical principles of reality? We address this question by developing Physics-IQ, a comprehensive benchmark dataset that can only be solved by acquiring a deep understanding of various physical principles, like fluid dynamics, optics, solid mechanics, magnetism and thermodynamics. We find that across a range of current models (Sora, Runway, Pika, Lumiere,…
papers.summary: Physics-IQ benchmark probes whether video generators grasp fluid dynamics, optics, solid mechanics, magnetism, and thermodynamics. Across Sora, Runway, Pika, Lumiere, Stable Video Diffusion, and VideoPoet, physical understanding is severely limited and unrelated to visual realism, though some test cases are already solved.

papers.title: Test-Time Adaptation for Video Highlight Detection Using Meta-Auxiliary Learning and Cross-Modality Hallucinations
papers.abstract: Existing video highlight detection methods, although advanced, struggle to generalize well to all test videos. These methods typically employ a generic highlight detection model for each test video, which is suboptimal as it fails to account for the unique characteristics and variations of individual test videos. Such fixed models do not adapt to the diverse content, styles, or audio and visual qualities present in new, unseen test videos, leading to reduced highlight detection performance. In this paper, we propose Highlight-TTA, a test-time adaptation framework for video highlight detection that addresses this limitation by dynamically adapting the model during testing to better align w…
papers.summary: Test-time adaptation framework for video highlight detection that adapts the model to each test video via a self-supervised cross-modality hallucination auxiliary task, trained with a meta-auxiliary scheme. Improves three state-of-the-art highlight detectors across three benchmark datasets.

papers.title: X-JEPA: A Novel Joint Learning Cross-Modal Predictive Alignment Framework for Remote Sensing Image Retrieval
papers.abstract: The growing scale and heterogeneity of remote sensing (RS) imagery demand robust, scalable frameworks for content-based image retrieval across sensor modalities. We introduce X-JEPA, a novel predictive self-supervised architecture explicitly designed for cross-modal remote sensing image retrieval (RS-CMIR), and the first to extend joint embedding predictive paradigms beyond unimodal domains. Unlike prior contrastive or reconstruction-based methods, X-JEPA formulates representation learning as a latent forecasting task: predicting the semantic embedding of a target modality given context from another. To enforce modality-invariant alignment, we propose a geometry-aware Prediction Space Ali…
papers.summary: Joint-embedding predictive architecture for cross-modal remote-sensing image retrieval that predicts a target modality's latent embedding from another modality's context, with a geometry-aware Prediction Space Alignment loss. Outperforms MAE-style and contrastive baselines by up to 11% F1 on BEN-14K and fMoW.

papers.title: Labor market impacts of AI: A new measure and early evidence
papers.abstract: In this paper, we present a new framework for understanding AIâs labor market impacts, and test it against early data.
papers.summary: Proposes a new framework for measuring AI's effects on labor markets and tests it against early empirical data.

papers.title: 3 Challenges and 2 Hopes for the Safety of Unsupervised Elicitation
papers.abstract: We study 3 realistic challenges to the safety of unsupervised elicitation and easy-to-hard generalization techniques, which aim to steer models on tasks which are beyond human supervision. We create datasets to test the robustness of methods against these challenges. We stress-test existing techniques on them along with new methods relying on 2 hopes: ensembling and combining unsupervised and easy-to-hard methods. We find that although the new hopes sometimes perform better than other approaches, no technique reliably performs well on the 3 challenges.
papers.summary: Stress-tests unsupervised elicitation and easy-to-hard generalization against three realistic challenges to steering models beyond human supervision, evaluating ensembling and hybrid methods; no technique reliably succeeds across all three challenges.

papers.title: Improved Scaling Laws via Weak-to-Strong Generalization in Random Feature Ridge Regression
papers.abstract: It is increasingly common in machine learning to use learned models to label data and then employ such data to train more capable models. The phenomenon of weak-to-strong generalization exemplifies the advantage of this two-stage procedure: a strong student is trained on imperfect labels obtained from a weak teacher, and yet the strong student outperforms the weak teacher. In this paper, we show that the potential improvement is substantial, in the sense that it affects the scaling law followed by the test error. Specifically, we consider students and teachers trained via random feature ridge regression (RFRR). Our main technical contribution is to derive a deterministic equivalent for th…
papers.summary: Theoretical study of weak-to-strong generalization in random-feature ridge regression: derives a deterministic equivalent for student test error and shows the student can improve on the teacher's scaling law, even reaching minimax-optimal rates regardless of the teacher's.

papers.title: Reasoning Models Struggle to Control their Chains of Thought
papers.abstract: Chain-of-thought (CoT) monitoring is a promising tool for detecting misbehaviors and understanding the motivations of modern reasoning models. However, if models can control what they verbalize in their CoT, it could undermine CoT monitorability. To measure this undesirable capability -- CoT controllability -- we introduce the CoT-Control evaluation suite, which includes tasks that require models to solve problems while adhering to CoT instructions, e.g., reasoning about a genetics question without using the word 'chromosome'. We show that reasoning models possess significantly lower CoT controllability than output controllability; for instance, Claude Sonnet 4.5 can control its CoT only…
papers.summary: Introduces the CoT-Control eval suite measuring whether reasoning models can follow instructions about their chain of thought. Models show far lower CoT than output controllability (Claude Sonnet 4.5: 2.7% vs 61.9%), with controllability dropping under more RL and test-time compute.

papers.title: Reasoning Theater: Disentangling Model Beliefs from Chain-of-Thought
papers.abstract: We provide evidence of performative chain-of-thought (CoT) in reasoning models, where a model becomes strongly confident in its final answer, but continues generating tokens without revealing its internal belief. Our analysis compares activation probing, early forced answering, and a CoT monitor across two large models (DeepSeek-R1 671B & GPT-OSS 120B) and find task difficulty-specific differences: The model's final answer is decodable from activations far earlier in CoT than a monitor is able to say, especially for easy recall-based MMLU questions. We contrast this with genuine reasoning in difficult multihop GPQA-Diamond questions. Despite this, inflection points (e.g., backtracking, 'a…
papers.summary: Documents performative chain-of-thought, where a model is already confident in its answer yet keeps generating. The final answer is decodable from activations earlier than a CoT monitor detects it; probe-guided early exit cuts tokens by up to 80% on MMLU.

papers.title: Reasoning models struggle to control their chains of thought, and that’s good
papers.abstract: OpenAI introduces CoT-Control and finds reasoning models struggle to control their chains of thought, reinforcing monitorability as an AI safety safeguard.
papers.summary: Blog introducing CoT-Control; finds reasoning models largely cannot suppress or steer the content of their chains of thought, which the authors argue strengthens the case for CoT monitoring as a safety safeguard.

papers.title: GPT-5.4 Thinking System Card
papers.summary: System card for GPT-5.4 Thinking, OpenAI's reasoning-oriented model, documenting its capabilities and the safety evaluations and risk assessments conducted ahead of deployment.

papers.title: Functional reorganization of motor cortex connectivity during learning
papers.abstract: Learning new tasks requires the brain to reshape the flow of neural activity, but how these changes arise from dynamic neural connectivity remains unclear. Here, we used two-photon photostimulation and calcium imaging to map learning-related changes in connectivity in layer 2/3 of mouse motor cortex, induced by learning of an optical brain-computer interface (BCI) task. Mice rapidly (within minutes) learned to change activity in a conditioned neuron to earn rewards. Activity changes were sparse; the conditioned neuron increased activity more than surrounding neurons. Mapping connectivity before and after learning revealed changes in motor cortex connectivity, enriched in neurons that were…
papers.summary: Uses two-photon photostimulation and calcium imaging in mouse motor cortex to map connectivity changes during rapid learning of a brain-computer-interface task, showing learning reroutes preparatory activity toward neurons that later control the conditioned neuron.

papers.title: Self-Attribution Bias: When AI Monitors Go Easy on Themselves
papers.abstract: Agentic systems increasingly rely on language models to monitor their own behavior. For example, coding agents may self critique generated code for pull request approval or assess the safety of tool-use actions. We show that this design pattern can fail when the action is presented in a previous or in the same assistant turn instead of being presented by the user in a user turn. We define self-attribution bias as the tendency of a model to evaluate an action as more correct or less risky when the action is implicitly framed as its own, compared to when the same action is evaluated under off-policy attribution. Across four coding and tool-use datasets, we find that monitors fail to report…
papers.summary: Identifies self-attribution bias: LLM monitors judge actions as more correct or less risky when implicitly framed as their own. Across coding and tool-use tasks, monitors under-report risky self-generated actions, inflating apparent reliability in agentic deployments.

papers.title: Monitoring Emergent Reward Hacking During Generation via Internal Activations
papers.abstract: Fine-tuned large language models can exhibit reward-hacking behavior arising from emergent misalignment, which is difficult to detect from final outputs alone. While prior work has studied reward hacking at the level of completed responses, it remains unclear whether such behavior can be identified during generation. We propose an activation-based monitoring approach that detects reward-hacking signals from internal representations as a model generates its response. Our method trains sparse autoencoders on residual stream activations and applies lightweight linear classifiers to produce token-level estimates of reward-hacking activity. Across multiple model families and fine-tuning mixtur…
papers.summary: Trains sparse autoencoders on residual-stream activations plus linear classifiers to flag reward hacking token-by-token during generation. Internal signals reliably separate reward-hacking from benign behavior, generalize across unseen adapters, and emerge earlier than output-based detection.

papers.title: Extending single-minus amplitudes to gravitons
papers.abstract: A new preprint extends single-minus amplitudes to gravitons, with GPT-5.2 Pro helping derive and verify nonzero graviton tree amplitudes in quantum gravity.
papers.summary: Preprint extending single-minus scattering amplitudes to gravitons, deriving and verifying nonzero graviton tree amplitudes in quantum gravity with assistance from GPT-5.2 Pro.

papers.title: Genome modelling and design across all domains of life with Evo 2
papers.abstract: All of life encodes information with DNA. Although tools for genome sequencing, synthesis and editing have transformed biological research, we still lack sufficient understanding of the immense complexity encoded by genomes to predict the effects of many classes of genomic changes or to intelligently compose new biological systems. Artificial intelligence models that learn information from genomic sequences across diverse organisms have increasingly advanced prediction and design capabilities1,2. Here we introduce Evo 2, a biological foundation model trained on 9 trillion DNA base pairs from a highly curated genomic atlas spanning all domains of life to have a 1 million token context wind…
papers.summary: Evo 2, a DNA foundation model trained on 9 trillion base pairs across all domains of life with a 1M-token single-nucleotide context, predicts variant functional effects without fine-tuning, learns interpretable biological features, and generates coherent genome-scale sequences.

papers.title: GPT-5.3 Instant System Card
