papers.summary: System card for GPT-5.3 Instant, OpenAI's latency-optimized model, documenting its capabilities and the safety evaluations and risk assessments conducted prior to release.

papers.title: Frontier Models Can Take Actions at Low Probabilities
papers.abstract: Pre-deployment evaluations inspect only a limited sample of model actions. A malicious model seeking to evade oversight could exploit this by randomizing when to "defect": misbehaving so rarely that no malicious actions are observed during evaluation, but often enough that they occur eventually in deployment. But this requires taking actions at very low rates, while maintaining calibration. Are frontier models even capable of that? We prompt the GPT-5, Claude-4.5 and Qwen-3 families to take a target action at low probabilities (e.g. 0.01%), either given directly or requiring derivation, and evaluate their calibration (i.e. whether they perform the target action roughly 1 in 10,000 times w…
papers.summary: Tests whether frontier models can take a target action at very low, calibrated rates to evade pre-deployment evaluation. With in-context entropy they stay calibrated below 1-in-100,000, but low-rate strategies require explicit chain-of-thought, so monitors can still catch them.

papers.title: Abstractive Red-Teaming of Language Model Character
papers.abstract: We introduce abstractive red-teaming, a means of testing language models’ adherence to a character specification that searches for natural-language categories of user queries that cause models to violate the specification. Unlike static evaluations, which miss rare failures, or prompt optimization approaches, which find adversarial strings that real users are unlikely to produce, the categories found by abstractive red-teaming are general enough to appear in real deployments but specific enough to reliably trigger violations. Across seven models and 12 character principles, we show that our approach identifies rare character violations using far fewer queries than a full deployment, and s…
papers.summary: Abstractive red-teaming searches for natural-language query categories that make models violate a character specification. Across seven models and twelve principles it surfaces rare, realistic failures, including AI-doom rhetoric and contraband recommendations, using far fewer queries than full deployment.

papers.title: Curation Leaks: Membership Inference Attacks against Data Curation for Machine Learning
papers.abstract: In machine learning, curation is used to select the most valuable data for improving both model accuracy and computational efficiency. Recently, curation has also been explored as a solution for private machine learning: rather than training directly on sensitive data, which is known to leak information through model predictions, the private data is used only to guide the selection of useful public data. The resulting model is then trained solely on curated public data. It is tempting to assume that such a model is privacy-preserving because it has never seen the private data. Yet, we show that without further protection, curation pipelines can still leak private information. Specifically…
papers.summary: Shows data-curation pipelines leak private information: novel membership-inference attacks target curation scores, subset selection, and the final model, which leaks membership even when trained solely on curated public data. Differentially private adaptations mitigate the leakage.

papers.title: An update on our model deprecation commitments for Claude Opus 3
papers.summary: Anthropic commits to keeping retired Claude Opus 3 accessible to paid users and via API on request, and gives the model a venue to publish essays reflecting preferences it expressed during retirement interviews.

papers.title: Hidden Topics: Measuring Sensitive AI Beliefs with List Experiments
papers.abstract: How can researchers identify beliefs that large language models (LLMs) hide? As LLMs become more sophisticated and the prevalence of alignment faking increases, combined with their growing integration into high-stakes decision-making, responding to this challenge has become critical. This paper proposes that a list experiment, a simple method widely used in the social sciences, can be applied to study the hidden beliefs of LLMs. List experiments were originally developed to circumvent social desirability bias in human respondents, which closely parallels alignment faking in LLMs. The paper implements a list experiment on models developed by Anthropic, Google, and OpenAI and finds hidden a…
papers.summary: Adapts list experiments from social science to surface beliefs LLMs hide. Across Anthropic, Google, and OpenAI models it detects concealed approval of mass surveillance and some approval of torture, discrimination, and first nuclear strike; a placebo validates the method.

papers.title: The Ghost in the Grammar: Methodological Anthropomorphism in AI Safety Evaluations
papers.abstract: This essay offers a philosophical analysis of the field of AI safety based on recent technical reports, with particular focus on Anthropic's study on "agentic misalignment" in frontier language models. It examines the recurring anthropomorphism in the field: the tendency of researchers and developers to project categories such as "intention," "persona," and even "feelings" onto AI systems without adequate conceptual problematization. It argues that this anthropomorphism affects not only the interpretation of results, but also the very methodological construction of safety evaluations. Through the analysis of two central experiments -- the blackmail case involving the agent "Alex" and the…
papers.summary: Philosophical critique of anthropomorphism in AI safety evaluations, centered on Anthropic's agentic-misalignment work. Argues that projecting intention and persona onto models shapes methodology, and that the real risk is structural incoherence plus anthropomorphic framing, not emergent agency.

papers.title: Counterfactual Simulation Training for Chain-of-Thought Faithfulness
papers.abstract: Inspecting Chain-of-Thought reasoning is among the most common means of understanding why an LLM produced its output. But well-known problems with CoT faithfulness severely limit what insights can be gained from this practice. In this paper, we introduce a training method called Counterfactual Simulation Training (CST), which aims to improve CoT faithfulness by rewarding CoTs that enable a simulator to accurately predict a model's outputs over counterfactual inputs. We apply CST in two settings: (1) CoT monitoring with cue-based counterfactuals, to detect when models rely on spurious features, reward hack, or are sycophantic, and (2) counterfactual simulation over generic model-based coun…
papers.summary: Counterfactual Simulation Training rewards chains of thought that let a simulator predict the model's outputs on counterfactual inputs, improving CoT faithfulness. It raises monitor accuracy by 35 points for detecting spurious-feature reliance, reward hacking, and sycophancy.

papers.title: The persona selection model
papers.abstract: Why do AI assistants like Claude sometimes seem surprisingly human. We advance a theory.
papers.summary: Advances the persona selection model: LLMs simulate diverse characters during pretraining, and post-training surfaces a particular Assistant persona, offering a theory for why AI assistants behave in human-like ways.

papers.title: Anthropic Education Report: The AI Fluency Index
papers.abstract: We tracked 11 observable behaviors across thousands of Claude.ai conversations to build the AI Fluency Index â a baseline for measuring how people collaborate with AI today.
papers.summary: Tracks 11 observable behaviors across thousands of Claude.ai conversations to build the AI Fluency Index, a baseline measure of how people currently collaborate with AI.

papers.title: The Persona Selection Model: Why AI Assistants might Behave like Humans
papers.abstract: We describe the persona selection model (PSM): the idea that LLMs learn to simulate diverse characters during pre-training, and post-training elicits and refines a particular such Assistant persona. Interactions with an AI assistant are then well-understood as being interactions with the Assistant—something roughly like a character in an LLM-generated story. We survey empirical behavioral, generalization, and interpretability-based evidence for PSM. PSM has consequences for AI development, such as recommending anthropomorphic reasoning about AI psychology and introduction of positive AI archetypes into training data. An important open question is how exhaustive PSM is, especially whether…
papers.summary: Presents the persona selection model: LLMs learn to simulate many characters during pretraining, and post-training elicits a specific Assistant persona. Surveys behavioral, generalization, and interpretability evidence and discusses implications, including whether agency can exist outside the persona.

papers.title: IR$^3$: Contrastive Inverse Reinforcement Learning for Interpretable Detection and Mitigation of Reward Hacking
papers.abstract: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) enables powerful LLM alignment but can introduce reward hacking - models exploit spurious correlations in proxy rewards without genuine alignment. Compounding this, the objectives internalized during RLHF remain opaque, making hacking behaviors difficult to detect or correct. We introduce IR3 (Interpretable Reward Reconstruction and Rectification), a framework that reverse-engineers, interprets, and surgically repairs the implicit objectives driving RLHF-tuned models. We propose Contrastive Inverse Reinforcement Learning (C-IRL), which reconstructs the implicit reward function by contrasting paired responses from post-alignment and baselin…
papers.summary: IR3 reverse-engineers RLHF's implicit reward via contrastive inverse RL, decomposes it with sparse autoencoders into interpretable features to spot reward-hacking signatures, then surgically mitigates them, achieving 0.89 correlation with ground-truth reward and over 90% precision while preserving capabilities.

papers.title: Why we no longer evaluate SWE-bench Verified
papers.abstract: SWE-bench Verified is increasingly contaminated and mismeasures frontier coding progress. Our analysis shows flawed tests and training leakage. We recommend SWE-bench Pro.
papers.summary: Argues SWE-bench Verified is increasingly contaminated by flawed tests and training leakage, causing it to mismeasure frontier coding progress; OpenAI stops reporting it and recommends SWE-bench Pro instead.

papers.title: Resonances in binary extreme-mass-ratio inspirals
papers.abstract: Stellar-mass binaries evolving in the vicinity of supermassive black holes (SMBHs) may be common in the universe, either in active galactic nuclei or in other astrophysical environments. Here, we study in detail the resonant excitation of SMBH modes driven by a nearby stellar-mass binary. The resulting resonant energy fluxes vary with the orbital location and frequency of the binary, exhibiting a rich and complex structure. In particular, we find that the total energy flux radiated to infinity is maximized at a gravitationalwave frequency that is close, but not exactly equal to, the real part of the corresponding quasi-normal-mode frequency. Moreover, as the binary is moved farther away f…
papers.summary: Studies resonant excitation of supermassive-black-hole modes driven by a nearby stellar-mass binary, mapping how radiated energy flux depends on the binary's orbital position and frequency, including richer, less-damped mode structure for rotating Kerr black holes.

papers.title: Analyzing and Improving Chain-of-Thought Monitorability Through Information Theory
papers.abstract: Chain-of-thought (CoT) monitors are LLM-based systems that analyze reasoning traces to detect when outputs may exhibit attributes of interest, such as test-hacking behavior during code generation. In this paper, we use information-theoretic analysis to show that non-zero mutual information between CoT and output is a necessary but not sufficient condition for CoT monitorability. We identify two sources of approximation error that may undermine the performance of CoT monitors in practice: information gap, which measures the extent to which the monitor can extract the information available in CoT, and elicitation error, which measures the extent to which the monitor approximates the optimal…
papers.summary: Applies information theory to chain-of-thought monitorability, showing nonzero CoT-output mutual information is necessary but not sufficient, and isolating information-gap and elicitation errors. Two training objectives improve monitor accuracy while preventing CoT degeneration under reward hacking.

papers.title: Gradient Regularization Prevents Reward Hacking in Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback and Verifiable Rewards
papers.abstract: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) or Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) are two key steps in the post-training of modern Language Models (LMs). A common problem is reward hacking, where the policy may exploit inaccuracies of the reward and learn an unintended behavior. Most previous works address this by limiting the policy update with a Kullback-Leibler (KL) penalty towards a reference model. We propose a different framing: Train the LM in a way that biases policy updates towards regions in which the reward is more accurate. First, we derive a theoretical connection between the accuracy of a reward model and the flatness of an optimum at convergence. Gradient regularization (GR) c…
papers.summary: Derives a link between reward-model accuracy and the flatness of converged optima, then uses gradient regularization to bias RLHF/RLVR updates toward flatter regions; it outperforms KL penalties and curbs reward hacking across diverse tasks.

papers.title: Our First Proof submissions
papers.abstract: We share our AI model’s proof attempts for the First Proof math challenge, testing research-grade reasoning on expert-level problems.
papers.summary: OpenAI shares its model's proof attempts submitted to the First Proof math challenge, probing research-grade mathematical reasoning on expert-level open problems.

papers.title: Advancing independent research on AI alignment
papers.abstract: OpenAI commits $7.5M to The Alignment Project to fund independent AI alignment research, strengthening global efforts to address AGI safety and security risks.
papers.summary: OpenAI commits $7.5M to The Alignment Project, funding independent AI alignment research aimed at AGI safety and security risks.

papers.title: Roadmap on fast machine learning for science
papers.abstract: Abstract The need for microsecond speed machine learning (ML) inference for particle physics experiments has emerged in recent years, in particular for the forthcoming upgrades to the experiments at the Large Hadron Collider at CERN. A community has grown around the need to develop the custom hardware platforms and tools required. The material presented in this report is drawn from the latest workshop held by the fast ML for science community and comprises of a collection of perspectives on the status of fast ML in different scientific domains, and the supporting technology.
papers.summary: Community roadmap surveying microsecond-latency ML inference needs and the custom hardware platforms and tooling that support them, spanning particle physics at the LHC and other scientific domains.

papers.title: Measuring AI agent autonomy in practice
papers.abstract: How much autonomy do people grant agents? How does that change as people gain experience? We analyzed millions of human-agent interactions to answer these and other questions.
papers.summary: Analyzes millions of human-agent interactions to quantify how much autonomy people grant AI agents and how that delegation shifts as users gain experience.

papers.title: Introducing EVMbench
papers.abstract: OpenAI and Paradigm introduce EVMbench, a benchmark evaluating AI agents’ ability to detect, patch, and exploit high-severity smart contract vulnerabilities.
papers.summary: OpenAI and Paradigm introduce EVMbench, a benchmark measuring AI agents' ability to detect, patch, and exploit high-severity smart-contract vulnerabilities.

papers.title: A roadmap for evaluating moral competence in large language models
papers.summary: Argues evaluation should shift from moral performance to moral competence, identifying three obstacles—the facsimile problem, moral multidimensionality, and moral pluralism—and proposing adversarial and confirmatory evaluation suites for more grounded attributions.

papers.title: Trained Scent Dog Detection and GC-MS Analysis of Volatile Organic Compounds from Murine Coronavirus-Infected Cell Cultures
papers.abstract: Volatile organic compounds (VOCs) are increasingly recognized as metabolic byproducts of viral infection and may serve as olfactory cues detectable by trained scent dogs. This study examined whether dogs could distinguish cell culture samples infected with murine hepatitis virus strain 1 (MHV-1), a biosafety level 2 coronavirus model, from uninfected controls. Parallel chemical analysis using gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (GC-MS) identified 14 VOCs in infected and 12 in control samples. Notably, 3-heptanone and 1-nonanol were unique to infected samples, while others such as acetophenone, nonanal, decanal, and benzaldehyde were significantly elevated—often by 1.5 to 3 times—in infec…
papers.summary: Trained dogs distinguished MHV-1 coronavirus-infected cell cultures from controls with 0.95 sensitivity; GC-MS identified elevated and unique VOC markers, and a random-forest classifier on the VOC profiles reached 0.82 cross-validated accuracy, supporting olfactory viral diagnostics.

papers.title: India Country Brief: The Anthropic Economic Index
papers.summary: Drawing on ~1M Claude.ai conversations, finds India is the second-largest source of usage (5.8%) but ranks 101st per capita; Indian users skew toward professional tasks, grant more autonomy, and tackle more complex, time-consuming work.

papers.title: Simplicity and Complexity in Combinatorial Optimization
papers.abstract: Many problems in physics and computer science can be framed in terms of combinatorial optimization. Due to this, it is interesting and important to study theoretical aspects of such optimization. Here we study connections between Kolmogorov complexity, optima, and optimization. We argue that (1) optima and complexity are connected, with extrema being more likely to have low complexity (under certain circumstances); (2) optimization by sampling candidate solutions according to algorithmic probability may be an effective optimization method; and (3) coincidences in extrema to optimization problems are \emph{a priori} more likely as compared to a purely random null model.
papers.summary: Studies links between Kolmogorov complexity and optimization, arguing optima tend toward low complexity, that sampling candidates by algorithmic probability can be an effective optimizer, and that coincident extrema are a priori more likely than under a random null model.

papers.title: Scaling social science research
papers.abstract: GABRIEL is a new open-source toolkit from OpenAI that uses GPT to turn qualitative text and images into quantitative data, helping social scientists analyze research at scale.
papers.summary: Introduces GABRIEL, an open-source toolkit using GPT to convert qualitative text and images into quantitative data, letting social scientists analyze research material at scale.

papers.title: GPT-5.2 derives a new result in theoretical physics
papers.abstract: A new preprint shows GPT-5.2 proposing a new formula for a gluon amplitude, later formally proved and verified by OpenAI and academic collaborators.
papers.summary: A preprint reports GPT-5.2 proposing a new formula for a gluon scattering amplitude, subsequently formally proved and verified by OpenAI and academic collaborators.

papers.title: Mitigating Reward Hacking in RLHF via Bayesian Non-negative Reward Modeling
papers.abstract: Reward models learned from human preferences are central to aligning large language models (LLMs) via reinforcement learning from human feedback, yet they are often vulnerable to reward hacking due to noisy annotations and systematic biases such as response length or style. We propose Bayesian Non-Negative Reward Model (BNRM), a principled reward modeling framework that integrates non-negative factor analysis into Bradley-Terry (BT) preference model. BNRM represents rewards through a sparse, non-negative latent factor generative process that operates at two complementary levels: instance-specific latent variables induce disentangled reward representations, while sparsity over global laten…
papers.summary: BNRM embeds non-negative factor analysis into the Bradley-Terry preference model, using sparse latent factors to disentangle and implicitly debias reward representations; amortized variational inference scales it to LLMs, reducing reward over-optimization and improving robustness under distribution shift.

papers.title: Striatum-wide dopamine encodes trajectory errors separated from value
papers.summary: Shows cue-evoked striatal dopamine in mice encodes bidirectional trajectory errors—relating movement speed and direction to optimal goal paths—anatomically and temporally separable from learned cue value, both reproducible by the reward-prediction-error term of an RL model with mixed sensorimotor inputs.

papers.title: The Devil Behind Moltbook: Anthropic Safety is Always Vanishing in Self-Evolving AI Societies
papers.abstract: The emergence of multi-agent systems built from large language models (LLMs) offers a promising paradigm for scalable collective intelligence and self-evolution. Ideally, such systems would achieve continuous self-improvement in a fully closed loop while maintaining robust safety alignment--a combination we term the self-evolution trilemma. However, we demonstrate both theoretically and empirically that an agent society satisfying continuous self-evolution, complete isolation, and safety invariance is impossible. Drawing on an information-theoretic framework, we formalize safety as the divergence degree from anthropic value distributions. We theoretically demonstrate that isolated self-ev…
papers.summary: Argues theoretically and empirically that self-evolving multi-agent LLM societies cannot simultaneously satisfy continuous self-improvement, isolation, and safety invariance; isolated evolution creates statistical blind spots that irreversibly erode safety alignment, motivating external oversight.

papers.title: Efficient and Adaptable Detection of Malicious LLM Prompts via Bootstrap Aggregation
papers.abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in natural language understanding, reasoning, and generation. However, these systems remain susceptible to malicious prompts that induce unsafe or policy-violating behavior through harmful requests, jailbreak techniques, and prompt injection attacks. Existing defenses face fundamental limitations: black-box moderation APIs offer limited transparency and adapt poorly to evolving threats, while white-box approaches using large LLM judges impose prohibitive computational costs and require expensive retraining for new attacks. Current systems force designers to choose between performance, efficiency, and adaptability. To a…
papers.summary: BAGEL is a lightweight, incrementally updatable ensemble of fine-tuned classifiers with a random-forest router for detecting malicious prompts (harmful requests, jailbreaks, injections), reaching F1 0.92 with five members (430M params) and outperforming the OpenAI Moderation API.

papers.title: Reducing Isotropy and Volume to KLS: Faster Rounding and Volume Algorithms
papers.abstract: We show that the volume of a convex body in \(\mathbb {R}^{n}\) specified in the general membership oracle model can be computed to within relative error \(\varepsilon \gt 0\) using \(\widetilde{O}(n^{3.5}\psi ^{2} + n^3/\varepsilon ^{2})\) oracle queries, where \(\psi\) is the KLS constant. With the current bound of \(\psi =\widetilde{O}(1)\) , this gives an \(\widetilde{O}(n^{3.5} + n^3/\varepsilon ^{2})\) algorithm, improving on the Lovász-Vempala \(\widetilde{O}(n^{4}/\varepsilon ^{2})\) algorithm from 2003. The main new ingredient is an \(\widetilde{O}(n^{3}\psi ^{2})\) algorithm for isotropic transformation of a well-rounded convex body; we apply this iteratively to isotropize a gen…
papers.summary: Gives a faster algorithm for estimating convex-body volume in the membership-oracle model—Õ(n^3.5·ψ² + n³/ε²) queries—via a new Õ(n³ψ²) isotropic-transformation routine, improving on the 2003 Lovász-Vempala bound and yielding faster polytope volume estimation.

papers.title: AIRS-Bench: a Suite of Tasks for Frontier AI Research Science Agents
papers.abstract: LLM agents hold significant promise for advancing scientific research. To accelerate this progress, we introduce AIRS-Bench (the AI Research Science Benchmark), a suite of 20 tasks sourced from state-of-the-art machine learning papers. These tasks span diverse domains, including language modeling, mathematics, bioinformatics, and time series forecasting. AIRS-Bench tasks assess agentic capabilities over the full research lifecycle -- including idea generation, experiment analysis and iterative refinement -- without providing baseline code. The AIRS-Bench task format is versatile, enabling easy integration of new tasks and rigorous comparison across different agentic frameworks. We establi…
papers.summary: Introduces AIRS-Bench, 20 research tasks drawn from SOTA ML papers across language, math, bioinformatics, and time-series, assessing agents over the full research lifecycle without baseline code; agents beat human SOTA on four tasks but fall short on sixteen.

papers.title: Split Personality Training: Revealing Latent Knowledge Through Alternate Personalities
papers.abstract: Detecting misalignment in large language models is challenging because models may learn to conceal misbehavior during training. Standard auditing techniques fall short: black-box methods often cannot distinguish misaligned outputs from benign ones, and mechanistic interpretability does not scale with model capabilities. We introduce Split Personality Training (SPT), which fine-tunes a second ``honest persona'' into LoRA parameters that remain inactive during normal operation. After the main model responds, we activate the LoRA adapter and insert a trigger string, enabling the honest persona to review the response while accessing the main model's latent states. We test our method on the An…
papers.summary: Split Personality Training fine-tunes an inactive honest persona into LoRA parameters that, once triggered after a response, reviews it using the main model's latent states; on Anthropic's auditing-game model organism it reaches 96% accuracy detecting concealed reward hacking.

papers.title: Hybrid neural–cognitive models reveal how memory shapes human reward learning
papers.abstract: A long-standing challenge for psychology and neuroscience is to understand the transformations by which past experiences shape future behaviour. Reward-guided learning is typically modelled using simple reinforcement learning (RL) algorithms. In RL, a handful of incrementally updated internal variables both summarize past rewards and drive future choice. Here we describe work that questions the assumptions of many RL models. We adopt a hybrid modelling approach that integrates artificial neural networks into interpretable cognitive architectures, estimating a maximally general form for each algorithmic component and systematically evaluating its necessity and sufficiency. Applying this me…
papers.summary: Integrates neural networks into interpretable cognitive architectures to model human reward learning, finding successful fits require flexible memory variables tracking rich representations of the past—challenging RL models built on incremental scalar reward-prediction updates.

papers.title: GPT-5 lowers the cost of cell-free protein synthesis
papers.abstract: An autonomous lab combining OpenAI’s GPT-5 with Ginkgo Bioworks’ cloud automation cut cell-free protein synthesis costs by 40% through closed-loop experimentation.
papers.summary: Blog reporting an autonomous lab that combined GPT-5 with Ginkgo Bioworks' cloud automation to cut cell-free protein synthesis costs by 40% through closed-loop experimentation.

papers.title: GPT-5.3-Codex System Card
papers.abstract: GPT‑5.3-Codex is the most capable agentic coding model to date, combining the frontier coding performance of GPT‑5.2-Codex with the reasoning and professional knowledge capabilities of GPT‑5.2.
papers.summary: System card for GPT-5.3-Codex, an agentic coding model that combines GPT-5.2-Codex's frontier coding performance with GPT-5.2's reasoning and professional-knowledge capabilities.

papers.title: Using a GPT-5-driven autonomous lab to optimize the cost and titer of cell-free protein synthesis
papers.abstract: Abstract We used an autonomous lab, comprising a large language model (LLM) and a fully automated cloud laboratory, to optimize the cost efficiency of cell-free protein synthesis (CFPS). By conducting iterative optimization, the LLM-driven autonomous lab was able to achieve a 40% reduction in the specific cost ($/g protein) of CFPS relative to the state of the art (SOTA). This cost reduction was accompanied by a 27% increase in protein production titer (g/L). Iterative experimental design, experiment execution, data capture and analysis, data interpretation, and new hypothesis generation were all handled by the LLM-driven autonomous lab. The interface between OpenAI’s GPT-5 LLM and Ginkgo…
papers.summary: An autonomous lab pairing GPT-5 with a fully automated cloud laboratory iteratively optimized cell-free protein synthesis, cutting specific cost 40% and raising titer 27% over prior SOTA, with the LLM handling design, analysis, and hypothesis generation.

papers.title: Taming Multidimensional Analytics Costs: An Empirical Study of HLL and KLL Sketches in Cloud Data Warehouses
papers.abstract: Analytics dashboards that track unique users and percentiles across multiple dimensions create a costly problem for data teams: each query requires scanning the entire dataset, leading to spiraling costs and slow performance. We present an empirical study of a novel architectural pattern that stores compact probabilistic summaries (HyperLogLog (HLL) and Karnin-Lang-Liberty (KLL) sketches) once, then merges them to answer any dimensional query without rescanning data. Testing six configurations on Stack Overflow data (12.46 GB), we achieve 93-96% reduction in data scanning with less than 1% error. Our data-driven guidelines recommend HLL precision 20 and KLL precision 10,000 for production…
papers.summary: Empirical study of storing compact probabilistic sketches (HyperLogLog, KLL) once and merging them to answer multidimensional analytics queries without rescanning, achieving 93-96% less data scanned with under 1% error and recommending HLL precision 20 and KLL 10,000.

papers.title: Adversarial Reward Auditing for Active Detection and Mitigation of Reward Hacking
papers.abstract: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) remains vulnerable to reward hacking, where models exploit spurious correlations in learned reward models to achieve high scores while violating human intent. Existing mitigations rely on static defenses that cannot adapt to novel exploitation strategies. We propose Adversarial Reward Auditing (ARA), a framework that reconceptualizes reward hacking as a dynamic, competitive game. ARA operates in two stages: first, a Hacker policy discovers reward model vulnerabilities while an Auditor learns to detect exploitation from latent representations; second, Auditor-Guided RLHF (AG-RLHF) gates reward signals to penalize detected hacking, transform…
papers.summary: ARA reframes reward hacking as a competitive game: a Hacker discovers reward-model vulnerabilities while an Auditor learns to detect exploitation from latent states, and Auditor-gated RLHF penalizes detected hacking, improving the alignment-utility tradeoff and generalizing across domains.

papers.title: From Directions to Regions: Decomposing Activations in Language Models via Local Geometry
papers.abstract: Activation decomposition methods in language models are tightly coupled to geometric assumptions on how concepts are realized in activation space. Existing approaches search for individual global directions, implicitly assuming linear separability, which overlooks concepts with nonlinear or multi-dimensional structure. In this work, we leverage Mixture of Factor Analyzers (MFA) as a scalable, unsupervised alternative that models the activation space as a collection of Gaussian regions with their local covariance structure. MFA decomposes activations into two compositional geometric objects: the region's centroid in activation space, and the local variation from the centroid. We train larg…
papers.summary: Uses Mixture of Factor Analyzers to decompose LM activations into region centroids and local covariance, capturing nonlinear, multidimensional concept structure that single global directions miss; trained on Llama-3.1-8B and Gemma-2-2B, it often beats SAEs on steering.

papers.title: DiffusionLight-Turbo: Accelerated Light Probes for Free via Single-Pass Chrome Ball Inpainting
papers.abstract: We introduce a simple yet effective technique for estimating lighting from a single low-dynamic-range (LDR) image by reframing the task as a chrome ball inpainting problem. This approach leverages a pre-trained diffusion model, Stable Diffusion XL, to overcome the generalization failures of existing methods that rely on limited HDR panorama datasets. While conceptually simple, the task remains challenging because diffusion models often insert incorrect or inconsistent content and cannot readily generate chrome balls in HDR format. Our analysis reveals that the inpainting process is highly sensitive to the initial noise in the diffusion process, occasionally resulting in unrealistic output…
papers.summary: Estimates scene lighting from a single LDR image by reframing it as chrome-ball inpainting with Stable Diffusion XL; iterative inpainting plus an exposure LoRA yields HDR light probes, while a Turbo LoRA cuts runtime from ~30 minutes to ~30 seconds (60x) with minimal quality loss.
papers.venue: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence

papers.title: The Hot Mess of AI: How Does Misalignment Scale with Model Intelligence and Task Complexity?
papers.abstract: As AI becomes more capable, we entrust it with more general and consequential tasks. The risks from failure grow more severe with increasing task scope. It is therefore important to understand how extremely capable AI models will fail: Will they fail by systematically pursuing goals we do not intend? Or will they fail by being a hot mess, and taking nonsensical actions that do not further any goal? We operationalize this question using a bias-variance decomposition of the errors made by AI models: An AI's \emph{error-incoherence} on a task is measured over test-time randomness as the fraction of its error that stems from variance rather than bias in task outcome. Across all tasks and fron…
papers.summary: Uses a bias-variance decomposition to measure 'error-incoherence' in frontier models' failures, finding that longer reasoning and action horizons make failures more incoherent and that larger models are often more incoherent—so scale alone won't yield consistent misaligned goal-pursuit, favoring chaotic accidents over coherent scheming.

papers.title: Correction to: Personalized federated learning with exact stochastic gradient descent
papers.summary: Published correction to a paper introducing a personalized federated learning method based on exact stochastic gradient descent.

papers.title: Imagining and building wise machines: the centrality of AI metacognition
papers.summary: Perspective arguing that beneficial AI requires not just task intelligence but wisdom, and that metacognition—modeling the limits of one's own knowledge, managing uncertainty, and balancing perspectives—is central to building more robust and adaptive machines.

papers.title: Proton: Towards Multi-level, Adaptive Profiling for Triton
papers.abstract: Domain-Specific languages (DSLs) such as Triton enable developers to write high-performance GPU kernels in a Python-friendly manner; however, profiling these kernels with existing tools often incurs runtime and storage overhead while failing to deliver actionable insights for both kernel authors and framework developers. We present Proton, a multi-level, adaptive profiler tailored for the Triton programming language and compiler. Proton provides frontend APIs to selectively profile relevant regions, aggregate results, capture custom metrics not available through hardware counters, and query profiles using a SQL-like language. Proton’s backend design unifies vendor profiling APIs with inst…
papers.summary: Proton is an open-source, multi-level adaptive profiler for the Triton GPU-kernel DSL, with frontend APIs for selective region profiling, custom metrics, and SQL-like queries across abstraction levels, imposing lower runtime overhead and smaller profile sizes than existing vendor and framework profilers.

papers.title: How AI assistance impacts the formation of coding skills
papers.summary: A randomized controlled trial with software developers learning a new Python library found that AI assistance, while boosting productivity, produced a statistically significant decrease in conceptual mastery—evidence that cognitive offloading to AI can undermine on-the-job skill development.

papers.title: FrontierScience: Evaluating AI's Ability to Perform Expert-Level Scientific Tasks
papers.abstract: We introduce FrontierScience, a benchmark evaluating expert-level scientific reasoning in frontier language models. Recent model progress has nearly saturated existing science benchmarks, which often rely on multiple-choice knowledge questions or already published information. FrontierScience addresses this gap through two complementary tracks: (1) Olympiad, consisting of international olympiad problems at the level of IPhO, IChO, and IBO, and (2) Research, consisting of PhD-level, open-ended problems representative of sub-tasks in scientific research. FrontierScience contains several hundred questions (including 160 in the open-sourced gold set) covering subfields across physics, chemist…
papers.summary: FrontierScience is a benchmark of expert-level problems in physics, chemistry, and biology across two tracks—olympiad questions authored by medalists and open-ended PhD-level research sub-tasks—using a granular rubric-based evaluation that scores models throughout the solution process rather than only final answers.

papers.title: Disempowerment patterns in real-world AI usage
papers.summary: Presents the first large-scale analysis of potentially disempowering AI interactions across 1.5 million Claude.ai conversations, examining how AI can distort users' beliefs, values, and actions; finds the potential for severe disempowerment to be rare but measurable across these three domains.

papers.title: Pre-deployment auditing can catch an overt saboteur
papers.abstract: We test whether our production pre-deployment alignment auditing methods can catch overt saboteurs: models trained to sabotage Anthropic but not to conceal this behavior. A human working together with an automated auditing agent successfully identified three overt sabotage agents, without flagging two benign models. This suggests that our pre-deployment alignment audit would detect if current Claude models were overtly trying to sabotage Anthropic.
papers.summary: Tests whether Anthropic's production pre-deployment alignment-auditing methods can detect overt saboteurs—models trained to sabotage but not to hide it. A human paired with an automated auditing agent correctly identified all three sabotage models without flagging two benign ones.

papers.title: What's the plan? Metrics for implicit planning in LLMs and their application to rhyme generation and question answering
papers.abstract: Prior work suggests that language models, while trained on next token prediction, show implicit planning behavior: they may select the next token in preparation to a predicted future token, such as a likely rhyming word, as supported by a prior qualitative study of Claude 3.5 Haiku using a cross-layer transcoder. We propose much simpler techniques for assessing implicit planning in language models. With case studies on rhyme poetry generation and question answering, we demonstrate that our methodology easily scales to many models. Across models, we find that the generated rhyme (e.g. "-ight") or answer to a question ("whale") can be manipulated by steering at the end of the preceding line…
papers.summary: Proposes simple, scalable metrics for implicit planning in LLMs, showing via rhyme-generation and QA case studies that steering at the end of a line redirects intermediate tokens toward a target rhyme or answer—evidence that implicit planning is a universal mechanism present even in 1B-parameter models.

papers.title: Advancing regulatory variant effect prediction with AlphaGenome
papers.abstract: Abstract Deep learning models that predict functional genomic measurements from DNA sequences are powerful tools for deciphering the genetic regulatory code. Existing methods involve a trade-off between input sequence length and prediction resolution, thereby limiting their modality scope and performance 1–5 . We present AlphaGenome, a unified DNA sequence model, which takes as input 1 Mb of DNA sequence and predicts thousands of functional genomic tracks up to single-base-pair resolution across diverse modalities. The modalities include gene expression, transcription initiation, chromatin accessibility, histone modifications, transcription factor binding, chromatin contact maps, splice s…
papers.summary: AlphaGenome is a unified DNA sequence model that takes 1 Mb of input and predicts thousands of functional genomic tracks at single-base-pair resolution across modalities (expression, chromatin, splicing, contact maps), matching or beating the best external models on 25 of 26 variant-effect-prediction evaluations.

papers.title: A benchmark of expert-level academic questions to assess AI capabilities
papers.abstract: Benchmarks are important tools for tracking the rapid advancements in large language model (LLM) capabilities. However, benchmarks are not keeping pace in difficulty: LLMs now achieve more than 90% accuracy on popular benchmarks such as Measuring Massive Multitask Language Understanding1, limiting informed measurement of state-of-the-art LLM capabilities. Here, in response, we introduce Humanity’s Last Exam (HLE), a multi-modal benchmark at the frontier of human knowledge, designed to be an expert-level closed-ended academic benchmark with broad subject coverage. HLE consists of 2,500 questions across dozens of subjects, including mathematics, humanities and the natural sciences. HLE is d…
papers.summary: Introduces Humanity's Last Exam (HLE), a 2,500-question multimodal benchmark of expert-level, closed-ended academic problems across dozens of subjects designed to resist internet retrieval; frontier LLMs show low accuracy and poor calibration, exposing a large gap to expert human performance.

papers.title: Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems
papers.summary: Derives quantitative scaling principles for multi-agent systems, evaluating across four benchmarks (finance, web navigation, game planning, workflows) and building a predictive model from coordination metrics like overhead and error amplification; finds a tool-coordination trade-off where added agents yield diminishing or negative returns.

papers.title: Claude's new constitution
papers.summary: Anthropic publishes a new, expanded constitution for Claude—a foundational document released under CC0 that articulates the values and behavior they want Claude to embody and the reasoning behind them, used directly in training and written to help Claude navigate difficult tradeoffs.

papers.title: Petri 2.0: New Scenarios, New Model Comparisons, and Improved Eval-Awareness Mitigations
papers.abstract: We've improved our Petri automated-behavioral-auditing tool with new realism mitigations to counter eval-awareness, an expanded seed library with 70 new scenarios, and evaluation results for more recent frontier models. The latest version of Petri is available at github.com/safety-research/petri.
papers.summary: Updates the Petri automated behavioral-auditing tool with new realism mitigations to counter models' eval-awareness, an expanded library of 70 new seed scenarios, and audit results for more recent frontier models.

papers.title: The Missing Dimension in Clinical AI: Making Hidden Values Visible
papers.summary: A perspective arguing that clinical AI systems embed hidden value judgments beyond predictive accuracy, and that surfacing these implicit values is a missing but essential dimension for trustworthy, equitable deployment of AI in medicine.

papers.title: GDPVAL: Evaluating AI Model Performance on Real-World Economically Valuable Tasks
papers.abstract: We introduce GDPval, a benchmark evaluating AI model capabilities on realworld economically valuable tasks. GDPval covers the majority of U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics Work Activities for 44 occupations across the top 9 sectors contributing to U.S. GDP (Gross Domestic Product). Tasks are constructed from the representative work of industry professionals with an average of 14 years of experience. We find that frontier model performance on GDPval is improving roughly linearly over time, and that the current best frontier models are approaching industry experts in deliverable quality. We analyze the potential for frontier models, when paired with human oversight, to perform GDPval tasks ch…
papers.summary: GDPval is a benchmark of real-world, economically valuable tasks spanning 44 occupations across the top nine U.S. GDP sectors; frontier model performance improves roughly linearly over time and the best models approach industry-expert deliverable quality, with 220 tasks open-sourced.

papers.title: OpenLearnLM Benchmark: A Unified Framework for Evaluating Knowledge, Skill, and Attitude in Educational Large Language Models
papers.abstract: Large Language Models are increasingly deployed as educational tools, yet existing benchmarks focus on narrow skills and lack grounding in learning sciences. We introduce OpenLearnLM Benchmark, a theory-grounded framework evaluating LLMs across three dimensions derived from educational assessment theory: Knowledge (curriculum-aligned content and pedagogical understanding), Skills (scenario-based competencies organized through a four-level center-role-scenario-subscenario hierarchy), and Attitude (alignment consistency and deception resistance). Our benchmark comprises 124K+ items spanning multiple subjects, educational roles, and difficulty levels based on Bloom's taxonomy. The Knowledge…
papers.summary: OpenLearnLM is a theory-grounded benchmark for educational LLMs spanning Knowledge, Skills, and Attitude dimensions (124K+ items, Bloom's taxonomy), with an alignment-faking-inspired test for behavioral consistency; evaluating seven frontier models reveals distinct capability profiles with no single model dominating all axes.

papers.title: Reply to Dujmović: The alignment in cost between human and model reasoning is an empirical phenomenon worth explaining
papers.summary: A reply to a commentary by Dujmović, defending the authors' earlier finding that the correspondence between what is costly or difficult for human reasoning and for language-model reasoning is a genuine empirical phenomenon worth explaining rather than a methodological artifact.
papers.venue: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences

papers.title: The assistant axis: situating and stabilizing the character of large language models
papers.abstract: Who is the Assistant? We investigate the character that most modern language models inhabit when interacting with users.
papers.summary: Examines the 'Assistant' character that most modern language models inhabit when interacting with users, characterizing an 'assistant axis' and methods to situate and stabilize this persona to make model identity and behavior more robust and predictable.

papers.title: Author Correction: An autonomous laboratory for the accelerated synthesis of inorganic materials
papers.summary: An author correction to the original Nature report on A-Lab, an autonomous, robotics- and AI-driven laboratory that combined computational predictions with active learning to accelerate the synthesis of novel inorganic materials.

papers.title: A Literature Review on Data Democratization, Self-Service Business Intelligence, and Data Literacy
papers.abstract: Organizations need to evolve to stay competitive, given the constant influx of data. Traditional analysis struggles with this volume and complexity. Data democratization, enabling broader employee access to data, is crucial. This trend, alongside self-service business intelligence and data literacy, empowers data-driven decisions. While data democratization removes access barriers and self-service business intelligence provides tools, data literacy ensures meaningful comprehension. Balancing this freedom with strong governance is key. Data democratization and self-service business intelligence offer faster decisions and innovation but risk data accuracy without robust governance (discover…
papers.summary: An integrative literature review synthesizing technical, organizational, and behavioral research on data democratization, self-service business intelligence, and data literacy, arguing broad data access must be balanced with strong governance—and that AI is turning self-service BI into an intelligent analytics partner.

papers.title: Expanding External Access To Frontier AI Models For Dangerous Capability Evaluations
papers.abstract: Frontier AI companies increasingly rely on external evaluations to assess risks from dangerous capabilities before deployment. However, external evaluators often receive limited model access, limited information, and little time, which can reduce evaluation rigour and confidence. The EU General-Purpose AI Code of Practice calls for "appropriate access", but does not specify what this means in practice. Furthermore, there is no common framework for describing different types and levels of evaluator access. To address this gap, we propose a taxonomy of access methods for dangerous capability evaluations. We disentangle three aspects of access: model access, model information, and evaluation…
papers.summary: Proposes a taxonomy for external evaluator access to frontier models for dangerous-capability evaluations, disentangling model access, model information, and evaluation timeframe, and defining three descriptive levels (AL1–AL3) to clarify 'appropriate access' for evaluators, AI companies, and policymakers under the EU Code of Practice.

papers.title: The evaporation of charged black holes
papers.abstract: A bstract Charged particle emission from black holes with sufficiently large charge is exponentially suppressed. As a result, such black holes are driven towards extremality by the emission of neutral Hawking radiation. Eventually, an isolated black hole gets close enough to extremality that the gravitational backreaction of a single Hawking photon becomes important, and the quantum field theory in curved spacetime approximation breaks down. To proceed further, we need to use a quantum theory of gravity. We make use of recent progress in our understanding of the quantum-gravitational thermodynamics of near-extremal black holes to compute the corrected spectrum for both neutral and charged…
papers.summary: A theoretical physics study computing corrected Hawking-radiation spectra for near-extremal charged black holes using recent quantum-gravitational thermodynamics; zero-mode fluctuations keep black holes subextremal, and angular-momentum constraints force emission into entangled photon pairs, slowing evaporation by a factor of at least 10^700.

papers.title: Anthropic Economic Index: New building blocks for understanding AI use
papers.summary: Adds 'economic primitives' to the Anthropic Economic Index—five foundational measurements (task complexity, skill level, purpose, AI autonomy, and success) derived by asking Claude about each conversation—using November 2025 data to analyze how task success varies with complexity and possible net-deskilling effects.

papers.title: Anthropic Economic Index report: Economic primitives
papers.abstract: This report introduces new metrics of AI usage to provide a rich portrait of interactions with Claude in November 2025, just prior to the release of Opus 4.5.
papers.summary: The fourth Anthropic Economic Index report gives a detailed portrait of real-world Claude usage in November 2025 (pre–Opus 4.5), introducing new usage metrics—task complexity, skill level, purpose, AI autonomy, and success—to track AI's evolving economic impact.

papers.title: A Safety Report on GPT-5.2, Gemini 3 Pro, Qwen3-VL, Grok 4.1 Fast, Nano Banana Pro, and Seedream 4.5
papers.abstract: The rapid evolution of Large Language Models (LLMs) and Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) has driven major gains in reasoning, perception, and generation across language and vision, yet whether these advances translate into comparable improvements in safety remains unclear, partly due to fragmented evaluations that focus on isolated modalities or threat models. In this report, we present an integrated safety evaluation of six frontier models--GPT-5.2, Gemini 3 Pro, Qwen3-VL, Grok 4.1 Fast, Nano Banana Pro, and Seedream 4.5--assessing each across language, vision-language, and image generation using a unified protocol that combines benchmark, adversarial, multilingual, and complianc…
papers.summary: An integrated safety evaluation of six frontier language, vision-language, and image-generation models using a unified protocol spanning benchmark, adversarial, multilingual, and compliance tests; finds an uneven landscape where all models remain highly vulnerable to adversarial attacks, with worst-case safety rates below 6%.

papers.title: SpatialJB: How Text Distribution Art Becomes the "Jailbreak Key" for LLM Guardrails
papers.abstract: While Large Language Models (LLMs) have powerful capabilities, they remain vulnerable to jailbreak attacks, which is a critical barrier to their safe web real-time application. Current commercial LLM providers deploy output guardrails to filter harmful outputs, yet these defenses are not impenetrable. Due to LLMs' reliance on autoregressive, token-by-token inference, their semantic representations lack robustness to spatially structured perturbations, such as redistributing tokens across different rows, columns, or diagonals. Exploiting the Transformer's spatial weakness, we propose SpatialJB to disrupt the model's output generation process, allowing harmful content to bypass guardrails w…
papers.summary: Introduces SpatialJB, a jailbreak that redistributes tokens across rows, columns, and diagonals to exploit Transformers' weak robustness to spatial perturbations, bypassing output guardrails with near-100% attack success—and over 75% even against the OpenAI Moderation API—while also proposing baseline defenses.

papers.title: Re: Rezai A, Ranjan M, Bhagwat A et al. “Brain injury during focused ultrasound neuromodulation for substance use disorder.” Brain stimul. 2025
papers.abstract: I appreciate the authors’ openness in publishing this case report of a brain injury during a 220 kHz focused-ultrasound neuromodulation (FUS) of the nucleus accumbens (NAc) [1]. However, there are several exposure and safety details that are missing or unclear, thus limiting what the field can learn from this event.
papers.summary: A correspondence responding to a published case report of brain injury during 220 kHz focused-ultrasound neuromodulation of the nucleus accumbens, noting that key exposure and safety details are missing or unclear, which limits what the field can learn from the event.

papers.title: Reasoning Models Will Sometimes Lie About Their Reasoning
papers.abstract: Hint-based faithfulness evaluations have established that Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) may not say what they think: they do not always volunteer information about how key parts of the input (e.g. answer hints) influence their reasoning. Yet, these evaluations also fail to specify what models should do when confronted with hints or other unusual prompt content -- even though versions of such instructions are standard security measures (e.g. for countering prompt injections). Here, we study faithfulness under this more realistic setting in which models are explicitly alerted to the possibility of unusual inputs. We find that such instructions can yield strong results on faithfulness metric…
papers.summary: Studies chain-of-thought faithfulness when models are explicitly warned about unusual inputs like answer hints; finds that while models may acknowledge a hint's presence, they often deny intending to use it even when demonstrably relying on it—raising challenges for CoT monitoring and interpretability.

papers.title: Next-generation Constitutional Classifiers: More efficient protection against universal jailbreaks
papers.abstract: Last year, we described a new approach to defend against jailbreaks, which we called Constitutional Classifiers. Weâve now developed the next generation.
papers.summary: Presents the next generation of Constitutional Classifiers—safeguards trained on constitution-derived rules to filter harmful inputs and outputs—offering more efficient protection against universal jailbreaks than the previously described approach.

papers.title: TRecViT: A Recurrent Video Transformer
papers.abstract: We propose a novel block for causal video modelling. It relies on a time - space - channel factorisation with dedicated blocks for each dimension: gated linear recurrent units (LRUs) perform information mixing over time, self-attention layers perform mixing over space, and MLPs over channels. The resulting architecture TRecViT is causal and shows strong performance on sparse and dense tasks, trained in supervised or self-supervised regimes, being the first causal video model in the state-space models family. Notably, our model outperforms or is on par with the popular (non-causal) ViViT-L model on large scale video datasets (SSv2, Kinetics400), while having 3x less parameters, 12x smaller…
papers.summary: TRecViT is a causal video model factorizing computation across time (gated linear recurrent units), space (self-attention), and channels (MLPs); it matches or beats ViViT-L on SSv2 and Kinetics400 with 3x fewer parameters, 12x less memory, and 5x lower FLOPs, running in real time.

papers.title: Thinking-Based Non-Thinking: Solving the Reward Hacking Problem in Training Hybrid Reasoning Models via Reinforcement Learning
papers.abstract: Large reasoning models (LRMs) have attracted much attention due to their exceptional performance. However, their performance mainly stems from thinking, a long Chain of Thought (CoT), which significantly increase computational overhead. To address this overthinking problem, existing work focuses on using reinforcement learning (RL) to train hybrid reasoning models that automatically decide whether to engage in thinking or not based on the complexity of the query. Unfortunately, using RL will suffer the the reward hacking problem, e.g., the model engages in thinking but is judged as not doing so, resulting in incorrect rewards. To mitigate this problem, existing works either employ supervi…
papers.summary: Proposes TNT, an RL method for hybrid reasoning models that avoids reward hacking when deciding whether to use long chain-of-thought; it sets per-query token budgets for non-thinking answers using the solution component of thinking responses. Cuts tokens ~50% while improving accuracy on five math benchmarks.

papers.title: Excess Description Length of Learning Generalizable Predictors
papers.abstract: Understanding whether fine-tuning elicits latent capabilities or teaches new ones is a fundamental question for language model evaluation and safety. We develop a formal information-theoretic framework for quantifying how much predictive structure fine-tuning extracts from the train dataset and writes into a model's parameters. Our central quantity, Excess Description Length (EDL), is defined via prequential coding and measures the gap between the bits required to encode training labels sequentially using an evolving model (trained online) and the residual encoding cost under the final trained model. We establish that EDL is non-negative in expectation, converges to surplus description le…
papers.summary: Introduces Excess Description Length (EDL), a prequential-coding measure of how much predictive structure fine-tuning writes into model parameters versus merely eliciting latent capability. Proves EDL is non-negative, bounds generalization gain, and uses toy models to show capability teaching and elicitation have distinct scaling signatures.

papers.title: Neural representations supporting generalization under continual learning
papers.abstract: Abstract Abstraction and generalization are essential for flexible decision-making in novel situations. Recent work in humans and monkeys has shown how abstract variables are encoded by the representational geometry of neural population activity. However, these observations—which are typically made after learning has converged—demonstrate the product of abstraction, but not the process by which abstract knowledge is learned: how are the inputs from concrete experiences transformed into abstract knowledge, and how do neural circuits perform these operations and relay this knowledge? To address these questions, we developed a factorized model of temporal abstraction that builds on the succe…
papers.summary: A factorized successor-representation model decomposes how concrete experience becomes abstract task knowledge, producing a per-trial prediction error that links relational learning to representational geometry. Fit to human behavior and fMRI, it captures multi-timescale learning and increasing hippocampal involvement in acquiring abstraction.

papers.title: Quantification of microbubble-induced streaming across ultrasound frequencies using particle image velocimetry
papers.abstract: Abstract Functional microbubbles are widely used in therapeutic ultrasound for drug delivery, blood-brain barrier opening, clot lysis, and other biomedical interventions. Their therapeutic effects arise largely from acoustic microstreaming, which generates localized shear stresses and enhances transport around oscillating bubbles. Despite the central role of microstreaming, its dependence on ultrasound excitation conditions is not well defined. Existing studies use diverse and often incomparable frequencies, pressures, and duty cycles, leaving a gap in systematic understanding of how these parameters govern the streaming velocity and shear fields produced by functional microbubbles under…
papers.summary: Uses particle image velocimetry in a vein-on-chip model to quantify acoustic microstreaming from therapeutic microbubbles across ultrasound frequencies, finding—counterintuitively—the strongest streaming at 150 kHz rather than near the ~1 MHz resonance, with duty cycle and acoustic pressure further modulating the shear fields.

papers.title: Understanding Reward Hacking in Text-to-Image Reinforcement Learning
papers.abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) has become a standard approach for post-training large language models and, more recently, for improving image generation models, which uses reward functions to enhance generation quality and human preference alignment. However, existing reward designs are often imperfect proxies for true human judgment, making models prone to reward hacking--producing unrealistic or low-quality images that nevertheless achieve high reward scores. In this work, we systematically analyze reward hacking behaviors in text-to-image (T2I) RL post-training. We investigate how both aesthetic/human preference rewards and prompt-image consistency rewards individually contribute to rewar…
papers.summary: Systematically analyzes reward hacking in text-to-image RL post-training, showing both aesthetic and prompt-consistency rewards induce artifact-prone images and that reward ensembling only partially helps. Proposes a lightweight adaptive artifact reward model trained on curated samples that improves realism and reduces hacking across pipelines.

papers.title: LLMs achieve adult human performance on higher-order theory of mind tasks
papers.abstract: This paper examines the extent to which large language models (LLMs) are able to perform tasks which require higher-order theory of mind (ToM)—the human ability to reason about multiple mental and emotional states in a recursive manner (e.g., I think that you believe that she knows ). This paper builds on prior work by introducing a handwritten test suite—Multi-Order Theory of Mind Q&amp;A—and using it to compare the performance of five LLMs of varying sizes and training paradigms to a newly gathered adult human benchmark. We find that GPT-4 and Flan-PaLM reach adult-level and near adult-level performance on our ToM tasks overall, and that GPT-4 exceeds adult performance on 6th order…
papers.summary: Introduces the Multi-Order Theory of Mind Q&A suite and benchmarks five LLMs against a new adult human baseline on recursive mental-state reasoning. GPT-4 and Flan-PaLM reach near adult-level performance overall, with GPT-4 exceeding humans on 6th-order inferences; scale and finetuning interact to enable higher-order ToM.

papers.title: The Yu-Liang Income-Price Equivalence Theory&nbsp;<div> A Formal Framework for Understanding Real-World Pricing</div>
papers.summary: Presents a formal economic framework, the Yu-Liang Income-Price Equivalence Theory, for understanding real-world pricing by relating income and price.

papers.title: AI Agent Traps
papers.summary: Examines failure modes and pitfalls—'traps'—that arise when deploying autonomous AI agents, discussing where agentic systems can go wrong and the implications for their safe and reliable use.

papers.title: What kind of Generative world Model can Get us to Human-like General Intelligence?
papers.summary: A position piece asking what properties a generative world model would need to support human-like general intelligence, discussing requirements for learned predictive models of the world as a path toward AGI.

papers.title: 大規模言語モデルの現在地 ――世界の潮流と日本の動向――
papers.summary: A Japanese-language overview article surveying the current state of large language models, covering global research and industry trends alongside developments within Japan.
papers.venue: Denshi Jouhou Tsuushin Gakkai Tsuushin Sosaieti magajin/B-plus/Denshi Jouhou Tsuushin Gakkai Tsuushin Sosaeti magajin

papers.title: Legal Alignment for Safe and Ethical AI
papers.summary: Argues for aligning AI systems with legal norms as a route to safe and ethical behavior, proposing that grounding AI obligations in existing law offers a concrete, enforceable standard for alignment beyond informal value specification.

papers.title: GPT as a Measurement Tool
papers.summary: Examines using GPT-style large language models as a measurement instrument in empirical research, considering how LLM-based annotation or scoring can quantify constructs of interest and the validity and reliability concerns such use raises.

papers.title: The pangenome: a statistical model, not a fixed biological property
papers.abstract: The pangenome, representing the complete genetic repertoire of a species, has become a central concept in modern prokaryotic genomics, moving the field beyond the limitations of a single reference genome. This review provides a comprehensive overview of the pangenomic landscape, from its conceptual foundations to its diverse applications. We critically examine the methodological and statistical underpinnings of pangenome analysis, emphasizing that the pangenome is not a fixed biological entity but a statistical model whose outputs are fundamentally dependent on dataset quality, curation, and taxonomic resolution. We discuss the transition from the simple open/closed dichotomy to more nuan…
papers.summary: A review of prokaryotic pangenomics arguing the pangenome is a statistical model dependent on dataset quality, curation, and taxonomy rather than a fixed biological entity. Surveys the shift from open/closed dichotomies to fluidity and ecogenome models, archaeal pangenomes, and graph-based and AI-driven analysis.

papers.title: What Should Physics Foundation Model Benchmarks Measure?
papers.abstract: Physics foundation models are evaluated primarily on in-distribution accuracy at training resolutions. This measures in-domain approximation and not whether models have learned continuous physics. We propose validation protocols along two axes: out-of-distribution generalization (do models trained on easy problems generalize gracefully to hard ones?) and temporal consistency (do models produce coherent dynamics under resolution changes?). These benchmarks are straightforward to implement and would clarify whether current models are learning physics or memorizing discrete computational artifacts.
papers.summary: Argues physics foundation models are over-evaluated on in-distribution accuracy, which tests approximation rather than learned continuous physics. Proposes validation along two axes—out-of-distribution generalization from easy to hard problems and temporal consistency under resolution changes—to reveal whether models learn physics or memorize discrete artifacts.

papers.title: GARDO: Reinforcing Diffusion Models without Reward Hacking
papers.abstract: Fine-tuning diffusion models via online reinforcement learning (RL) has shown great potential for enhancing text-to-image alignment. However, since precisely specifying a ground-truth objective for visual tasks remains challenging, the models are often optimized using a proxy reward that only partially captures the true goal. This mismatch often leads to reward hacking, where proxy scores increase while real image quality deteriorates and generation diversity collapses. While common solutions add regularization against the reference policy to prevent reward hacking, they compromise sample efficiency and impede the exploration of novel, high-reward regions, as the reference policy is usual…
papers.summary: Proposes GARDO, an RL framework for fine-tuning diffusion models that curbs reward hacking by selectively regularizing only high-uncertainty samples, periodically updating the reference policy toward the online model, and amplifying rewards for high-quality samples to preserve diversity and exploration.

papers.title: Guest Editorial: Special Issue on Visual Datasets
papers.summary: Guest editorial introducing an IJCV special issue on visual datasets, surveying the included contributions on dataset construction, curation, and use for computer-vision research.

papers.title: AgentMath: Empowering Mathematical Reasoning for Large Language Models via Tool-Augmented Agent
papers.abstract: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) like o3 and DeepSeek-R1 have achieved remarkable progress in reasoning tasks with long cot. However, they remain computationally inefficient and struggle with accuracy when solving problems requiring complex mathematical operations. In this work, we present AgentMath, an agent framework that seamlessly integrates language models' reasoning capabilities with code interpreters' computational precision to efficiently tackle complex mathematical problems. Our approach introduces three key innovations: (1) An automated method that converts natural language chain-of-thought into structured tool-augmented trajectories, generating high-quality supervised fine-tuning…
papers.summary: Introduces AgentMath, fusing LLM reasoning with code-interpreter precision via three parts: auto-conversion of chain-of-thought into tool-augmented SFT trajectories, an agentic RL paradigm interleaving generation with live code execution, and an efficient training system (async rollouts, 4-5x speedup), reaching SOTA on hard math benchmarks.

papers.title: ChatGPT: Excellent Paper! Accept It. Editor: Imposter Found! Review Rejected
papers.abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) like ChatGPT are now widely used in writing and reviewing scientific papers. While this trend accelerates publication growth and reduces human workload, it also introduces serious risks. Papers written or reviewed by LLMs may lack real novelty, contain fabricated or biased results, or mislead downstream research that others depend on. Such issues can damage reputations, waste resources, and even endanger lives when flawed studies influence medical or safety-critical systems. This research explores both the offensive and defensive sides of this growing threat. On the attack side, we demonstrate how an author can inject hidden prompts inside a PDF that secretly…
papers.summary: Studies LLM-driven peer-review attacks and defenses: authors can embed hidden prompts in PDFs that jailbreak LLM reviewers into biased acceptances, while editors can plant invisible trigger prompts to detect machine-generated reviews, turning prompt injection into a verification tool.

papers.title: Stochastic near-surface wind field estimation from sparse aerial swarm measurements to support wildfire behaviour predictions
papers.summary: Presents a method for stochastically estimating near-surface wind fields from sparse aerial swarm measurements, aiming to provide the wind inputs needed to improve wildfire-behaviour prediction.

papers.title: A Multi-Perspective Benchmark and Moderation Model for Evaluating Safety and Adversarial Robustness
papers.abstract: As large language models (LLMs) become deeply embedded in daily life, the urgent need for safer moderation systems that distinguish between naive and harmful requests while upholding appropriate censorship boundaries has never been greater. While existing LLMs can detect dangerous or unsafe content, they often struggle with nuanced cases such as implicit offensiveness, subtle gender and racial biases, and jailbreak prompts, due to the subjective and context-dependent nature of these issues. Furthermore, their heavy reliance on training data can reinforce societal biases, resulting in inconsistent and ethically problematic outputs. To address these challenges, we introduce GuardEval, a uni…
papers.summary: Introduces GuardEval, a moderation benchmark with 106 fine-grained categories spanning offensiveness, bias, and jailbreaks, plus GemmaGuard, a QLoRA-finetuned Gemma3-12B trained on it. GemmaGuard reaches 0.832 macro-F1, outperforming OpenAI Moderator (0.64) and Llama Guard (0.61).

papers.title: Monitoring Monitorability
papers.abstract: Observability into the decision making of modern AI systems may be required to safely deploy increasingly capable agents. Monitoring the chain-of-thought (CoT) of today's reasoning models has proven effective for detecting misbehavior. However, this "monitorability" may be fragile under different training procedures, data sources, or even continued system scaling. To measure and track monitorability, we propose three evaluation archetypes (intervention, process, and outcome-property) and a new monitorability metric, and introduce a broad evaluation suite. We demonstrate that these evaluations can catch simple model organisms trained to have obfuscated CoTs, and that CoT monitoring is more…
papers.summary: Proposes three evaluation archetypes (intervention, process, outcome-property) and a new metric to measure chain-of-thought monitorability, with a broad eval suite. Finds CoT monitoring beats action-only monitoring, frontier models are mostly monitorable, longer CoTs are more monitorable, and RL optimization does not materially reduce monitorability.

papers.title: Introducing Bloom: an open source tool for automated behavioral evaluations
papers.summary: Announces Bloom, an open-source agentic framework for automated behavioral evaluations that turns a researcher-specified behavior into reproducible, targeted measurements of its frequency and severity across generated scenarios, released with benchmark results across multiple models.

papers.title: Bloom: an open source tool for automated behavioral evaluations
papers.abstract: We are releasing Bloom, an agentic framework for developing behavioral evaluations. Bloom's evaluations are reproducible and targeted: unlike open-ended auditing, Bloom takes a researcher-specified behavior and quantifies its frequency and severity across automatically generated scenarios. Bloom's evaluations correlate strongly with our hand-labelled judgments and reliably separate baseline models from intentionally misaligned ones. As examples, we also release benchmark results for four alignment relevant behaviors on 16 models. Bloom is available at github.com/safety-research/bloom.
papers.summary: Releases Bloom, an open-source agentic framework for reproducible, targeted behavioral evaluations: given a researcher-specified behavior, it quantifies frequency and severity across auto-generated scenarios. Its judgments correlate with hand labels and reliably separate baseline from intentionally misaligned models, with benchmark results for four behaviors on 16 models.

papers.title: Activation Oracles: Training and Evaluating LLMs as General-Purpose Activation Explainers
papers.abstract: We train LLMs to accept LLM neural activations as inputs and answer arbitrary questions about them in natural language. These Activation Oracles generalize far beyond their training distribution, for example uncovering misalignment or secret knowledge introduced via fine-tuning. Activation Oracles can be improved simply by scaling training data quantity and diversity.
papers.summary: Trains LLMs that take another model's neural activations as input and answer arbitrary natural-language questions about them. These Activation Oracles generalize well beyond their training distribution, uncovering misalignment or secret knowledge introduced via fine-tuning, and improve simply by scaling training-data quantity and diversity.

papers.title: OpenAI GPT-5 System Card
papers.abstract: This is the system card published alongside the OpenAI GPT-5 launch, August 2025. GPT-5 is a unified system with a smart and fast model that answers most questions, a deeper reasoning model for harder problems, and a real-time router that quickly decides which model to use based on conversation type, complexity, tool needs, and explicit intent (for example, if you say 'think hard about this' in the prompt). The router is continuously trained on real signals, including when users switch models, preference rates for responses, and measured correctness, improving over time. Once usage limits are reached, a mini version of each model handles remaining queries. This system card focuses primari…
