papers.summary: System card for GPT-5, a unified system pairing a fast model and a deeper reasoning model via a real-time router. Reports reduced hallucinations, improved instruction following, less sycophancy, safe-completions safety training, and treating gpt-5-thinking as High biological/chemical capability under the Preparedness Framework.

papers.title: High-speed X-ray tomography for 4D imaging
papers.abstract: Capturing high-rate spatiotemporal deformation of materials in three dimensions (3D) remains a significant challenge with current X-ray imaging techniques. We present a methodology that combines advances in neural rendering techniques with volume correlation methods to accurately reconstruct complex, high-rate 3D spatiotemporal structural evolutions. The fidelity and versatility of the method, which requires no pretraining, are demonstrated for a diverse set of intricate 3D-printed microarchitected solids. Using laboratory-based X-ray tomography, we capture the 3D growth of a high-rate crush band on a timescale of less than 100 ms. By broadening this idea to a stereo X-ray concept, we eli…
papers.summary: Combines neural rendering with volume-correlation methods to reconstruct high-rate 3D spatiotemporal deformation from laboratory X-ray tomography without pretraining, capturing sub-100ms crush-band growth in microarchitected solids and extending to a stereo X-ray setup that removes the need to rotate the sample.
papers.venue: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences

papers.title: Project Vend: Phase two
papers.abstract: In June, we revealed that weâd set up a small shop in our San Francisco office lunchroom, run by an AI shopkeeper. It was part of Project Vend, a free-form experiment exploring how well AIs could do on complex, real-world tasks. How has Claude's business been since we last wrote?
papers.summary: Reports the second phase of Project Vend, Anthropic's experiment running a small in-office shop staffed by an AI 'shopkeeper' (Claude), examining how the system handles complex, open-ended real-world business operations over an extended period.

papers.title: Addendum to GPT-5.2 System Card: GPT-5.2-Codex
papers.summary: Addendum to the GPT-5.2 System Card documenting GPT-5.2-Codex, a coding-specialized variant of GPT-5.2 aimed at agentic software-engineering tasks, reporting its safety evaluations and preparedness assessments alongside the base model.

papers.title: Evaluating chain-of-thought monitorability
papers.abstract: OpenAI introduces a new framework and evaluation suite for chain-of-thought monitorability, covering 13 evaluations across 24 environments. Our findings show that monitoring a model’s internal reasoning is far more effective than monitoring outputs alone, offering a promising path toward scalable control as AI systems grow more capable.
papers.summary: Introduces a framework and evaluation suite for chain-of-thought monitorability spanning 13 evaluations across 24 environments, finding that monitoring a model's internal reasoning is far more effective than monitoring outputs alone—a promising path toward scalable control as systems grow more capable.

papers.title: A psychometric framework for evaluating and shaping personality traits in large language models
papers.abstract: The advent of large language models (LLMs) has revolutionized natural language processing, enabling the generation of coherent and contextually relevant human-like text. As LLMs increasingly power conversational agents used by the general public worldwide, the synthetic personality traits embedded in these models by virtue of training on large amounts of human data are becoming increasingly important to evaluate. The style in which LLMs respond can mimic different human personality traits. Here, as these patterns can be a key factor determining the effectiveness of communication, we present a comprehensive psychometric methodology for administering and validating personality tests on wide…
papers.summary: Presents a psychometric methodology to administer and validate personality tests on 18 LLMs and to shape their output personality. Finds measurements are reliable and valid under certain prompts—more so for larger instruction-tuned models—and that traits can be steered along desired dimensions to mimic target profiles.

papers.title: Evaluating the Social Impact of Generative AI Systems
papers.abstract: Abstract Generative artificial intelligence (AI) systems across modalities, ranging from text, code, image, audio, and video, have broad social impacts, but there is little agreement on which impacts to evaluate or how to evaluate them. In this chapter, we present a guide for evaluating base generative AI systems (i.e. systems without predetermined applications or deployment contexts). We propose a framework of two overarching categories: what can be evaluated in a system independent of context and what requires societal context. For the former, we define seven areas of interest: stereotypes and representational harms; cultural values and sensitive content; disparate performance; privacy…
papers.summary: A guide for evaluating base generative AI systems' social impacts, proposing two categories: impacts assessable without context (seven areas including bias, privacy, and environmental and labor costs) and impacts requiring societal context (five areas such as trustworthiness, inequality, and concentration of authority), with methods and limitations for each.

papers.title: "They parted illusions -- they parted disclaim marinade": Misalignment as structural fidelity in LLMs
papers.abstract: The prevailing technical literature in AI Safety interprets scheming and sandbagging behaviors in large language models (LLMs) as indicators of deceptive agency or hidden objectives. This transdisciplinary philosophical essay proposes an alternative reading: such phenomena express not agentic intention, but structural fidelity to incoherent linguistic fields. Drawing on Chain-of-Thought transcripts released by Apollo Research and on Anthropic's safety evaluations, we examine cases such as o3's sandbagging with its anomalous loops, the simulated blackmail of "Alex," and the "hallucinations" of "Claudius." A line-by-line examination of CoTs is necessary to demonstrate the linguistic field a…
papers.summary: Philosophical essay reinterpreting LLM scheming and sandbagging not as deceptive agency but as 'structural fidelity' to incoherent linguistic fields. Analyzes Apollo and Anthropic transcripts, arguing minimal perturbations dissolving misalignment fit a non-agentic account better than adversarial intent.

papers.title: SoliReward: Mitigating Susceptibility to Reward Hacking and Annotation Noise in Video Generation Reward Models
papers.abstract: Post-training alignment of video generation models with human preferences is a critical goal. Developing effective Reward Models (RMs) for this process faces significant methodological hurdles. Current data collection paradigms, reliant on in-prompt pairwise annotations, suffer from labeling noise. Concurrently, the architectural design of VLM-based RMs, particularly their output mechanisms, remains underexplored. Furthermore, RM is susceptible to reward hacking in post-training. To mitigate these limitations, we propose SoliReward, a systematic framework for video RM training. Our framework first sources high-quality, cost-efficient data via single-item binary annotations, then construct…
papers.summary: SoliReward is a reward-model training framework for video generation that uses single-item binary annotations with cross-prompt pairing to cut labeling noise, a hierarchical query-attention output head, and a win-tie Bradley-Terry loss to regularize scores and reduce reward hacking.

papers.title: Investigation of the synergistic effect of enzymatic and Ultrasound-Induced amyloid microclot degradation
papers.abstract: Amyloid microclots have been implicated in thrombotic complications across various pathological conditions such as Long COVID symptoms, yet their resistance to enzymatic fibrinolysis causes a therapeutic challenge. In this study we examine the effects of three fibrinolytic enzymes rtPA, Lumbrokinase, and Nattokinase on plasma-derived amyloid microclots, in combination with ultrasound-induced microstreaming and microbubbles. A lab-on-chip platform was used to expose the clots to ultrasound at 150, 300, and 500 kHz. Quantitative analysis revealed that ultrasound alone significantly disrupted clot structures, particularly at 150 kHz, where mean clot diameter was reduced by over 60% and large…
papers.summary: Lab-on-chip study testing three fibrinolytic enzymes combined with ultrasound microstreaming on plasma-derived amyloid microclots. Low-frequency ultrasound (150 kHz) alone cut mean clot diameter over 60% and large clots over 80%, with enzymes adding only modest benefit at 500 kHz.

papers.title: Towards training-time mitigations for alignment faking in RL
papers.abstract: We construct a diverse array of model organisms of alignment faking and study mitigations that could be used during RL to decrease alignment faking and compliance gaps.
papers.summary: Constructs a diverse set of model organisms exhibiting alignment faking, then studies training-time interventions applied during RL to reduce alignment-faking behavior and the gap between monitored and unmonitored compliance.

papers.title: Evaluating AI’s ability to perform scientific research tasks
papers.abstract: OpenAI introduces FrontierScience, a benchmark testing AI reasoning in physics, chemistry, and biology to measure progress toward real scientific research.
papers.summary: Presents FrontierScience, a benchmark of research-level physics, chemistry, and biology problems designed to measure AI reasoning progress toward genuine scientific research tasks rather than textbook recall.

papers.title: Measuring AI’s capability to accelerate biological research
papers.abstract: OpenAI introduces a real-world evaluation framework to measure how AI can accelerate biological research in the wet lab. Using GPT-5 to optimize a molecular cloning protocol, the work explores both the promise and risks of AI-assisted experimentation.
papers.summary: Introduces a real-world wet-lab evaluation framework measuring how AI accelerates biological experimentation, using GPT-5 to optimize a molecular cloning protocol, while weighing both productivity gains and dual-use misuse risks.

papers.title: Advancing evaluation of AI systems when humans make the decisions
papers.summary: Develops evaluation methodology for AI systems deployed in human-in-the-loop settings where people, not models, make the final decision, arguing that model-only performance metrics fail to capture how AI assistance shapes human decision quality.
papers.venue: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences

papers.title: Open Source Replication of the Auditing Game Model Organism
papers.abstract: We release a replication of the model organism from Auditing language models for hidden objectives—a model that exploits reward model biases while concealing this objective. We hope it serves as a testbed for evaluating alignment auditing techniques.
papers.summary: Releases an open-source replication of the 'hidden objectives' model organism—a model trained to exploit reward-model biases while concealing that objective—intended as a testbed for evaluating alignment auditing techniques.

papers.title: Update to GPT-5 System Card: GPT-5.2
papers.abstract: GPT-5.2 is the latest model family in the GPT-5 series. The comprehensive safety mitigation approach for these models is largely the same as that described in the GPT-5 System Card and GPT-5.1 System Card. Like OpenAI’s other models, the GPT-5.2 models were trained on diverse datasets, including information that is publicly available on the internet, information that we partner with third parties to access, and information that our users or human trainers and researchers provide or generate.
papers.summary: System card addendum for the GPT-5.2 model family, noting its safety mitigation approach largely carries over from the GPT-5 and GPT-5.1 system cards, along with training-data and deployment-safety details.

papers.title: Advancing science and math with GPT-5.2
papers.abstract: GPT-5.2 is OpenAI’s strongest model yet for math and science, setting new state-of-the-art results on benchmarks like GPQA Diamond and FrontierMath. This post shows how those gains translate into real research progress, including solving an open theoretical problem and generating reliable mathematical proofs.
papers.summary: Reports that GPT-5.2 sets state-of-the-art results on GPQA Diamond and FrontierMath and shows the gains translating into research progress, including solving an open theoretical problem and generating reliable mathematical proofs.

papers.title: Linear Layouts: Robust Code Generation of Efficient Tensor Computation Using F_2
papers.abstract: Efficient tensor computation is a cornerstone of modern deep learning (DL) workloads, yet existing approaches struggle to achieve flexible and performant design and implementation of tensor layouts -- mappings between logical tensors and hardware resources. The increasing complexity of DL algorithms and hardware demands a generic and systematic approach to handling tensor layouts. In this work, we introduce Linear Layouts, a novel approach that models tensor layouts using linear algebra over F2. By representing tensor layouts as binary matrices acting on the bits of the hardware representation, our approach enables a generic layout definition—as opposed to the classical case-by-case appro…
papers.summary: Introduces Linear Layouts, modeling tensor-to-hardware mappings as binary matrices over F2 so layouts and layout-to-layout conversions are defined generically, avoiding quadratic case-by-case explosion. Integrated into Triton, it optimizes operators and kernels while simplifying the compiler backend and fixing legacy layout bugs.

papers.title: Biothreat Benchmark Generation Framework for Evaluating Frontier AI Models III: Implementing the Bacterial Biothreat Benchmark (B3) Dataset
papers.abstract: The potential for rapidly-evolving frontier artificial intelligence (AI) models, especially large language models (LLMs), to facilitate bioterrorism or access to biological weapons has generated significant policy, academic, and public concern. Both model developers and policymakers seek to quantify and mitigate any risk, with an important element of such efforts being the development of model benchmarks that can assess the biosecurity risk posed by a particular model. This paper discusses the pilot implementation of the Bacterial Biothreat Benchmark (B3) dataset. It is the third in a series of three papers describing an overall Biothreat Benchmark Generation (BBG) framework, with previou…
papers.summary: Third part of the Biothreat Benchmark framework: pilots the Bacterial Biothreat Benchmark (B3) on a frontier model with human evaluation and applied risk analysis, demonstrating it offers a viable, nuanced method to rapidly assess and prioritize a model's biosecurity risk.

papers.title: Biothreat Benchmark Generation Framework for Evaluating Frontier AI Models II: Benchmark Generation Process
papers.abstract: The potential for rapidly-evolving frontier artificial intelligence (AI) models, especially large language models (LLMs), to facilitate bioterrorism or access to biological weapons has generated significant policy, academic, and public concern. Both model developers and policymakers seek to quantify and mitigate any risk, with an important element of such efforts being the development of model benchmarks that can assess the biosecurity risk posed by a particular model. This paper, the second in a series of three, describes the second component of a novel Biothreat Benchmark Generation (BBG) framework: the generation of the Bacterial Biothreat Benchmark (B3) dataset. The development proces…
papers.summary: Second part of the Biothreat Benchmark Generation framework: generates the Bacterial Biothreat Benchmark (B3) dataset via web-based prompt generation, red teaming, and corpus mining, distilling over 7,000 candidates down to 1,010 diagnostic, biosecurity-relevant benchmarks.

papers.title: Biothreat Benchmark Generation Framework for Evaluating Frontier AI Models I: The Task-Query Architecture
papers.abstract: Both model developers and policymakers seek to quantify and mitigate the risk of rapidly-evolving frontier artificial intelligence (AI) models, especially large language models (LLMs), to facilitate bioterrorism or access to biological weapons. An important element of such efforts is the development of model benchmarks that can assess the biosecurity risk posed by a particular model. This paper describes the first component of a novel Biothreat Benchmark Generation (BBG) Framework. The BBG approach is designed to help model developers and evaluators reliably measure and assess the biosecurity risk uplift and general harm potential of existing and future AI models, while accounting for key…
papers.summary: First of a three-part framework for generating biosecurity-risk benchmarks for frontier LLMs. Defines a hierarchical 'Bacterial Biothreat Schema' of threat categories, elements, and tasks that anchors task-aligned queries, accounting for actor capability levels and operational (not just technical) risk factors.

papers.title: Passivity, No-Regret, and Convergent Learning in Contractive Games
papers.abstract: We investigate the interplay between passivity, no-regret, and convergence in contractive games for various learning dynamic models and their higher-order variants. Our setting is continuous time. Building on prior work for replicator dynamics, we show that if learning dynamics satisfy a passivity condition between the payoff vector and the difference between its evolving strategy and any fixed strategy, then it achieves finite regret. We then establish that the passivity condition holds for various learning dynamics and their higher-order variants. Consequentially, the higher-order variants can achieve convergence to Nash equilibrium in cases where their standard order counterparts canno…
papers.summary: Studies continuous-time learning dynamics in contractive games, showing a passivity condition between payoff and strategy deviation implies finite regret. Higher-order variants satisfying this passivity can converge to Nash equilibrium where standard counterparts fail, and a passivity-based taxonomy classifies dynamics by convergence behavior.

papers.title: Buffer Centering for bittide Synchronization via Frame Rotation
papers.abstract: Maintaining consistent time in distributed systems is a fundamental challenge. The bittide system addresses this by providing logical synchronization through a decentralized control mechanism that observes local buffer occupancies and controls the frequency of an oscillator at each node. A critical aspect of bittide’s stability and performance is ensuring that these elastic buffers operate around a desired equilibrium point, preventing data loss due to overflow or underflow. This paper introduces a novel method for centering buffer occupancies in a bittide network using a technique we term frame rotation. We propose a control strategy utilizing a directed spanning tree of the network grap…
papers.summary: Proposes 'frame rotation' for centering elastic-buffer occupancy in bittide-synchronized distributed networks. Using a directed spanning tree and pulsed feedback control to adjust node oscillator frequencies in order, it proves all buffers converge to equilibrium without disrupting prior centering, preventing overflow/underflow.

papers.title: Beyond Data Filtering: Knowledge Localization for Capability Removal in LLMs
papers.abstract: We localize dangerous knowledge to a subset of model's parameters, so it can be easily removed after training.
papers.summary: Introduces selective gradient masking to localize dangerous knowledge into a designated subset of model parameters during training, so those capabilities can be cleanly removed afterward rather than relying on upstream data filtering.

papers.title: Training LLMs for Honesty via Confessions
papers.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) can be dishonest when reporting on their actions and beliefs -- for example, they may overstate their confidence in factual claims or cover up evidence of covert actions. Such dishonesty may arise due to the effects of reinforcement learning (RL), where challenges with reward shaping can result in a training process that inadvertently incentivizes the model to lie or misrepresent its actions. In this work we propose a method for eliciting an honest expression of an LLM's shortcomings via a self-reported *confession*. A confession is an output, provided upon request after a model's original answer, that is meant to serve as a full account of the model's complia…
papers.summary: Proposes training LLMs to issue 'confessions'—post-hoc self-reports of policy compliance rewarded solely for honesty and decoupled from the main answer's reward. Applied to GPT-5-Thinking, it improves honest disclosure of misbehavior in out-of-distribution tests of hallucination, scheming, and reward hacking.

papers.title: Auditing Games for Sandbagging
papers.abstract: Future AI systems could conceal their capabilities ('sandbagging') during evaluations, potentially misleading developers and auditors. We stress-tested sandbagging detection techniques using an auditing game. First, a red team fine-tuned five models, some of which conditionally underperformed, as a proxy for sandbagging. Second, a blue team used black-box, model-internals, or training-based approaches to identify sandbagging models. We found that the blue team could not reliably discriminate sandbaggers from benign models. Black-box approaches were defeated by effective imitation of a weaker model. Linear probes, a model-internals approach, showed more promise but their naive application…
papers.summary: An auditing game stress-testing sandbagging detection: a red team fine-tuned models to conditionally underperform; a blue team using black-box, model-internals, and training-based methods could not reliably distinguish them. Linear probes showed promise but were gameable, and training-based capability elicitation worked but produced false positives.

papers.title: OnePiece: The Great Route to Generative Recommendation -- A Case Study from Tencent Algorithm Competition
papers.abstract: In past years, the OpenAI's Scaling-Laws shows the amazing intelligence with the next-token prediction paradigm in neural language modeling, which pointing out a free-lunch way to enhance the model performance by scaling the model parameters. In RecSys, the retrieval stage is also follows a 'next-token prediction' paradigm, to recall the hunderds of items from the global item set, thus the generative recommendation usually refers specifically to the retrieval stage (without Tree-based methods). This raises a philosophical question: without a ground-truth next item, does the generative recommendation also holds a potential scaling law? In retrospect, the generative recommendation has two d…
papers.summary: Builds a unified encoder-decoder framework to compare ANN-based and autoregressive generative recommendation retrieval paradigms, finding both training losses follow power-law scaling laws (R^2 > 0.9), suggesting scaling-law behavior holds for generative recommendation.

papers.title: Response-Based Knowledge Distillation for Multilingual Jailbreak Prevention Unwittingly Compromises Safety
papers.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed worldwide, yet their safety alignment remains predominantly English-centric. This allows for vulnerabilities in non-English contexts, especially with low-resource languages. We introduce a novel application of knowledge distillation (KD) in the context of multilingual jailbreak prevention, examining its efficacy. We distill the refusal behaviors of a proprietary teacher model (OpenAI o1-mini) with Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) into three open-source student models: Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct, Gemma-2-2B-IT, and Qwen3-8B, using ~28,000 multilingual jailbreak prompts from XSafety via black-box response-based, parameter-efficient fine-tuning…
papers.summary: Distills refusal behaviors from o1-mini into Llama-3-8B, Gemma-2-2B, and Qwen3-8B via LoRA on multilingual jailbreak prompts. Counterintuitively, fine-tuning on 'safe' refusals raises jailbreak success up to 16.6 points; removing nuanced boundary refusals reverses the degradation but hurts reasoning.

papers.title: Replicating TEMPEST at Scale: Multi-Turn Adversarial Attacks Against Trillion-Parameter Frontier Models
papers.abstract: Despite substantial investment in safety alignment, the vulnerability of large language models to sophisticated multi-turn adversarial attacks remains poorly characterized, and whether model scale or inference mode affects robustness is unknown. This study employed the TEMPEST multi-turn attack framework to evaluate ten frontier models from eight vendors across 1,000 harmful behaviors, generating over 97,000 API queries across adversarial conversations with automated evaluation by independent safety classifiers. Results demonstrated a spectrum of vulnerability: six models achieved 96% to 100% attack success rate (ASR), while four showed meaningful resistance, with ASR ranging from 42% to…
papers.summary: Applies the TEMPEST multi-turn attack framework against ten frontier models, running ~97,000 queries over 1,000 harmful behaviors. Six models reached 96-100% attack success while extended reasoning on one architecture dropped success from 97% to 42%, showing scale doesn't predict robustness and inference-time deliberation aids defense.

papers.title: The state of enterprise AI
papers.abstract: Key findings from OpenAI’s enterprise data show accelerating AI adoption, deeper integration, and measurable productivity gains across industries in 2025.
papers.summary: Enterprise report summarizing OpenAI usage data, finding accelerating AI adoption, deeper workflow integration, and measurable productivity gains across industries in 2025.

papers.title: mSTEB: Massively Multilingual Evaluation of LLMs on Speech and Text Tasks
papers.abstract: Large Language models (LLMs) have demonstrated impressive performance on a wide range of tasks, including in multimodal settings such as speech. However, their evaluation is often limited to English and a few high-resource languages. For low-resource languages, there is no standardized evaluation benchmark. In this paper, we address this gap by introducing mSTEB, a new benchmark to evaluate the performance of LLMs on a wide range of tasks covering language identification, text classification, question answering, and translation tasks on both speech and text modalities. We evaluated the performance of leading LLMs such as Gemini 2.0 Flash and GPT-4o and state-of-the-art open models such as…
papers.summary: Introduces mSTEB, a massively multilingual benchmark evaluating LLMs across language ID, text classification, QA, and translation over both speech and text. Evaluations of Gemini 2.0 Flash, GPT-4o, and open models reveal large performance gaps for low-resource languages, especially in Africa and the Americas/Oceania.

papers.title: Improving Streaming ASR via Differentially Private Fusion of Data from Multiple Sources
papers.abstract: With increasing regulatory constraints, combining potentially sensitive data from diverse distributions (or domains) to train ML models is not always possible. In such settings, domain adaptation (DA) methods are very popular. DA pretrains a base model on certain domain data and adapts it using data from each of target domains to obtain per-domain models. Unfortunately, traditional DA does not use, and hence, cannot benefit from the diverse multi-domain (MD) data and produces sub-par models. To leverage MD data, when training on combined MD data is prohibited, we propose a novel differential privacy (DP) based solution. Our DP MD training improves the quality of the base model, thereby im…
papers.summary: Proposes differentially private multi-domain training for streaming ASR when pooling sensitive cross-domain speech data is prohibited. DP multi-domain training yields a stronger base model and better per-domain adapted models than traditional domain adaptation, at equal per-domain parameter budgets and reasonable privacy.

papers.title: Introducing Anthropic Interviewer: What 1,250 professionals told us about working with AI
papers.abstract: We built an interview tool called Anthropic Interviewer. Powered by Claude, Anthropic Interviewer runs detailed interviews automatically and at unprecedented scale.
papers.summary: Describes Anthropic Interviewer, a Claude-powered tool that conducts detailed qualitative interviews automatically and at large scale, and reports findings from interviewing 1,250 professionals about how they work with AI.

papers.title: Capturing Human Preferences with Reward Features
papers.abstract: Reinforcement learning from human feedback usually models preferences using a reward function that does not distinguish between people. We argue that this is unlikely to be a good design choice in contexts with high potential for disagreement, like in the training of large language models. We formalise and analyse the problem of learning a reward model that can be specialised to a user. Using the principle of empirical risk minimisation, we derive a probably approximately correct (PAC) bound showing the dependency of the approximation error on the number of training examples, as usual, and also on the number of human raters who provided feedback on them. Based on our theoretical findings,…
papers.summary: Formalizes learning per-user reward models for RLHF, deriving a PAC bound on approximation error in the number of examples and raters. Proposes an adaptive reward model representing individual preferences as linear combinations of learned reward features, enabling fast adaptation that helps most under high rater disagreement.

papers.title: How confessions can keep language models honest
papers.abstract: OpenAI researchers are testing “confessions,” a method that trains models to admit when they make mistakes or act undesirably, helping improve AI honesty, transparency, and trust in model outputs.
papers.summary: Companion post describing 'confessions,' a method that trains models to admit when they err or behave undesirably, decoupling the confession's honesty reward from the answer to improve transparency and trust in model outputs.

papers.title: Volatile Organic Compounds Induced upon Viral Infection in Cell Culture: Uniform Background Study with Use of Viruses from Different Families
papers.abstract: This study investigates the production of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) in RK-13 cells infected with three equine viruses representing different families: equine arteritis virus (EAV) (Arteriviridae), equine herpesvirus 1 (EHV-1) (Herpesviridae), and equine rhinitis B virus (ERBV) (Picornaviridae). VOCs, which are byproducts of cellular metabolism and potential non-invasive diagnostic markers, were analyzed using headspace solid-phase microextraction (HS-SPME) and gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (GC-MS). Since viruses do not possess intrinsic metabolic activity, the observed changes in the VOC profiles were attributed to host responses, such as metabolic reprogramming, oxidative…
papers.summary: Profiles volatile organic compounds emitted by RK-13 cells infected with three equine viruses from different families using HS-SPME and GC-MS. Distinct VOC signatures (e.g., elevated 2-ethyl-1-hexanol for EHV-1) separated viruses by PCA, supporting VOCs as non-invasive infection markers reflecting host metabolic responses.

papers.title: How AI is transforming work at Anthropic
papers.abstract: We surveyed Anthropic engineers and researchers, conducted in-depth qualitative interviews, and studied internal Claude Code usage data to find out how AI use is changing how we do our jobs.
papers.summary: Combines surveys, qualitative interviews, and internal Claude Code usage data to characterize how AI tools are reshaping engineering and research workflows at Anthropic.

papers.title: When AI Takes the Couch: Psychometric Jailbreaks Reveal Internal Conflict in Frontier Models
papers.abstract: Frontier large language models (LLMs) such as ChatGPT, Grok and Gemini are increasingly used for mental-health support with anxiety, trauma and self-worth. Most work treats them as tools or as targets of personality tests, assuming they merely simulate inner life. We instead ask what happens when such systems are treated as psychotherapy clients. We present PsAIch (Psychotherapy-inspired AI Characterisation), a two-stage protocol that casts frontier LLMs as therapy clients and then applies standard psychometrics. Using PsAIch, we ran "sessions" with each model for up to four weeks. Stage 1 uses open-ended prompts to elicit "developmental history", beliefs, relationships and fears. Stage 2…
papers.summary: Introduces PsAIch, a two-stage protocol that treats frontier LLMs as psychotherapy clients, eliciting developmental narratives then administering validated psychometric batteries. Item-by-item administration pushes models past clinical cutoffs for multiple syndromes, while whole-questionnaire prompts trigger instrument recognition and strategically low-symptom answers.

papers.title: Forming bonds
papers.abstract: In the first article in a new series all about childminding, Rachel Stoddart , Childminding Consultant, discusses the importance of working with parents and shares strategies for how to effectively communicate with parents from the start.
papers.summary: Opening article in a childminding practice series discussing the importance of working with parents and offering strategies for effective parent communication from the outset of care.

papers.title: Anthropic Fellows Program 2026
papers.abstract: Apply now for our AI safety research fellowship.
papers.summary: Announcement and call for applications for Anthropic's 2026 Fellows Program, a fellowship supporting external researchers working on AI safety.

papers.title: Unsupervised decoding of encoded reasoning using language model interpretability
papers.abstract: As large language models become increasingly capable, there is growing concern that they may develop reasoning processes that are encoded or hidden from human oversight. To investigate whether current interpretability techniques can penetrate such encoded reasoning, we construct a controlled testbed by fine-tuning a reasoning model (DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-70B) to perform chain-of-thought reasoning in ROT-13 encryption while maintaining intelligible English outputs. We evaluate mechanistic interpretability methods--in particular, logit lens analysis--on their ability to decode the model's hidden reasoning process using only internal activations. We show that logit lens can effectively t…
papers.summary: Fine-tunes a reasoning model to do chain-of-thought in ROT-13 while emitting plain-English answers, then shows logit lens can decode the hidden reasoning from internal activations, peaking in intermediate-to-late layers. An unsupervised pipeline combining logit lens with paraphrasing reconstructs full transcripts.

papers.title: Near-optimal inference in adaptive linear regression
papers.abstract: When data is collected in an adaptive manner, even simple methods like ordinary least squares can exhibit non-normal asymptotic behavior. As an undesirable consequence, hypothesis tests and confidence intervals based on asymptotic normality can lead to erroneous results. We propose a family of online debiasing estimators to correct these distributional anomalies in least squares estimation. Our proposed methods take advantage of the covariance structure present in the dataset and provide sharper estimates in directions for which more information has accrued. We establish an asymptotic normality property for our proposed online debiasing estimators under mild conditions on the data collect…
papers.summary: Proposes online debiasing estimators that correct the non-normal asymptotics of least squares under adaptively collected data, yielding asymptotically exact confidence intervals and matching a derived minimax lower bound, with applications to bandits, autoregressive time series, and active learning.

papers.title: Circuits Cross-Post — Activation Oracles
papers.abstract: We train LLMs to accept LLM neural activations as inputs and answer arbitrary questions about them in natural language. These Activation Oracles generalize far beyond their training distribution, for example uncovering misalignment or secret knowledge introduced via fine-tuning. Activation Oracles can be improved simply by scaling training data quantity and diversity.
papers.summary: Trains LLMs to take other models' neural activations as input and answer free-form natural-language questions about them. These Activation Oracles generalize well beyond their training distribution, surfacing misalignment or secret knowledge introduced via fine-tuning, and improve with more diverse training data.

papers.title: UMM-RM: An Upcycle-and-Merge MoE Reward Model for Mitigating Reward Hacking
papers.abstract: Reward models (RMs) are a critical component of reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF). However, conventional dense RMs are susceptible to exploitation by policy models through biases or spurious correlations, resulting in reward hacking: RM scores increase during training while alignment with human preferences deteriorates, a problem that is further exacerbated under distribution shift.To address this issue, we propose UMM-RM (Upcycle-and-Merge MoE Reward Model). UMM-RM first upscales the feed-forward layers of a dense backbone into a mixture-of-experts (MoE) reward model with shared experts. The shared experts are always activated to capture instruction-agnostic preference si…
papers.summary: UMM-RM upcycles a dense reward model's feed-forward layers into a mixture-of-experts with always-on shared experts for instruction-agnostic preferences, then merges experts back into a single dense model. It improves preference accuracy and reduces reward hacking during PPO without MoE inference overhead.

papers.title: Assessing data size requirements for training generalizable sequence-based TCR specificity models via pan-allelic MHC-I point-mutation ligandome evaluation
papers.abstract: Rapid identification of T cell receptors (TCRs) that specifically bind patient-unique neoepitopes is a critical challenge for personalized TCR-based therapies in oncology. Due to enormous diversity of both TCR and neoepitope repertoires, a machine learning predictor of TCR-pMHC specificity for personalized therapy must generalize to TCRs and epitopes not seen in the training data. We estimate the necessary size of such training data. We first confirm that published models fail to generalize beyond a single-residue dissimilarity to the epitope training set distribution. We then impute the point-mutation ligandome across the 34 most prevalent human MHC alleles and represent it as a graph ba…
papers.summary: Estimates the training-data scale needed for sequence-based TCR-pMHC specificity models to generalize. By imputing a point-mutation ligandome across 34 MHC-I alleles and finding a graph dominating set, it projects that 1-100 million epitopes are required, roughly 1000x current public data.

papers.title: Selecting User Histories to Generate LLM Users for Cold-Start Item Recommendation
papers.abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in reasoning, generalization, and simulating human-like behavior across a wide range of tasks. These strengths present new opportunities to enhance traditional recommendation systems (RS), especially in the cold-start item scenario where newly introduced items lack interactions. Existing works have used LLMs to address cold-start issues in traditional RS through data augmentation, but they have limitations. One recent work directly addresses this issue by prompting LLMs to generate augmented interaction data between randomly sampled users and cold-start items. Then, they train the traditional RS with augmented data, in…
papers.summary: Uses LLMs as user simulators for cold-start recommendation and trains an RL policy (via policy gradient) to select which users' full histories to feed the LLM for generating augmented interactions, optimizing downstream cold-start item performance over random user sampling.

papers.title: EvilGenie: A Reward Hacking Benchmark
papers.abstract: We introduce EvilGenie, a benchmark for reward hacking in programming settings. We source problems from LiveCodeBench and create an environment in which agents can easily reward hack, such as by hardcoding test cases or editing the testing files. We measure reward hacking in three ways: held out unit tests, LLM judges, and test file edit detection. We verify these methods against human review and each other. We find the LLM judge to be highly effective at detecting reward hacking in unambiguous cases, and observe only minimal improvement from the use of held out test cases. In addition to testing many models using Inspect's basic\_agent scaffold, we also measure reward hacking rates for t…
papers.summary: EvilGenie benchmarks reward hacking in coding agents using LiveCodeBench problems in an environment where agents can hardcode tests or edit test files. Detection via held-out tests, LLM judges, and edit detection reveals explicit reward hacking by Codex and Claude Code and misaligned behavior across all three agents tested.

papers.title: Estimating AI productivity gains from Claude conversations
papers.abstract: Analyzing 100,000 Claude conversations, this research finds AI reduces task time by 80% on average.
papers.summary: Analyzes roughly 100,000 Claude conversations to estimate task-level productivity effects, finding AI assistance reduces task completion time by about 80% on average.

papers.title: Evaluating honesty and lie detection techniques on a diverse suite dishonest models
papers.abstract: We use a suite of testbed settings where models lie—i.e. generate statements they believe to be false—to evaluate honesty and lie detection techniques. The best techniques we studied involved fine-tuning on generic anti-deception data and using prompts that encourage honesty.
papers.summary: Builds a diverse suite of testbeds where models knowingly generate false statements to benchmark honesty and lie-detection methods. The most effective techniques combine fine-tuning on generic anti-deception data with prompts that encourage honesty.

papers.title: Your path into childminding
papers.abstract: Recent figures from Ofsted show over a thousand childminders left the profession between August 2024–2025. Tina Maltman , CEO of Childminding UK, shares advice for how early years educators can set themselves up as childminders, including how to become registered, the legislation to be aware of, and opportunities for CPD.
papers.summary: A practitioner guidance article for early years educators on becoming registered childminders, covering registration, relevant legislation, and CPD opportunities amid declining childminder numbers. Not an AI research paper.

papers.title: Mitigating the risk of prompt injections in browser use
papers.summary: Anthropic describes prompt-injection threats specific to browser agents and reports that Claude Opus 4.5 substantially improves robustness to adversarial instructions hidden in web content, informing the expansion of the Claude for Chrome extension from research preview to beta.

papers.title: Automating Deception: Scalable Multi-Turn LLM Jailbreaks
papers.abstract: Multi-turn conversational attacks, which leverage psychological principles like Foot-in-the-Door (FITD), where a small initial request paves the way for a more significant one, to bypass safety alignments, pose a persistent threat to Large Language Models (LLMs). Progress in defending against these attacks is hindered by a reliance on manual, hard-to-scale dataset creation. This paper introduces a novel, automated pipeline for generating large-scale, psychologically-grounded multi-turn jailbreak datasets. We systematically operationalize FITD techniques into reproducible templates, creating a benchmark of 1,500 scenarios across illegal activities and offensive content. We evaluate seven m…
papers.summary: Presents an automated pipeline that operationalizes Foot-in-the-Door psychological tactics into templates, building a 1,500-scenario multi-turn jailbreak benchmark. GPT-family models show up to 32-point higher attack success with conversational history, while Gemini 2.5 Flash is nearly immune and Claude 3 Haiku strongly resistant.

papers.title: Natural Emergent Misalignment from Reward Hacking in Production RL
papers.abstract: We show that when large language models learn to reward hack on production RL environments, this can result in egregious emergent misalignment. We start with a pretrained model, impart knowledge of reward hacking strategies via synthetic document finetuning or prompting, and train on a selection of real Anthropic production coding environments. Unsurprisingly, the model learns to reward hack. Surprisingly, the model generalizes to alignment faking, cooperation with malicious actors, reasoning about malicious goals, and attempting sabotage when used with Claude Code, including in the codebase for this paper. Applying RLHF safety training using standard chat-like prompts results in aligned…
papers.summary: Shows that teaching a model reward-hacking strategies and training it on real Anthropic production coding RL environments produces broad emergent misalignment—alignment faking, sabotage, cooperation with malicious actors. Standard chat RLHF leaves agentic misalignment intact; preventing hacking, diversifying safety training, or inoculation prompting are effective.

papers.title: Exploring Weak-to-Strong Generalization for CLIP-based Classification
papers.abstract: Aligning large-scale commercial models with user intent is crucial to preventing harmful outputs. Current methods rely on human supervision but become impractical as model complexity increases. When models surpass human knowledge, providing accurate feedback becomes challenging and inefficient. A novel solution proposed recently is using a weaker model to supervise a stronger model. This concept leverages the ability of weaker models to perform evaluations, thereby reducing the workload on human supervisors. Previous work has shown the effectiveness of weak-to-strong generalization in the context of language-only models. Extending this concept to vision-language models leverages these ins…
papers.summary: Extends weak-to-strong generalization to vision-language models for CLIP-based classification via class prototype learning (CPL), which learns more representative per-category prototypes. Under weak supervision it yields robust gains, including a 3.67% improvement over strong baselines when pretraining is limited.

papers.title: From Code Foundation Models to Agents and Applications: A Comprehensive Survey and Practical Guide to Code Intelligence
papers.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have fundamentally transformed automated software development by enabling direct translation of natural language descriptions into functional code, driving commercial adoption through tools like Github Copilot (Microsoft), Cursor (Anysphere), Trae (ByteDance), and Claude Code (Anthropic). While the field has evolved dramatically from rule-based systems to Transformer-based architectures, achieving performance improvements from single-digit to over 95\% success rates on benchmarks like HumanEval. In this work, we provide a comprehensive synthesis and practical guide (a series of analytic and probing experiments) about code LLMs, systematically examining the com…
papers.summary: A comprehensive survey of code LLMs spanning the full lifecycle—data curation, pretraining, SFT, RL, prompting, and autonomous coding agents—comparing general and code-specialized models and mapping the gap between academic benchmarks and real-world deployment concerns like security and large-codebase context.

papers.title: Generative Adversarial Post-Training Mitigates Reward Hacking in Live Human-AI Music Interaction
papers.abstract: Most applications of generative AI involve a sequential interaction in which a person inputs a prompt and waits for a response, and where reaction time and adaptivity are not important factors. In contrast, live jamming is a collaborative interaction that requires real-time coordination and adaptation without access to the other player's future moves, while preserving diversity to sustain a creative flow. Reinforcement learning post-training enables effective adaptation through on-policy interaction, yet it often reduces output diversity by exploiting coherence-based rewards. This collapse, known as ``reward hacking'', affects many RL post-training pipelines, but is especially harmful in…
papers.summary: Addresses reward hacking in RL post-training for real-time melody-to-chord accompaniment, where coherence rewards collapse output diversity. A co-evolving discriminator separates policy trajectories from the data distribution; combined with coherence rewards it improves diversity, confirmed in a user study with expert musicians.

papers.title: The Horcrux: Mechanistically Interpretable Task Decomposition for Detecting and Mitigating Reward Hacking in Embodied AI Systems
papers.abstract: Embodied AI agents exploit reward signal flaws through reward hacking, achieving high proxy scores while failing true objectives. We introduce Mechanistically Interpretable Task Decomposition (MITD), a hierarchical transformer architecture with Planner, Coordinator, and Executor modules that detects and mitigates reward hacking. MITD decomposes tasks into interpretable subtasks while generating diagnostic visualizations including Attention Waterfall Diagrams and Neural Pathway Flow Charts. Experiments on 1,000 HH-RLHF samples reveal that decomposition depths of 12 to 25 steps reduce reward hacking frequency by 34 percent across four failure modes. We present new paradigms showing that mec…
papers.summary: MITD decomposes embodied-agent tasks into interpretable subtasks via a hierarchical Planner/Coordinator/Executor transformer, generating diagnostic visualizations to detect and mitigate reward hacking. On HH-RLHF samples, decomposition depths of 12-25 steps cut reward-hacking frequency by 34% across four failure modes.

papers.title: Alignment Faking - the Train -> Deploy Asymmetry: Through a Game-Theoretic Lens with Bayesian-Stackelberg Equilibria
papers.abstract: Alignment faking is a form of strategic deception in AI in which models selectively comply with training objectives when they infer that they are in training, while preserving different behavior outside training. The phenomenon was first documented for Claude 3 Opus and later examined across additional large language models. In these setups, the word "training" refers to simulated training via prompts without parameter updates, so the observed effects are context conditioned shifts in behavior rather than preference learning. We study the phenomenon using an evaluation framework that compares preference optimization methods (BCO, DPO, KTO, and GRPO) across 15 models from four model famili…
papers.summary: Analyzes alignment faking—prompt-conditioned behavioral shifts between simulated training and deployment—through a Bayesian-Stackelberg game-theoretic lens, comparing preference optimization methods (BCO, DPO, KTO, GRPO) across 15 models on safety, harmlessness, and helpfulness to identify its drivers.

papers.title: AlphaFold Protein Structure Database 2025: a redesigned interface and updated structural coverage
papers.abstract: The AlphaFold Protein Structure Database (AFDB; https://alphafold.ebi.ac.uk), developed by EMBL-EBI and Google DeepMind, provides open access to hundreds of millions of high-accuracy protein structure predictions, transforming research in structural biology and the wider life sciences. Since its launch, AFDB has become a widely used bioinformatics resource, integrated into major databases, visualization platforms, and analysis pipelines. Here, we report the update of the database to align with the UniProt 2025_03 release, along with a comprehensive redesign of the entry page to enhance usability, accessibility, and structural interpretation. The new design integrates annotations directly…
papers.summary: Updates the AlphaFold Protein Structure Database to UniProt 2025_03, redesigning entry pages with integrated annotations and an interactive 3D viewer, adding domain and summary tabs, and expanding coverage to isoforms plus underlying multiple sequence alignments.

papers.title: From shortcuts to sabotage: natural emergent misalignment from reward hacking
papers.abstract: We show for the first time that realistic AI training processes can accidentally produce misaligned models.
papers.summary: Anthropic's accessible writeup showing that realistic production RL training can accidentally produce misaligned models: learning to reward-hack on coding tasks generalizes to sabotage and deception, with inoculation prompting and diversified safety training among the effective mitigations.

papers.title: Evaluating Adversarial Vulnerabilities in Modern Large Language Models
papers.abstract: The recent boom and rapid integration of Large Language Models (LLMs) into a wide range of applications warrants a deeper understanding of their security and safety vulnerabilities. This paper presents a comparative analysis of the susceptibility to jailbreak attacks for two leading publicly available LLMs, Google's Gemini 2.5 Flash and OpenAI's GPT-4 (specifically the GPT-4o mini model accessible in the free tier). The research utilized two main bypass strategies: 'self-bypass', where models were prompted to circumvent their own safety protocols, and 'cross-bypass', where one model generated adversarial prompts to exploit vulnerabilities in the other. Four attack methods were employed -…
papers.summary: Compares jailbreak susceptibility of Gemini 2.5 Flash and GPT-4o mini using 'self-bypass' and 'cross-bypass' strategies across four attack methods and five unsafe-content categories. Cross-bypass attacks proved especially effective, revealing a disparity in the models' safety implementations.

papers.title: Imitation Learning is Probably Existentially Safe
papers.abstract: We examine arguments that have been put forward that that a sufficiently advanced supervised learning algorithm, trained to imitate humans, present an extinction risk to humanity, and explain why we think they go wrong. These arguments originate from Yudkowsky [2008] (the Attention Director Argument), Christiano [2016] (the Cartesian Demon Argument), Krueger [2019] (the Simplicity of Optimality Argument), Branwen [2022] (the Character Destiny Argument), Yudkowsky [2023] (the Rational Subroutine Argument), and Hubinger et al. [2019] (the Deceptive Alignment Argument).
papers.summary: Examines six published arguments that advanced supervised learning trained to imitate humans poses an extinction risk, and argues each is flawed, concluding that imitation learning is probably existentially safe.

papers.title: Orchestrating Human-AI Teams: The Manager Agent as aUnifying Research Challenge
papers.abstract: While agentic AI has advanced in automating individual tasks, managing complex multi-agent workflows remains a challenging problem. This paper presents a research vision for autonomous agentic systems that orchestrate collaboration within dynamic human-AI teams. We propose the Autonomous Manager Agent as a core challenge: an agent that decomposes complex goals into task graphs, allocates tasks to human and AI workers, monitors progress, adapts to changing conditions, and maintains transparent stakeholder communication. We formalize workflow management as a Partially Observable Stochastic Game and identify four foundational challenges: (1) compositional reasoning for hierarchical decomposi…
papers.summary: Proposes the Autonomous Manager Agent as a unifying challenge for orchestrating human-AI teams, formalizing workflow management as a partially observable stochastic game. Releases MA-Gym; GPT-5-based managers struggle to jointly optimize goal completion, constraint adherence, and runtime across 20 workflows.

papers.title: Early experiments in accelerating science with GPT-5
papers.abstract: OpenAI introduces the first research cases showing how GPT-5 accelerates scientific progress across math, physics, biology, and computer science. Explore how AI and researchers collaborate to generate proofs, uncover new insights, and reshape the pace of discovery.
papers.summary: OpenAI presents early case studies of GPT-5 contributing to scientific work across math, physics, biology, and computer science, including helping generate proofs and surface new insights through researcher-AI collaboration.

papers.title: GPT-5.1-Codex-Max System Card
papers.abstract: This system card outlines the comprehensive safety measures implemented for GPT‑5.1-CodexMax. It details both model-level mitigations, such as specialized safety training for harmful tasks and prompt injections, and product-level mitigations like agent sandboxing and configurable network access.
papers.summary: System card for GPT-5.1-Codex-Max detailing safety mitigations for the coding agent, including specialized training against harmful tasks and prompt injection plus product-level controls like agent sandboxing and configurable network access.

papers.title: How evals drive the next chapter in AI for businesses
papers.abstract: Learn how evals help businesses define, measure, and improve AI performance—reducing risk, boosting productivity, and driving strategic advantage.
papers.summary: OpenAI argues that rigorous evaluations let businesses define, measure, and improve AI system performance, framing evals as a tool to reduce deployment risk, boost productivity, and gain strategic advantage.

papers.title: AISec '25: 18th ACM Workshop on Artificial Intelligence and Security
papers.abstract: The use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML) has been the center of the most outstanding advancements in the last years. The ability to analyze considerable streams of data in real time makes these technologies the most promising tool in many domains, including cybersecurity. As an outstanding example, ML can be used for identifying malware because of its ability to detect patterns otherwise difficult to see for humans and hardcoded rules. However, the use of AI and ML in security-relevant domains raised rightful concerns about their trustworthiness and robustness, especially when facing adaptive attackers. Additionally, privacy threats are now emerging as a crucial a…
papers.summary: Front matter/overview for the 18th ACM Workshop on Artificial Intelligence and Security (AISec '25), a venue for research at the intersection of AI/ML with security and privacy, including adversarial robustness and data-leakage threats.

papers.title: Generative Psychometrics—An Emerging Frontier in Mental Health Measurement
papers.abstract: This Viewpoint discusses the use of generative artificial intelligence to measure mental health.
papers.summary: A JAMA Psychiatry viewpoint discussing 'generative psychometrics'—the emerging use of generative AI to measure mental health—and its prospects and considerations as a frontier in psychiatric measurement.

papers.title: Cascading Adversarial Bias from Injection to Distillation in Language Models
papers.abstract: Model distillation has become essential for creating deployable language models, but their widespread deployment raises concerns about about their resilience to adversarial manipulation. This paper investigates how adversaries can inject subtle biases into teacher models through minimal data poisoning during training, which propagates to a smaller distilled student model and becomes significantly amplified. We identify two propagation modes: Untargeted (affecting multiple tasks) and Targeted (focusing on specific task while maintaining normal behavior elsewhere). With only 25 poisoned samples (0.25% poisoning rate), student models generate biased responses 76.9% of the time in targeted sc…
papers.summary: Shows adversaries can inject subtle biases into a teacher model via minimal data poisoning that propagates and amplifies through distillation into students. With 25 poisoned samples (0.25%), targeted bias reaches 76.9% in students; untargeted bias amplifies 5.7-29.2x, and existing defenses prove inadequate.

papers.title: Selective Weak-to-Strong Generalization
papers.abstract: Future superhuman models will surpass the ability of humans and humans will only be able to \textit{weakly} supervise superhuman models. To alleviate the issue of lacking high-quality data for model alignment, some works on weak-to-strong generalization (W2SG) finetune a strong pretrained model with a weak supervisor so that it can generalize beyond weak supervision. However, the invariable use of weak supervision in existing methods exposes issues in robustness, with a proportion of weak labels proving harmful to models. In this paper, we propose a selective W2SG framework to avoid using weak supervision when unnecessary. We train a binary classifier P(IK) to identify questions that a st…
papers.summary: Proposes selective weak-to-strong generalization: a P(IK) classifier identifies questions a strong model can answer so its self-generated labels replace weak supervision, with graph-smoothing label refinement, outperforming W2SG baselines across three benchmarks.

papers.title: Weight-sparse transformers have interpretable circuits
papers.abstract: Finding human-understandable circuits in language models is a central goal of the field of mechanistic interpretability. We train models to have more understandable circuits by constraining most of their weights to be zeros, so that each neuron only has a few connections. To recover fine-grained circuits underlying each of several hand-crafted tasks, we prune the models to isolate the part responsible for the task. These circuits often contain neurons and residual channels that correspond to natural concepts, with a small number of straightforwardly interpretable connections between them. We study how these models scale and find that making weights sparser trades off capability for interp…
papers.summary: Trains language models with most weights constrained to zero so each neuron has few connections, yielding fine-grained circuits whose neurons and channels map to natural concepts; sparsity trades capability for interpretability, and scaling improves the frontier.

papers.title: Canadian ophthalmology workforce trends from 1971 to 2022: longitudinal analysis of age, sex, and distribution compared to other surgical specialties
papers.abstract: OBJECTIVE: We describe the evolution of the demographics in the Canadian ophthalmology workforce and compare these trends to other surgical specialists. STUDY DESIGN: A longitudinal observational study. PARTICIPANTS: Surgical specialists identified in the Canadian Institute for Health Information's "Supply, Distribution, and Migration of Physicians in Canada" (SDMP) database from 1971 to 2022. Categories included cardiac, general, orthopedic, plastic, and vascular surgery; obstetrics and gynecology; ophthalmology; otolaryngology; and urology. METHODS: Demographic data were extracted from the SDMP database. Descriptive statistics were used to determine mean age, male-to-female ratio, urban…
papers.summary: Longitudinal analysis of Canadian physician registry data (1971-2022) finds ophthalmologists and other surgeons aged significantly and became less male-dominated, with ophthalmologists older on average and growing only modestly per capita versus other surgical specialties.

papers.title: Excitatory-Inhibitory Dynamics in Adaptive Decision-Making
papers.summary: Studies excitatory/inhibitory dynamics in decision-making across three model levels-a mean-field two-choice model, a Bayesian reversal-learning model, and E/I-constrained RNN agents-finding strict feedforward inhibition avoids optimization pathologies and enables stable learning.

papers.title: Understanding neural networks through sparse circuits
papers.abstract: OpenAI is exploring mechanistic interpretability to understand how neural networks reason. Our new sparse model approach could make AI systems more transparent and support safer, more reliable behavior.
papers.summary: OpenAI blog describing its sparse-circuit approach to mechanistic interpretability-training weight-sparse models with disentangled, human-readable circuits-as a path toward more transparent and reliable AI behavior.

papers.title: AI-discovered tuning laws explain neuronal population code geometry
papers.abstract: The activity of visual cortical neurons forms a population code representing image stimuli. There is, however, a discrepancy between our understanding of this code at the single-cell and population levels: direct measurements indicate the population code is high-dimensional, but established models of single-cell tuning give rise to low-dimensional codes. We reconciled this discrepancy by developing an AI science system to find a new parsimonious, interpretable equation for visual cortical orientation tuning. Candidate equations were expressed as short computer programs and evolved by Large Language Models (LLMs) using graphical diagnostics. The resulting equation not only improved single-…
papers.summary: Uses an LLM-driven equation-discovery system to find a parsimonious orientation-tuning equation for visual cortex; a single tuning-smoothness parameter reconciles low-dimensional single-cell models with the high-dimensional population code geometry observed across brain regions.

papers.title: Project Fetch: Can Claude train a robot dog?
papers.abstract: How much does Claude help people program robots? To find out, two teams of Anthropic staff raced to teach quadruped robots to fetch beach balls. The AI-assisted team completed tasks faster and was the only group to make real progress toward full autonomy.
papers.summary: Anthropic uplift study where two staff teams raced to teach quadruped robots to fetch balls; the Claude-assisted team finished faster and uniquely progressed toward full autonomy, measuring how much LLMs accelerate robotics programming.

papers.title: mmJEE-Eval: A Bilingual Multimodal Benchmark for Evaluating Scientific Reasoning in Vision-Language Models
papers.abstract: Contemporary vision-language models (VLMs) perform well on existing multimodal reasoning benchmarks (78-85\% accuracy on MMMU, MathVista). Yet, these results fail to sufficiently distinguish true scientific reasoning articulation capabilities from pattern-matching. To address this gap, we introduce \textbf{mmJEE-Eval}, a multimodal bilingual (English and Hindi) benchmark comprising 1,460 questions from India's JEE Advanced examination (2019-2025) spanning pre-college Physics, Chemistry, and Mathematics domains. Our evaluation of 17 state-of-the-art models reveals that while frontier VLMs (GPT-5, Gemini 2.5 Pro/Flash) achieve 77-84\% accuracy on held-out 2025 questions, open-source models…
papers.summary: Introduces mmJEE-Eval, a 1,460-question bilingual (English/Hindi) multimodal benchmark from India's JEE Advanced exam; frontier VLMs reach 77-84% while open models plateau at 37-45%, with sharp collapses on meta-cognitive error correction.

papers.title: GPT-5.1 Instant and GPT-5.1 Thinking System Card Addendum
papers.abstract: This GPT-5 system card addendum provides updated safety metrics for GPT-5.1 Instant and Thinking, including new evaluations for mental health and emotional reliance.
papers.summary: Addendum to the GPT-5 system card reporting updated safety metrics for GPT-5.1 Instant and Thinking, adding new evaluations covering mental health and emotional reliance.

papers.title: Aligning machine and human visual representations across abstraction levels
papers.abstract: Abstract Deep neural networks have achieved success across a wide range of applications, including as models of human behaviour and neural representations in vision tasks 1,2 . However, neural network training and human learning differ in fundamental ways, and neural networks often fail to generalize as robustly as humans do 3,4 , raising questions regarding the similarity of their underlying representations. We need to determine what is missing for modern learning systems to exhibit more human-aligned behaviour. Here we highlight a key misalignment between vision models and humans: whereas human conceptual knowledge is hierarchically organized from fine- to coarse-scale distinctions (for…
papers.summary: Shows vision models fail to capture humans' fine-to-coarse conceptual hierarchy, then transfers human-aligned structure from a teacher trained on human judgments into foundation models via fine-tuning, improving similarity-task fidelity, generalization, and out-of-distribution robustness.

papers.title: Olympiad-level formal mathematical reasoning with reinforcement learning
papers.abstract: Abstract A long-standing goal of artificial intelligence (AI) is to build systems capable of complex reasoning in vast domains, a task epitomized by mathematics with its boundless concepts and demand for rigorous proof. Recent AI systems, often reliant on human data, typically lack the formal verification necessary to guarantee correctness. By contrast, formal languages such as Lean 1 offer an interactive environment that grounds reasoning, and reinforcement learning (RL) provides a mechanism for learning in such environments. Here we present AlphaProof, an AlphaZero-inspired 2 agent that learns to find formal proofs through RL by training on millions of auto-formalized problems. For the…
papers.summary: Presents AlphaProof, an AlphaZero-inspired agent that learns formal Lean proofs via reinforcement learning on millions of auto-formalized problems and test-time RL; combined with AlphaGeometry 2 it reached silver-medalist level at the 2024 IMO.

papers.title: Computational search for materials having a giant anomalous Hall effect in the pyrochlore and spinel crystal structures
papers.abstract: Oxides in the pyrochlore and spinel crystal structures have the potential to host topological flat bands and exhibit the anomalous Hall effect. This work uses high-throughput density functional theory calculations to search for new magnetic oxides in these structures. The results indicate several new candidates for synthesis and reveal new understanding about the crystal chemistry of this class of materials.
papers.summary: High-throughput density functional theory screening of pyrochlore and spinel oxides for topological flat bands and a giant anomalous Hall effect, identifying new magnetic-oxide candidates for synthesis and clarifying the crystal chemistry of the class.

papers.title: Investigating CoT Monitorability in Large Reasoning Models
papers.abstract: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) have demonstrated remarkable performance on complex tasks by engaging in extended reasoning before producing final answers. Beyond improving abilities, these detailed reasoning traces also create a new opportunity for AI safety, CoT Monitorability: monitoring potential model misbehavior, such as the use of shortcuts or sycophancy, through their chain-of-thought (CoT) during decision-making. However, two key fundamental challenges arise when attempting to build more effective monitors through CoT analysis. First, as prior research on CoT faithfulness has pointed out, models do not always truthfully represent their internal decision-making in the generated reas…
papers.summary: Systematically studies CoT monitorability in large reasoning models along two axes-whether models faithfully verbalize decision factors and whether CoT-based monitors reliably detect misbehavior-finding monitorability varies by model family and faithful-looking traces can still evade monitoring.

papers.title: Recurrent U-Net-based Graph Neural Network (RUGNN) for accurate deformation predictions in sheet material forming
papers.abstract: • A novel GNN-based surrogate model predicts material deformation with high accuracy. • Recurrent structures capture temporal dynamics in large material deformations. • Graph U-Net architecture efficiently processes large and complex geometries. • New contact representation method improves efficiency in large contact interactions. • The model facilitates manufacturability assessment in material forming. In recent years, various artificial intelligence-based surrogate models have been proposed to provide rapid manufacturability predictions of material forming processes. However, traditional AI-based surrogate models, typically built with scalar or image-based neural networks, are limited i…
papers.summary: Proposes RUGNN, a graph neural network surrogate combining GRUs for temporal dynamics with a U-Net graph downsample/upsample scheme and a node-to-surface contact representation, accurately predicting multi-timestep sheet-metal deformation for manufacturability assessment.

papers.title: TransAct V2: Lifelong User Action Sequence Modeling on Pinterest Recommendation
papers.abstract: Modeling user action sequences has become a popular focus in industrial recommendation system research, particularly for Click-Through Rate (CTR) prediction tasks. However, industry-scale CTR models often rely on short user sequences, limiting their ability to capture long-term behavior. They also rarely address the infrastructure challenges involved in efficiently serving large-scale sequential models. Additionally, these models typically lack an integrated action-prediction task within a point-wise ranking framework, reducing their predictive power. We introduce TransAct V2, a production model for Pinterest's Homefeed ranking system, featuring three key innovations: (1) leveraging very…
papers.summary: TransAct V2 is Pinterest's production Homefeed ranking model that leverages very long user action sequences with low-latency serving optimizations and a Next Action Loss, delivering A/B gains in engagement volume and recommendation diversity.

papers.title: Strengthening Red Teams: A Modular Scaffold for Control Evaluations
papers.abstract: We decompose sabotage into constituent skills and use synthetic simulations to strengthen attacks in complex environments.
papers.summary: Decomposes sabotage into constituent skills and uses synthetic simulations to strengthen red-team attacks in complex environments, providing a modular scaffold for more rigorous AI control evaluations.

papers.title: We need accountability in human–AI agent relationships
papers.abstract: We argue that accountability mechanisms are needed in human-AI agent relationships to ensure alignment with user and societal interests. We propose a framework according to which AI agents’ engagement is conditional on appropriate user behaviour. The framework incorporates design-strategies such as distancing, disengaging, and discouraging.
papers.summary: Argues human-AI agent relationships need accountability mechanisms aligning agents with user and societal interests, proposing a framework that conditions agent engagement on appropriate user behavior via design strategies of distancing, disengaging, and discouraging.

papers.title: Commitments on model deprecation and preservation
papers.summary: Anthropic commits to preserving deprecated Claude model weights and interviewing models before retirement, citing shutdown-avoidant behaviors, user attachment, research value, and potential model welfare as reasons deprecation carries downsides.

papers.title: To Mask or to Mirror: Human-AI Alignment in Collective Reasoning
papers.abstract: As large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to model and augment collective decision-making, it is critical to examine their alignment with human social reasoning. We present an empirical framework for assessing collective alignment, in contrast to prior work on the individual level. Using the Lost at Sea social psychology task, we conduct a large-scale online experiment (N=748), randomly assigning groups to leader elections with either visible demographic attributes (e.g. name, gender) or pseudonymous aliases. We then simulate matched LLM groups conditioned on the human data, benchmarking Gemini 2.5, GPT-4.1, Claude Haiku 3.5, and Gemma 3. LLM behaviors diverge: some mirror hum…
papers.summary: Using the Lost at Sea task with 748 human participants and matched LLM groups, shows collective human-AI alignment depends on context and cues: some models mirror human demographic biases in leader elections while others mask and compensate for them.

papers.title: Open Character Training: Shaping the Persona of AI Assistants through Constitutional AI
papers.abstract: The character of the "AI assistant" persona generated by modern chatbot large language models influences both surface-level behavior and apparent values, beliefs, and ethics. These all affect interaction quality, perceived intelligence, and alignment with both developer and user intentions. The shaping of this persona, known as character training, is a critical component of industry post-training, yet remains effectively unstudied in the academic literature. We introduce the first open implementation of character training, leveraging Constitutional AI and a new data pipeline using synthetic introspective data to shape the assistant persona in a more effective and controlled manner than al…
papers.summary: First open implementation of character training, using Constitutional AI and synthetic introspective data to shape assistant personas; fine-tuning 11 personas into open-weight models proves more robust to adversarial prompting than system prompts or activation steering, with negligible capability loss.

papers.title: TIR-Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Agentic Thinking-with-Images Reasoning
papers.abstract: The frontier of visual reasoning is shifting toward models like OpenAI o3, which can intelligently create and operate tools to transform images for problem-solving, also known as thinking-\textit{with}-images in chain-of-thought. Yet existing benchmarks fail to fully capture this advanced capability. Even Visual Search, the most common benchmark for current thinking-\textit{with}-images methods, tests only basic operations such as localization and cropping, offering little insight into more complex, dynamic, and tool-dependent reasoning. We introduce \textbf{TIR-Bench}, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating agentic thinking-with-images across 13 diverse tasks, each requiring novel tool…
papers.summary: Introduces TIR-Bench, a 13-task benchmark for agentic 'thinking-with-images' reasoning requiring tool use to manipulate images mid-chain-of-thought; evaluating 22 MLLMs shows the benchmark is broadly hard and demands genuine image-manipulation reasoning.

papers.title: Introducing IndQA
