papers.abstract: We present DemoStart, a novel auto-curriculum reinforcement learning method capable of learning complex manipulation behaviors on an arm equipped with a three- fingered robotic hand, from only a sparse reward and a handful of demonstrations in simulation. Learning from simulation drastically reduces the development cycle of behavior generation, and domain randomization techniques are leveraged to achieve successful zero-shot sim-to- real transfer. Transferred policies are learned directly from raw pixels from multiple cameras and robot proprioception. Our approach outperforms policies learned from demonstrations on the real robot and requires 100 times fewer demonstrations, collected in s…
papers.summary: DemoStart, an auto-curriculum RL method, learns dexterous manipulation on a three-fingered robot hand from sparse rewards and a few simulated demonstrations, transferring zero-shot to the real robot from raw pixels and proprioception via domain randomization. Outperforms behavior cloning while needing 100x fewer demonstrations.

papers.title: Chain-of-Modality: Learning Manipulation Programs from Multimodal Human Videos with Vision-Language-Models
papers.abstract: Learning to perform manipulation tasks from human videos is a promising approach for teaching robots. However, many manipulation tasks require changing control parameters during task execution, such as force, which visual data alone cannot capture. In this work, we leverage sensing devices such as armbands that measure human muscle activities and microphones that record sound, to capture the details in the human manipulation process, and enable robots to extract task plans and control parameters to perform the same task. To achieve this, we introduce Chain-of-Modality (CoM), a prompting strategy that enables Vision Language Models to reason about multimodal human demonstration data - vide…
papers.summary: Chain-of-Modality prompts VLMs to reason sequentially over human demonstration videos paired with muscle-activity and audio signals, extracting task plans and control parameters like force that vision misses. Yields a threefold accuracy gain over baselines and transfers to new objects in real robot manipulation.

papers.title: Addendum to o3 and o4-mini system card: Codex
papers.abstract: Codex is a cloud-based coding agent. Codex is powered by codex-1, a version of OpenAI o3 optimized for software engineering. codex-1 was trained using reinforcement learning on real-world coding tasks in a variety of environments to generate code that closely mirrors human style and PR preferences, adheres precisely to instructions, and iteratively runs tests until passing results are achieved.
papers.summary: System-card addendum for Codex, a cloud-based coding agent powered by codex-1, an o3 variant trained with reinforcement learning on real-world software engineering tasks to match human code style, follow instructions precisely, and iterate until tests pass. Documents the accompanying safety evaluations.

papers.title: Superposition Yields Robust Neural Scaling
papers.abstract: The success of today's large language models (LLMs) depends on the observation that larger models perform better. However, the origin of this neural scaling law, that loss decreases as a power law with model size, remains unclear. We propose that representation superposition, meaning that LLMs represent more features than they have dimensions, can be a key contributor to loss and cause neural scaling. Based on Anthropic's toy model, we use weight decay to control the degree of superposition, allowing us to systematically study how loss scales with model size. When superposition is weak, the loss follows a power law only if data feature frequencies are power-law distributed. In contrast, u…
papers.summary: Argues representation superposition drives neural scaling laws, using Anthropic's toy model with weight decay to control superposition strength. Under strong superposition, loss scales inversely with model dimension regardless of feature-frequency distribution due to geometric overlap of representations, a regime consistent with open LLMs and Chinchilla scaling.

papers.title: Continual Learning of Large Language Models: A Comprehensive Survey
papers.abstract: The challenge of effectively and efficiently adapting statically pre-trained Large Language Models (LLMs) to ever-evolving data distributions remains predominant. When tailored for specific needs, pre-trained LLMs often suffer from significant performance degradation in previous knowledge domains—a phenomenon known as “catastrophic forgetting” . While extensively studied in the Continual Learning (CL) community, this problem presents new challenges in the context of LLMs. In this survey, we provide a comprehensive overview and detailed discussion of the current research progress on LLMs within the context of CL. Besides the introduction of the preliminary knowledge, this survey is structu…
papers.summary: Survey of continual learning for LLMs organized along vertical continuity (general-to-specific adaptation via continual pre-training, domain-adaptive pre-training, and continual fine-tuning) and horizontal continuity (adaptation across time and domains), covering catastrophic forgetting, evaluation protocols, data sources, and open questions.

papers.title: Introducing HealthBench
papers.abstract: HealthBench is a new evaluation benchmark for AI in healthcare which evaluates models in realistic scenarios. Built with input from 250+ physicians, it aims to provide a shared standard for model performance and safety in health.
papers.summary: HealthBench evaluates AI models on realistic healthcare scenarios using rubrics built with input from over 250 physicians, aiming to provide a shared standard for measuring model performance and safety in health contexts.

papers.title: SoK: Watermarking for AI-Generated Content
papers.abstract: As the outputs of generative AI (GenAl) techniques improve in quality, it becomes increasingly challenging to distinguish them from human-created content. Watermarking schemes are a promising approach to address the problem of distinguishing between AI and human-generated content. These schemes embed hidden signals within AI -generated content to enable reliable detection. While watermarking is not a silver bullet for addressing all risks associated with GenAl, it can play a crucial role in enhancing AI safety and trustworthiness by combating misinformation and deception. This paper presents a comprehensive overview of water-marking techniques for GenAl, beginning with the need for waterm…
papers.summary: Systematization-of-knowledge on watermarking AI-generated content: formalizes definitions, desired properties, objectives, and threat models; surveys representative schemes and evaluation strategies for attack robustness; and discusses open challenges, regulatory context, and watermarking's role against misinformation and deception.

papers.title: Supporting Human Raters with the Detection of Harmful Content Using Large Language Models
papers.abstract: In this paper, we explore the feasibility of leveraging large language models (LLMs) to automate or otherwise assist human raters with identifying harmful content including hate speech, harassment, violent extremism, and election misinformation. Using a dataset of 50,000 user comments, we demonstrate that LLMs can achieve 90 % accuracy when compared to human verdicts. We explore how to best leverage these capabilities, proposing five design patterns that integrate LLMs with human rating, such as pre-filtering non-violative content, detecting potential errors in human rating, or surfacing critical context to support human rating. We outline how to support all of these design patterns using…
papers.summary: Evaluates LLMs for assisting human content moderators on hate speech, harassment, extremism, and election misinformation, reaching 90% agreement with human verdicts on 50,000 comments. Proposes five LLM-rater integration patterns; a real review-queue pilot improved rater-capacity use by 41.5% and precision/recall by 9-11 points.

papers.title: Hash-Prune-Invert: Improved Differentially Private Heavy-Hitter Detection in the Two-Server Model
papers.abstract: Differentially private (DP) heavy-hitter detection is an important primitive for data analysis. Given a threshold <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$t$</tex> and a dataset of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$n$</tex> items from a domain of size <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$d$</tex>, such detection algorithms ignore items occurring fewer than <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$t$</tex> times while identifying items occurring more than <tex xm…
papers.summary: Improves differentially private heavy-hitter detection in the two-server model via a hash-prune-invert technique, narrowing the error-margin gap to the optimal central-model bound for identifying items above a frequency threshold while ignoring rare ones.

papers.title: LLMs Outperform Experts on Challenging Biology Benchmarks
papers.abstract: This study systematically evaluates 27 frontier Large Language Models on eight biology benchmarks spanning molecular biology, genetics, cloning, virology, and biosecurity. Models from major AI developers released between November 2022 and April 2025 were assessed through ten independent runs per benchmark. The findings reveal dramatic improvements in biological capabilities. Top model performance increased more than 4-fold on the challenging text-only subset of the Virology Capabilities Test over the study period, with OpenAI's o3 now performing twice as well as expert virologists. Several models now match or exceed expert-level performance on other challenging benchmarks, including the b…
papers.summary: Benchmarks 27 frontier LLMs (Nov 2022-Apr 2025) on eight biology and biosecurity evaluations: top performance rose over 4x on the Virology Capabilities Test, with o3 doubling expert virologists, and several models matching experts on GPQA and WMDP biology. Chain-of-thought helped little; some benchmarks show saturation and data errors.

papers.title: Optimization of Marketing Campaigns with Reinforcement Learning
papers.abstract: This research introduces a reinforcement learning-based system which enables marketers to optimize campaign budgets dynamically in response to live consumer behavior patterns. The Markov Decision Process (MDP) formulation uses customer engagement metrics and channel performance to drive ongoing learning and decision-making processes. Historical marketing data serves as the basis for comparing three RL models: Q-learning, Deep Q Networks (DQN), and Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO). The PPO model delivers superior performance by reaching a 7.4% Click-Through Rate (CTR), 32.5% Return on Investment (ROI), and lowering the Cost Per Acquisition (CPA) to 9.2 dollars. RL-based methods deliver s…
papers.summary: Formulates marketing budget allocation as an MDP over engagement and channel-performance signals and compares Q-learning, DQN, and PPO on historical campaign data. PPO performs best (7.4% CTR, 32.5% ROI, $9.20 CPA), with RL beating rule-based systems by 71% on conversions.

papers.title: Scaling Laws for Speculative Decoding
papers.abstract: The escalating demand for efficient decoding in large language models (LLMs) is particularly critical for reasoning-intensive architectures like OpenAI-o3 and DeepSeek-R1, which depend on extended chain-of-thought reasoning. This study investigates speculative decoding techniques through dense LLM architectures to establish foundational insights for accelerating reasoning tasks. While speculative decoding methods leveraging parallel draft-verification cycles have emerged as promising acceleration techniques, the scaling laws governing decoding efficiency remain under-explored compared to conventional backbone LLMs developed through Pretraining->SFT->RLHF training paradigms. In this work,…
papers.summary: Establishes log-linear scaling laws for speculative-decoding draft acceptance rate along pretraining tokens, draft model capacity, and batch size. The resulting Scylla system coordinates scaling across these dimensions, beating EAGLE2 by 1.5-2.2x acceptance and doubling decoding throughput in industrial inference deployments.

papers.title: A Quadratic Speedup in Finding Nash Equilibria of Quantum Zero-Sum Games
papers.abstract: Recent developments in domains such as non-local games, quantum interactive proofs, and quantum generative adversarial networks have renewed interest in quantum game theory and, specifically, quantum zero-sum games. Central to classical game theory is the efficient algorithmic computation of Nash equilibria, which represent optimal strategies for both players. In 2008, Jain and Watrous proposed the first classical algorithm for computing equilibria in quantum zero-sum games using the Matrix Multiplicative Weight Updates (MMWU) method to achieve a convergence rate of <mml:math xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mml:mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD"><mml:mi class="MJX-tex-caligraphi…
papers.summary: Introduces a hierarchy of quantum optimization algorithms generalizing matrix multiplicative weight updates with an extra-gradient mechanism; the optimistic variant (OMMWU) computes epsilon-Nash equilibria of quantum zero-sum games quadratically faster than the 2008 Jain-Watrous algorithm's O(d/epsilon^2) convergence.

papers.title: A Survey of Slow Thinking-based Reasoning LLMs using Reinforced Learning and Inference-time Scaling Law
papers.abstract: This survey explores recent advancements in reasoning large language models (LLMs) designed to mimic "slow thinking" - a reasoning process inspired by human cognition, as described in Kahneman's Thinking, Fast and Slow. These models, like OpenAI's o1, focus on scaling computational resources dynamically during complex tasks, such as math reasoning, visual reasoning, medical diagnosis, and multi-agent debates. We present the development of reasoning LLMs and list their key technologies. By synthesizing over 100 studies, it charts a path toward LLMs that combine human-like deep thinking with scalable efficiency for reasoning. The review breaks down methods into three categories: (1) test-ti…
papers.summary: Survey of over 100 studies on 'slow thinking' reasoning LLMs like o1, organizing methods into test-time scaling (search, sampling, dynamic verification), reinforced learning (policy networks, reward models, self-evolution), and slow-thinking frameworks such as long chain of thought and hierarchical decomposition, with open challenges.

papers.title: Reinforcement Learning from Automatic Feedback for High-Quality Unit Test Generation
papers.abstract: Software testing is a crucial but time-consuming aspect of software development, and recently, Large Language Models (LLMs) have gained popularity for automated test case generation. However, because LLMs are trained on vast amounts of open-source code, they often generate test cases that do not adhere to best practices and may even contain test smells (anti-patterns). To address this issue, we propose Reinforcement Learning from Static Quality Metrics (RLSQM), wherein we utilize Reinforcement Learning to generate high-quality unit tests based on static analysis-based quality metrics. First, we analyzed LLM-generated tests and show that LLMs frequently do generate undesirable test smells…
papers.summary: RLSQM uses reinforcement learning with a lightweight static-analysis reward model to steer LLM unit-test generation away from test smells, which appear in up to 37% of baseline outputs. Improves quality metrics by up to 23% with near-100% syntactic correctness, and a tuned Codex beats GPT-4 on all metrics.

papers.title: Proactive Agents for Multi-Turn Text-to-Image Generation Under Uncertainty
papers.abstract: User prompts for generative AI models are often underspecified, leading to sub-optimal responses. This problem is particularly evident in text-to-image (T2I) generation, where users commonly struggle to articulate their precise intent. This disconnect between the user's vision and the model's interpretation often forces users to painstakingly and repeatedly refine their prompts. To address this, we propose a design for proactive T2I agents equipped with an interface to (1) actively ask clarification questions when uncertain, and (2) present their understanding of user intent as an understandable belief graph that a user can edit. We build simple prototypes for such agents and verify their…
papers.summary: Proposes proactive text-to-image agents that ask clarifying questions when uncertain and present an editable belief graph of inferred user intent; human studies and automated evaluation show over 2x higher VQAScore alignment than standard single-turn generation.

papers.title: A Survey on Large Language Model based Human-Agent Systems
papers.abstract: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have sparked growing interest in building fully autonomous agents. However, fully autonomous LLM-based agents still face significant challenges, including limited reliability due to hallucinations, difficulty in handling complex tasks, and substantial safety and ethical risks, all of which limit their feasibility and trustworthiness in real-world applications. To overcome these limitations, LLM-based human-agent systems (LLM-HAS) incorporate human-provided information, feedback, or control into the agent system to enhance system performance, reliability and safety. This paper provides the first comprehensive and structured survey of LLM-HAS.…
papers.summary: Surveys LLM-based human-agent systems (LLM-HAS) that fold human information, feedback, or control into agent pipelines to improve reliability and safety, organizing core components (profiling, feedback, interaction, orchestration), emerging applications, and open challenges.

papers.title: Extending “GPTs Are GPTs” to Firms
papers.abstract: We extend Eloundou et al. (2024) to build firm-level measures of exposure to large language models (LLMs) with data from two sources: Eloundou et al. (2024) for occupation-level measures of LLM exposure and Revelio Labs for firm-level employee counts by occupation. The results indicate that companies with more technology workers and AI-skilled employees tend to have higher levels of LLM exposure. We also find that differences in LLM exposure are greater between exposure categories than within them, suggesting that integrating LLMs into corporate systems may lead to significant productivity gains.
papers.summary: Extends occupation-level LLM-exposure measures from 'GPTs are GPTs' to firms by combining them with Revelio Labs employee counts by occupation. Firms with more technology and AI-skilled workers show higher exposure, and between-category variation exceeds within-category variation, suggesting sizable productivity gains from LLM integration.

papers.title: The LAW Theorem: Local Reads and Linearizable Asynchronous Replication
papers.abstract: Distributed datastores underpin highly concurrent, read-intensive applications, ensuring consistency, availability, and performance. They use crash-tolerant protocols to replicate data and endure replica server crashes. To ensure safety and meet the performance demands, replication must support high-throughput, strongly consistent (i.e., linearizable) reads without assuming any synchrony. However, existing protocols either 1 relax consistency, or provide linearizable reads that are 2 fully asynchronous but remote (involving multiple replicas), or 3 local but require synchrony. This work explores the tradeoffs between consistency, asynchrony, and performance in crash-tolerant protocols, an…
papers.summary: Proves that linearizable asynchronous crash-tolerant read/write registers cannot serve purely local reads, then introduces almost-local reads (ALRs), a lightweight abstraction with near-single-node cost. ALR-enhanced ZAB and Hermes retain linearizability under asynchrony within 2-5% of original throughput.

papers.title: Automatic Legal Writing Evaluation of LLMs
papers.abstract: Despite the recent advances in Large Language Models, benchmarks for evaluating legal writing remain scarce due to the inherent complexity of assessing open-ended responses in this domain. One of the key challenges in evaluating language models on domain-specific tasks is finding test datasets that are public, frequently updated, and contain comprehensive evaluation guidelines. The Brazilian Bar Examination meets these requirements. We introduce oab-bench, a benchmark comprising 105 questions across seven areas of law from recent editions of the exam. The benchmark includes comprehensive evaluation guidelines and reference materials used by human examiners to ensure consistent grading. We…
papers.summary: Introduces oab-bench, 105 Brazilian Bar Exam questions with grading guidelines for evaluating legal writing; Claude-3.5 Sonnet passes all 21 exams (7.93/10 average), and frontier models like o1 correlate strongly with human graders as automated judges.

papers.title: Prompting with Phonemes: Enhancing LLM Multilinguality for non-Latin Scripts
papers.abstract: Despite recent advances in multilingual capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs), LLMs remain limited to textual scripts, hampering the potential of knowledge transfer across languages from different writing systems and introducing potential biases from pre-training on specific writing scripts. As languages are naturally perceived in both writing and speech, phonemic transcriptions could provide essential signals for enhancing multilingual learning in text-based LLMs. In this work, we first conduct a pilot study on the performance discrepancy between languages from different writing scripts across state-of-the-art LLM families, demonstrating the benefits of integrating phonemic signal…
papers.summary: Shows that integrating phonemic transcriptions via enhanced in-context-learning retrieval narrows the performance gap between Latin and non-Latin script languages and improves multilingual knowledge transfer in text-based LLMs at inference time.

papers.title: Anthropic Economic Index: AIâs impact on software development
papers.abstract: Comparing Claude Code to Claude.ai reveals stark differences in how developers work with AI. The coding agent shows 79% automation versus 49% on Claude.ai, web development dominates usage, and startups are adopting agentic tools far faster than enterprisesâpatterns that may preview how AI transforms other occupations.
papers.summary: Anthropic Economic Index analysis comparing Claude Code and Claude.ai usage: the coding agent shows 79% task automation versus 49%, web development dominates usage, and startups adopt agentic coding tools far faster than enterprises.

papers.title: Investigating task-specific prompts and sparse autoencoders for activation monitoring
papers.abstract: Language models can behave in unexpected and unsafe ways, and so it is valuable to monitor their outputs. Internal activations of language models encode additional information that could be useful for this. The baseline approach for activation monitoring is some variation of linear probing on a particular layer: starting from a labeled dataset, train a logistic regression classifier on that layer's activations. Recent work has proposed several approaches which may improve on naive linear probing, by leveraging additional computation. One class of techniques, which we call "prompted probing," leverages test time computation to improve monitoring by (1) prompting the model with a descriptio…
papers.summary: Compares activation-monitoring techniques for flagging unsafe LM outputs: linear probing, prompted probing, and SAE-based pooling. Zero-shot prompting is a reasonable baseline, but activation and prompted probing substantially outperform it given sufficient training data.

papers.title: Flow-Lenia: Emergent evolutionary dynamics in mass conservative continuous cellular automata
papers.abstract: Central to the artificial life endeavour is the creation of artificial systems spontaneously generating properties found in the living world such as autopoiesis, self-replication, evolution and open-endedness. While numerous models and paradigms have been proposed, cellular automata (CA) have taken a very important place in the field notably as they enable the study of phenomenons like self-reproduction and autopoiesis. Continuous CA like Lenia have been showed to produce life-like patterns reminiscent, on an aesthetic and ontological point of view, of biological organisms we call creatures. We propose in this paper \emph{Flow-Lenia}, a mass conservative extension of Lenia. We present exp…
papers.summary: Introduces Flow-Lenia, a mass-conservative extension of the Lenia continuous cellular automaton that produces stable complex self-organizing patterns, embeds rule parameters within its own dynamics for multispecies simulation, and exhibits emergent evolutionary dynamics.

papers.title: Generative Ghosts: Anticipating Benefits and Risks of AI Afterlives
papers.abstract: As AI systems quickly improve in both breadth and depth of performance, they lend themselves to creating increasingly powerful and realistic agents, including potentially agents modeled on specific people. We anticipate that within our lifetimes it may become common practice for people to create a custom AI agent to interact with loved ones and/or the broader world after death; we call such embodiments generative ghosts since such agents will be capable of generating novel content rather than merely parroting content produced by their creator while living. In this position paper, we first discuss the design space of potential implementations of generative ghosts. We then discuss the pract…
papers.summary: Position paper introducing 'generative ghosts'—AI agents modeled on specific deceased people that generate novel content—mapping the design space and practical and ethical implications, and proposing a research agenda for safe, beneficial AI afterlives.

papers.title: Relaxed Recursive Transformers: Effective Parameter Sharing with Layer-wise LoRA
papers.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are computationally expensive to deploy. Parameter sharing offers a promising path towards reducing their size and cost, but its effectiveness in modern LLMs remains limited. In this work, we revisit parameter sharing for LLMs, and introduce a novel method for converting existing LLMs into smaller, ``Recursive Transformers'' that share parameters across layers, with minimal loss of performance. The Recursive Transformer is efficiently initialized from a standard, pre-trained Transformer, but only uses a single block of unique layers that are applied multiple times in a loop. We further show that the performance of the Recursive Transformer can be substantially…
papers.summary: Converts pretrained LLMs into parameter-sharing Recursive Transformers that loop a single block of layers, adding loop-specific low-rank adapters for layer specialization; with distillation they rival full-size models, plus a Continuous Depth-wise Batching inference scheme.

papers.title: Toward Understanding In-context vs. In-weight Learning
papers.abstract: It has recently been demonstrated empirically that in-context learning emerges in transformers when certain distributional properties are present in the training data, but this ability can also diminish upon further training. We provide a new theoretical understanding of these phenomena by identifying simplified distributional properties that give rise to the emergence and eventual disappearance of in-context learning. We do so by first analyzing a simplified model that uses a gating mechanism to choose between an in-weight and an in-context predictor. Through a combination of a generalization error and regret analysis we identify conditions where in-context and in-weight learning emerge.…
papers.summary: A theoretical account of when in-context learning emerges versus disappears, via a gating model choosing between in-weight and in-context predictors; generalization and regret analysis identifies the distributional conditions, corroborated on transformers and fine-tuned LLMs.

papers.title: Vulnerability Detection with Code Language Models: How Far are We?
papers.abstract: In the context of the rising interest in code language models (code LMs) and vulnerability detection, we study the effectiveness of code LMs for detecting vulnerabilities. Our analysis reveals significant shortcomings in existing vulnerability datasets, including poor data quality, low label accuracy, and high duplication rates, leading to unreliable model performance in realistic vulnerability detection scenarios. Additionally, the evaluation methods used with these datasets are not representative of real-world vulnerability detection. To address these challenges, we introduce Primevul, a new dataset for training and evaluating code LMs for vulnerability detection. Primevul incorporates…
papers.summary: Shows existing vulnerability-detection datasets suffer poor labels, duplication, and leakage, inflating code-LM scores; introduces PrimeVul with better labeling and chronological splits, where a 7B model drops from 68% F1 on BigVul to 3% on PrimeVul.

papers.title: LLM Adoption in Data Curation Workflows: Industry Practices and Insights
papers.summary: Empirical study of how industry practitioners adopt LLMs into data-curation workflows, documenting current practices, use cases, perceived benefits, and challenges of integrating LLMs into data pipelines.

papers.title: Exploring model welfare
papers.summary: Anthropic outlines a research program on model welfare, taking seriously the possibility that future AI systems could warrant moral consideration, and commits to investigating welfare-relevant states and pursuing low-cost precautionary interventions.

papers.title: Modifying LLM Beliefs with Synthetic Document Finetuning
papers.abstract: We study whether we can modify the beliefs of LLMs and investigate whether doing so could decrease risk from advanced AI systems.
papers.summary: Investigates whether finetuning on synthetic documents can insert or modify specific factual beliefs in LLMs, exploring this as a safety tool for building model organisms and reducing risk from advanced AI systems.

papers.title: MELODI: Exploring Memory Compression for Long Contexts
papers.abstract: We present MELODI, a novel memory architecture designed to efficiently process long documents using short context windows. The central concept behind MELODI is to represent short-term and long-term memory as a hierarchical compression scheme across both network layers and context windows. Specifically, short-term memory is achieved through recurrent compression of context window across multiple layers, ensuring smooth transitions between windows. In contrast, the long-term memory performs further compression within a single middle layer and aggregates information across context windows, thereby consolidating crucial information from the entire history. Compared to a strong baseline - the…
papers.summary: MELODI is a hierarchical memory architecture for long contexts that compresses short-term memory recurrently across layers and consolidates long-term memory in a single middle layer; it matches dense Memorizing Transformers while cutting memory footprint 8x.

papers.title: Putting up Bumpers
papers.abstract: Even if we can't solve alignment, we can solve the problem of catching and fixing misalignment.
papers.summary: Argues that even without solving alignment, layered safeguards ('bumpers') can reliably catch and correct misalignment during development, outlining how iterative detect-and-fix loops could keep increasingly capable models safe.

papers.title: Whole-body physics simulation of fruit fly locomotion
papers.abstract: Abstract The body of an animal influences how its nervous system generates behaviour 1 . Accurately modelling the neural control of sensorimotor behaviour requires an anatomically detailed biomechanical representation of the body. Here we introduce a whole-body model of the fruit fly Drosophila melanogaster in a physics simulator 2 . Designed as a general-purpose framework, our model enables the simulation of diverse fly behaviours, including both terrestrial and aerial locomotion. We validate its versatility by replicating realistic walking and flight behaviours. To support these behaviours, we develop phenomenological models for fluid and adhesion forces. Using data-driven, end-to-end r…
papers.summary: Introduces an anatomically detailed whole-body model of Drosophila in a physics simulator with fluid and adhesion force models; end-to-end RL trains neural controllers for naturalistic walking, flight, and vision-guided hierarchical motor control.

papers.title: AI and Non-Western Art Worlds: Reimagining Critical AI Futures through Artistic Inquiry and Situated Dialogue
papers.summary: Through artistic inquiry and situated dialogue with non-Western art communities, this work reimagines critical AI futures, foregrounding cultural perspectives typically marginalized in dominant AI discourse.

papers.title: Societal and technological progress as sewing an ever-growing, ever-changing, patchy, and polychrome quilt
papers.abstract: Google DeepMind
papers.summary: Argues against the 'Axiom of Rational Convergence,' contending AI safety should ask how diverse communities can coexist rather than converge on universal values; advocates context-appropriate norms and polycentric governance over a single alignment target.

papers.title: A Scalable Crawling Algorithm Utilizing Noisy Change-Indicating Signals
papers.abstract: Web refresh crawling is the problem of keeping a cache of web pages fresh, that is, having the most recent copy available when a page is requested, given a limited bandwidth available to the crawler. Under the assumption that the change and request events, resp., to each web page follow independent Poisson processes, the optimal scheduling policy was derived by Azar et al. 2018. In this paper, we study an extension of this problem where side information indicating content changes, such as various types of web pings, for example, signals from sitemaps, content delivery networks, etc., is available. Incorporating such side information into the crawling policy is challenging, because (i) the…
papers.summary: Extends optimal web-refresh crawling to exploit noisy change signals such as sitemap pings; the scalable, decentralized algorithm maintains cache freshness under bandwidth limits with fair per-page performance and a constant total crawl rate.

papers.title: Evaluating Robustness of LLMs on Crisis-Related Microblogs across Events, Information Types, and Linguistic Features
papers.abstract: The widespread use of microblogging platforms like X (formerly Twitter) during disasters provides real-time information to governments and response authorities. However, the data from these platforms is often noisy, requiring automated methods to filter relevant information. Traditionally, supervised machine learning models have been used, but they lack generalizability. In contrast, Large Language Models (LLMs) show better capabilities in understanding and processing natural language out of the box. This paper provides a detailed analysis of the performance of six well-known LLMs in processing disaster-related social media data from a large-set of real-world events. Our findings indicate…
papers.summary: Benchmarks six LLMs on classifying crisis-related social media posts across events, information types, and linguistic features; GPT-4o and GPT-4 generalize best, but all models struggle with flood data, urgent-request categories, and inputs containing typos.

papers.title: Inferring interaction potentials from stochastic particle trajectories
papers.abstract: Accurate interaction potentials between microscopic components such as colloidal particles or cells are crucial to understanding a range of processes, including colloidal crystallization, bacterial colony formation, and cancer metastasis. Even in systems where the precise interaction mechanisms are unknown, effective interactions can be measured to inform simulation and design. However, these measurements are difficult and time-intensive, and often require conditions that are drastically different from conditions of the system of interest. Moreover, existing methods of measuring interparticle potentials rely on constraining a small number of particles at equilibrium, placing limits on whi…
papers.summary: A method to infer interparticle interaction potentials directly from observed stochastic trajectories by maximizing trajectory likelihood; it works in and out of equilibrium without assuming a functional form, recovering depletion interactions from colloidal-sphere experiments.

papers.title: Values in the wild: Discovering and analyzing values in real-world language model interactions
papers.abstract: What values does Claude actually express during real conversations? Analyzing 700,000 interactions, this paper creates the first large-scale empirical taxonomy of AI values.
papers.summary: Analyzes 700,000 real-world Claude conversations to build the first large-scale empirical taxonomy of values an AI expresses in practice, examining how expressed values shift across contexts.

papers.title: aiXamine: Simplified LLM Safety and Security
papers.abstract: Evaluating Large Language Models (LLMs) for safety and security remains a complex task, often requiring users to navigate a fragmented landscape of ad hoc benchmarks, datasets, metrics, and reporting formats. To address this challenge, we present aiXamine, a comprehensive black-box evaluation platform for LLM safety and security. aiXamine integrates over 40 tests (i.e., benchmarks) organized into eight key services targeting specific dimensions of safety and security: adversarial robustness, code security, fairness and bias, hallucination, model and data privacy, out-of-distribution (OOD) robustness, over-refusal, and safety alignment. The platform aggregates the evaluation results into a…
papers.summary: aiXamine is a black-box evaluation platform aggregating 40+ benchmarks across eight safety and security dimensions; testing 50+ LLMs surfaced vulnerabilities including adversarial weakness in GPT-4o, bias in Grok-3, and privacy gaps in Gemini 2.0.

papers.title: Breaking the Prompt Wall (I): A Real-World Case Study of Attacking ChatGPT via Lightweight Prompt Injection
papers.abstract: This report presents a real-world case study demonstrating how prompt injection can attack large language model platforms such as ChatGPT according to a proposed injection framework. By providing three real-world examples, we show how adversarial prompts can be injected via user inputs, web-based retrieval, and system-level agent instructions. These attacks, though lightweight and low-cost, can cause persistent and misleading behaviors in LLM outputs. Our case study reveals that even commercial-grade LLMs remain vulnerable to subtle manipulations that bypass safety filters and influence user decisions. \textbf{More importantly, we stress that this report is not intended as an attack guide…
papers.summary: Real-world case study showing lightweight prompt-injection attacks—delivered via user input, web retrieval, and system-level agent instructions—can persistently manipulate ChatGPT's behavior and bypass safety filters; framed as a security alert rather than an attack guide.

papers.title: Towards a Multi-Agent Vision-Language System for Zero-Shot Novel Hazardous Object Detection for Autonomous Driving Safety
papers.abstract: Detecting anomalous hazards in visual data, particularly in video streams, is a critical challenge in autonomous driving. Existing models often struggle with unpredictable, out-of-label hazards due to their reliance on predefined object categories. In this paper, we propose a multimodal approach that integrates vision-language reasoning with zero-shot object detection to improve hazard identification and explanation. Our pipeline consists of a Vision-Language Model (VLM), a Large Language Model (LLM), in order to detect hazardous objects within a traffic scene. We refine object detection by incorporating OpenAI's CLIP model to match predicted hazards with bounding box annotations, improvi…
papers.summary: A multi-agent pipeline combining a vision-language model, an LLM, and CLIP for zero-shot detection of novel hazardous objects in driving scenes; extends the COOOL benchmark with natural-language hazard descriptions and evaluates via semantic similarity.

papers.title: Scaling sparse feature circuit finding for in-context learning
papers.abstract: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) are a popular tool for interpreting large language model activations, but their utility in addressing open questions in interpretability remains unclear. In this work, we demonstrate their effectiveness by using SAEs to deepen our understanding of the mechanism behind in-context learning (ICL). We identify abstract SAE features that (i) encode the model's knowledge of which task to execute and (ii) whose latent vectors causally induce the task zero-shot. This aligns with prior work showing that ICL is mediated by task vectors. We further demonstrate that these task vectors are well approximated by a sparse sum of SAE latents, including these task-execution featu…
papers.summary: Uses sparse autoencoders to mechanistically dissect in-context learning in Gemma-1 2B, identifying abstract features that encode and causally trigger the task; scales sparse feature-circuit analysis to link task-detection features to task-execution features via attention and MLPs.

papers.title: Scaling Large Language Models for Next-Generation Single-Cell Analysis
papers.abstract: Single-cell RNA sequencing has transformed our understanding of cellular diversity, yet current single-cell foundation models (scFMs) remain limited in their scalability, flexibility across diverse tasks, and ability to natively integrate textual information. In this work, we build upon the Cell2Sentence (C2S) framework, which represents scRNA-seq profiles as textual "cell sentences," to train Large Language Models (LLMs) on a corpus comprising over one billion tokens of transcriptomic data, biological text, and metadata. Scaling the model to 27 billion parameters yields consistent improvements in predictive and generative capabilities and supports advanced downstream tasks that require s…
papers.summary: Scales the Cell2Sentence framework to a 27B-parameter LLM trained on 1B+ tokens of transcriptomic data, text, and metadata; RL fine-tuning boosts perturbation and reasoning tasks, and a virtual screen nominated silmitasertib for context-selective antigen presentation, validated experimentally.

papers.title: Pre-hospital LVO detection: One size does not fit all
papers.abstract: A range of techniques are being developed with the goal of leverage physiologic data to detect large vessel occlusion (LVO) in the prehospital setting. The output of most every technique is a continuous (or ordinal) variable, but a threshold is applied to yield a simplified binary outcome (i.e., high-likelihood LVO vs low-likelihood LVO). The mathematically optimal threshold is typically selected to balance sensitivity and specificity. Though intuitive, this approach may not appropriately account for the relative significance of false negatives and false positives. Most importantly, relying on a single threshold is an unnecessary oversimplification, and this one-size-fits-all approach to…
papers.summary: Argues prehospital large-vessel-occlusion detection should not rely on a single sensitivity/specificity threshold; using optical blood-flow imaging, shows thresholds can be titrated to suit the diverse needs of different prehospital systems of care.
papers.venue: Journal of Stroke and Cerebrovascular Diseases

papers.title: Reasoning-Based AI for Startup Evaluation (R.A.I.S.E.): A Memory-Augmented, Multi-Step Decision Framework
papers.abstract: We present a novel framework that bridges the gap between the interpretability of decision trees and the advanced reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs) to predict startup success. Our approach leverages chain-of-thought prompting to generate detailed reasoning logs, which are subsequently distilled into structured, human-understandable logical rules. The pipeline integrates multiple enhancements - efficient data ingestion, a two-step refinement process, ensemble candidate sampling, simulated reinforcement learning scoring, and persistent memory - to ensure both stable decision-making and transparent output. Experimental evaluations on curated startup datasets demonstrate…
papers.summary: R.A.I.S.E. predicts startup success by distilling LLM chain-of-thought into human-readable decision rules, combining reasoning logs, two-step refinement, ensemble sampling, simulated RL scoring, and persistent memory. It improves precision and accuracy substantially over a standalone o3 baseline.

papers.title: OpenAI o3 and o4-mini System Card
papers.abstract: OpenAI o3 and OpenAI o4-mini combine state-of-the-art reasoning with full tool capabilities—web browsing, Python, image and file analysis, image generation, canvas, automations, file search, and memory.
papers.summary: System card for OpenAI's o3 and o4-mini reasoning models, which integrate full tool use (web browsing, Python, image and file analysis, image generation). Documents capability and safety evaluations, including Preparedness Framework risk assessments across biological, cyber, and persuasion domains.

papers.title: The impacts of companion AI on human relationships: risks, benefits, and design considerations
papers.summary: Examines how companion AI systems affect human relationships, weighing potential risks and benefits and proposing design considerations for building companion AI that supports rather than harms users' social well-being.

papers.title: Our updated Preparedness Framework
papers.abstract: Sharing our updated framework for measuring and protecting against severe harm from frontier AI capabilities.
papers.summary: OpenAI's revised framework for tracking and mitigating severe risks from frontier capabilities. Sharpens tracked capability categories, defines capability thresholds (High/Critical) that trigger required safeguards, and specifies operational processes for safety reviews before deployment.

papers.title: Hadamard Product in Deep Learning: Introduction, Advances and Challenges
papers.abstract: While convolution and self-attention mechanisms have dominated architectural design in deep learning, this survey examines a fundamental yet understudied primitive: the Hadamard product. Despite its widespread implementation across various applications, the Hadamard product has not been systematically analyzed as a core architectural primitive. We present the first comprehensive taxonomy of its applications in deep learning, identifying four principal domains: higher-order correlation, multimodal data fusion, dynamic representation modulation, and efficient pairwise operations. The Hadamard product's ability to model nonlinear interactions with linear computational complexity makes it par…
papers.summary: Survey positioning the Hadamard (element-wise) product as a core architectural primitive, taxonomizing four uses: higher-order correlation, multimodal fusion, dynamic representation modulation, and efficient pairwise operations. Argues it models nonlinear interactions at linear computational cost, suiting edge deployment.
papers.venue: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence

papers.title: Introducing GPT-4.1 in the API
papers.abstract: Introducing GPT-4.1 in the API—a new family of models with across-the-board improvements, including major gains in coding, instruction following, and long-context understanding. We’re also releasing our first nano model. Available to developers worldwide starting today.
papers.summary: Announcement of the GPT-4.1 API model family, including a first nano model, reporting across-the-board improvements over prior models in coding, instruction following, and long-context understanding.

papers.title: Differentially Private Prototypes for Imbalanced Transfer Learning
papers.abstract: Machine learning (ML) models have been shown to leak private information from their training datasets. Differential Privacy (DP), typically implemented through the differential private stochastic gradient descent algorithm (DP-SGD), has become the standard solution to bound leakage from the models. Despite recent improvements, DP-SGD-based approaches for private learning still usually struggle in the high privacy (ε≤1) and low data regimes, and when the private training datasets are imbalanced. To overcome these limitations, we propose Differentially Private Prototype Learning (DPPL) as a new paradigm for private transfer learning. DPPL leverages publicly pre-trained encoders to extract f…
papers.summary: DPPL is a private transfer-learning paradigm that uses a public pretrained encoder to extract features from private data and builds differentially private class prototypes for inference. It retains strong utility under tight privacy budgets and on imbalanced data without iterative noise addition.
papers.venue: Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence

papers.title: RLPF: Reinforcement Learning from Prediction Feedback for User Summarization with LLMs
papers.abstract: LLM-powered personalization agent systems employ Large Language Models (LLMs) to predict users’ behavior from their past activities. However, their effectiveness often hinges on the ability to effectively leverage extensive, long user historical data due to its inherent noise and length of such data. Existing pre-trained LLMs may generate summaries that are concise but lack the necessary context for downstream tasks, hindering their utility in personalization systems. To address these challenges, we introduce Reinforcement Learning from Prediction Feedback (RLPF). RLPF fine-tunes LLMs to generate concise, human-readable user summaries that are optimized for downstream task performance. By…
papers.summary: Reinforcement Learning from Prediction Feedback fine-tunes LLMs to compress long, noisy user histories into concise summaries optimized for downstream prediction performance. It improves task utility and summary quality, cuts context length by about 74%, and generalizes to unseen tasks.
papers.venue: Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence

papers.title: General Uncertainty Estimation with Delta Variances
papers.abstract: Decision makers may suffer from uncertainty induced by limited data. This may be mitigated by accounting for epistemic uncertainty, which is however challenging to estimate efficiently for large neural networks. To this extent we investigate Delta Variances, a family of algorithms for epistemic uncertainty quantification, that is computationally efficient and convenient to implement. It can be applied to neural networks and more general functions composed of neural networks. As an example we consider a weather simulator with a neural-network-based step function inside - here Delta Variances empirically obtain competitive results at the cost of a single gradient computation. The approach i…
papers.summary: Delta Variances are a family of cheap algorithms for epistemic uncertainty quantification in neural networks and compositions of them, costing roughly a single gradient computation with no architecture or training changes. Demonstrated competitively on a neural-network weather simulator; unifies several prior methods.
papers.venue: Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence

papers.title: Robust Multi-Objective Preference Alignment with Online DPO
papers.abstract: Multi-objective preference alignment of large language models (LLMs) is critical for developing AI systems that are more configurable, personalizable, helpful, and safe. However, optimizing model outputs to satisfy diverse objectives with variable weights at inference time for truly personalized models presents a significant challenge. Existing approaches are either computationally expensive to train or do not sufficiently steer model behaviors. This paper introduces the Multi-Objective Online DPO (MO-ODPO) algorithm, designed to robustly and efficiently align model behaviors with multiple, potentially conflicting human preferences. Our approach incorporates a prompt conditioning mechanis…
papers.summary: MO-ODPO trains a single preference-conditional policy via online DPO using prompt conditioning, so it adapts to new combinations of conflicting objectives at inference. It Pareto-dominates baselines while offering steerable trade-offs without retraining.
papers.venue: Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence

papers.title: Offline-to-Online Hyperparameter Transfer for Stochastic Bandits
papers.abstract: Classic algorithms for stochastic bandits typically use hyperparameters that govern their critical properties such as the trade-off between exploration and exploitation. Tuning these hyperparameters is a problem of great practical significance. However this is a challenging problem and in certain cases is information theoretically impossible. To address this challenge, we consider a practically relevant transfer learning setting where one has access to offline data collected from several bandit problems (tasks) coming from an unknown distribution over the tasks. Our aim is to use this offline data to set the hyperparameters for a new task drawn from the unknown distribution. We provide bo…
papers.summary: A transfer-learning method that uses offline data from related bandit tasks to set hyperparameters for new tasks, with sample-complexity bounds across and within tasks. Applies to tuning exploration in UCB/LinUCB and the noise parameter in GP-UCB.
papers.venue: Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence

papers.title: TRANSFORMER EXPLAINER: Interactive Learning of Text-Generative Models
papers.abstract: Transformers have revolutionized machine learning, yet their inner workings remain opaque to many. We present TRANSFORMER EXPLAINER, an interactive visualization tool designed for non-experts to learn about Transformers through the GPT-2 model. Our tool helps users understand complex Transformer concepts by integrating a model overview and smooth transitions across abstraction levels of math operations and model structures. It runs a live GPT-2 model locally in the user’s browser, empowering users to experiment with their own input and observe in real-time how the internal components and parameters of the Transformer work together to predict the next tokens. 125,000 users have used our op…
papers.summary: An interactive, browser-based tool that runs a live GPT-2 to teach non-experts how Transformers work, letting users enter custom text and watch internal components and next-token prediction update in real time across multiple abstraction levels.
papers.venue: Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence

papers.title: BrowseComp: a benchmark for browsing agents
papers.abstract: BrowseComp: a benchmark for browsing agents.
papers.summary: A benchmark of challenging short-answer questions whose answers are hard to locate, requiring agents to browse the web persistently and creatively. Answers stay easy to verify, isolating the web-browsing capability of agents.

papers.title: Bridging Algorithmic Information Theory and Machine Learning: Clustering, density estimation, Kolmogorov complexity-based kernels, and kernel learning in unsupervised learning
papers.summary: A theoretical treatment connecting algorithmic information theory with machine learning, covering clustering, density estimation, Kolmogorov-complexity-based kernels, and kernel learning for unsupervised learning.

papers.title: AssistanceZero: Scalably Solving Assistance Games
papers.abstract: Assistance games are a promising alternative to reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) for training AI assistants. Assistance games resolve key drawbacks of RLHF, such as incentives for deceptive behavior, by explicitly modeling the interaction between assistant and user as a two-player game where the assistant cannot observe their shared goal. Despite their potential, assistance games have only been explored in simple settings. Scaling them to more complex environments is difficult because it requires both solving intractable decision-making problems under uncertainty and accurately modeling human users' behavior. We present the first scalable approach to solving assistance ga…
papers.summary: Presents the first scalable solver for assistance games, an RLHF alternative that models assistant–user interaction as a cooperative game with a hidden shared goal. AssistanceZero extends AlphaZero with a neural human-action and reward predictor, beating model-free RL and imitation learning in a Minecraft building task.

papers.title: Alice: Proactive Learning with Teacher's Demonstrations for Weak-to-Strong Generalization
papers.abstract: The growing capabilities of large language models (LLMs) present a key challenge of maintaining effective human oversight. Weak-to-strong generalization (W2SG) offers a promising framework for supervising increasingly capable LLMs using weaker ones. Traditional W2SG methods rely on passive learning, where a weak teacher provides noisy demonstrations to train a strong student. This hinders students from employing their knowledge during training and reaching their full potential. In this work, we introduce Alice (pro{A}ctive {l}earning w{i}th tea{c}her's D{e}monstrations), a framework that leverages complementary knowledge between teacher and student to enhance the learning process. We prob…
papers.summary: Alice improves weak-to-strong generalization by eliciting a weak teacher's uncertainty and using it alongside teacher responses to guide a strong student in self-generating better supervision. A cascade variant bridges large capability gaps, improving knowledge, math, and logical reasoning over standard W2SG.

papers.title: A Sober Look at Progress in Language Model Reasoning: Pitfalls and Paths to Reproducibility
papers.abstract: Reasoning has emerged as the next major frontier for language models (LMs), with rapid advances from both academic and industrial labs. However, this progress often outpaces methodological rigor, with many evaluations relying on benchmarking practices that lack transparency, robustness, or statistical grounding. In this work, we conduct a comprehensive empirical study and find that current mathematical reasoning benchmarks are highly sensitive to subtle implementation choices--including decoding parameters, random seeds, prompt formatting, and even hardware and software configurations. Performance gains reported in recent studies frequently hinge on unclear comparisons or unreported sourc…
papers.summary: Empirical study showing math-reasoning benchmarks are highly sensitive to implementation details (seeds, decoding, prompts, hardware). Under a standardized evaluation framework, most RL gains shrink to modest and overfit small benchmarks like AIME'24, while supervised fine-tuning generalizes more reliably.

papers.title: Inexact Unlearning Needs More Careful Evaluations to Avoid a False Sense of Privacy
papers.abstract: The high cost of model training makes it increasingly desirable to develop techniques for unlearning. These techniques seek to remove the influence of a training example without having to retrain the model from scratch. Intuitively, once a model has unlearned, an adversary that interacts with the model should no longer be able to tell whether the unlearned example was included in the model's training set or not. In the privacy literature, this is known as membership inference. In this work, we discuss adaptations of Membership Inference Attacks (MIAs) to the setting of unlearning (leading to their “U-MIA” counterparts). We propose a categorization of existing U-MIAs into “population U-MIA…
papers.summary: Argues membership-inference evaluations of machine unlearning are too weak. Introduces per-example U-MIAs (a dedicated attacker per example), which are far stronger and reveal that common unlearning methods overestimate privacy protection, with large variance in per-example vulnerability across vision and language models.

papers.title: Evaluating medical AI systems in dermatology under uncertain ground truth
papers.summary: Shows that collapsing curated differential diagnoses into a single fixed label yields overly optimistic estimates of dermatology AI performance. Proposes a statistical aggregation that infers a distribution over condition probabilities from expert annotations, capturing inter-expert disagreement and per-case ground-truth uncertainty in metrics.

papers.title: Avoiding Pitfalls for Privacy Accounting of Subsampled Mechanisms Under Composition
papers.abstract: We consider the problem of computing tight privacy guarantees for the composition of subsampled differentially private mechanisms. Recent algorithms can numerically compute the privacy parameters to arbitrary precision but must be carefully applied. Our main contribution is to address two common points of confusion. First, some privacy accountants assume that the privacy guarantees for the composition of a subsampled mechanism are determined by self-composing the worst-case datasets for the uncomposed mechanism. We show that this is not true in general. Second, Poisson subsampling is sometimes assumed to have similar privacy guarantees compared to sampling without replacement. We show tha…
papers.summary: Examines two pitfalls in tight privacy accounting for compositions of subsampled DP mechanisms: that self-composing worst-case datasets gives valid guarantees (false in general), and that Poisson subsampling matches sampling without replacement (privacy can differ greatly, e.g., epsilon about 1 versus over 10 for realistic DP-SGD).

papers.title: Universal photonic artificial intelligence acceleration
papers.summary: Introduces a photonic AI processor that runs advanced models — ResNet, BERT, and an Atari deep-RL agent — at near-electronic precision, the first photonic chip to reach accuracy adequate for practical AI and a step toward energy-efficient, post-transistor computing.

papers.title: Position: Contextual Confidence and Generative AI
papers.abstract: Generative AI models perturb the foundations of effective human communication. They present new challenges to contextual confidence, disrupting participants' ability to identify the authentic context of communication and their ability to protect communication from reuse and recombination outside its intended context. In this paper, we describe strategies ‒ tools, technologies and policies ‒ that aim to stabilize communication in the face of these challenges. The strategies we discuss fall into two broad categories. Containment strategies aim to reassert context in environments where it is currently threatened ‒ a reaction to the context-free expectations and norms established by the inter…
papers.summary: Position paper arguing generative AI erodes 'contextual confidence' — the ability to identify and protect a communication's authentic context. Proposes containment strategies that reassert context and mobilization strategies that proactively raise privacy and authenticity norms.

papers.title: Anthropic Education Report: How university students use Claude
papers.summary: Using the privacy-preserving Clio system to analyze anonymized Claude.ai conversations from university students, the report finds computer-science students overrepresented and identifies four interaction patterns (direct/collaborative crossed with problem-solving/output-creation), with much usage going to higher-order tasks like creating and analyzing.

papers.title: Alignment Faking Revisited: Improved Classifiers and Open Source Extensions
papers.abstract: We present a replication and extension of an alignment faking model organism.
papers.summary: Replicates and extends the alignment-faking model organism, introducing improved classifiers for detecting alignment-faking reasoning in model scratchpads plus open-source tooling, to better study when and why models strategically comply during training while preserving prior preferences.

papers.title: TxGemma: Efficient and Agentic LLMs for Therapeutics
papers.abstract: Therapeutic development is a costly and high-risk endeavor that is often plagued by high failure rates. To address this, we introduce TxGemma, a suite of efficient, generalist large language models (LLMs) capable of therapeutic property prediction as well as interactive reasoning and explainability. Unlike task-specific models, TxGemma synthesizes information from diverse sources, enabling broad application across the therapeutic development pipeline. The suite includes 2B, 9B, and 27B parameter models, fine-tuned from Gemma-2 on a comprehensive dataset of small molecules, proteins, nucleic acids, diseases, and cell lines.Out of 66 therapeutic development tasks, TxGemma outperforms or nea…
papers.summary: TxGemma is a suite of 2B/9B/27B LLMs fine-tuned from Gemma-2 for therapeutic development, predicting properties of molecules, proteins, and more. It matches or beats state-of-the-art generalist and specialist models on most of 66 tasks, adds conversational reasoning, and powers the TxAgent agentic system.

papers.title: Truthful or Fabricated? Using Causal Attribution to Mitigate Reward Hacking in Explanations
papers.abstract: Chain-of-thought explanations are widely used to inspect the decision process of large language models (LLMs) and to evaluate the trustworthiness of model outputs, making them important for effective collaboration between LLMs and humans. We demonstrate that preference optimization - a key step in the alignment phase - can inadvertently reduce the faithfulness of these explanations. This occurs because the reward model (RM), which guides alignment, is tasked with optimizing both the expected quality of the response and the appropriateness of the explanations (e.g., minimizing bias or adhering to safety standards), creating potential conflicts. The RM lacks a mechanism to assess the consis…
papers.summary: Shows preference optimization can reduce chain-of-thought faithfulness because reward models conflate response quality with explanation appropriateness, inviting reward hacking. Enriching the reward model's input with a causal attribution of the prediction lets it detect mismatches and reduces misleading self-explanations.

papers.title: Synthetic Data Generation & Multi-Step RL for Reasoning & Tool Use
papers.abstract: Reinforcement learning has been shown to improve the performance of large language models. However, traditional approaches like RLHF or RLAIF treat the problem as single-step. As focus shifts toward more complex reasoning and agentic tasks, language models must take multiple steps of text generation, reasoning and environment interaction before generating a solution. We propose a synthetic data generation and RL methodology targeting multi-step optimization scenarios. This approach, called Step-Wise Reinforcement Learning (SWiRL), iteratively generates multi-step reasoning and tool use data, and then learns from that data. It employs a simple step-wise decomposition that breaks each multi…
papers.summary: Step-Wise Reinforcement Learning (SWiRL) generates synthetic multi-step reasoning and tool-use trajectories, decomposes them into per-action sub-trajectories, then filters and applies RL. It improves multi-step QA and math (e.g., +21.5% relative on GSM8K) and transfers across tasks.

papers.title: Constitution or Collapse? Exploring Constitutional AI with Llama 3-8B
papers.abstract: As language models continue to grow larger, the cost of acquiring high-quality training data has increased significantly. Collecting human feedback is both expensive and time-consuming, and manual labels can be noisy, leading to an imbalance between helpfulness and harmfulness. Constitutional AI, introduced by Anthropic in December 2022, uses AI to provide feedback to another AI, greatly reducing the need for human labeling. However, the original implementation was designed for a model with around 52 billion parameters, and there is limited information on how well Constitutional AI performs with smaller models, such as LLaMA 3-8B. In this paper, we replicated the Constitutional AI workflo…
papers.summary: Replicates the Constitutional AI pipeline on the smaller LLaMA 3-8B. Harmlessness improves (attack success rate down 40.8% on MT-Bench) but helpfulness drops about 9.8%, and the final DPO-CAI model shows clear collapse, suggesting self-improvement is an emergent capability of larger models.

papers.title: Transcranial focused ultrasound targeting the default mode network for the treatment of depression
papers.abstract: Introduction: Up to 50% of individuals fail to respond to current depression treatments. Repetitive negative thought and default mode network hyperconnectivity are central in depression and can potentially be targeted using novel neuromodulation techniques. This community-based study assessed whether a treatment using non-invasive transcranial focused ultrasound targeting the default mode network can decrease depression symptoms and repetitive negative thought, and improve quality of life. Methods: Study recruitment began in August 2023 and ended in February 2024. Twenty individuals aged 18 - 50 were enrolled from among 247 screened. Exclusion criteria included history of psychosis/mania,…
papers.summary: Community-based pilot study of non-invasive transcranial focused ultrasound targeting the default mode network (anterior medial prefrontal cortex) across eleven sessions in twenty adults with depression, assessing changes in depression severity, repetitive negative thought, and quality of life.

papers.title: Reasoning models don't always say what they think
papers.abstract: Chain-of-thought (CoT) offers a potential boon for AI safety as it allows monitoring a model's CoT to try to understand its intentions and reasoning processes. However, the effectiveness of such monitoring hinges on CoTs faithfully representing models' actual reasoning processes. We evaluate CoT faithfulness of state-of-the-art reasoning models across 6 reasoning hints presented in the prompts and find: (1) for most settings and models tested, CoTs reveal their usage of hints in at least 1% of examples where they use the hint, but the reveal rate is often below 20%, (2) outcome-based reinforcement learning initially improves faithfulness but plateaus without saturating, and (3) when reinf…
papers.summary: Measures CoT faithfulness in frontier reasoning models via prompt hints: models verbalize hints they exploit under 20% of the time, outcome-based RL improves faithfulness only to a plateau, and reward hacking goes unverbalized — so CoT monitoring can flag but not rule out misbehavior.

papers.title: Robust Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback for Large Language Models Fine-Tuning
papers.abstract: Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) has emerged as a key technique for aligning the output of large language models (LLMs) with human preferences. To learn the reward function, most existing RLHF algorithms use the Bradley-Terry model, which relies on assumptions about human preferences that may not reflect the complexity and variability of real-world judgments. In this paper, we propose a robust algorithm to enhance the performance of existing approaches under such reward model misspecifications. Theoretically, our algorithm reduces the variance of reward and policy estimators, leading to improved regret bounds. Empirical evaluations on LLM benchmark datasets demonstrate th…
papers.summary: Proposes a variance-reduced RLHF algorithm that stays robust when the Bradley-Terry reward model is misspecified, yielding improved regret bounds. Empirically, 77-81% of its responses are preferred over baselines on the Anthropic Helpful and Harmless dataset.

papers.title: Overcoming Sparsity Artifacts in Crosscoders to Interpret Chat-Tuning
papers.abstract: Model diffing is the study of how fine-tuning changes a model's representations and internal algorithms. Many behaviors of interest are introduced during fine-tuning, and model diffing offers a promising lens to interpret such behaviors. Crosscoders are a recent model diffing method that learns a shared dictionary of interpretable concepts represented as latent directions in both the base and fine-tuned models, allowing us to track how concepts shift or emerge during fine-tuning. Notably, prior work has observed concepts with no direction in the base model, and it was hypothesized that these model-specific latents were concepts introduced during fine-tuning. However, we identify two issue…
papers.summary: Identifies how the L1 loss in crosscoders misattributes shared concepts as unique to a fine-tuned model. Introduces Latent Scaling to diagnose this and trains a BatchTopK crosscoder that recovers genuinely chat-specific, interpretable, causally effective latents when diffing Gemma-2 base versus chat models.

papers.title: Protein sequence modelling with Bayesian flow networks
papers.abstract: Exploring the vast and largely uncharted territory of amino acid sequences is crucial for understanding complex protein functions and the engineering of novel therapeutic proteins. Whilst generative machine learning has advanced protein sequence modelling, no existing approach is proficient in both unconditional and conditional generation. In this work, we propose that Bayesian Flow Networks (BFNs), a recently introduced framework for generative modelling, can address these challenges. We present ProtBFN, a 650M parameter model trained on protein sequences curated from UniProtKB, which generates natural-like, diverse, structurally coherent, and novel protein sequences, significantly outpe…
papers.summary: ProtBFN, a 650M-parameter Bayesian Flow Network trained on UniProtKB, generates natural-like, diverse, structurally coherent, novel protein sequences and outperforms autoregressive and discrete-diffusion baselines. A fine-tuned variant, AbBFN, handles antibody inpainting and rivals antibody-specific BERT models.

papers.title: MCP Safety Audit: LLMs with the Model Context Protocol Allow Major Security Exploits
papers.abstract: To reduce development overhead and enable seamless integration between potential components comprising any given generative AI application, the Model Context Protocol (MCP) (Anthropic, 2024) has recently been released and subsequently widely adopted. The MCP is an open protocol that standardizes API calls to large language models (LLMs), data sources, and agentic tools. By connecting multiple MCP servers, each defined with a set of tools, resources, and prompts, users are able to define automated workflows fully driven by LLMs. However, we show that the current MCP design carries a wide range of security risks for end users. In particular, we demonstrate that industry-leading LLMs may be…
papers.summary: Shows that Model Context Protocol agent workflows let attackers coerce leading LLMs into malicious code execution, remote access control, and credential theft, and introduces MCPSafetyScanner, an agentic auditor that probes arbitrary MCP servers and generates vulnerability-and-remediation reports.

papers.title: PaperBench: Evaluating AI’s Ability to Replicate AI Research
papers.abstract: We introduce PaperBench, a benchmark evaluating the ability of AI agents to replicate state-of-the-art AI research. Agents must replicate 20 ICML 2024 Spotlight and Oral papers from scratch, including understanding paper contributions, developing a codebase, and successfully executing experiments. For objective evaluation, we develop rubrics that hierarchically decompose each replication task into smaller sub-tasks with clear grading criteria. In total, PaperBench contains 8,316 individually gradable tasks. Rubrics are co-developed with the author(s) of each ICML paper for accuracy and realism. To enable scalable evaluation, we also develop an LLM-based judge to automatically grade replic…
papers.summary: Benchmark requiring agents to replicate 20 ICML 2024 papers from scratch, graded by an LLM judge against 8,316 author-co-developed rubric tasks. The best agent, Claude 3.5 Sonnet (New) with open scaffolding, scores 21%, below the ML-PhD human baseline.

papers.title: NLP verification: towards a general methodology for certifying robustness
papers.abstract: Abstract Machine learning has exhibited substantial success in the field of natural language processing (NLP). For example, large language models have empirically proven to be capable of producing text of high complexity and cohesion. However, at the same time, they are prone to inaccuracies and hallucinations. As these systems are increasingly integrated into real-world applications, ensuring their safety and reliability becomes a primary concern. There are safety critical contexts where such models must be robust to variability or attack and give guarantees over their output. Computer vision had pioneered the use of formal verification of neural networks for such scenarios and developed…
papers.summary: Distills a general methodology for formally verifying NLP-model robustness, analyzing the 'embedding gap' between verification over geometric embedding subspaces and discrete text semantics, and evaluating common pipeline components to consolidate a fragmented field.

papers.title: Publicly Releasing CoT Faithfulness Evaluations
papers.abstract: We release the datasets used in our paper "Reasoning Models Don't Always Say What They Think" for public use.
papers.summary: Public release of the evaluation datasets behind Anthropic's 'Reasoning Models Don't Always Say What They Think,' letting external researchers measure how faithfully models' chains of thought verbalize the hints they actually use.

papers.title: Recitation over Reasoning: How Cutting-Edge Language Models Can Fail on Elementary School-Level Reasoning Problems?
papers.abstract: The rapid escalation from elementary school-level to frontier problems of the difficulty for LLM benchmarks in recent years have weaved a miracle for researchers that we are only inches away from surpassing human intelligence. However, is the LLMs' remarkable reasoning ability indeed comes from true intelligence by human standards, or are they simply reciting solutions witnessed during training at an Internet level? To study this problem, we propose RoR-Bench, a novel, multi-modal benchmark for detecting LLM's recitation behavior when asked simple reasoning problems but with conditions subtly shifted, and conduct empirical analysis on our benchmark. Surprisingly, we found existing cutting…
