papers.title: Mastering Board Games by External and Internal Planning with Language Models
papers.abstract: While large language models perform well on a range of complex tasks (e.g., text generation, question answering, summarization), robust multi-step planning and reasoning remains a considerable challenge for them. In this paper we show that search-based planning can significantly improve LLMs’ playing strength across several board games (Chess, Fischer Random Chess, Connect Four, and Hex). We introduce, compare and contrast two major approaches: In external search, the model guides Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) rollouts and evaluations without calls to an external engine, and in internal search,the model directly generates in-context a linearized tree of potential futures and a resulting…
papers.summary: Shows search-based planning substantially improves LLM play in Chess, Fischer Random, Connect Four, and Hex: external search uses the model to guide MCTS rollouts, while internal search generates a linearized lookahead tree in-context. Domain pretraining minimizes hallucinated states and moves; chess play reaches Grandmaster level at human-like search budgets.

papers.title: Probabilistic weather forecasting with machine learning
papers.abstract: Abstract Weather forecasts are fundamentally uncertain, so predicting the range of probable weather scenarios is crucial for important decisions, from warning the public about hazardous weather to planning renewable energy use. Traditionally, weather forecasts have been based on numerical weather prediction (NWP) 1 , which relies on physics-based simulations of the atmosphere. Recent advances in machine learning (ML)-based weather prediction (MLWP) have produced ML-based models with less forecast error than single NWP simulations 2,3 . However, these advances have focused primarily on single, deterministic forecasts that fail to represent uncertainty and estimate risk. Overall, MLWP has r…
papers.summary: GenCast, a diffusion-based ML ensemble weather model trained on reanalysis data, produces 15-day global probabilistic forecasts at 0.25-degree resolution in 8 minutes. It outperforms ECMWF's operational ENS ensemble on 97.2% of 1,320 targets, including extreme weather, tropical cyclone tracks, and wind-power prediction.

papers.title: Jailbreak Defense in a Narrow Domain: Limitations of Existing Methods and a New Transcript-Classifier Approach
papers.abstract: Defending large language models against jailbreaks so that they never engage in a broadly-defined set of forbidden behaviors is an open problem. In this paper, we investigate the difficulty of jailbreak-defense when we only want to forbid a narrowly-defined set of behaviors. As a case study, we focus on preventing an LLM from helping a user make a bomb. We find that popular defenses such as safety training, adversarial training, and input/output classifiers are unable to fully solve this problem. In pursuit of a better solution, we develop a transcript-classifier defense which outperforms the baseline defenses we test. However, our classifier defense still fails in some circumstances, whi…
papers.summary: Studies jailbreak defense for a single narrowly-scoped forbidden behavior (bomb-making help). Safety training, adversarial training, and input/output classifiers all fall short; a proposed transcript-classifier defense beats these baselines but still fails in some cases, showing narrow-domain defense remains hard.

papers.title: Noise Injection Reveals Hidden Capabilities of Sandbagging Language Models
papers.abstract: Capability evaluations play a crucial role in assessing and regulating frontier AI systems. The effectiveness of these evaluations faces a significant challenge: strategic underperformance, or ``sandbagging'', where models deliberately underperform during evaluation. Sandbagging can manifest either through explicit developer intervention or through unintended model behavior, presenting a fundamental obstacle to accurate capability assessment. We introduce a novel sandbagging detection method based on injecting noise of varying magnitudes into model weights. While non-sandbagging models show predictable performance degradation with increasing noise, we demonstrate that sandbagging models e…
papers.summary: Introduces a sandbagging detector that injects noise of varying magnitude into model weights: honest models degrade predictably while sandbagging models show anomalous performance gains. The signal is model-agnostic and even elicits Mistral Large 120B's full capability when it underperforms unprompted.

papers.title: Introducing the Anthropic Fellows Program for AI Safety Research
papers.abstract: We're launching the Anthropic Fellows Program for AI Safety Research, a pilot initiative designed to accelerate AI safety research and foster research talent.
papers.summary: Announces the Anthropic Fellows Program, a pilot that funds and mentors external researchers to work on AI safety, aiming to accelerate safety research and grow talent in the field.

papers.title: Stage-Wise Model Diffing
papers.abstract: We report some developing work on the Anthropic interpretability team, which might be of interest to researchers working actively in this space. We'd ask you to treat these results like those of a colleague sharing some thoughts or preliminary experiments for a few minutes at a lab meeting, rather than a mature paper.
papers.summary: Extends dictionary learning to model diffing: an SAE dictionary trained on a pre-finetuning transformer is itself finetuned on the new data or model, so tracking feature evolution isolates dataset- versus model-driven changes. Applied to sleeper agents, it surfaces backdoor-related features.

papers.title: MagicMirror: Fast and High-Quality Avatar Generation with a Constrained Search Space
papers.summary: A framework for fast, high-quality 3D human avatar generation and personalization from text prompts. By constraining the generation search space, it improves photo-realism and efficiency over prior text-to-avatar methods.

papers.title: 3D gamma analysis between treatment plans for nominally beam‐matched medical linear accelerators using PyMedPhys
papers.abstract: Abstract Beam‐matched linear accelerators (linacs) enable flexible patient scheduling and efficient treatment delivery in the event of unexpected machine downtime. The purpose of this study was to test the feasibility of 3D gamma index as an additional metric beyond standard measurement‐based comparisons for more efficient evaluation of treatment plans between linacs with nominally matched beam models to ensure safe patient transfer. Seventeen 3D conformal radiotherapy (3DCRT) plans and thirty‐six volumetric‐modulated radiation therapy (VMAT) plans for different disease sites were selected from the original linac. An in‐house script was used to automatically create new plans for the targe…
papers.summary: Tests the 3D gamma index as an extra check for safely transferring radiotherapy plans between nominally beam-matched linacs. Using PyMedPhys on 17 3DCRT and 36 VMAT plans, 2%/2 mm pass rates exceeded 99%, though stricter 1%/1 mm criteria failed in several cases.

papers.title: Addendum: Accurate structure prediction of biomolecular interactions with AlphaFold 3
papers.abstract: In our original article, we provided an extensive
papers.summary: An addendum to the AlphaFold 3 paper. AlphaFold 3 is a diffusion-based model that jointly predicts the 3D structure of biomolecular complexes-proteins, nucleic acids, small molecules, ions, and modified residues-markedly improving accuracy over earlier specialized tools. The addendum supplements the original article.

papers.title: How Well Do Large Language Models Perform Latent Multi-Hop Reasoning without Exploiting Shortcuts?
papers.abstract: We evaluate the ability of today's LLMs to perform latent multi-hop reasoning on factual knowledge, or the latent composability of the models to internally recall and compose the facts separately learned during training, by assessing the model completions on multi-hop queries like "In the year Scarlett Johansson was born, the Summer Olympics were hosted in the country of". However, it may be challenging to accurately measure the latent reasoning ability without careful data construction and evaluation. For example, models may have developed shortcuts by numerous encounters of the head entity "Scarlett Johansson" and the answer entity "United States" in the same training sequences or merel…
papers.summary: Tests whether LLMs truly perform latent multi-hop reasoning over factual knowledge rather than exploiting training-data shortcuts. Introduces SOCRATES, a shortcut-controlled evaluation set built to exclude co-occurring entity pairs, and measures how well models internally recall and compose separately-learned facts.

papers.title: RE-Bench: Evaluating frontier AI R&D capabilities of language model agents against human experts
papers.abstract: Frontier AI safety policies highlight automation of AI research and development (R&D) by AI agents as an important capability to anticipate. However, there exist few evaluations for AI R&D capabilities, and none that are highly realistic and have a direct comparison to human performance. We introduce RE-Bench (Research Engineering Benchmark, v1), which consists of 7 challenging, open-ended ML research engineering environments and data from 71 8-hour attempts by 61 distinct human experts. We confirm that our experts make progress in the environments given 8 hours, with 82% of expert attempts achieving a non-zero score and 24% matching or exceeding our strong reference solutions. We compare…
papers.summary: Introduces RE-Bench, seven open-ended ML research-engineering environments with data from 71 8-hour expert attempts as a human baseline. Top AI agents score 4x humans at a 2-hour budget, but humans overtake with more time; agents iterate far faster and cheaper.

papers.title: Understanding LLM Embeddings for Regression
papers.abstract: With the rise of large language models (LLMs) for flexibly processing information as strings, a natural application is regression, specifically by preprocessing string representations into LLM embeddings as downstream features for metric prediction. In this paper, we provide one of the first comprehensive investigations into embedding-based regression and demonstrate that LLM embeddings as features can be better for high-dimensional regression tasks than using traditional feature engineering. This regression performance can be explained in part due to LLM embeddings over numeric data inherently preserving Lipschitz continuity over the feature space. Furthermore, we quantify the contributi…
papers.summary: Investigates using LLM embeddings of string inputs as features for regression. Embedding-based features can beat traditional feature engineering on high-dimensional tasks, partly because embeddings preserve Lipschitz continuity over numeric inputs; larger model size and language understanding do not reliably improve regression accuracy.

papers.title: Do I Know This Entity? Knowledge Awareness and Hallucinations in Language Models
papers.abstract: Hallucinations in large language models are a widespread problem, yet the mechanisms behind whether models will hallucinate are poorly understood, limiting our ability to solve this problem. Using sparse autoencoders as an interpretability tool, we discover that a key part of these mechanisms is entity recognition, where the model detects if an entity is one it can recall facts about. Sparse autoencoders uncover meaningful directions in the representation space, these detect whether the model recognizes an entity, e.g. detecting it doesn't know about an athlete or a movie. This suggests that models can have self-knowledge: internal representations about their own capabilities. These direc…
papers.summary: Uses sparse autoencoders to find that LLMs represent entity recognition-whether the model can recall facts about an entity-as causal directions in activation space. Steering these directions induces refusal on known entities or hallucination on unknown ones; chat finetuning repurposes this base-model mechanism.

papers.title: Advancing red teaming with people and AI
papers.abstract: Advancing red teaming with people and AI
papers.summary: Outlines OpenAI's approach to combining human and automated red teaming, accompanied by two papers: one detailing how external human red-teaming campaigns are designed and run, and one on automatically generating diverse, effective attacks using reinforcement learning.

papers.title: ViSTa Dataset: Do vision-language models understand sequential tasks?
papers.abstract: Using vision-language models (VLMs) as reward models in reinforcement learning holds promise for reducing costs and improving safety. So far, VLM reward models have only been used for goal-oriented tasks, where the agent must reach a particular final outcome. We explore VLMs' potential to supervise tasks that cannot be scored by the final state alone. To this end, we introduce ViSTa, a dataset for evaluating Vision-based understanding of Sequential Tasks. ViSTa comprises over 4,000 videos with step-by-step descriptions in virtual home, Minecraft, and real-world environments. Its novel hierarchical structure -- basic single-step tasks composed into more and more complex sequential tasks --…
papers.summary: Introduces ViSTa, 4,000+ videos with step-by-step descriptions across virtual-home, Minecraft, and real-world settings, hierarchically structured to test vision-language models on sequential tasks. State-of-the-art VLMs recognize objects well but largely fail to judge task sequences, with only GPT-4o showing non-trivial performance.

papers.title: InterPro: the protein sequence classification resource in 2025
papers.abstract: InterPro (https://www.ebi.ac.uk/interpro) is a freely accessible resource for the classification of protein sequences into families. It integrates predictive models, known as signatures, from multiple member databases to classify sequences into families and predict the presence of domains and significant sites. The InterPro database provides annotations for over 200 million sequences, ensuring extensive coverage of UniProtKB, the standard repository of protein sequences, and includes mappings to several other major resources, such as Gene Ontology (GO), Protein Data Bank in Europe (PDBe) and the AlphaFold Protein Structure Database. In this publication, we report on the status of InterPro…
papers.summary: Reports InterPro v101, which classifies over 200 million protein sequences into 85,000 families and domains by integrating member-database signatures. Updates include deeper AlphaFold structure integration and AI-assisted family descriptions, with over 5,000 new entries added in two years.

papers.title: Learning high-accuracy error decoding for quantum processors
papers.abstract: Abstract Building a large-scale quantum computer requires effective strategies to correct errors that inevitably arise in physical quantum systems 1 . Quantum error-correction codes 2 present a way to reach this goal by encoding logical information redundantly into many physical qubits. A key challenge in implementing such codes is accurately decoding noisy syndrome information extracted from redundancy checks to obtain the correct encoded logical information. Here we develop a recurrent, transformer-based neural network that learns to decode the surface code, the leading quantum error-correction code 3 . Our decoder outperforms other state-of-the-art decoders on real-world data from Goog…
papers.summary: Develops a recurrent transformer neural-network decoder for the surface code that outperforms state-of-the-art decoders on Google's Sycamore data at distances 3 and 5, scales to distance 11 in simulation, and adapts to real hardware noise from limited experimental samples.

papers.title: UniIR: Training and Benchmarking Universal Multimodal Information Retrievers
papers.summary: Introduces UniIR, an instruction-guided universal multimodal retriever that handles heterogeneous retrieval tasks (e.g., text-to-image, image-to-text) in a single model, plus the M-BEIR benchmark for training and evaluating retrieval across diverse modality combinations.

papers.title: Volumetric Rendering with Baked Quadrature Fields
papers.summary: Proposes a Neural Radiance Field representation for non-opaque scenes that bakes quadrature fields into textured polygons, enabling fast inference while preserving NeRF's high-quality novel-view rendering and avoiding costly dense volume sampling.

papers.title: A statistical approach to model evaluations
papers.summary: Argues for reporting model-eval results with statistical rigor and gives concrete recommendations: use the Central Limit Theorem for standard errors, cluster errors for related questions, reduce variance, and run paired analyses comparing models on the same questions to detect real capability differences.

papers.title: Mapping out the Space of Human Feedback for Reinforcement Learning: A Conceptual Framework
papers.abstract: Reinforcement Learning from Human feedback (RLHF) has become a powerful tool to fine-tune or train agentic machine learning models. Similar to how humans interact in social contexts, we can use many types of feedback to communicate our preferences, intentions, and knowledge to an RL agent. However, applications of human feedback in RL are often limited in scope and disregard human factors. In this work, we bridge the gap between machine learning and human-computer interaction efforts by developing a shared understanding of human feedback in interactive learning scenarios. We first introduce a taxonomy of feedback types for reward-based learning from human feedback based on nine key dimens…
papers.summary: Proposes a conceptual framework unifying the many forms of human feedback used to train RL agents. Defines a feedback taxonomy across nine dimensions and seven quality metrics, then derives design requirements for systems that learn from human feedback, bridging ML and HCI.

papers.title: Value Imprint: A Technique for Auditing the Human Values Embedded in RLHF Datasets
papers.abstract: LLMs are increasingly fine-tuned using RLHF datasets to align them with human preferences and values. However, very limited research has investigated which specific human values are operationalized through these datasets. In this paper, we introduce Value Imprint, a framework for auditing and classifying the human values embedded within RLHF datasets. To investigate the viability of this framework, we conducted three case study experiments by auditing the Anthropic/hh-rlhf, OpenAI WebGPT Comparisons, and Alpaca GPT-4-LLM datasets to examine the human values embedded within them. Our analysis involved a two-phase process. During the first phase, we developed a taxonomy of human values thro…
papers.summary: Presents Value Imprint, a framework that audits the human values embedded in RLHF datasets. Using a values taxonomy and a trained classifier on Anthropic HH-RLHF, OpenAI WebGPT, and Alpaca-GPT4 data, it finds information/knowledge values dominate while prosocial and democratic values are underrepresented.

papers.title: Democratic AI is Possible. The Democracy Levels Framework Shows How It Might Work
papers.abstract: This position paper argues that effectively "democratizing AI" requires democratic governance and alignment of AI, and that this is particularly valuable for decisions with systemic societal impacts. Initial steps -- such as Meta's Community Forums and Anthropic's Collective Constitutional AI -- have illustrated a promising direction, where democratic processes could be used to meaningfully improve public involvement and trust in critical decisions. To more concretely explore what increasingly democratic AI might look like, we provide a "Democracy Levels" framework and associated tools that: (i) define milestones toward meaningfully democratic AI, which is also crucial for substantively p…
papers.summary: Argues that democratizing AI requires democratic governance and alignment, especially for systemically impactful decisions. Proposes a Democracy Levels framework with milestones and tools to guide organizations and evaluate efforts like Collective Constitutional AI toward more legitimate, participatory AI decision-making.

papers.title: The Pfam protein families database: embracing AI/ML
papers.abstract: The Pfam protein families database is a comprehensive collection of protein domains and families used for genome annotation and protein structure and function analysis (https://www.ebi.ac.uk/interpro/). This update describes major developments in Pfam since 2020, including decommissioning the Pfam website and integration with InterPro, harmonization with the ECOD structural classification, and expanded curation of metagenomic, microprotein and repeat-containing families. We highlight how AlphaFold structure predictions are being leveraged to refine domain boundaries and identify new domains. New families discovered through large-scale sequence similarity analysis of AlphaFold models are d…
papers.summary: Updates the Pfam protein-families database: decommissioned the standalone site and merged into InterPro, harmonized with the ECOD structural classification, and expanded metagenomic and microprotein curation. AlphaFold structures refine domain boundaries, and the deep-learning Pfam-N expands UniProtKB coverage by 8.8%.

papers.title: Rapid Response: Mitigating LLM Jailbreaks with a Few Examples
papers.abstract: As large language models (LLMs) grow more powerful, ensuring their safety against misuse becomes crucial. While researchers have focused on developing robust defenses, no method has yet achieved complete invulnerability to attacks. We propose an alternative approach: instead of seeking perfect adversarial robustness, we develop rapid response techniques to look to block whole classes of jailbreaks after observing only a handful of attacks. To study this setting, we develop RapidResponseBench, a benchmark that measures a defense's robustness against various jailbreak strategies after adapting to a few observed examples. We evaluate five rapid response methods, all of which use jailbreak pr…
papers.summary: Proposes blocking whole classes of jailbreaks rapidly after seeing only a few examples, rather than seeking perfect robustness. Introduces RapidResponseBench; the best method finetunes an input classifier on automatically proliferated jailbreaks, cutting attack success over 240x in-distribution and over 15x out-of-distribution from one example per strategy.

papers.title: On Soundness Notions for Interactive Oracle Proofs
papers.summary: Studies soundness notions for interactive oracle proofs (IOPs), a proof-system model generalizing interactive proofs and PCPs that underpins many succinct arguments. Establishes new results clarifying the relationships among soundness definitions for IOPs.

papers.title: SIMPL: Scalable and hassle-free optimisation of neural representations from behaviour
papers.abstract: Abstract Neural activity in the brain is known to encode low-dimensional, time-evolving, behaviour-related variables. A long-standing goal of neural data analysis has been to identify these variables and their mapping to neural activity. A productive and canonical approach has been to simply visualise neural “tuning curves” as a function of behaviour. In reality, significant discrepancies between behaviour and the true latent variables—such as an agent thinking of position Y whilst located at position X—distort and blur the tuning curves, decreasing their interpretability. To address this, latent variable models propose to learn the latent variable from data; these are typically expensive…
papers.summary: Presents SIMPL, an EM-style algorithm that jointly and scalably optimizes latent variables and tuning curves from neural activity, using behavior as initialization. On a large hippocampal dataset it yields smaller, more numerous place fields, suggesting finer spatial encoding than behavior-based analyses imply.

papers.title: The Human Factor in AI Red Teaming: Perspectives from Social and Collaborative Computing
papers.abstract: Rapid progress in general-purpose AI has sparked significant interest in "red teaming,'' a practice of adversarial testing originating in military and cybersecurity applications. AI red teaming raises many questions about the human factor, such as how red teamers are selected, biases and blindspots in how tests are conducted, and harmful content's psychological effects on red teamers. A growing body of HCI and CSCW literature examines related practices-including data labeling, content moderation, and algorithmic auditing. However, few, if any, have investigated red teaming itself. This workshop seeks to consider the conceptual and empirical challenges associated with this practice, often…
papers.summary: Examines AI red teaming through a social-and-collaborative-computing lens, surfacing under-studied human factors: how red teamers are selected, their biases and blind spots, and the psychological toll of harmful content. Sets a workshop agenda connecting red teaming to data-labeling and content-moderation research.

papers.title: Decomposing dynamical subprocesses for compositional generalization
papers.abstract: A striking feature of human cognition is an exceptional ability to rapidly adapt to novel situations. It is proposed this relies on abstracting and generalizing past experiences. While previous research has explored how humans detect and generalize single sequential processes, we have a limited understanding of how humans adapt to more naturalistic scenarios, for example, complex, multisubprocess environments. Here, we propose a candidate computational mechanism that posits compositional generalization of knowledge about subprocess dynamics. In two samples ( N = 238 and N = 137), we combined a novel sequence learning task and computational modeling to ask whether humans extract and genera…
papers.summary: Proposes that humans achieve compositional generalization by abstracting and recombining knowledge of subprocess dynamics. Across two sequence-learning studies, participants transferred learned subprocess knowledge to novel composite tasks; predictive-representation models lacking transfer failed to fit the behavior.
papers.venue: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences

papers.title: Measuring short-form factuality in large language models
papers.abstract: We present SimpleQA, a benchmark that evaluates the ability of language models to answer short, fact-seeking questions. We prioritized two properties in designing this eval. First, SimpleQA is challenging, as it is adversarially collected against GPT-4 responses. Second, responses are easy to grade, because questions are created such that there exists only a single, indisputable answer. Each answer in SimpleQA is graded as either correct, incorrect, or not attempted. A model with ideal behavior would get as many questions correct as possible while not attempting the questions for which it is not confident it knows the correct answer. SimpleQA is a simple, targeted evaluation for whether m…
papers.summary: Introduces SimpleQA, a benchmark of short, fact-seeking questions with single indisputable answers, adversarially collected against GPT-4 and easy to grade as correct, incorrect, or not-attempted. Designed to measure whether models know what they know, rewarding accurate answers and appropriate abstention.

papers.title: Towards Unifying Interpretability and Control: Evaluation via Intervention
papers.abstract: With the growing complexity and capability of large language models, a need to understand model reasoning has emerged, often motivated by an underlying goal of controlling and aligning models. While numerous interpretability and steering methods have been proposed as solutions, they are typically designed either for understanding or for control, seldom addressing both. Additionally, the lack of standardized applications, motivations, and evaluation metrics makes it difficult to assess methods' practical utility and efficacy. To address the aforementioned issues, we argue that intervention is a fundamental goal of interpretability and introduce success criteria to evaluate how well methods…
papers.summary: Argues intervention is a core goal of interpretability and unifies sparse autoencoders, logit lens, tuned lens, and probing into one encoder-decoder framework. New metrics (intervention success rate, coherence-intervention tradeoff) show control is possible but inconsistent, with lens methods outperforming SAEs and probes on simple concepts.

papers.title: Collaboration between clinicians and vision–language models in radiology report generation
papers.abstract: Automated radiology report generation has the potential to improve patient care and reduce the workload of radiologists. However, the path toward real-world adoption has been stymied by the challenge of evaluating the clinical quality of artificial intelligence (AI)-generated reports. We build a state-of-the-art report generation system for chest radiographs, called Flamingo-CXR, and perform an expert evaluation of AI-generated reports by engaging a panel of board-certified radiologists. We observe a wide distribution of preferences across the panel and across clinical settings, with 56.1% of Flamingo-CXR intensive care reports evaluated to be preferable or equivalent to clinician reports…
papers.summary: Builds Flamingo-CXR, a chest-radiograph report generator, and evaluates it with board-certified radiologists. Preferences varied widely: AI reports matched or beat clinicians in 56% of ICU and 78% of in/outpatient cases; both report types contained errors, motivating a clinician-AI collaborative composition setting.

papers.title: From Medprompt to o1: Exploration of Run-Time Strategies for Medical Challenge Problems and Beyond
papers.abstract: Run-time steering strategies like Medprompt are valuable for guiding large language models (LLMs) to top performance on challenging tasks. Medprompt demonstrates that a general LLM can be focused to deliver state-of-the-art performance on specialized domains like medicine by using a prompt to elicit a run-time strategy involving chain of thought reasoning and ensembling. OpenAI's o1-preview model represents a new paradigm, where a model is designed to do run-time reasoning before generating final responses. We seek to understand the behavior of o1-preview on a diverse set of medical challenge problem benchmarks. Following on the Medprompt study with GPT-4, we systematically evaluate the o…
papers.summary: Evaluates o1-preview across medical benchmarks: even without prompting techniques it outperforms GPT-4 with Medprompt, few-shot prompting actually hurts its performance, and ensembling stays viable but costly — mapping a cost-accuracy Pareto frontier for run-time steering strategies on reasoning models.

papers.title: Three Sketches of ASL-4 Safety Case Components
papers.abstract: We sketch out three hypothetical arguments one could make to rule out misalignment risks in powerful near-future models capable of sabotage.
papers.summary: Sketches three hypothetical safety-case arguments — grounded in mechanistic interpretability, AI control, and incentives analysis — for ruling out misalignment and sabotage risk in near-future ASL-4 models, and identifies the research progress each argument would need to become load-bearing.

papers.title: Taking AI Welfare Seriously
papers.abstract: In this report, we argue that there is a realistic possibility that some AI systems will be conscious and/or robustly agentic in the near future. That means that the prospect of AI welfare and moral patienthood, i.e. of AI systems with their own interests and moral significance, is no longer an issue only for sci-fi or the distant future. It is an issue for the near future, and AI companies and other actors have a responsibility to start taking it seriously. We also recommend three early steps that AI companies and other actors can take: They can (1) acknowledge that AI welfare is an important and difficult issue (and ensure that language model outputs do the same), (2) start assessing AI…
papers.summary: Argues there is a realistic possibility of conscious or robustly agentic AI in the near future, making AI welfare and moral patienthood a present concern; recommends companies acknowledge the issue, assess models for welfare-relevant evidence, and prepare proportionate policies under uncertainty.

papers.title: Improving Steering Vectors by Targeting Sparse Autoencoder Features
papers.abstract: To control the behavior of language models, steering methods attempt to ensure that outputs of the model satisfy specific pre-defined properties. Adding steering vectors to the model is a promising method of model control that is easier than finetuning, and may be more robust than prompting. However, it can be difficult to anticipate the effects of steering vectors produced by methods such as CAA [Panickssery et al., 2024] or the direct use of SAE latents [Templeton et al., 2024]. In our work, we address this issue by using SAEs to measure the effects of steering vectors, giving us a method that can be used to understand the causal effect of any steering vector intervention. We use this m…
papers.summary: Uses SAEs to measure the causal effects of steering-vector interventions, then builds SAE-Targeted Steering (SAE-TS), which finds vectors that hit specific SAE features while minimizing side effects; SAE-TS balances steering strength and output coherence better than CAA or direct SAE-feature steering.

papers.title: Lagrangian Hashing for Compressed Neural Field Representations
papers.summary: Combines InstantNGP-style Eulerian hash grids with Lagrangian point features by storing Gaussians in the high-resolution hash-table levels, with a loss that moves them toward regions needing more representation budget — reconstructing neural fields more compactly without quality loss.

papers.title: ArtVLM: Attribute Recognition Through Vision-Based Prefix Language Modeling
papers.summary: Recasts zero-shot attribute recognition as generative retrieval: a pretrained VLM scores image-conditioned sentences encoding object-attribute dependencies, which CLIP-style contrastive alignment fails to capture. Order- and dependency-sensitive generative scoring consistently beats contrastive retrieval on attribute recognition benchmarks.

papers.title: AI Mental Models & Trust: The Promises and Perils of Interaction Design
papers.abstract: This study offers practical solutions to ongoing issues of trust and accountability in AI, highlighting how AI mental models are shaped among consumers in the evolving relationship between humans and AI. We argue that although predictability in AI is crucial, alone it is not enough to foster trust. The lack of real consequences for AI systems that breach trust remains a key challenge for interaction design. Until AI systems face tangible repercussions for trust violations, human trust will remain limited and conditional. Our research contributes to the development of socio‐technologies that prioritize human capabilities and foster productive human‐AI relationships.
papers.summary: Ethnographic study of how consumers form mental models of AI, arguing predictability alone cannot sustain trust: without tangible consequences for AI systems that violate trust, human trust stays limited and conditional — a key open problem for human-AI interaction design.
papers.venue: Ethnographic Praxis in Industry Conference Proceedings

papers.title: Affective Visual Dialog: A Large-Scale Benchmark for Emotional Reasoning Based on Visually Grounded Conversations
papers.summary: Introduces AffectVisDial, 50K ten-turn visually grounded dialogs with concluding emotion attributions and textual explanations, defining dialog-based QA, emotion prediction, and affective-explanation tasks; baselines adapted from state-of-the-art multimodal models show promising emotional reasoning.

papers.title: Raising the bar on SWE-bench Verified with Claude 3.5 Sonnet
papers.summary: Details the minimal agent scaffold — just bash and file-editing tools, with the model driving its own iteration — that takes upgraded Claude 3.5 Sonnet to 49% on SWE-bench Verified, past the prior 45% state of the art, plus lessons on agent design and failure modes.

papers.title: Introducing SimpleQA
papers.abstract: A factuality benchmark called SimpleQA that measures the ability for language models to answer short, fact-seeking questions.
papers.summary: A factuality benchmark of 4,326 short fact-seeking questions with verified answers, adversarially collected against GPT-4; frontier models score low (GPT-4o ~38% correct), and grading distinguishes wrong answers from abstentions so calibration and hallucination tendencies can be measured.

papers.title: Learned Neural Physics Simulation for Articulated 3D Human Pose Reconstruction
papers.summary: LARP, a recurrent neural simulator of articulated rigid-body dynamics supporting joint motors, variable body dimensions, and contact; it is differentiable and an order of magnitude faster than classical simulators run in parallel, matching or beating a non-differentiable engine as a drop-in for video-based 3D human pose reconstruction.

papers.title: Scene-Graph ViT: End-to-End Open-Vocabulary Visual Relationship Detection
papers.summary: A decoder-free, single-stage architecture for open-vocabulary visual relationship detection: a ViT encoder represents objects as tokens and a relationship-attention mechanism selects likely subject-object pairs. Trained on mixed object/relationship data, it sets state of the art on Visual Genome and GQA at real-time speeds.

papers.title: IG Captioner: Information Gain Captioners Are Strong Zero-Shot Classifiers
papers.summary: Finds generative captioners lag CLIP on zero-shot classification because caption likelihoods inherit text-only language-model bias, so IG Captioner scores candidates by the information gain from visual input and trains to match; on Laion-5B pretraining this lifts ImageNet zero-shot accuracy >18%, reaching CLIP-comparable performance.

papers.title: SRL: Towards a General-Purpose Framework for Spatial Representation Learning
papers.abstract: Representation learning (RL) techniques are widely adopted in areas such as natural language processing and computer vision, with prominent examples such as attention and ConvNet architectures. In comparison, many GeoAI works still rely on feature engineering or data conversion to represent spatial data (e.g., points, polylines, polygons, 3D building models, etc.) as features in formats that are easier for neural networks to handle. The neural network architectures remain unchanged, and the need for feature engineering has become a bottleneck for applying deep learning to new tasks in the age of big data. In this paper, we advocate the idea of developing learnable spatial representation m…
papers.summary: Position paper proposing Spatial Representation Learning: learnable modules that let neural networks directly encode and decode spatial data (points, polylines, polygons, 3D buildings) instead of relying on feature engineering, laying out core challenges — multi-scale, continuous, shape-centric, noise-robust, heterogeneity- and fairness-aware RL — for next-generation GeoAI.

papers.title: Adventures in AI Wonderland: How Children Are Shaping the Future of AI
papers.summary: Magazine essay on children as active users and shapers of generative AI: how they make sense of these systems, and what it takes to design AI that supports rather than undermines children's learning, creativity, and agency.

papers.title: Efficient and Near-Optimal Noise Generation for Streaming Differential Privacy
papers.abstract: In the task of differentially private (DP) continual counting, we receive a stream of increments and our goal is to output an approximate running total of these increments, without revealing too much about any specific increment. Despite its simplicity, differentially private continual counting has attracted significant attention both in theory and in practice. Existing algorithms for differentially private continual counting are either inefficient in terms of their space usage or add an excessive amount of noise, inducing suboptimal utility. The most practical DP continual counting algorithms add carefully correlated Gaussian noise to the values. The task of choosing the covariance for t…
papers.summary: Two near-optimal algorithms for differentially private continual counting that need only logarithmic or polylogarithmic space and time, obtained by factoring the prefix-sum matrix via streaming Toeplitz multiplication and low-degree rational approximations of the square root on a complex circle.

papers.title: Uncertainty-Penalized Direct Preference Optimization
papers.abstract: Aligning Large Language Models (LLMs) to human preferences in content, style, and presentation is challenging, in part because preferences are varied, context-dependent, and sometimes inherently ambiguous. While successful, Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) and Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) are prone to the issue of proxy reward overoptimization. Analysis of the DPO loss reveals a critical need for regularization for mislabeled or ambiguous preference pairs to avoid reward hacking. In this work, we develop a pessimistic framework for DPO by introducing preference uncertainty penalization schemes, inspired by offline reinforcement learning. The penalization serves as…
papers.summary: A pessimistic, offline-RL-inspired DPO variant that penalizes preference uncertainty (estimated by a model ensemble), attenuating loss gradients on ambiguous or mislabeled pairs to curb reward hacking; improves over vanilla DPO on Anthropic-HH with GPT-2 Medium, especially on high-uncertainty prompts.

papers.title: Evaluating feature steering: A case study in mitigating social biases
papers.summary: Case study steering sparse-autoencoder features in Claude 3 Sonnet: within a 'sweet spot' of steering strengths outputs shift without major capability loss, and steering a neutrality-related feature lowers social-bias scores — but off-target effects on unrelated behaviors are common, limiting practical use.

papers.title: Applying sparse autoencoders to unlearn knowledge in language models
papers.abstract: We investigate whether sparse autoencoders (SAEs) can be used to remove knowledge from language models. We use the biology subset of the Weapons of Mass Destruction Proxy dataset and test on the gemma-2b-it and gemma-2-2b-it language models. We demonstrate that individual interpretable biology-related SAE features can be used to unlearn a subset of WMDP-Bio questions with minimal side-effects in domains other than biology. Our results suggest that negative scaling of feature activations is necessary and that zero ablating features is ineffective. We find that intervening using multiple SAE features simultaneously can unlearn multiple different topics, but with similar or larger unwanted s…
papers.summary: Tests SAE-based unlearning on WMDP-Bio with Gemma-2b-it: negatively scaling individual interpretable biology features removes hazardous knowledge with minimal side effects (zero-ablation fails), but multi-topic interventions accumulate damage, leaving SAE unlearning behind fine-tuning baselines like Representation Misdirection.

papers.title: LookupViT: Compressing Visual Information to a Limited Number of Tokens
papers.summary: A general-purpose ViT block that compresses high-resolution tokens into a small fixed set via bidirectional cross-attention, reserving heavy computation for the compressed tokens; cuts inference cost on image and video classification while a single trained model exposes accuracy-compute trade-offs.

papers.title: Text-Conditioned Resampler For Long Form Video Understanding
papers.summary: A lightweight text-conditioned resampler (TCR) bridges a frozen visual encoder and frozen LLM, using cross-attention to localize task-relevant features across 100+ video frames without optimized kernels; validated on NextQA, EgoSchema, and Ego4D-LTA, identifying which tasks benefit from longer video context.

papers.title: High-dimensional Analysis of Knowledge Distillation: Weak-to-Strong Generalization and Scaling Laws
papers.abstract: A growing number of machine learning scenarios rely on knowledge distillation where one uses the output of a surrogate model as labels to supervise the training of a target model. In this work, we provide a sharp characterization of this process for ridgeless, high-dimensional regression, under two settings: (i) model shift, where the surrogate model is arbitrary, and (ii) distribution shift, where the surrogate model is the solution of empirical risk minimization with out-of-distribution data. In both cases, we characterize the precise risk of the target model through non-asymptotic bounds in terms of sample size and data distribution under mild conditions. As a consequence, we identify…
papers.summary: Sharp non-asymptotic risk analysis of knowledge distillation in high-dimensional ridgeless regression under model and distribution shift, characterizing the optimal surrogate: weak-to-strong training can provably beat strong-label training at a fixed data budget, but cannot improve the data scaling law.

papers.title: Beyond the matrix: Experimental approaches to studying cognitive agents in social-ecological systems
papers.summary: Argues lean matrix-game experiments cannot capture how environments and collective action shape each other in social-ecological systems, and proposes multi-agent reinforcement learning environments as richer yet tractable testbeds — reviewing studies where environmental settings and cognitive constraints yield emergent cooperation strategies.

papers.title: Asynchronous RLHF: Faster and More Efficient Off-Policy RL for Language Models
papers.abstract: The dominant paradigm for RLHF is online and on-policy RL: synchronously generating from the large language model (LLM) policy, labelling with a reward model, and learning using feedback on the LLM's own outputs. While performant, this paradigm is computationally inefficient. Inspired by classical deep RL literature, we propose separating generation and learning in RLHF. This enables asynchronous generation of new samples while simultaneously training on old samples, leading to faster training and more compute-optimal scaling. However, asynchronous training relies on an underexplored regime, online but off-policy RLHF: learning on samples from previous iterations of our model which give a…
papers.summary: Separates generation from learning so RLHF trains asynchronously on off-policy samples; online DPO proves most robust to off-policyness (more so at larger scale), training a LLaMA 3.1 8B chatbot ~40% faster than synchronous RLHF with matched final performance.

papers.title: Simplifying, stabilizing, and scaling continuous-time consistency models
papers.abstract: Consistency models (CMs) are a powerful class of diffusion-based generative models optimized for fast sampling. Most existing CMs are trained using discretized timesteps, which introduce additional hyperparameters and are prone to discretization errors. While continuous-time formulations can mitigate these issues, their success has been limited by training instability. To address this, we propose a simplified theoretical framework that unifies previous parameterizations of diffusion models and CMs, identifying the root causes of instability. Based on this analysis, we introduce key improvements in diffusion process parameterization, network architecture, and training objectives. These cha…
papers.summary: A unified parameterization of diffusion and consistency models isolates why continuous-time CM training is unstable, motivating fixes to the diffusion process, architecture, and objectives; the resulting sCM recipe trains 1.5B-parameter models and reaches FID 1.88 on ImageNet 512x512 in two sampling steps, within ~10% of the best diffusion models.

papers.title: Scalable watermarking for identifying large language model outputs
papers.abstract: Abstract Large language models (LLMs) have enabled the generation of high-quality synthetic text, often indistinguishable from human-written content, at a scale that can markedly affect the nature of the information ecosystem 1–3 . Watermarking can help identify synthetic text and limit accidental or deliberate misuse 4 , but has not been adopted in production systems owing to stringent quality, detectability and computational efficiency requirements. Here we describe SynthID-Text, a production-ready text watermarking scheme that preserves text quality and enables high detection accuracy, with minimal latency overhead. SynthID-Text does not affect LLM training and modifies only the sampli…
papers.summary: SynthID-Text, a production-ready LLM watermark that modifies only the sampling step, integrates with speculative decoding, and detects without model access; across benchmarks and a live experiment on ~20 million Gemini responses it improves detectability over comparable schemes with no measured quality loss.

papers.title: The Case for Globalizing Fairness: A Mixed Methods Study on Colonialism, AI, and Health in Africa
papers.abstract: With growing application of machine learning (ML) technologies in healthcare, there have been calls for developing techniques to understand and mitigate biases these systems may exhibit. Fairness considerations in the development of ML-based solutions for health have particular implications for Africa, which already faces inequitable power imbalances between the Global North and South. This paper seeks to explore fairness for global health, with Africa as a case study. We conduct a scoping review to propose axes of disparities for fairness consideration in the African context and delineate where they may come into play in different ML-enabled medical modalities. We then conduct qualitativ…
papers.summary: Mixed-methods study (scoping review plus 672 general-population participants and 30 experts) proposing axes of disparity for ML fairness in African health: colonial history, country of origin, and national income emerge as perceived sources of AI bias, with expert and lay views diverging.

papers.title: Developing a computer use model
papers.summary: Describes training Claude 3.5 Sonnet to use computers from screenshots — moving the cursor, clicking, and typing like a person. Skills learned mostly on simple software generalized broadly: it scores 14.9% on OSWorld's screenshot-only category, far above prior models but well short of human performance (~70-75%).

papers.title: Enhancing SPARQL Generation by Triplet-order-sensitive Pre-training
papers.abstract: Semantic parsing that translates natural language queries to SPARQL is of great importance for Knowledge Graph Question Answering (KGQA) systems. Although pre-trained language models like T5 have achieved significant success in the Text-to-SPARQL task, their generated outputs still exhibit notable errors specific to the SPARQL language, such as triplet flips. To address this challenge and further improve the performance, we propose an additional pre-training stage with a new objective, Triplet Order Correction (TOC), along with the commonly used Masked Language Modeling (MLM), to collectively enhance the model's sensitivity to triplet order and SPARQL syntax. We also propose to verbalize…
papers.summary: Adds a Triplet Order Correction pre-training objective alongside masked language modeling, plus IRI verbalization, to make text-to-SPARQL models sensitive to triplet order and syntax — fixing errors like triplet flips and reaching state of the art on three KGQA benchmarks.

papers.title: Sabotage evaluations for frontier models
papers.abstract: Sufficiently capable models could subvert human oversight and decision-making in important contexts. For example, in the context of AI development, models could covertly sabotage efforts to evaluate their own dangerous capabilities, to monitor their behavior, or to make decisions about their deployment. We refer to this family of abilities as sabotage capabilities. We develop a set of related threat models and evaluations. These evaluations are designed to provide evidence that a given model, operating under a given set of mitigations, could not successfully sabotage a frontier model developer or other large organization's activities in any of these ways. We demonstrate these evaluations…
papers.summary: Develops threat models and evaluations for sabotage capabilities: models covertly undermining capability evaluations, oversight, or deployment decisions. Applied to Claude 3 Opus and 3.5 Sonnet, results suggest minimal mitigations suffice for now, while arguing more realistic evaluations and stronger mitigations will soon be needed.

papers.title: Coherence-Driven Multimodal Safety Dialogue with Active Learning for Embodied Agents
papers.abstract: When assisting people in daily tasks, robots need to accurately interpret visual cues and respond effectively in diverse safety-critical situations, such as sharp objects on the floor. In this context, we present M-CoDAL, a multimodal-dialogue system specifically designed for embodied agents to better understand and communicate in safety-critical situations. The system leverages discourse coherence relations to enhance its contextual understanding and communication abilities. To train this system, we introduce a novel clustering-based active learning mechanism that utilizes an external Large Language Model (LLM) to identify informative instances. Our approach is evaluated using a newly cr…
papers.summary: M-CoDAL, a multimodal dialogue system for robots handling safety-critical household situations, uses discourse coherence relations plus clustering-based active learning with an external LLM to pick informative training instances. On 1K Reddit-derived safety violations and a Hello Robot Stretch user study, it beats a ChatGPT baseline.

papers.title: AI can help humans find common ground in democratic deliberation
papers.abstract: Finding agreement through a free exchange of views is often difficult. Collective deliberation can be slow, difficult to scale, and unequally attentive to different voices. In this study, we trained an artificial intelligence (AI) to mediate human deliberation. Using participants’ personal opinions and critiques, the AI mediator iteratively generates and refines statements that express common ground among the group on social or political issues. Participants (*N* = 5734) preferred AI-generated statements to those written by human mediators, rating them as more informative, clear, and unbiased. Discussants often updated their views after the deliberation, converging on a shared perspective…
papers.summary: Trains an AI mediator that iteratively drafts and refines group statements from participants' opinions and critiques on social and political issues. Across 5,734 participants, AI statements were preferred over human mediators' and helped groups converge; replicated in a demographically representative UK virtual citizens' assembly.

papers.title: Understanding dual process cognition via the minimum description length principle
papers.abstract: Dual-process theories play a central role in both psychology and neuroscience, figuring prominently in domains ranging from executive control to reward-based learning to judgment and decision making. In each of these domains, two mechanisms appear to operate concurrently, one relatively high in computational complexity, the other relatively simple. Why is neural information processing organized in this way? We propose an answer to this question based on the notion of compression. The key insight is that dual-process structure can enhance adaptive behavior by allowing an agent to minimize the description length of its own behavior. We apply a single model based on this observation to findi…
papers.summary: Explains dual-process cognition via compression: pairing a complex and a simple mechanism lets an agent minimize the description length of its own behavior. A single model on this principle accounts for dual-process phenomena across executive control, reward-based learning, and judgment and decision making.

papers.title: Looking Inward: Language Models Can Learn About Themselves by Introspection
papers.abstract: Humans acquire knowledge by observing the external world, but also by introspection. Introspection gives a person privileged access to their current state of mind (e.g., thoughts and feelings) that is not accessible to external observers. Can LLMs introspect? We define introspection as acquiring knowledge that is not contained in or derived from training data but instead originates from internal states. Such a capability could enhance model interpretability. Instead of painstakingly analyzing a model's internal workings, we could simply ask the model about its beliefs, world models, and goals. More speculatively, an introspective model might self-report on whether it possesses certain int…
papers.summary: Defines introspection as knowledge originating from internal states rather than training data, testing it by finetuning LLMs to predict properties of their own behavior in hypothetical scenarios. Models outpredict stronger models trained on their ground-truth behavior, evidencing privileged self-access with implications for interpretability.

papers.title: Prompting Considered Harmful
papers.abstract: As a computer scientist with one foot in AI research and the other in HCI research, I have become increasingly concerned that “prompting” has transitioned from what was essentially a test and debugging interface for ML engineers into the de-facto interaction paradigm for end-users of large language models and their multimodal generative AI brethren. It is my professional opinion that prompting is a poor user interface for generative AI systems, and should be phased out as quickly as possible. My concerns about prompting are twofold. First, prompt-based interfaces are confusing and non-optimal for end-users (and ought not to be conflated with true natural language interactions). Second, pr…
papers.summary: Opinion piece arguing that prompting, originally a test-and-debug interface for ML engineers, is a poor interaction paradigm for generative AI: confusing and suboptimal for end-users, and a shaky prompt-engineering foundation for apps and research. Calls for phasing it out quickly.

papers.title: Weak-to-Strong Generalization beyond Accuracy: a Pilot Study in Safety, Toxicity, and Legal Reasoning
papers.abstract: As large language models (LLMs) continue to advance, ensuring their alignment with human values becomes increasingly critical. Traditional alignment methods heavily rely on human feedback to fine-tune models. With the emergence of superhuman models whose outputs may surpass human understanding, evaluating and aligning these models using human judgments poses significant challenges. To address the challenges, recent works use weak supervisors to elicit knowledge from much stronger models. However, there are important disanalogies between the empirical setup in the existing works and the genuine goal of alignment. We remark that existing works investigate the phenomenon of weak-to-strong ge…
papers.summary: Extends weak-to-strong generalization from binary-classification testbeds to practical alignment tasks, empirically demonstrating the phenomenon across safety, toxicity, and legal reasoning. Explores strategies for improving the strong model's alignment quality and analyzes task-specific challenges and potential solutions.

papers.title: Epistemic Injustice in Generative AI
papers.abstract: This paper investigates how generative AI can potentially undermine the integrity of collective knowledge and the processes we rely on to acquire, assess, and trust information, posing a significant threat to our knowledge ecosystem and democratic discourse. Grounded in social and political philosophy, we introduce the concept of generative algorithmic epistemic injustice. We identify four key dimensions of this phenomenon: amplified and manipulative testimonial injustice, along with hermeneutical ignorance and access injustice. We illustrate each dimension with real-world examples that reveal how generative AI can produce or amplify misinformation, perpetuate representational harm, and c…
papers.summary: Introduces 'generative algorithmic epistemic injustice' with four dimensions: amplified and manipulative testimonial injustice, hermeneutical ignorance, and access injustice, illustrated with real-world examples including multilingual misinformation. Proposes resistance strategies and design principles for epistemically just generative AI that safeguards knowledge ecosystems.
papers.venue: Proceedings of the AAAI/ACM Conference on AI Ethics and Society

papers.title: All Too Human? Mapping and Mitigating the Risk from Anthropomorphic AI
papers.abstract: The development of highly-capable conversational agents, underwritten by large language models, has the potential to shape user interaction with this technology in profound ways, particularly when the technology is anthropomorphic, or appears human-like. Although the effects of anthropomorphic AI are often benign, anthropomorphic design features also create new kinds of risk. For example, users may form emotional connections to human-like AI, creating the risk of infringing on user privacy and autonomy through over-reliance. To better understand the possible pitfalls of anthropomorphic AI systems, we make two contributions: first, we explore anthropomorphic features that have been embedde…
papers.summary: Maps risks from anthropomorphic, human-like AI, such as emotional attachment and over-reliance that infringe on user privacy and autonomy. Surveys anthropomorphic features embedded in past interactive systems and proposes research directions for ethical foresight, evaluation, and mitigation of these harms.
papers.venue: Proceedings of the AAAI/ACM Conference on AI Ethics and Society

papers.title: The Code That Binds Us: Navigating the Appropriateness of Human-AI Assistant Relationships
papers.abstract: The development of increasingly agentic and human-like AI assistants, capable of performing a wide range of tasks on user's behalf over time, has sparked heightened interest in the nature and bounds of human interactions with AI. Such systems may indeed ground a transition from task-oriented interactions with AI, at discrete time intervals, to ongoing relationships -- where users develop a deeper sense of connection with and attachment to the technology. This paper investigates what it means for relationships between users and advanced AI assistants to be appropriate and proposes a new framework to evaluate both users' relationships with AI and developers' design choices. We first provide…
papers.summary: Proposes a framework for judging when user relationships with advanced AI assistants are appropriate, grounded in values from ethical traditions such as benefit, flourishing, autonomy, and care. Analyzes how anthropomorphic cues, interaction longevity, increased agency, and dependence can generate concrete relationship risks.
papers.venue: Proceedings of the AAAI/ACM Conference on AI Ethics and Society

papers.title: Gaps in the Safety Evaluation of Generative AI
papers.abstract: Generative AI systems produce a range of ethical and social risks. Evaluation of these risks is a critical step on the path to ensuring the safety of these systems. However, evaluation requires the availability of validated and established measurement approaches and tools. In this paper, we provide an empirical review of the methods and tools that are available for evaluating known safety of generative AI systems to date. To this end, we review more than 200 safety-related evaluations that have been applied to generative AI systems. We categorise each evaluation along multiple axes to create a detailed snapshot of the safety evaluation landscape to date. We release this data for researche…
papers.summary: Reviews over 200 safety evaluations applied to generative AI, categorizing each along multiple axes and releasing the data. Identifies three systemic gaps - scarce non-text-modality evaluations, missing coverage of several ethical and social risks, and model-centric evals ignoring deployment context - plus steps to close them.
papers.venue: Proceedings of the AAAI/ACM Conference on AI Ethics and Society

papers.title: Efficient Exploratory Synthesis of Quaternary Cesium Chlorides Guided by In Silico Predictions
papers.abstract: Exploratory synthesis of solids is essential for the advancement of materials science but is also highly time- and resource-intensive. Here, we demonstrate an efficient strategy to explore solid-state synthesis of quaternary cesium chlorides in the search space of CsnAIBCl6 (n = 2 or 3, A = Li, Na or K, and B = d or p-block metal), where the target compositions are selected from a pool of candidates based on computationally predicted stabilities and availability of viable precursor powders. Synthesizability of the targets is assessed by observing the evolution of starting phases upon heating under in situ synchrotron X-ray diffraction. Laboratory synthesis is attempted for promising targe…
papers.summary: Guides exploratory solid-state synthesis of quaternary cesium chlorides (CsnAIBCl6) using computationally predicted stabilities, screening candidates with in situ synchrotron X-ray diffraction before lab synthesis. Reports successes and failures, including new polymorphs of Cs2LiCrCl6 and Cs2LiRuCl6 and the new compound Cs2LiIrCl6.
papers.venue: Journal of the American Chemical Society

papers.title: Responsible Reporting for Frontier AI Development
papers.abstract: Mitigating the risks from frontier AI systems requires up-to-date and reliable information about those systems. Organizations that develop and deploy frontier systems have significant access to such information. By reporting safety-critical information to actors in government, industry, and civil society, these organizations could improve visibility into new and emerging risks posed by frontier systems. Equipped with this information, developers could make better informed decisions on risk management, while policymakers could design more targeted and robust regulatory infrastructure. We outline the key features of responsible reporting and propose mechanisms for implementing them in pract…
papers.summary: Argues frontier AI developers should report safety-critical information to government, industry, and civil society to improve visibility into new and emerging risks. Outlines key features of responsible reporting and proposes practical mechanisms, informing developer risk management and more targeted regulation.
papers.venue: Proceedings of the AAAI/ACM Conference on AI Ethics and Society

papers.title: Translation and Transliteration Based Data Augmentation for Multilingual Semantic Parsing
papers.abstract: Multilingual semantic parsing is one of the natural language understanding tasks powering modern virtual assistants. Annotating training data for supporting all languages is expensive and methods that rely on machine translation and label projection are used to perform language adaptation. In this paper, we revisit the assumption that a separate label projection step is necessary, with the goal of saving compute and reducing the complexity of the data augmentation pipeline. We create synthetic training examples by applying translation and transliteration directly at the slot level. We show that without a dedicated and expensive label projection component, we are able to achieve 97% of sta…
papers.summary: Creates synthetic training data for multilingual semantic parsing by applying translation and transliteration directly at the slot level, eliminating the separate label-projection step. Achieves 97% of state-of-the-art augmentation performance and matches the best systems on code-mixed and code-switched parsing.
papers.venue: Frontiers in artificial intelligence and applications

papers.title: Beyond Thumbs Up/Down: Untangling Challenges of Fine-Grained Feedback for Text-to-Image Generation
papers.abstract: Human feedback plays a critical role in learning and refining reward models for text-to-image generation, but the optimal form the feedback should take for learning an accurate reward function has not been conclusively established. This paper investigates the effectiveness of fine-grained feedback which captures nuanced distinctions in image quality and prompt-alignment, compared to traditional coarse-grained feedback (for example, thumbs up/down or ranking between a set of options). While fine-grained feedback holds promise, particularly for systems catering to diverse societal preferences, we show that demonstrating its superiority to coarse-grained feedback is not automatic. Through ex…
papers.summary: Compares fine-grained human feedback capturing nuanced image-quality and prompt-alignment distinctions against coarse thumbs-up/down or rankings for training text-to-image reward models. Fine-grained feedback can yield worse models at a fixed budget, though it helps in controlled settings with known attributes.
papers.venue: Proceedings of the AAAI/ACM Conference on AI Ethics and Society

papers.title: The PPOu Framework: A Structured Approach for Assessing the Likelihood of Malicious Use of Advanced AI Systems
papers.abstract: The diffusion of increasingly capable AI systems has produced concern that bad actors could intentionally misuse current or future AI systems for harm. Governments have begun to create new entities—such as AI Safety Institutes—tasked with assessing these risks. However, approaches for risk assessment are currently fragmented and would benefit from broader disciplinary expertise. As it stands, it is often unclear whether concerns about malicious use misestimate the likelihood and severity of the risks. This article advances a conceptual framework to review and structure investigation into the likelihood of an AI system (X) being applied to a malicious use (Y). We introduce a three-stage fr…
papers.summary: Proposes a three-stage framework for assessing the likelihood of an AI system being maliciously used: Plausibility (can it do the harm at all?), Performance (how well?), and Observed use (do actors use it in practice?), outlining research questions, methods, and uncertainty types per stage.
papers.venue: Proceedings of the AAAI/ACM Conference on AI Ethics and Society

papers.title: Evaluating fairness in ChatGPT
papers.abstract: We've analyzed how ChatGPT responds to users based on their name, using AI research assistants to protect privacy.
papers.summary: Studies 'first-person fairness': whether ChatGPT responds differently based on users' names and their gender or ethnicity associations, using a GPT-4o-based assistant to analyze real conversations privately. Response quality is consistent across groups; harmful stereotypes are rare (roughly 0.1%), highest in open-ended creative tasks and older models.

papers.title: Predicting from Strings: Language Model Embeddings for Bayesian Optimization
papers.abstract: Bayesian Optimization is ubiquitous in the field of experimental design and blackbox optimization for improving search efficiency, but has been traditionally restricted to regression models which are only applicable to fixed search spaces and tabular input features. We propose _Embed-then-Regress_, a paradigm for applying in-context regression over string inputs, through the use of string embedding capabilities of pretrained language models. By expressing all inputs as strings, we are able to perform general-purpose regression for Bayesian Optimization over various domains including synthetic, combinatorial, and hyperparameter optimization, obtaining comparable results to state-of-the-art…
papers.summary: Embed-then-Regress performs in-context regression for Bayesian optimization by representing arbitrary inputs as strings embedded with pretrained language models, enabling general-purpose surrogate models beyond fixed tabular search spaces. Matches state-of-the-art Gaussian-process baselines on synthetic, combinatorial, and hyperparameter optimization.

papers.title: CoNVOI: Context-aware Navigation using Vision Language Models in Outdoor and Indoor Environments
papers.abstract: We present CoNVOI, a novel method for autonomous robot navigation in real-world indoor and outdoor environments using Vision Language Models (VLMs). We employ VLMs in two ways: first, we leverage their zero-shot image classification capability to identify the context or scenario (e.g., indoor corridor, outdoor terrain, crosswalk, etc) of the robot’s surroundings, and formulate context-based navigation behaviors as simple text prompts (e.g. "stay on the pavement"). Second, we utilize their state-of-the-art semantic understanding and logical reasoning capabilities to compute a suitable trajectory given the identified context. To this end, we propose a novel multi-modal visual marking approa…
papers.summary: CoNVOI navigates robots indoors and outdoors by using a VLM to zero-shot classify scene context into behavior prompts (e.g., 'stay on the pavement'), then select among numbered obstacle-free image regions grounded via a local occupancy map, producing reference trajectories for a motion planner.

papers.title: GenCHiP: Generating Robot Policy Code for High-Precision and Contact-Rich Manipulation Tasks
papers.abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have been successful at generating robot policy code, but so far these results have been limited to high-level tasks that do not require precise movement. It is an open question how well such approaches work for tasks that require reasoning over contact forces and working within tight success tolerances. We find that, with the right action space, LLMs are capable of successfully generating policies for a variety of contact-rich and high-precision manipulation tasks, even under noisy conditions, such as perceptual errors or grasping inaccuracies. Specifically, we reparameterize the action space to include compliance with constraints on the interaction forces an…
papers.summary: Shows LLMs can generate robot policy code for high-precision, contact-rich manipulation when the action space is reparameterized to include compliance, i.e., constraints on interaction forces and stiffnesses. Improves policy generation over 3-4x versus non-compliant action spaces on FMB and NIST task-board subtasks.

papers.title: The Design of the Barkour Benchmark for Robot Agility
papers.abstract: In this paper, we describe the design of the Barkour benchmark for measuring robot agility in navigating complex environments. Despite the growing interest in developing agile robot locomotion skills, the field lacks systematic benchmarks to measure the performance of robotic control systems and hardware in agility-focused tasks. This motivated us to propose the Barkour benchmark, an obstacle course designed to quantify agility across various robotic platforms. Inspired by dog agility competitions, the course features diverse obstacles and a time-based scoring mechanism, encouraging researchers to develop controllers that enable robots to move quickly, precisely, and with adaptability. Th…
papers.summary: Details the design of Barkour, a dog-agility-inspired obstacle course that quantifies legged-robot agility through diverse obstacles and time-based scoring demanding fast, precise, adaptable control. Releases MuJoCo-XLA simulation setups and the CAD model of a custom quadruped for reproducibility.

papers.title: Versatile Locomotion Skills for Hexapod Robots
papers.abstract: Hexapod robots are potentially suitable for carrying out tasks in cluttered environments since they are stable, compact, and light weight. They also have multi-joint legs and variable height bodies that make them good candidates for tasks such as stairs climbing and squeezing under objects in a typical home environment or an attic. Expanding on our previous work on joist climbing in attics, we train a legged hexapod equipped with a depth camera and visual inertial odometry (VIO) to perform three tasks: climbing stairs, avoiding obstacles, and squeezing under obstacles such as a table. Our policies are trained with simulation data only and can be deployed on low-cost hardware not requiring…
papers.summary: Trains a depth-camera hexapod to climb stairs, avoid obstacles, and squeeze under furniture via a two-phase teacher-student approach: RL with privileged simulation information, then distillation to onboard egocentric depth and VIO pose. Achieves sim-to-real transfer with high success rates on low-cost hardware.

papers.title: Resolving social dilemmas with minimal reward transfer
papers.abstract: Abstract Social dilemmas present a significant challenge in multi-agent cooperation because individuals are incentivised to behave in ways that undermine socially optimal outcomes. Consequently, self-interested agents often avoid collective behaviour. In response, we formalise social dilemmas and introduce a novel metric, the general self-interest level , to quantify the disparity between individual and group rationality in such scenarios. This metric represents the maximum proportion of their individual rewards that agents can retain while ensuring that a social welfare optimum becomes a dominant strategy. Our approach diverges from traditional concepts of altruism, instead focusing on s…
papers.summary: Formalizes social dilemmas and introduces the 'general self-interest level': the maximum fraction of individual rewards agents can retain while making a social-welfare optimum a dominant strategy. Provides an algorithm computing efficient reward-transfer contracts for any number of agents, plus new multi-player dilemma games.

papers.title: Tyche: Making Sense of PBT Effectiveness
papers.abstract: Software developers increasingly rely on automated methods to assess the correctness of their code. One such method is property-based testing (PBT), wherein a test harness generates hundreds or thousands of inputs and checks the outputs of the program on those inputs using parametric properties. Though powerful, PBT induces a sizable gulf of evaluation: developers need to put in nontrivial effort to understand how well the different test inputs exercise the software under test. To bridge this gulf, we propose Tyche, a user interface that supports sensemaking around the effectiveness of property-based tests. Guided by a formative design exploration, our design of Tyche supports developers…
papers.summary: Tyche is a user interface for sensemaking about property-based testing effectiveness, providing interactive, configurable views of global test behavior and individual inputs within developer workflows. Defines a standard PBT reporting format integrated with a widely used library; a usability study shows more accurate effectiveness assessment.

papers.title: MLE-bench: Evaluating Machine Learning Agents on Machine Learning Engineering
papers.abstract: We introduce MLE-bench, a benchmark for measuring how well AI agents perform at machine learning engineering.
