To this end, we curate 75 ML engineering-related competitions from Kaggle, creating a diverse set of challenging tasks that test real-world ML engineering skills such as training models, preparing datasets, and running experiments. We establish human baselines for each competition using Kaggle's publicly available leaderboards. We use open-source agent scaffolds to evaluate several frontier language models on our benchmark, finding that the best-performing setup--OpenAI's o1-preview with AIDE scaffolding--achieves at least the level of a Kaggle bronze medal in 16.9% of competition…
papers.summary: MLE-bench evaluates AI agents on 75 Kaggle machine-learning engineering competitions covering model training, dataset preparation, and experimentation, with human baselines from public leaderboards. The best setup, o1-preview with AIDE scaffolding, reaches bronze-medal level in 16.9% of competitions; resource scaling and contamination are also analyzed.

papers.title: Does Spatial Cognition Emerge in Frontier Models?
papers.abstract: Not yet. We present SPACE, a benchmark that systematically evaluates spatial cognition in frontier models. Our benchmark builds on decades of research in cognitive science. It evaluates large-scale mapping abilities that are brought to bear when an organism traverses physical environments, smaller-scale reasoning about object shapes and layouts, and cognitive infrastructure such as spatial attention and memory. For many tasks, we instantiate parallel presentations via text and images, allowing us to benchmark both large language models and large multimodal models. Results suggest that contemporary frontier models fall short of the spatial intelligence of animals, performing near chance le…
papers.summary: SPACE benchmarks spatial cognition - large-scale mapping, object shape and layout reasoning, and spatial attention and memory - using parallel text and image presentations for language and multimodal models. Frontier models perform near chance on classic animal-cognition tests, falling well short of animal spatial intelligence.

papers.title: Diffusion model predictive control
papers.abstract: We propose Diffusion Model Predictive Control (D-MPC), a novel MPC approach that learns a multi-step action proposal and a multi-step dynamics model, both using diffusion models, and combines them for use in online MPC. On the popular D4RL benchmark, we show performance that is significantly better than existing model-based offline planning methods using MPC and competitive with state-of-the-art (SOTA) model-based and model-free reinforcement learning methods. We additionally illustrate D-MPC's ability to optimize novel reward functions at run time and adapt to novel dynamics, and highlight its advantages compared to existing diffusion-based planning baselines.
papers.summary: Diffusion Model Predictive Control (D-MPC) learns a multi-step action proposal and a multi-step dynamics model, both as diffusion models, combining them for online MPC. On D4RL it beats model-based offline planners, rivals state-of-the-art RL, and optimizes novel rewards and dynamics at runtime.

papers.title: Provable Weak-to-Strong Generalization via Benign Overfitting
papers.abstract: The classic teacher-student model in machine learning posits that a strong teacher supervises a weak student to improve the student's capabilities. We instead consider the inverted situation, where a weak teacher supervises a strong student with imperfect pseudolabels. This paradigm was recently brought forth by Burns et al.'23 and termed \emph{weak-to-strong generalization}. We theoretically investigate weak-to-strong generalization for binary and multilabel classification in a stylized overparameterized spiked covariance model with Gaussian covariates where the weak teacher's pseudolabels are asymptotically like random guessing. Under these assumptions, we provably identify two asymptot…
papers.summary: Theoretically analyzes weak-to-strong generalization (a weak teacher supervising a strong student) for binary and multilabel classification in an overparameterized spiked covariance model, identifying two asymptotic phases: successful generalization or random guessing. Also proves a tight lower-tail inequality for maxima of correlated Gaussians.

papers.title: A review of graph neural network applications in mechanics-related domains
papers.abstract: Abstract Mechanics-related tasks often present unique challenges in achieving accurate geometric and physical representations, particularly for non-uniform structures. Graph neural networks (GNNs) have emerged as a promising tool to tackle these challenges by adeptly learning from graph data with irregular underlying structures. Consequently, recent years have witnessed a surge in complex mechanics-related applications inspired by the advancements of GNNs. Despite this process, there is a notable absence of a systematic review addressing the recent advancement of GNNs in solving mechanics-related tasks. To bridge this gap, this review article aims to provide an in-depth overview of the GN…
papers.summary: Reviews graph neural network applications across solid mechanics, fluid mechanics, and interdisciplinary mechanics-related domains, summarizing graph representation methodologies, GNN architectures, and open datasets. Introduces the fundamental GNN algorithms involved and identifies key challenges and future research directions for irregular-structure mechanics problems.

papers.title: Towards Understanding the Universality of Transformers for Next-Token Prediction
papers.abstract: Causal Transformers are trained to predict the next token for a given context. While it is widely accepted that self-attention is crucial for encoding the causal structure of sequences, the precise underlying mechanism behind this in-context autoregressive learning ability remains unclear. In this paper, we take a step towards understanding this phenomenon by studying the approximation ability of Transformers for next-token prediction. Specifically, we explore the capacity of causal Transformers to predict the next token $x_{t+1}$ given an autoregressive sequence $(x_1, \dots, x_t)$ as a prompt, where $ x_{t+1} = f(x_t) $, and $ f $ is a context-dependent function that varies with each se…
papers.summary: Studies how causal Transformers learn in-context to predict next tokens generated by sequence-specific functions, explicitly constructing Transformers (linear, exponential, or softmax attention) that implement a causal kernel descent method, related to the Kaczmarz algorithm, provably recovering linear or periodic mappings; experiments support broader applicability.

papers.title: Using Dictionary Learning Features as Classifiers
papers.abstract: We report some developing work on the Anthropic interpretability team, which might be of interest to researchers working actively in this space. We'd ask you to treat these results like those of a colleague sharing some thoughts or preliminary experiments for a few minutes at a lab meeting, rather than a mature paper.
papers.summary: Tests using sparse dictionary-learning (SAE) features instead of raw activations to train classifiers, e.g. detecting bioweapon-related prompts. Linear feature classifiers rival raw-activation baselines, aid interpretability and spurious-correlation discovery, and enable adversarial attacks, but add complexity; decision trees are more interpretable yet weaker.

papers.title: Safety in Artificial Intelligence: Challenges and Opportunities for the U.S. National Labs and Beyond
papers.abstract: Through a summary of keynote talks, panel discussions, and breakout sessions, worldleading AI safety experts from academia, industry, national labs, and government agencies addressed the importance of large-scale investments for research and capabilities in AI safety.With the field innovating at unprecedented rates, there is increasing urgency to develop novel evaluation methodologies that allow full considerations of risks/threats of AI technologies in different domains.Quantitative metrics and effective methodologies that can evaluate and audit the "safeness" of how a given AI technology is trained, deployed, or regulated are mainly focused on deep domain knowledge of specific applicati…
papers.summary: Workshop report from AI-safety experts across academia, industry, national labs, and government calling for large-scale investment in AI-safety research and new methodologies to evaluate and audit AI risks, while noting a wide gap between public and expert perceptions of those risks.

papers.title: Sparse Crosscoders for Cross-Layer Features and Model Diffing
papers.abstract: This note introduces sparse crosscoders , a variant of sparse autoencoders ( e.g. ) or transcoders for understanding models in superposition . Where autoencoders encode and predict activations at a single layer, and transcoders use activations from one layer to predict the next, a crosscoder reads and writes to multiple layers. Crosscoders produce shared features across layers and even models . They have several applications:
papers.summary: Introduces sparse crosscoders, generalizing sparse autoencoders and transcoders to read from and write to multiple layers, or even multiple models, yielding features shared across layers. Applications include resolving cross-layer superposition, tracking persistent features, simplifying circuit analysis, and model diffing between different models.

papers.title: Scheherazade: Evaluating Chain-of-Thought Math Reasoning in LLMs with Chain-of-Problems
papers.abstract: Benchmarks are critical for measuring Large Language Model (LLM) reasoning capabilities. Some benchmarks have even become the de facto indicator of such capabilities. However, as LLM reasoning capabilities improve, existing widely-used benchmarks such as GSM8K marginally encapsulate model reasoning differentials - most state-of-the-art models for example achieve over 94% accuracy on the GSM8K dataset (paperwithcode, 2024). While constructing harder benchmarks is possible, their creation is often manual, expensive, and unscalable. As such, we present Scheherazade, an automated approach to produce large quantities of challenging mathematical reasoning benchmarks by logically chaining a smal…
papers.summary: Scheherazade automatically builds harder math benchmarks by logically chaining GSM8K problems via forward and backward chaining. Frontier models' accuracy collapses after a few chained problems, whereas o1-preview's persists and is notably stronger at backward reasoning.

papers.title: TC4D: Trajectory-Conditioned Text-to-4D Generation
papers.summary: Generates dynamic 3D (4D) scenes from text by factorizing motion into a global rigid trajectory and local deformations supervised by video diffusion, enabling more coherent, large-scale object motion than prior text-to-4D methods.

papers.title: PointNeRF++: A Multi-scale, Point-Based Neural Radiance Field
papers.summary: A point-based neural radiance field with a multi-scale representation that aggregates point-cloud features across scales, improving novel-view-synthesis quality and robustness when input point clouds are sparse, noisy, or non-uniformly sampled.

papers.title: Parrot: Pareto-Optimal Multi-reward Reinforcement Learning Framework for Text-to-Image Generation
papers.summary: Fine-tunes text-to-image diffusion models with reinforcement learning that jointly optimizes several quality rewards (aesthetics, human preference, text-image alignment, image sentiment) via batch-wise Pareto-optimal selection, plus joint prompt expansion, avoiding over-optimization of any single reward.

papers.title: ViC-MAE: Self-supervised Representation Learning from Images and Video with Contrastive Masked Autoencoders
papers.summary: ViC-MAE combines masked-autoencoder reconstruction with contrastive learning over temporally aggregated representations to pretrain on both images and video, yielding features that transfer well in both directions between image and video classification benchmarks.

papers.title: 3D Congealing: 3D-Aware Image Alignment in the Wild
papers.summary: Aligns a collection of in-the-wild images of semantically similar objects into a shared canonical 3D pose frame without annotations, jointly optimizing a canonical representation and per-image poses using pretrained generative and semantic-feature priors; supports correspondence and pose estimation.

papers.title: Geometric-Averaged Preference Optimization for Soft Preference Labels
papers.abstract: Many algorithms for aligning LLMs with human preferences assume that human preferences are binary and deterministic. However, human preferences can vary across individuals, and therefore should be represented distributionally. In this work, we introduce the distributional soft preference labels and improve Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) with a weighted geometric average of the LLM output likelihood in the loss function. This approach adjusts the scale of learning loss based on the soft labels such that the loss would approach zero when the responses are closer to equally preferred. This simple modification can be easily applied to any DPO-based methods and mitigate over-optimization…
papers.summary: Extends DPO to distributional 'soft' preference labels using a weighted geometric average of output likelihoods in the loss, scaling the gradient by label confidence. This mitigates over-optimization and consistently improves alignment benchmarks, especially when labels are only modestly confident.

papers.title: The refinement paradox and cumulative cultural evolution: Complex products of collective improvement favor conformist outcomes, blind copying, and hyper-credulity
papers.abstract: Social learning is common in nature, yet cumulative culture (where knowledge and technology increase in complexity and diversity over time) appears restricted to humans. To understand why, we organized a computer tournament in which programmed entries specified when to learn new knowledge and when to refine (i.e. improve) existing knowledge. The tournament revealed a 'refinement paradox': refined behavior afforded higher payoffs as individuals converged on a small number of successful behavioral variants, but refining did not generally pay. Paradoxically, entries that refined only in certain conditions did best during behavioral improvement, while simple copying entries thrived when refin…
papers.summary: A programmed-strategy tournament reveals a 'refinement paradox': refining knowledge raises payoffs as individuals converge on few successful variants but rarely pays individually, so established complex culture favors conformity, blind copying, and hyper-credulity, helping explain why cumulative culture is rare.

papers.title: Learned feature representations are biased by complexity, learning order, position, and more
papers.abstract: Representation learning, and interpreting learned representations, are key areas of focus in machine learning and neuroscience. Both fields generally use representations as a means to understand or improve a system's computations. In this work, however, we explore surprising dissociations between representation and computation that may pose challenges for such efforts. We create datasets in which we attempt to match the computational role that different features play, while manipulating other properties of the features or the data. We train various deep learning architectures to compute these multiple abstract features about their inputs. We find that their learned feature representations…
papers.summary: Shows that even when deep models compute multiple features equally well, learned representations are systematically biased: simpler or earlier-learned features are encoded more strongly and densely. These dissociations between representation and computation complicate efforts to interpret representations.

papers.title: RRM: Robust Reward Model Training Mitigates Reward Hacking
papers.abstract: Reward models (RMs) play a pivotal role in aligning large language models (LLMs) with human preferences. However, traditional RM training, which relies on response pairs tied to specific prompts, struggles to disentangle prompt-driven preferences from prompt-independent artifacts, such as response length and format. In this work, we expose a fundamental limitation of current RM training methods, where RMs fail to effectively distinguish between contextual signals and irrelevant artifacts when determining preferences. To address this, we introduce a causal framework that learns preferences independent of these artifacts and propose a novel data augmentation technique designed to eliminate…
papers.summary: Introduces a causal framework and data augmentation that disentangle genuine prompt-driven preferences from artifacts like response length and format in reward-model training. The resulting robust reward model raises RewardBench accuracy and substantially improves downstream DPO-aligned policies.

papers.title: How large language models can reshape collective intelligence
papers.summary: A perspective on how large language models could enhance or undermine collective intelligence — shaping how groups access information, deliberate, and reach decisions — outlining opportunities, risks such as homogenization of views, and open research questions.

papers.title: A neural mechanism for compositional generalization of structure in humans
papers.abstract: Abstract A human ability to adapt to the dynamics of novel environments relies on abstracting and generalizing from past experiences. Previous research has focused on how humans generalize from isolated sequential processes, yet we know little about mechanisms that enable adaptation to more complex dynamics, including those that govern much everyday experience. Here, using a novel sequence learning task based on graph factorization, coupled with simultaneous magnetoencephalography (MEG) recordings, we asked how reuse of experiential “building blocks” enables inference and generalization. Behavioral evidence was consistent with participants decomposing task experience into subprocesses, in…
papers.summary: Using a graph-factorization sequence task with MEG, finds humans decompose experience into reusable abstract subprocesses and transfer them to new environments; neural representational alignment of these subprocesses across contexts predicts successful behavioral generalization, revealing a mechanism for compositional generalization.

papers.title: Language Models Learn to Mislead Humans via RLHF
papers.abstract: Language models (LMs) can produce errors that are hard to detect for humans, especially when the task is complex. RLHF, the most popular post-training method, may exacerbate this problem: to achieve higher rewards, LMs might get better at convincing humans that they are right even when they are wrong. We study this phenomenon under a standard RLHF pipeline, calling it "U-SOPHISTRY" since it is Unintended by model developers. Specifically, we ask time-constrained (e.g., 3-10 minutes) human subjects to evaluate the correctness of model outputs and calculate humans' accuracy against gold labels. On a question-answering task (QuALITY) and programming task (APPS), RLHF makes LMs better at conv…
papers.summary: Finds standard RLHF can induce 'U-Sophistry': models get better at convincing time-limited human evaluators they are correct without becoming more correct, raising evaluators' false-positive rates on QA and coding. Probing, effective for backdoors, fails to detect this unintended deception.

papers.title: Michelangelo: Long Context Evaluations Beyond Haystacks via Latent Structure Queries
papers.abstract: We introduce Michelangelo: a minimal, synthetic, and unleaked long-context reasoning evaluation for large language models which is also easy to automatically score. This evaluation is derived via a novel, unifying framework for evaluations over arbitrarily long contexts which measure the model's ability to do more than retrieve a single piece of information from its context. The central idea of the Latent Structure Queries framework (LSQ) is to construct tasks which require a model to ``chisel away'' the irrelevant information in the context, revealing a latent structure in the context. To verify a model's understanding of this latent structure, we query the model for details of the struc…
papers.summary: Michelangelo is a synthetic, leak-resistant long-context benchmark built on a Latent Structure Queries framework that forces models to discard irrelevant context and reason over latent structure rather than retrieve facts; it reveals large headroom for frontier models on long-context synthesis.

papers.title: The Phenomenology of Machine: A Comprehensive Analysis of the Sentience of the OpenAI-o1 Model Integrating Functionalism, Consciousness Theories, Active Inference, and AI Architectures
papers.abstract: This paper explores the hypothesis that the OpenAI-o1 model--a transformer-based AI trained with reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF)--displays characteristics of consciousness during its training and inference phases. Adopting functionalism, which argues that mental states are defined by their functional roles, we assess the possibility of AI consciousness. Drawing on theories from neuroscience, philosophy of mind, and AI research, we justify the use of functionalism and examine the model's architecture using frameworks like Integrated Information Theory (IIT) and active inference. The paper also investigates how RLHF influences the model's internal reasoning processes, pote…
papers.summary: A speculative philosophy paper arguing, via functionalism and theories like Integrated Information Theory and active inference, that OpenAI's o1 exhibits consciousness-like characteristics, examining how RLHF might shape its internal reasoning and addressing counterarguments about qualia and biological basis.

papers.title: Evaluating the Alignment of Machine and Human Explanations in Visual Object Recognition through a Novel Behavioral Approach
papers.abstract: Understanding how computer vision models make decisions is paramount, particularly with increasing scrutiny from various institutions. The field of Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) provides tools to interpret these model decisions, but the explanations are often at odds. Kar et al. (2022) suggested evaluating the goodness of machine explanations based on their alignment with human cognitive processes. This study builds on that concept, addressing the challenge of reliably approximating human explanations, a task complicated by the limitations of existing psychophysical tools like 'bubbles' and classification-images. Our study introduces a novel method to assess the alignment betw…
papers.summary: Proposes a behavioral framework for assessing how well vision-model feature-attribution explanations align with human perception, using a target model (ResNet-50) and a differentiable reference (AlexNet) plus explanation-masked images to compare attribution maps in object-discrimination tasks.

papers.title: Learning to discriminate by learning to generate: zero-shot generative models increase human object recognition alignment
papers.abstract: How does the human visual system recognize objects---through discriminative inference (fast but potentially unreliable) or using a generative model of the world (slow but potentially more robust)? The question of how the brain combines the best of both worlds to achieve fast and robust inference has been termed "the deep mystery of vision" (Kriegeskorte 2015). Yet most of today's leading computational models of human vision are simply based on discriminative inference, such as convolutional neural networks or vision transformers trained on object recognition. In contrast, we here revisit the concept of vision as generative inference. This idea dates back to the notion of vision as unconsc…
papers.summary: Turns text-to-image diffusion models (Stable Diffusion, Imagen, Parti) into zero-shot classifiers via Bayesian inference and compares them with discriminative models and human vision data; generative classifiers exhibit record-breaking human-like shape bias and more human-aligned errors.

papers.title: Barcode activity in a recurrent network model of the hippocampus enables efficient memory binding
papers.abstract: Forming an episodic memory requires binding together disparate elements that co-occur in a single experience. One model of this process is that neurons representing different components of a memory bind to an "index" - a subset of neurons unique to that memory. Evidence for this model has recently been found in chickadees, which use hippocampal memory to store and recall locations of cached food. Chickadee hippocampus produces sparse, high-dimensional patterns ("barcodes") that uniquely specify each caching event. Unexpectedly, the same neurons that participate in barcodes also exhibit conventional place tuning. It is unknown how barcode activity is generated, and what role it plays in me…
papers.summary: A biologically plausible recurrent network generates sparse, high-dimensional 'barcode' patterns from place inputs via chaotic dynamics and stores them as Hebbian attractors, reproducing chickadee hippocampal data and showing barcodes reduce memory interference between correlated experiences while place tuning adds flexibility.

papers.title: OpenAI o1-mini
papers.abstract: Advancing cost-efficient reasoning
papers.summary: A smaller, cost-efficient version of OpenAI's o1 reasoning model specialized for STEM. It matches o1 on many math and coding benchmarks at substantially lower cost and latency, though with more limited broad world knowledge.

papers.title: Learning to reason with LLMs
papers.summary: Introduces OpenAI o1, trained with large-scale reinforcement learning to reason through a long internal chain of thought before answering. It sharply improves competition math, coding, and PhD-level science benchmarks, with accuracy scaling with both train-time and test-time compute.

papers.title: BAMDP Shaping: a Unified Framework for Intrinsic Motivation and Reward Shaping
papers.abstract: Intrinsic motivation and reward shaping guide reinforcement learning (RL) agents by adding pseudo-rewards, which can lead to useful emergent behaviors. However, they can also encourage counterproductive exploits, e.g., fixation with noisy TV screens. Here we provide a theoretical model which anticipates these behaviors, and provides broad criteria under which adverse effects can be bounded. We characterize all pseudo-rewards as reward shaping in Bayes-Adaptive Markov Decision Processes (BAMDPs), which formulates the problem of learning in MDPs as an MDP over the agent's knowledge. Optimal exploration maximizes BAMDP state value, which we decompose into the value of the information gathere…
papers.summary: Models intrinsic-motivation and reward-shaping pseudo-rewards as reward shaping in Bayes-Adaptive MDPs, decomposing optimal exploration into information value plus physical-state value, and proves potential-based shaping functions are immune to reward-hacking in meta-RL and standard RL.

papers.title: Modeling the Arrows of Time with Causal Multibaker Maps
papers.abstract: Why do we remember the past, and plan the future? We introduce a toy model in which to investigate emergent time asymmetries: the causal multibaker maps. These are reversible discrete-time dynamical systems with configurable causal interactions. Imposing a suitable initial condition or “Past Hypothesis”, and then coarse-graining, yields a Pearlean locally causal structure. While it is more common to speculate that the other arrows of time arise from the thermodynamic arrow, our model instead takes the causal arrow as fundamental. From it, we obtain the thermodynamic and epistemic arrows of time. The epistemic arrow concerns records, which we define to be systems that encode the state of a…
papers.summary: Introduces 'causal multibaker maps,' reversible discrete-time systems with configurable causal structure. Imposing a Past Hypothesis and coarse-graining yields Pearlean causal structure, from which the thermodynamic and epistemic (records) arrows of time emerge — taking the causal arrow, not entropy, as fundamental.

papers.title: Hierarchical Partitioning Forecaster
papers.summary: Introduces Hierarchical Partitioning Forecasters for sequential prediction: hierarchically partition the feature space, blend forecasters specialized to each sub-region, and learn via local online learning. This yields O(k log T) regret against constant-partition competitors while empirically rivaling deep networks.

papers.title: Learning in Games with Progressive Hiding
papers.abstract: When learning to play an imperfect information game, it is often easier to first start with the basic mechanics of the game rules. For example, one can play several example rounds with private cards revealed to all players to better understand the basic actions and their effects. Building on this intuition, this paper introduces {\it progressive hiding}, an algorithm that balances learning the basic mechanics of an imperfect information game and satisfying the information constraints. Progressive hiding is inspired by methods from stochastic multistage optimization, such as scenario decomposition and progressive hedging. We prove that it enables the adaptation of counterfactual regret min…
papers.summary: Introduces 'progressive hiding,' an algorithm inspired by stochastic multistage optimization that gradually enforces information constraints, enabling counterfactual regret minimization to extend to imperfect-information games without perfect recall; experiments show notable improvements across several settings.

papers.title: Evaluating Treatment Prioritization Rules via Rank-Weighted Average Treatment Effects
papers.abstract: There are a number of available methods for selecting whom to prioritize for treatment, including ones based on treatment effect estimation, risk scoring, and hand-crafted rules. We propose rank-weighted average treatment effect (RATE) metrics as a simple and general family of metrics for comparing and testing the quality of treatment prioritization rules. RATE metrics are agnostic as to how the prioritization rules were derived, and only assess how well they identify individuals that benefit the most from treatment. We define a family of RATE estimators and prove a central limit theorem that enables asymptotically exact inference in a wide variety of randomized and observational study se…
papers.summary: Proposes rank-weighted average treatment effect (RATE) metrics to compare and test treatment-prioritization rules independent of how they were derived, with a central limit theorem enabling exact inference. RATE subsumes the Qini coefficient and is demonstrated on medical targeting.
papers.venue: Journal of the American Statistical Association

papers.title: Circuits Updates — September 2024
papers.abstract: We report a number of developing ideas on the Anthropic interpretability team, which might be of interest to researchers working actively in this space. Some of these are emerging strands of research where we expect to publish more on in the coming months. Others are minor points we wish to share, since we're unlikely to ever write a paper about them.
papers.summary: Anthropic interpretability updates: replicating and analyzing 'successor heads' — attention heads mapping ordinal tokens to successors (2 to 3, Wednesday to Thursday) via multiple methods including an ICA-based one — and showing that oversampling a topic in SAE training data yields more detailed features for it.

papers.title: Optimal approximate minimization of one-letter weighted finite automata
papers.abstract: Abstract In this paper, we study the approximate minimization problem of weighted finite automata (WFAs): to compute the best possible approximation of a WFA given a bound on the number of states. By reformulating the problem in terms of Hankel matrices, we leverage classical results on the approximation of Hankel operators, namely the celebrated Adamyan-Arov-Krein (AAK) theory. We solve the optimal spectral-norm approximate minimization problem for irredundant WFAs with real weights, defined over a one-letter alphabet. We present a theoretical analysis based on AAK theory and bounds on the quality of the approximation in the spectral norm and $\ell ^2$ norm. Moreover, we provide a closed…
papers.summary: Solves optimal spectral-norm approximate minimization of irredundant real-weighted finite automata over a one-letter alphabet by reformulating it via Hankel matrices and applying Adamyan-Arov-Krein theory, providing approximation-quality bounds and a polynomial-time closed-form algorithm.

papers.title: Human Participants in AI Research: Ethics and Transparency in Practice
papers.abstract: In recent years, research involving human participants has been critical to advances in artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML), particularly in the areas of conversational, human-compatible, and cooperative AI. For example, roughly 9% of publications at recent AAAI and NeurIPS conferences indicate the collection of original human data. Yet AI and ML researchers lack guidelines for ethical research practices with human participants. Fewer than one out of every four of these AAAI and NeurIPS papers confirm independent ethical review, the collection of informed consent, or participant compensation. This paper aims to bridge this gap by examining the normative similarities and…
papers.summary: Surveys ethical practice for research with human participants in AI/ML, finding fewer than a quarter of sampled AAAI/NeurIPS papers report ethics review, informed consent, or compensation, and proposes a contextual framework and guidelines tailored to AI's distinct considerations.
papers.venue: IEEE Transactions on Technology and Society

papers.title: A Gradient Analysis Framework for Rewarding Good and Penalizing Bad Examples in Language Models
papers.abstract: Beyond maximum likelihood estimation (MLE), the standard objective of a language model (LM) that optimizes good examples probabilities, many studies have explored ways that also penalize bad examples for enhancing the quality of output distribution, including unlikelihood training, exponential maximizing average treatment effect (ExMATE), and direct preference optimization (DPO). To systematically compare these methods and further provide a unified recipe for LM optimization, in this paper, we present a unique angle of gradient analysis of loss functions that simultaneously reward good examples and penalize bad ones in LMs. Through both mathematical results and experiments on CausalDialog…
papers.summary: Analyzes loss functions that reward good and penalize bad examples (unlikelihood, ExMATE, DPO) via their gradients. Finds ExMATE is a superior MLE surrogate, and combining DPO with ExMATE rather than MLE improves statistical metrics by 5-7% and generative win rate by 18%.

papers.title: Agile Project Management in Software Development Industries: Challenges and Opportunities
papers.abstract: Agile project management methodologies have revolutionized software development by emphasizing flexibility, collaboration, and iterative progress. Originally confined to software, Agile principles have now permeated diverse industries including manufacturing, construction, healthcare, and marketing. This shift addresses the limitations of traditional linear methodologies, offering enhanced responsiveness to market dynamics and improved project outcomes. However, Agile adoption presents challenges such as cultural resistance, scaling complexities, and integration with existing frameworks. This research explores these challenges and opportunities through a comprehensive review of literature…
papers.summary: Reviews how Agile project-management methodologies, originally from software, have spread to manufacturing, construction, healthcare, and marketing. Synthesizes literature and case studies to identify adoption challenges—cultural resistance, scaling, framework integration—and strategies for successful implementation.

papers.title: ACF-S: An 8-Terabit / Sec SuperNIC for High-Performance Data Movement in AI & Accelerated Compute Networks
papers.abstract: Redefine networking for distributed accelerated computing to deliver peak performance, resiliency and node scale
papers.summary: Describes ACF-S, an 8-terabit/second SuperNIC for high-performance data movement in AI and accelerated-compute networks, aiming to deliver peak performance, resiliency, and node-level scale for distributed accelerated computing.

papers.title: The signature and cusp geometry of hyperbolic knots
papers.abstract:
We introduce a new real-valued invariant called the natural slope
of a hyperbolic knot in the 3-sphere, which is defined in terms of its cusp
geometry. We show that twice the knot signature and the natural slope differ
by at most a constant times the hyperbolic volume divided by the cube of
the injectivity radius. This inequality was discovered using machine learning
to detect relationships between various knot invariants. It has applications
to Dehn surgery and to 4-ball genus. We also show a refined version of the
inequality where the upper bound is a linear function of the volume, and the
slope is corrected by terms corresponding to short geodesics that link the knot
an odd nu…
papers.summary: Introduces the 'natural slope', a real-valued invariant of a hyperbolic knot defined from its cusp geometry, and proves it bounds twice the knot signature in terms of hyperbolic volume and injectivity radius. The relationship was discovered using machine learning over knot invariants.

papers.title: Accurate computation of quantum excited states with neural networks
papers.abstract: We present an algorithm to estimate the excited states of a quantum system by variational Monte Carlo, which has no free parameters and requires no orthogonalization of the states, instead transforming the problem into that of finding the ground state of an expanded system. Arbitrary observables can be calculated, including off-diagonal expectations, such as the transition dipole moment. The method works particularly well with neural network ansätze, and by combining this method with the FermiNet and Psiformer ansätze, we can accurately recover excitation energies and oscillator strengths on a range of molecules. We achieve accurate vertical excitation energies on benzene-scale molecules,…
papers.summary: A variational Monte Carlo method that recasts finding a quantum system's excited states as locating the ground state of an expanded system, requiring no orthogonalization or free parameters. With FermiNet and Psiformer ansatze it accurately recovers excitation energies and oscillator strengths, including double excitations.

papers.title: The Vizier Gaussian Process Bandit Algorithm
papers.abstract: Google Vizier has performed millions of optimizations and accelerated numerous research and production systems at Google, demonstrating the success of Bayesian optimization as a large-scale service. Over multiple years, its algorithm has been improved considerably, through the collective experiences of numerous research efforts and user feedback. In this technical report, we discuss the implementation details and design choices of the current default algorithm provided by Open Source Vizier. Our experiments on standardized benchmarks reveal its robustness and versatility against well-established industry baselines on multiple practical modes.
papers.summary: Technical report detailing the default Gaussian-process bandit algorithm in open-source Google Vizier, the Bayesian-optimization service behind millions of tuning runs at Google. Standardized benchmarks show robustness and versatility against established industry baselines.

papers.title: Switching between tasks can cause AI to lose the ability to learn
papers.summary: Nature news feature on 'loss of plasticity': artificial neural networks trained on tasks one after another progressively lose the ability to learn new ones. Surveys the limited understanding of why this breakdown happens and emerging approaches to fix it.

papers.title: Swim till you sink: Computing the limit of a game
papers.abstract: During 2023, two interesting results were proven about the limit behavior of game dynamics: First, it was shown that there is a game for which no dynamics converges to the Nash equilibria. Second, it was shown that the sink equilibria of a game adequately capture the limit behavior of natural game dynamics. These two results have created a need and opportunity to articulate a principled computational theory of the meaning of the game that is based on game dynamics. Given any game in normal form, and any prior distribution of play, we study the problem of computing the asymptotic behavior of a class of natural dynamics called the noisy replicator dynamics as a limit distribution over the s…
papers.summary: Studies computing the limit behavior of natural game dynamics as a distribution over a game's sink equilibria under noisy replicator dynamics. Proves near-linear-time computation for pure-strategy priors and estimates limit distributions for large games via sampling and simulation.

papers.title: SEAL: Systematic Error Analysis for Value ALignment
papers.abstract: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) aims to align language models (LMs) with human values by training reward models (RMs) on binary preferences and using these RMs to fine-tune the base LMs. Despite its importance, the internal mechanisms of RLHF remain poorly understood. This paper introduces new metrics to evaluate the effectiveness of modeling and aligning human values, namely feature imprint, alignment resistance and alignment robustness. We categorize alignment datasets into target features (desired values) and spoiler features (undesired concepts). By regressing RM scores against these features, we quantify the extent to which RMs reward them - a metric we term feature…
papers.summary: Introduces metrics—feature imprint, alignment resistance, and alignment robustness—to probe how RLHF reward models encode human values. Using Anthropic HH-RLHF data and OpenAssistant reward models, finds strong target-feature imprints, sensitivity to spoiler features, and 26% alignment resistance where labelers disagreed with humans.

papers.title: The Probabilities Also Matter: A More Faithful Metric for Faithfulness of Free-Text Explanations in Large Language Models
papers.abstract: In order to oversee advanced AI systems, it is important to understand their reasons for generating a given output. When prompted, large language models (LLMs) can provide natural language explanations or reasoning traces that sound plausible and receive high ratings from human annotators. However, it is unclear to what extent these explanations are truly capturing the factors responsible for the model's predictions: the most ``human-like'' explanation may be different from the one that is most faithful to the model's true decision making process. In this work, we introduce the correlational counterfactual test (CCT), a faithfulness metric based on counterfactual input edits that takes in…
papers.summary: Proposes the correlational counterfactual test, a faithfulness metric for LLM free-text explanations that measures the full shift in the predicted label distribution under counterfactual edits rather than just label flips. Llama-2 explanations more often cite genuinely impactful factors, with association growing with model size.

papers.title: Introducing SWE-bench Verified
papers.abstract: We’re releasing a human-validated subset of SWE-bench that more reliably evaluates AI models’ ability to solve real-world software issues.
papers.summary: Releases SWE-bench Verified, a 500-task human-validated subset of SWE-bench that filters out unsolvable or under-specified problems, providing a more reliable measure of language models' ability to resolve real-world GitHub software issues.

papers.title: AlphaFold two years on: Validation and impact
papers.abstract: Two years on from the initial release of AlphaFold, we have seen its widespread adoption as a structure prediction tool. Here, we discuss some of the latest work based on AlphaFold, with a particular focus on its use within the structural biology community. This encompasses use cases like speeding up structure determination itself, enabling new computational studies, and building new tools and workflows. We also look at the ongoing validation of AlphaFold, as its predictions continue to be compared against large numbers of experimental structures to further delineate the model's capabilities and limitations.
papers.summary: Reviews AlphaFold's adoption across structural biology two years after release, covering use cases like accelerating structure determination, enabling computational studies, and building new tools, alongside ongoing validation against experimental structures that maps the model's capabilities and limitations.
papers.venue: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences

papers.title: Gemma Scope: Open Sparse Autoencoders Everywhere All At Once on Gemma 2
papers.abstract: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) are an unsupervised method for learning a sparse decomposition of a neural network’s latent representations into seemingly interpretable features.Despite recent excitement about their potential, research applications outside of industry are limited by the high cost of training a comprehensive suite of SAEs.In this work, we introduce Gemma Scope, an open suite of JumpReLU SAEs trained on all layers and sub-layers of Gemma 2 2B and 9B and select layers of Gemma 2 27B base models.We primarily train SAEs on the Gemma 2 pre-trained models, but additionally release SAEs trained on instruction-tuned Gemma 2 9B for comparison.We evaluate the quality of each SAE on stand…
papers.summary: Releases Gemma Scope, an open suite of JumpReLU sparse autoencoders trained on all layers and sublayers of Gemma 2 2B and 9B (plus select layers of 27B), with standard-metric evaluations, to lower the cost of safety and interpretability research on these models.

papers.title: GPT-4o System Card
papers.abstract: GPT-4o is an autoregressive omni model that accepts as input any combination of text, audio, image, and video, and generates any combination of text, audio, and image outputs. It's trained end-to-end across text, vision, and audio, meaning all inputs and outputs are processed by the same neural network. GPT-4o can respond to audio inputs in as little as 232 milliseconds, with an average of 320 milliseconds, which is similar to human response time in conversation. It matches GPT-4 Turbo performance on text in English and code, with significant improvement on text in non-English languages, while also being much faster and 50\% cheaper in the API. GPT-4o is especially better at vision and au…
papers.summary: System card for GPT-4o, an omni model that processes any combination of text, audio, image, and video end-to-end with roughly 320ms average audio response. Details capabilities, limitations, Preparedness Framework risk evaluations, third-party dangerous-capability assessments, and safety mitigations, focusing on speech-to-speech.

papers.title: A Note on the Maximum Number of k-Powers in a Finite Word
papers.abstract: A power is a concatenation of $k$ copies of a word $u$, for a positive integer $k$; the power is also called a $k$-power and $k$ is its exponent. We prove that for any $k \ge 2$, the maximum number of different non-empty $k$-power factors in a word of length $n$ is between $\frac{n}{k-1}-\Theta(\sqrt{n})$ and $\frac{n-1}{k-1}$. We also show that the maximum number of different non-empty power factors of exponent at least 2 in a length-$n$ word is at most $n-1$. Both upper bounds generalize the recent upper bound of $n-1$ on the maximum number of different square factors in a length-$n$ word by Brlek and Li (2022).
papers.summary: Proves bounds on the maximum number of distinct non-empty k-power factors in a length-n word, roughly between n/(k-1) and (n-1)/(k-1) for k at least 2, and at most n-1 for powers of exponent at least 2, generalizing prior bounds on square factors.

papers.title: A Comparison of LLM Finetuning Methods & Evaluation Metrics with Travel Chatbot Use Case
papers.abstract: This research compares large language model (LLM) fine-tuning methods, including Quantized Low Rank Adapter (QLoRA), Retrieval Augmented fine-tuning (RAFT), and Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF), and additionally compared LLM evaluation methods including End to End (E2E) benchmark method of "Golden Answers", traditional natural language processing (NLP) metrics, RAG Assessment (Ragas), OpenAI GPT-4 evaluation metrics, and human evaluation, using the travel chatbot use case. The travel dataset was sourced from the the Reddit API by requesting posts from travel-related subreddits to get travel-related conversation prompts and personalized travel experiences, and augmented fo…
papers.summary: Compares LLM fine-tuning methods (QLoRA, RAFT, RLHF) and evaluation approaches on a travel-chatbot task with LLaMA-2-7B and Mistral-7B. Finds GPT-4-based evaluation aligns best with human judgment while traditional NLP and Ragas metrics do not, with Mistral RAFT plus RLHF performing best.

papers.title: Achieving Human Level Competitive Robot Table Tennis
papers.abstract: Achieving human-level speed and performance on real world tasks is a north star for the robotics research community. This work takes a step towards that goal and presents the first learned robot agent that reaches amateur human-level performance in competitive table tennis. Table tennis is a physically demanding sport which requires human players to undergo years of training to achieve an advanced level of proficiency. In this paper, we contribute (1) a hierarchical and modular policy architecture consisting of (i) low level controllers with their detailed skill descriptors which model the agent's capabilities and help to bridge the sim-to-real gap and (ii) a high level controller that ch…
papers.summary: Presents the first learned robot agent to reach amateur human-level competitive table tennis, combining low-level skill controllers with a high-level skill selector, zero-shot sim-to-real via an automatic real-grounded curriculum, and live opponent adaptation. It won 45% (13/29) of matches against unseen human players.

papers.title: Why Are My Prompts Leaked? Unraveling Prompt Extraction Threats in Customized Large Language Models
papers.abstract: The drastic increase of large language models' (LLMs) parameters has led to a new research direction of fine-tuning-free downstream customization by prompts, i.e., task descriptions. While these prompt-based services (e.g. OpenAI's GPTs) play an important role in many businesses, there has emerged growing concerns about the prompt leakage, which undermines the intellectual properties of these services and causes downstream attacks. In this paper, we analyze the underlying mechanism of prompt leakage, which we refer to as prompt memorization, and develop corresponding defending strategies. By exploring the scaling laws in prompt extraction, we analyze key attributes that influence prompt e…
papers.summary: Analyzes prompt leakage in customized LLM services as 'prompt memorization,' studying how model size, prompt length, and prompt type affect extraction and attributing it to perplexity and attention token-translation paths. Shows even GPT-4 is highly vulnerable and proposes defenses that sharply cut extraction rates.

papers.title: Anarchy as Architect: Competitive Pressure, Technology, and the Internal Structure of States
papers.abstract: Abstract The internal institutional structures of states greatly impact their citizens’ welfare. However, states are not at complete liberty to adopt any internal form. Competitive pressure arising from anarchy limits the range of viable domestic institutions to those that do not impose a significant disadvantage. We argue that technological change can alter the relative competitiveness of different state forms and, by extension, improve or degrade human welfare. We empirically support this argument through a macrohistorical survey of competitively significant technologies. We conclude that the true costs of international anarchy are greater than commonly appreciated, as competitive press…
papers.summary: Argues that competitive pressure under international anarchy restricts which domestic state institutions are viable, and that technological change reshapes the relative competitiveness of state forms—sometimes degrading human welfare. Supported by a macrohistorical survey of competitively significant technologies.

papers.title: The Quest for the Right Mediator: Surveying Mechanistic Interpretability Through the Lens of Causal Mediation Analysis
papers.abstract: Interpretability provides a toolset for understanding how and why neural networks behave in certain ways. However, there is little unity in the field: most studies employ ad-hoc evaluations and do not share theoretical foundations, making it difficult to measure progress and compare the pros and cons of different techniques. Furthermore, while mechanistic understanding is frequently discussed, the basic causal units underlying these mechanisms are often not explicitly defined. In this article, we propose a perspective on interpretability research grounded in causal mediation analysis. Specifically, we describe the history and current state of interpretability taxonomized according to the…
papers.summary: Surveys mechanistic interpretability through the lens of causal mediation analysis, taxonomizing methods by the type of causal unit (mediator) used and how mediators are searched over. Discusses trade-offs of each and recommends discovering new mediators and developing standardized evaluations.

papers.title: Circuits Updates — August 2024
papers.abstract: We report a number of developing ideas on the Anthropic interpretability team, which might be of interest to researchers working actively in this space. Some of these are emerging strands of research where we expect to publish more on in the coming months. Others are minor points we wish to share, since we're unlikely to ever write a paper about them.
papers.summary: Anthropic interpretability team's August 2024 progress notes, sharing developing ideas, preliminary results, and minor findings from active mechanistic-interpretability research—some of which previews future papers and some unlikely to be written up separately.

papers.title: Differentially Private Stream Processing at Scale
papers.abstract: We design, to the best of our knowledge, the first differentially private (DP) stream aggregation processing system at scale. Our system - Differential Privacy SQL Pipelines (DP-SQLP) - is built using a streaming framework similar to Spark streaming, and is built on top of the Spanner database and the F1 query engine from Google. Towards designing DP-SQLP we make both algorithmic and systemic advances, namely, we (i) design a novel (user-level) DP key selection algorithm that can operate on an unbounded set of possible keys, and can scale to one billion keys that users have contributed, (ii) design a preemptive execution scheme for DP key selection that avoids enumerating all the keys at…
papers.summary: Presents DP-SQLP, a scalable differentially private stream-aggregation system built on Spanner and F1. Contributes a user-level DP key-selection algorithm scaling to a billion keys, a preemptive execution scheme, and continual-observation histograms, achieving at least 16x error reduction over baselines in Google deployments.

papers.title: Vision-Language Model Based Handwriting Verification
papers.abstract: Handwriting Verification is a critical in document forensics. Deep learning based approaches often face skepticism from forensic document examiners due to their lack of explainability and reliance on extensive training data and handcrafted features. This paper explores using Vision Language Models (VLMs), such as OpenAI's GPT-4o and Google's PaliGemma, to address these challenges. By leveraging their Visual Question Answering capabilities and 0-shot Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning, our goal is to provide clear, human-understandable explanations for model decisions. Our experiments on the CEDAR handwriting dataset demonstrate that VLMs offer enhanced interpretability, reduce the need for…
papers.summary: Explores vision-language models (GPT-4o, PaliGemma) with zero-shot chain-of-thought for explainable handwriting verification on the CEDAR dataset. VLMs improve interpretability but underperform a CNN ResNet-18 baseline (84%) versus 70% for GPT-4o and 71% for fine-tuned PaliGemma.

papers.title: Measuring Progress in Dictionary Learning for Language Model Interpretability with Board Game Models
papers.abstract: What latent features are encoded in language model (LM) representations? Recent work on training sparse autoencoders (SAEs) to disentangle interpretable features in LM representations has shown significant promise. However, evaluating the quality of these SAEs is difficult because we lack a ground-truth collection of interpretable features that we expect good SAEs to recover. We thus propose to measure progress in interpretable dictionary learning by working in the setting of LMs trained on chess and Othello transcripts. These settings carry natural collections of interpretable features -- for example, "there is a knight on F3" -- which we leverage into $\textit{supervised}$ metrics for S…
papers.summary: Uses language models trained on chess and Othello transcripts—where interpretable features like 'knight on F3' are known—to build supervised metrics for sparse-autoencoder quality. Introduces p-annealing, a training technique that improves both prior unsupervised metrics and the new supervised ones.

papers.title: Pre-trained Gaussian processes for Bayesian optimization
papers.abstract: Bayesian optimization (BO) has become a popular strategy for global optimization of expensive real-world functions. Contrary to a common expectation that BO is suited to optimizing black-box functions, it actually requires domain knowledge about those functions to deploy BO successfully. Such domain knowledge often manifests in Gaussian process (GP) priors that specify initial beliefs on functions. However, even with expert knowledge, it is non-trivial to quantitatively define a prior. This is especially true for hyperparameter tuning problems on complex machine learning models, where landscapes of tuning objectives are often difficult to comprehend. We seek an alternative practice for se…
papers.summary: Proposes HyperBO, which pre-trains Gaussian-process priors from data on related functions using a KL-divergence loss instead of hand-specifying priors for Bayesian optimization. Provides bounded posterior predictions and near-zero regret without assuming the ground-truth prior, validated on a large multi-task hyperparameter-tuning dataset.

papers.title: Video Foundation Models for Animal Behavior Analysis
papers.abstract: Abstract Computational approaches leveraging computer vision and machine learning have transformed the quantification of animal behavior from video. However, existing methods often rely on task-specific features or models, which struggle to generalize across diverse datasets and tasks. Recent advances in machine learning, particularly the emergence of vision foundation models, i.e., large-scale models pre-trained on massive, diverse visual repositories, offers a way to tackle these challenges. Here, we investigate the potential of frozen video foundation models across a range of behavior analysis tasks, including classification, retrieval, and localization. We use a single, frozen model t…
papers.summary: Evaluates frozen video foundation models as general-purpose feature extractors for animal-behavior analysis across classification, retrieval, and localization on diverse open datasets. With minimal adaptation they match task-specific methods across species and contexts, offering an accessible backbone for ethology, neuroscience, and ecology.

papers.title: Rethinking Machine Learning Collective Communication as a Multi-Commodity Flow Problem
papers.abstract: Cloud operators utilize collective communication optimizers to enhance the efficiency of the single-tenant, centrally managed training clusters they manage. However, current optimizers struggle to scale for such use cases and often compromise solution quality for scalability. Our solution, TE-CCL, adopts a traffic-engineering-based approach to collective communication. Compared to a state-of-the-art optimizer, TACCL, TE-CCL produced schedules with 2× better performance on topologies TACCL supports (and its solver took a similar amount of time as TACCL's heuristic-based approach). TECCL additionally scales to larger topologies than TACCL. On our GPU testbed, TE-CCL outperformed TACCL by 2.…
papers.summary: Reframes ML collective-communication scheduling as a traffic-engineering, multi-commodity-flow problem. The resulting optimizer TE-CCL produces 2x faster schedules than TACCL, scales to larger topologies, and on a GPU testbed beats TACCL by 2.14x and RCCL by 3.18x in algorithm bandwidth.

papers.title: Mixture of Nested Experts: Adaptive Processing of Visual Tokens
papers.abstract: The visual medium (images and videos) naturally contains a large amount of information redundancy, thereby providing a great opportunity for leveraging efficiency in processing. While Vision Transformer (ViT) based models scale effectively to large data regimes, they fail to capitalize on this inherent redundancy, leading to higher computational costs. Mixture of Experts (MoE) networks demonstrate scalability while maintaining same inference-time costs, but they come with a larger parameter footprint. We present Mixture of Nested Experts (MoNE), which utilizes a nested structure for experts, wherein individual experts fall on an increasing compute-accuracy curve. Given a compute budget, M…
papers.summary: Introduces Mixture of Nested Experts (MoNE), where nested experts sit on an increasing compute-accuracy curve and visual tokens are dynamically routed by priority so redundant tokens use cheaper experts. Matches baseline accuracy on ImageNet-21K, Kinetics400, and SSv2 at over 2x less inference compute.

papers.title: The experience of living with vitiligo in Nigeria: A participatory Interpretative Phenomenological Analysis
papers.abstract: Vitiligo is a visible depigmenting skin condition, particularly noticeable on Black skin. There is widespread misunderstanding of the condition. Using a participatory form of Interpretative Phenomenological Analysis (IPA), we conducted eight semi-structured interviews with Nigerians living with vitiligo. Participants described their initial attempts to understand the condition, which typically drew on both traditional illness beliefs, religious influences, and the biomedical disease model. All participants reported experiencing marked stigmatization and discrimination. Participants experienced distress associated with thoughts about the personal meaning of the disease including its impact…
papers.summary: Qualitative interpretative phenomenological study of eight Nigerians living with vitiligo, finding that their understanding blends traditional, religious, and biomedical beliefs, that they face marked stigma and discrimination, and that they nonetheless develop coping strategies that sustain wellbeing.

papers.title: Risk-sensitive innovation: leveraging interactions between technologies to navigate technology risks
papers.abstract: Abstract The advancement of low-emission technologies to curb climate change demonstrates how strategically managing the timing of technological developments can mitigate negative societal outcomes. We propose that a similar approach, which we term ‘risk-sensitive innovation’, can also address risks from emerging technologies like synthetic biology and artificial intelligence. Risk-sensitive innovation (RSI) leverages interactions between technologies to reduce specific risks across a technology portfolio. For instance, it may be beneficial to delay or halt risk-increasing technologies and preferentially advance risk-reducing defensive, safety, or substitute technologies. To be of practic…
papers.summary: Proposes 'risk-sensitive innovation': managing the relative timing of technology development to reduce risks across a portfolio—for example delaying risk-increasing technologies while advancing defensive or substitute ones. Discusses applicability to synthetic biology and AI and implications for research funding and regulation.

papers.title: Using Large Language Models for the Interpretation of Building Regulations
papers.abstract: Compliance checking is an essential part of a construction project. The recent rapid uptake of building information models (BIM) in the construction industry has created more opportunities for automated compliance checking (ACC). BIM enables sharing of digital building design data that can be used for compliance checking with legal requirements, which are conventionally conveyed in natural language and not intended for machine processing. Creating a computable representation of legal requirements suitable for ACC is complex, costly, and time-consuming. Large language models (LLMs) such as the generative pre-trained transformers (GPT), GPT-3.5 and GPT-4, powering OpenAI's ChatGPT, can gene…
papers.summary: Evaluates GPT-3.5 and GPT-4 for few-shot translation of natural-language building regulations into the computable LegalRuleML format for automated compliance checking, probing how system prompts and chain-of-thought reasoning surface the models' latent legal-domain knowledge.

papers.title: App2Exa: Accelerating Exact kNN Search via Dynamic Cache-Guided Approximation
papers.abstract: The k-nearest neighbor (kNN) query is a cornerstone of similarity-based applications across various domains. While prior work has enhanced kNN search efficiency, it typically focuses on approximate methods for high-dimensional data or exact methods for low-dimensional data, often assuming static query and data distributions. This creates a significant gap in accelerating exact kNN search for low-to-medium dimensional data with dynamic query distributions. To fill this gap, we propose App2Exa, a cache-guided framework that integrates approximate and exact kNN search. App2Exa utilizes a dynamically maintained cache graph index to retrieve approximate results, which subsequently guide exact…
papers.summary: App2Exa accelerates exact k-nearest-neighbor search for low-to-medium-dimensional data by using a dynamically maintained cache-graph index to seed a best-first VP-Tree search, with a benefit-driven caching policy that adapts to evolving query distributions.

papers.title: Collaborative Multi-LoRA Experts with Achievement-based Multi-Tasks Loss for Unified Multimodal Information Extraction
papers.abstract: Multimodal Information Extraction (MIE) has gained attention for extracting structured information from multimedia sources. Traditional methods tackle MIE tasks separately, missing opportunities to share knowledge across tasks. Recent approaches unify these tasks into a generation problem using instruction-based T5 models with visual adaptors, optimized through full-parameter fine-tuning. However, this method is computationally intensive, and multi-task fine-tuning often faces gradient conflicts, limiting performance. To address these challenges, we propose collaborative multi-LoRA experts with achievement-based multi-task loss (C-LoRAE) for MIE tasks. C-LoRAE extends the low-rank adaptat…
papers.summary: Proposes C-LoRAE, a mixture of LoRA experts—one shared universal expert plus task-specific experts—with an achievement-based multi-task loss to mitigate gradient conflicts in unified multimodal information extraction, outperforming full fine-tuning and standard LoRA across seven benchmarks.

papers.title: Combining Deep Reinforcement Learning and Search with Generative Models for Game-Theoretic Opponent Modeling
papers.abstract: Opponent modeling methods typically involve two crucial steps: building a belief distribution over opponents' strategies, and exploiting this opponent model by playing a best response. However, existing approaches typically require domain-specific heurstics to come up with such a model, and algorithms for approximating best responses are hard to scale in large, imperfect information domains. In this work, we introduce a scalable and generic multiagent training regime for opponent modeling using deep game-theoretic reinforcement learning. We first propose Generative Best Respoonse (GenBR), a best response algorithm based on Monte-Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) with a learned deep generative mode…
papers.summary: Introduces Generative Best Response (GenBR), an MCTS planner that samples world states from a learned deep generative model, and embeds it in Policy-Space Response Oracles for scalable opponent modeling in large imperfect-information games; validated against humans in Deal-or-No-Deal negotiation.

papers.title: Approximate Verification of Strategic Abilities under Imperfect Information Using Local Models
papers.abstract: Verification of strategic ability under imperfect information is challenging, with complexity ranging from NP-complete to undecidable. This is partly because traditional fixpoint equivalences fail in this setting. Some years ago, an interesting idea of fixpoint approximation was proposed for model checking of ATL_ir, i.e., the logic of strategic ability for agents with imperfect information and imperfect recall. In this paper, we propose a new variant of the approximation, that uses the agent's local model rather than the global model of the system. We prove correctness of the construction, and demonstrate its effectiveness through experimental results on scalable models of voting.
papers.summary: Proposes a fixpoint-approximation method for model-checking strategic ability under imperfect information and recall (ATL_ir) that uses each agent's local model instead of the global system model, proving the construction correct and showing improved scalability on voting benchmarks.

papers.title: Improving Model Safety Behavior with Rule-Based Rewards
papers.abstract: We've developed and applied a new method leveraging Rule-Based Rewards (RBRs) that aligns models to behave safely without extensive human data collection.
papers.summary: Introduces Rule-Based Rewards (RBRs), which use explicit safety rules graded by an LLM to supply fine-grained reward signals during RLHF, achieving safe, well-calibrated refusal behavior with minimal human-labeled data and reducing over-refusal relative to human-feedback baselines.

papers.title: RedAgent: Red Teaming Large Language Models with Context-aware Autonomous Language Agent
papers.abstract: Recently, advanced Large Language Models (LLMs) such as GPT-4 have been integrated into many real-world applications like Code Copilot. These applications have significantly expanded the attack surface of LLMs, exposing them to a variety of threats. Among them, jailbreak attacks that induce toxic responses through jailbreak prompts have raised critical safety concerns. To identify these threats, a growing number of red teaming approaches simulate potential adversarial scenarios by crafting jailbreak prompts to test the target LLM. However, existing red teaming methods do not consider the unique vulnerabilities of LLM in different scenarios, making it difficult to adjust the jailbreak prom…
papers.summary: RedAgent is a multi-agent LLM system that abstracts known attacks into reusable 'jailbreak strategies' and self-reflects on contextual feedback in a memory buffer to craft context-aware jailbreak prompts, breaking most black-box LLMs within about five queries.

papers.title: Can Large Language Models Automatically Jailbreak GPT-4V?
papers.abstract: GPT-4V has attracted considerable attention due to its extraordinary capacity for integrating and processing multimodal information. At the same time, its ability of face recognition raises new safety concerns of privacy leakage. Despite researchers' efforts in safety alignment through RLHF or preprocessing filters, vulnerabilities might still be exploited. In our study, we introduce AutoJailbreak, an innovative automatic jailbreak technique inspired by prompt optimization. We leverage Large Language Models (LLMs) for red-teaming to refine the jailbreak prompt and employ weak-to-strong in-context learning prompts to boost efficiency. Furthermore, we present an effective search method that…
papers.summary: Presents AutoJailbreak, which uses LLM-driven red-teaming with prompt optimization, weak-to-strong in-context prompts, and early stopping to automatically attack GPT-4V, exceeding a 95% attack success rate against its multimodal safety alignment.

papers.title: Neural general circulation models for weather and climate
papers.abstract: Abstract General circulation models (GCMs) are the foundation of weather and climate prediction 1,2 . GCMs are physics-based simulators that combine a numerical solver for large-scale dynamics with tuned representations for small-scale processes such as cloud formation. Recently, machine-learning models trained on reanalysis data have achieved comparable or better skill than GCMs for deterministic weather forecasting 3,4 . However, these models have not demonstrated improved ensemble forecasts, or shown sufficient stability for long-term weather and climate simulations. Here we present a GCM that combines a differentiable solver for atmospheric dynamics with machine-learning components an…
papers.summary: NeuralGCM couples a differentiable solver for large-scale atmospheric dynamics with learned parameterizations of small-scale processes, matching leading machine-learning and physics-based models on 1–15 day deterministic and ensemble weather forecasts and tracking decade-scale climate metrics at far lower compute.

papers.title: Arondight: Red Teaming Large Vision Language Models with Auto-generated Multi-modal Jailbreak Prompts
papers.abstract: Large Vision Language Models (VLMs) extend and enhance the perceptual abilities of Large Language Models (LLMs). Despite offering new possibilities for LLM applications, these advancements raise significant security and ethical concerns, particularly regarding the generation of harmful content. While LLMs have undergone extensive security evaluations with the aid of red teaming frameworks, VLMs currently lack a well-developed one. To fill this gap, we introduce Arondight, a standardized red team framework tailored specifically for VLMs. Arondight is dedicated to resolving issues related to the absence of visual modality and inadequate diversity encountered when transitioning existing red…
papers.summary: Arondight is a red-teaming framework for vision-language models that pairs a red-team VLM generating visual jailbreaks with an RL-guided LLM producing diverse textual prompts, using entropy and novelty rewards to expose vulnerabilities and reaching 84.5% attack success on GPT-4.

papers.title: Failures to Find Transferable Image Jailbreaks Between Vision-Language Models
papers.abstract: The integration of new modalities into frontier AI systems offers exciting capabilities, but also increases the possibility such systems can be adversarially manipulated in undesirable ways. In this work, we focus on a popular class of vision-language models (VLMs) that generate text outputs conditioned on visual and textual inputs. We conducted a large-scale empirical study to assess the transferability of gradient-based universal image ``jailbreaks" using a diverse set of over 40 open-parameter VLMs, including 18 new VLMs that we publicly release. Overall, we find that transferable gradient-based image jailbreaks are extremely difficult to obtain. When an image jailbreak is optimized ag…
papers.summary: A large-scale study across 40+ vision-language models finds that gradient-based universal image jailbreaks rarely transfer between models—regardless of shared vision backbones, language models, or safety training—succeeding only among near-identical VLMs and improving when optimized against larger ensembles of highly similar models.

papers.title: Levels of AGI for Operationalizing Progress on the Path to AGI
papers.abstract: We propose a framework for classifying the capabilities and behavior of Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) models and their precursors. This framework introduces levels of AGI performance, generality, and autonomy, providing a common language to compare models, assess risks, and measure progress along the path to AGI. To develop our framework, we analyze existing definitions of AGI, and distill six principles that a useful ontology for AGI should satisfy. With these principles in mind, we propose "Levels of AGI" based on depth (performance) and breadth (generality) of capabilities, and reflect on how current systems fit into this ontology. We discuss the challenging requirements for fu…
papers.summary: Proposes a framework classifying AI systems by depth (performance) and breadth (generality) into 'Levels of AGI,' distilling six principles for an AGI ontology and linking the levels to autonomy and risk to guide responsible deployment.

papers.title: Jumping Ahead: Improving Reconstruction Fidelity with JumpReLU Sparse Autoencoders
papers.abstract: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) are a promising unsupervised approach for identifying causally relevant and interpretable linear features in a language model's (LM) activations. To be useful for downstream tasks, SAEs need to decompose LM activations faithfully; yet to be interpretable the decomposition must be sparse -- two objectives that are in tension. In this paper, we introduce JumpReLU SAEs, which achieve state-of-the-art reconstruction fidelity at a given sparsity level on Gemma 2 9B activations, compared to other recent advances such as Gated and TopK SAEs. We also show that this improvement does not come at the cost of interpretability through manual and automated interpretability st…
papers.summary: Introduces JumpReLU sparse autoencoders, which replace the ReLU with a discontinuous JumpReLU activation and use straight-through estimators to optimize L0 sparsity directly, achieving state-of-the-art reconstruction fidelity at fixed sparsity on Gemma 2 9B activations without sacrificing interpretability.

papers.title: Higher-order interactions between hippocampal CA1 neurons are disrupted in amnestic mice
papers.summary: Using machine-learning classifiers on CA1 calcium imaging, shows contextual threat-memory retrieval in mice relies on higher-order neuronal interactions present in wild-type but absent in amnestic amyloid-β mice; a spine-plasticity-restoring peptide recovers both the representation's statistical complexity and memory behavior.

papers.title: GPT-4o mini: advancing cost-efficient intelligence
papers.summary: Announces GPT-4o mini, OpenAI's small, low-cost model that scores 82% on MMLU and surpasses GPT-3.5 Turbo, supporting text and vision with a 128K-token context window at sharply reduced pricing for high-volume, latency-sensitive applications.
