papers.summary: Evaluates a single-parameter transcorrelated (TC) correlation factor on many-electron systems — the Li-Ne atoms, their cations, and small molecules — combined with TC-FCIQMC. It reaches near chemical accuracy with quadruple-zeta basis sets, only slightly less accurate than more elaborate correlation factors.

papers.title: Zero-Shot Video Question Answering via Frozen Bidirectional Language Models
papers.abstract: Video question answering (VideoQA) is a complex task that requires diverse multi-modal data for training. Manual annotation of question and answers for videos, however, is tedious and prohibits scalability. To tackle this problem, recent methods consider zero-shot settings with no manual annotation of visual question-answer. In particular, a promising approach adapts frozen autoregressive language models pretrained on Web-scale text-only data to multi-modal inputs. In contrast, we here build on frozen bidirectional language models (BiLM) and show that such an approach provides a stronger and cheaper alternative for zero-shot VideoQA. In particular, (i) we combine visual inputs with the fr…
papers.summary: Adapts frozen bidirectional language models to multimodal input via light trainable modules trained on web-scraped data, performing zero-shot VideoQA through masked-language-modeling inference. FrozenBiLM outperforms prior zero-shot methods across eight VideoQA datasets and stays competitive in few-shot and supervised settings.

papers.title: BYOL-Explore: Exploration by Bootstrapped Prediction
papers.abstract: We present BYOL-Explore, a conceptually simple yet general approach for curiosity-driven exploration in visually-complex environments. BYOL-Explore learns a world representation, the world dynamics, and an exploration policy all-together by optimizing a single prediction loss in the latent space with no additional auxiliary objective. We show that BYOL-Explore is effective in DM-HARD-8, a challenging partially-observable continuous-action hard-exploration benchmark with visually-rich 3-D environments. On this benchmark, we solve the majority of the tasks purely through augmenting the extrinsic reward with BYOL-Explore s intrinsic reward, whereas prior work could only get off the ground wi…
papers.summary: Curiosity-driven exploration that jointly learns a world representation, environment dynamics, and an exploration policy from a single latent-space prediction loss. Its intrinsic reward solves most DM-HARD-8 tasks without demonstrations and reaches superhuman scores on the ten hardest Atari exploration games.

papers.title: Emergent Abilities of Large Language Models
papers.abstract: Scaling up language models has been shown to predictably improve performance and sample efficiency on a wide range of downstream tasks. This paper instead discusses an unpredictable phenomenon that we refer to as emergent abilities of large language models. We consider an ability to be emergent if it is not present in smaller models but is present in larger models. Thus, emergent abilities cannot be predicted simply by extrapolating the performance of smaller models. The existence of such emergence implies that additional scaling could further expand the range of capabilities of language models.
papers.summary: Defines emergent abilities as capabilities absent in smaller models yet present in larger ones, which cannot be forecast by extrapolating smaller-model performance. Documents such sharp, unpredictable jumps across many tasks, arguing continued scaling may keep unlocking qualitatively new capabilities.

papers.title: AI-written critiques help humans notice flaws
papers.abstract: We trained “critique-writing” models to describe flaws in summaries. Human evaluators find flaws in summaries much more often when shown our model’s critiques. Larger models are better at self-critiquing, with scale improving critique-writing more than summary-writing. This shows promise for using AI systems to assist human supervision of AI systems on difficult tasks.
papers.summary: Trains models to write natural-language critiques exposing flaws in summaries; human evaluators catch substantially more flaws when shown them. Larger models self-critique better, with scale aiding critique-writing more than summary-writing, evidence that AI assistance can scale human oversight of AI.

papers.title: Techniques for training large neural networks
papers.abstract: Large neural networks are at the core of many recent advances in AI, but training them is a difficult engineering and research challenge which requires orchestrating a cluster of GPUs to perform a single synchronized calculation.
papers.summary: Explainer surveying how large neural networks are trained across GPU clusters: data, pipeline, and tensor (model) parallelism, mixture-of-experts, and memory-saving methods, along with the tradeoffs of each parallelism strategy.

papers.title: Core control principles of the eukaryotic cell cycle
papers.abstract: Abstract Cyclin-dependent kinases (CDKs) lie at the heart of eukaryotic cell cycle control, with different cyclin–CDK complexes initiating DNA replication (S-CDKs) and mitosis (M-CDKs) 1,2 . However, the principles on which cyclin–CDK complexes organize the temporal order of cell cycle events are contentious 3 . One model proposes that S-CDKs and M-CDKs are functionally specialized, with substantially different substrate specificities to execute different cell cycle events 4–6 . A second model proposes that S-CDKs and M-CDKs are redundant with each other, with both acting as sources of overall CDK activity 7,8 . In this model, increasing CDK activity, rather than CDK substrate specificity…
papers.summary: Phosphoproteomics in fission yeast shows S-CDK and M-CDK have nearly identical substrate specificities; removing protein phosphatase 1 from the centrosome lets S-CDK drive mitosis. Reconciles quantitative (CDK-activity-level) and qualitative (specificity) models of cell-cycle ordering.

papers.title: A probabilistic successor representation for context-dependent prediction
papers.abstract: Abstract The different strategies that animals use for predicting reward are often classified as model-based or model-free reinforcement learning (RL) algorithms. Model-based RL involves explicit simulation the future to make decisions while model-free strategies rely on learning associations between stimuli and predicted reward by trial and error. An alternative, intermediate strategy for RL is based on the “successor representation” (SR), an encoding of environmental states in terms of predicted future states. A recent theoretical proposal suggests that the hippocampus encodes the SR in order to facilitate prediction of future reward. However, this proposal does not take into account ho…
papers.summary: A theory of learning successor representations from prediction errors that balances new observations against prior knowledge, extended to multiple task-specific SRs with online context inference. Captures animal behavior in contextual-memory and generalization tasks, unifying SR theory with hippocampal decision-making.

papers.title: Softmax Linear Units
papers.summary: Introduces the Softmax Linear Unit (x times softmax(x)) as a transformer MLP activation that increases the fraction of neurons responding to single, human-interpretable concepts, reducing polysemanticity without hurting performance. Notes an added LayerNorm needed for stability may partly re-hide features.

papers.title: Kubric: A scalable dataset generator
papers.abstract: Data is the driving force of machine learning, with the amount and quality of training data often being more important for the performance of a system than architecture and training details. But collecting, processing and annotating real data at scale is difficult, expensive, and frequently raises additional privacy, fairness and legal concerns. Synthetic data is a powerful tool with the potential to address these shortcomings: 1) it is cheap 2) supports rich ground-truth annotations 3) offers full control over data and 4) can circumvent or mitigate problems regarding bias, privacy and licensing. Unfortunately, software tools for effective data generation are less mature than those for ar…
papers.summary: Open-source Python framework interfacing PyBullet and Blender to generate photo-realistic synthetic scenes with rich ground-truth annotations, scaling across thousands of machines. Demonstrated via 13 generated datasets spanning tasks from NeRF studies to optical-flow estimation.
papers.venue: 2022 IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR)

papers.title: TubeDETR: Spatio-Temporal Video Grounding with Transformers
papers.abstract: We consider the problem of localizing a spatio-temporal tube in a video corresponding to a given text query. This is a challenging task that requires the joint and efficient modeling of temporal, spatial and multi-modal interactions. To address this task, we propose TubeDETR, a transformer-based architecture inspired by the recent success of such models for text-conditioned object detection. Our model notably includes: (i) an efficient video and text encoder that models spatial multi-modal interactions over sparsely sampled frames and (ii) a space-time decoder that jointly performs spatio-temporal localization. We demonstrate the advantage of our proposed components through an extensive a…
papers.summary: Transformer architecture for spatio-temporal video grounding, localizing a space-time tube matching a text query, with an efficient video-text encoder over sparsely sampled frames and a space-time decoder for joint localization. Improves over the state of the art on VidSTG and HC-STVG.
papers.venue: 2022 IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR)

papers.title: Neural Mean Discrepancy for Efficient Out-of-Distribution Detection
papers.abstract: Various approaches have been proposed for out-of-distribution (OOD) detection by augmenting models, input examples, training sets, and optimization objectives. Deviating from existing work, we have a simple hypothesis that standard off-the-shelf models may already contain sufficient information about the training set distribution which can be leveraged for reliable OOD detection. Our empirical study on validating this hypothesis, which measures the model activation's mean for OOD and in-distribution (ID) minibatches, surprisingly finds that activation means of OOD mini-batches consistently deviate more from those of the training data. In addition, training data's activation means can be c…
papers.summary: Finds that activation means of out-of-distribution minibatches deviate more from training-data means than in-distribution ones. Proposes Neural Mean Discrepancy, comparing input and training neural means via a single forward pass plus a light classifier, outperforming state-of-the-art OOD detectors at lower cost.
papers.venue: 2022 IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR)

papers.title: SASIC: Stereo Image Compression with Latent Shifts and Stereo Attention
papers.abstract: We propose a learned method for stereo image compression that leverages the similarity of the left and right images in a stereo pair due to overlapping fields of view. The left image is compressed by a learned compression method based on an autoencoder with a hyperprior entropy model. The right image uses this information from the previously encoded left image in both the encoding and decoding stages. In particular, for the right image, we encode only the residual of its latent representation to the optimally shifted latent of the left image. On top of that, we also employ a stereo attention module to connect left and right images during decoding. The performance of the proposed method is…
papers.summary: Learned stereo image compression that encodes only the residual of the right image's latent relative to an optimally shifted left-image latent, adding a stereo-attention module at decoding. Outperforms prior stereo codecs on Cityscapes and InStereo2K with substantially smaller models.
papers.venue: 2022 IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR)

papers.title: Input-level Inductive Biases for 3D Reconstruction
papers.abstract: Much of the recent progress in 3D vision has been driven by the development of specialized architectures that incorporate geometrical inductive biases. In this paper we tackle 3D reconstruction using a domain agnostic architecture and study how to inject the same type of inductive biases directly as extra inputs to the model. This approach makes it possible to apply existing general models, such as Perceivers, on this rich domain, without the need for architectural changes, while simultaneously maintaining data efficiency of bespoke models. In particular we study how to encode cameras, projective ray incidence and epipolar geometry as model inputs, and demonstrate competitive multi-view d…
papers.summary: Injects geometric inductive biases (camera parameters, projective ray incidence, epipolar geometry) as extra inputs rather than architectural changes, letting domain-agnostic models like Perceivers do 3D reconstruction. Achieves competitive multi-view depth estimation while preserving the data efficiency of bespoke architectures.
papers.venue: 2022 IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR)

papers.title: Look for the Change: Learning Object States and State-Modifying Actions from Untrimmed Web Videos
papers.abstract: Human actions often induce changes of object states such as “cutting an apple”, “cleaning shoes” or “pouring coffee”. In this paper, we seek to temporally localize object states (e.g. “empty” and “full” cup) together with the corresponding state-modifying actions (“pouring coffee”) in long uncurated videos with minimal supervision. The contributions of this work are threefold. First, we develop a self-supervised model for jointly learning state-modifying actions together with the corresponding object states from an uncurated set of videos from the Internet. The model is self-supervised by the causal ordering signal, i.e. initialobject state → manipulating action → end state. Second, to co…
papers.summary: Self-supervised model that jointly learns object states and state-modifying actions from uncurated web videos, supervised by causal ordering (initial state then action then end state) plus noise-adaptive weighting. Releases a 2,600-hour dataset and improves action and object-state recognition.
papers.venue: 2022 IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR)

papers.title: More than Words: In-the-Wild Visually-Driven Prosody for Text-to-Speech
papers.abstract: In this paper we present VDTTS, a Visually-Driven Text-to-Speech model. Motivated by dubbing, VDTTS takes ad-vantage of video frames as an additional input alongside text, and generates speech that matches the video signal. We demonstrate how this allows VDTTS to, unlike plain TTS models, generate speech that not only has prosodic variations like natural pauses and pitch, but is also synchronized to the input video. Experimentally, we show our model produces well-synchronized outputs, approaching the video-speech synchronization quality of the ground-truth, on several challenging benchmarks including “in-the-wild” content from VoxCeleb2. Supplementary demo videos demonstrating video-speec…
papers.summary: VDTTS conditions text-to-speech on video frames as well as text, generating speech with natural prosody (pauses, pitch) that is synchronized to the input video, motivated by dubbing. Approaches ground-truth video-speech synchronization on in-the-wild benchmarks like VoxCeleb2.
papers.venue: 2022 IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR)

papers.title: The Emergence of Gender Associations in Child Language Development
papers.abstract: Gender associations have been a long-standing research topic in psychological and social sciences. Although it is known that children learn aspects of gender associations at a young age, it is not well understood how they might emerge through the course of development. We investigate whether gender associations, such as the association of dresses with women and bulldozers with men, are reflected in the linguistic communication of young children from ages 1-5. Drawing on recent methods from machine learning, we use word embeddings derived from large text corpora including news articles and web pages as a proxy for gender associations in society, and we compare those with the gender associa…
papers.summary: Compares gender associations in word embeddings from large corpora to those in caretaker and child speech across ages 1-5. Word-usage frequencies in 4,875 conversations correlate strongly with embedding-based gender associations, and these correlations weaken from the 1970s to the 1990s.

papers.title: Integrating Language Guidance into Vision-based Deep Metric Learning
papers.abstract: Deep Metric Learning (DML) proposes to learn metric spaces which encode semantic similarities as embedding space distances. These spaces should be transferable to classes beyond those seen during training. Commonly, DML methods task networks to solve contrastive ranking tasks defined over binary class assignments. However, such approaches ignore higher-level semantic relations between the actual classes. This causes learned embedding spaces to encode incomplete semantic context and misrepresent the semantic relation between classes, impacting the generalizability of the learned metric space. To tackle this issue, we propose a language guidance objective for visual similarity learning. Lev…
papers.summary: Adds a language-guidance objective to visual deep metric learning, using language embeddings of expert and pseudo class names to realign visual representation spaces with semantic relations. Yields model-agnostic, state-of-the-art improvements across standard metric-learning benchmarks.
papers.venue: 2022 IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR)

papers.title: Non-isotropy Regularization for Proxy-based Deep Metric Learning
papers.abstract: Deep Metric Learning (DML) aims to learn representation spaces on which semantic relations can simply be expressed through predefined distance metrics. Best performing approaches commonly leverage class proxies as sample stand-ins for better convergence and generalization. However, these proxy-methods solely optimize for sample-proxy distances. Given the inherent non-bijectiveness of used distance functions, this can induce locally isotropic sample distributions, leading to crucial semantic context being missed due to difficulties resolving local structures and intraclass relations between samples. To alleviate this problem, we propose non-isotropy regularization <tex xmlns:mml="http://ww…
papers.summary: Non-isotropy regularization for proxy-based deep metric learning uses normalizing flows to enforce unique, non-isotropic sample distributions around class proxies, helping embeddings capture local structure and intra-class relations. Achieves competitive results on CUB200-2011, Cars196, and Stanford Online Products while retaining fast convergence.
papers.venue: 2022 IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR)

papers.title: Robust fine-tuning of zero-shot models
papers.abstract: Large pre-trained models such as CLIP or ALIGN offer consistent accuracy across a range of data distributions when performing zero-shot inference (i.e., without fine-tuning on a specific dataset). Although existing fine-tuning methods substantially improve accuracy on a given target distribution, they often reduce robustness to distribution shifts. We address this tension by introducing a simple and effective method for improving robustness while fine-tuning: ensembling the weights of the zero-shot and fine-tuned models (WiSE-FT). Compared to standard fine-tuning, WiSE-FT provides large accuracy improvements under distribution shift, while preserving high accuracy on the target distributi…
papers.summary: Improves robustness of fine-tuned zero-shot models such as CLIP by ensembling the weights of the original and fine-tuned models. WiSE-FT raises accuracy under distribution shift by 4-6 points over prior work while also improving in-distribution accuracy, at no extra training or inference cost.
papers.venue: 2022 IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR)

papers.title: Mechanistic Interpretability, Variables, and the Importance of Interpretable Bases
papers.summary: Essay arguing that reverse-engineering neural networks hinges on identifying the right variables (features) and the basis they live in. Because features need not align with neurons, finding an interpretable basis, complicated by superposition and non-privileged bases, is framed as the central problem of mechanistic interpretability.

papers.title: Teaching models to express their uncertainty in words
papers.summary: Fine-tunes GPT-3 on a math task (CalibratedMath) to express its uncertainty in words, 'verbalized probability', rather than via logits. Shows the model produces reasonably calibrated confidence that generalizes under distribution shift, a first demonstration of calibrated natural-language uncertainty.

papers.title: Offline Meta-Reinforcement Learning for Industrial Insertion
papers.abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) can in principle let robots automatically adapt to new tasks, but current RL methods require a large number of trials to accomplish this. In this paper, we tackle rapid adaptation to new tasks through the framework of meta-learning, which utilizes past tasks to learn to adapt with a specific focus on industrial insertion tasks. Fast adaptation is crucial because prohibitively large number of on-robot trials will potentially damage hardware pieces. Additionally, effective adaptation is also feasible in that experience among different insertion applications can be largely leveraged by each other. In this setting, we address two specific challenges when applying m…
papers.summary: Offline meta-RL for robotic industrial insertion that replaces costly online meta-training with offline demonstrations and trials from prior tasks. Combines contextual meta-learning with online finetuning so similar tasks adapt instantly and dissimilar ones gradually, avoiding hardware-damaging trial counts.
papers.venue: 2022 International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA)

papers.title: Multiagent off-screen behavior prediction in football
papers.abstract: In multiagent worlds, several decision-making individuals interact while adhering to the dynamics constraints imposed by the environment. These interactions, combined with the potential stochasticity of the agents' dynamic behaviors, make such systems complex and interesting to study from a decision-making perspective. Significant research has been conducted on learning models for forward-direction estimation of agent behaviors, for example, pedestrian predictions used for collision-avoidance in self-driving cars. In many settings, only sporadic observations of agents may be available in a given trajectory sequence. In football, subsets of players may come in and out of view of broadcast…
papers.summary: The Graph Imputer combines graph networks and variational autoencoders with past and future observations to impute trajectories of unobserved, off-screen football players. Predicts the behavior of partially observable agents, validated against ground-truth match-tracking data.

papers.title: Few-Shot Keypoint Detection as Task Adaptation via Latent Embeddings
papers.abstract: Dense object tracking, the ability to localize specific object points with pixel-level accuracy, is an important computer vision task with numerous downstream applications in robotics. Existing approaches either compute dense keypoint embeddings in a single forward pass, meaning the model is trained to track everything at once, or allocate their full capacity to a sparse predefined set of points, trading generality for accuracy. In this paper we explore a middle ground based on the observation that the number of relevant points at a given time are typically relatively few, e.g. grasp points on a target object. Our main contribution is a novel architecture, inspired by few-shot task adapta…
papers.summary: Keypoint-tracking architecture that conditions a sparse-style network on a keypoint embedding indicating which point to track, blending dense-embedding generality with sparse-keypoint accuracy. Zero-shot transfers to new within-class object instances in a real-robot pick-and-place task.
papers.venue: 2022 International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA)

papers.title: Scaling Laws and Interpretability of Learning from Repeated Data
papers.abstract: Recent large language models have been trained on vast datasets, but also often on repeated data, either intentionally for the purpose of upweighting higher quality data, or unintentionally because data deduplication is not perfect and the model is exposed to repeated data at the sentence, paragraph, or document level. Some works have reported substantial negative performance effects of this repeated data. In this paper we attempt to study repeated data systematically and to understand its effects mechanistically. To do this, we train a family of models where most of the data is unique but a small fraction of it is repeated many times. We find a strong double descent phenomenon, in which…
papers.summary: Trains model families where a small data fraction is repeated many times, revealing a strong double-descent: repeating 0.1% of data 100x degrades an 800M model to 400M-model loss. Links the damage mechanistically to disrupted induction-head and copying circuits.

papers.title: Generating accurate assert statements for unit test cases using pretrained transformers
papers.abstract: Unit testing represents the foundational basis of the software testing pyramid, beneath integration and end-to-end testing. Automated software testing researchers have proposed a variety of techniques to assist developers in this time-consuming task.
papers.summary: Fine-tunes pretrained transformers to generate assert statements for unit test cases, aiming to improve the accuracy of automatically produced assertions relative to earlier automated test-generation techniques.

papers.title: From Dirichlet to Rubin: Optimistic Exploration in RL without Bonuses
papers.abstract: We propose the Bayes-UCBVI algorithm for reinforcement learning in tabular, stage-dependent, episodic Markov decision process: a natural extension of the Bayes-UCB algorithm by Kaufmann et al. (2012) for multi-armed bandits. Our method uses the quantile of a Q-value function posterior as upper confidence bound on the optimal Q-value function. For Bayes-UCBVI, we prove a regret bound of order $\widetilde{O}(\sqrt{H^3SAT})$ where $H$ is the length of one episode, $S$ is the number of states, $A$ the number of actions, $T$ the number of episodes, that matches the lower-bound of $Ω(\sqrt{H^3SAT})$ up to poly-$\log$ terms in $H,S,A,T$ for a large enough $T$. To the best of our knowledge, this…
papers.summary: Extends Bayes-UCB to tabular episodic RL: uses a quantile of the Q-value posterior as an upper confidence bound on the optimal Q-function. Proves near-optimal regret of order sqrt(H^3 S A T) without Bernstein-style bonuses, via a new anti-concentration bound for weighted Dirichlet sums.

papers.title: Learning to Answer Visual Questions From Web Videos
papers.abstract: Recent methods for visual question answering rely on large-scale annotated datasets. Manual annotation of questions and answers for videos, however, is tedious, expensive and prevents scalability. In this work, we propose to avoid manual annotation and generate a large-scale training dataset for video question answering making use of automatic cross-modal supervision. We leverage a question generation transformer trained on text data and use it to generate question-answer pairs from transcribed video narrations. Given narrated videos, we then automatically generate the HowToVQA69M dataset with 69M video-question-answer triplets. To handle the open vocabulary of diverse answers in this dat…
papers.summary: Generates HowToVQA69M, a 69M-triplet VideoQA dataset, by applying a question-generation transformer to transcribed narrations, then trains a video-question and answer transformer with a contrastive loss. Introduces zero-shot VideoQA and the low-bias iVQA benchmark, excelling on rare answers.
papers.venue: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence

papers.title: Learning Equilibria in Mean-Field Games: Introducing Mean-Field PSRO
papers.abstract: Recent advances in multiagent learning have seen the introduction of a family of algorithms that revolve around the population-based training method PSRO, showing convergence to Nash, correlated and coarse correlated equilibria. Notably, when the number of agents increases, learning best-responses becomes exponentially more difficult, and as such hampers PSRO training methods. The field of mean-field games provides an asymptotic solution to this problem when the considered games are anonymous-symmetric. Unfortunately, the mean-field approximation introduces non-linearities which prevent a straightforward adaptation of PSRO. Building upon optimization and adversarial regret minimization, t…
papers.summary: Adapts PSRO to mean-field games by replacing exact distribution computation with mean-field no-adversarial-regret learners or black-box optimization, enabling convergence to Nash, correlated, and coarse-correlated equilibria. Compressing temporal mixture weights greatly speeds empirical convergence.

papers.title: Sample-based Approximation of Nash in Large Many-Player Games via Gradient Descent
papers.abstract: Nash equilibrium is a central concept in game theory. Several Nash solvers exist, yet none scale to normal-form games with many actions and many players, especially those with payoff tensors too big to be stored in memory. In this work, we propose an approach that iteratively improves an approximation to a Nash equilibrium through joint play. It accomplishes this by tracing a previously established homotopy that defines a continuum of equilibria for the game regularized with decaying levels of entropy. This continuum asymptotically approaches the limiting logit equilibrium, proven by MKelvey and Palfrey (1995) to be unique in almost all games, thereby partially circumventing the well-know…
papers.summary: Approximates Nash equilibria in large many-player normal-form games by tracing an entropy-regularized homotopy toward the unique limiting logit equilibrium, minimizing average deviation incentive via stochastic gradient descent with adaptive payoff-tensor sampling. Scales to games where exact solvers cannot.

papers.title: D3C: Reducing the Price of Anarchy in Multi-Agent Learning
papers.abstract: In multiagent systems, the complex interaction of fixed incentives can lead agents to outcomes that are poor (inefficient) not only for the group, but also for each individual. Price of anarchy is a technical, game-theoretic definition that quantifies the inefficiency arising in these scenarios-- it compares the welfare that can be achieved through perfect coordination against that achieved by self-interested agents at a Nash equilibrium. We derive a differentiable, upper bound on a price of anarchy that agents can cheaply estimate during learning. Equipped with this estimator, agents can adjust their incentives in a way that improves the efficiency incurred at a Nash equilibrium. Agents…
papers.summary: Derives a differentiable upper bound on the price of anarchy that agents estimate during learning, then has them mix their reward with others' by descending its gradient (D3C), improving efficiency at Nash equilibria. Improves individual and group outcomes on social dilemmas like traffic networks.

papers.title: Reconstructing Training Data with Informed Adversaries
papers.abstract: Given access to a machine learning model, can an adversary reconstruct the model’s training data? This work studies this question from the lens of a powerful informed adversary who knows all the training data points except one. By instantiating concrete attacks, we show it is feasible to reconstruct the remaining data point in this stringent threat model. For convex models (e.g. logistic regression), reconstruction attacks are simple and can be derived in closed-form. For more general models (e.g. neural networks), we propose an attack strategy based on training a reconstructor network that receives as input the weights of the model under attack and produces as output the target data poin…
papers.summary: Studies an informed adversary who knows all training points but one and shows it can reconstruct the missing point: closed-form for convex models, and a trained reconstructor network for neural nets. Demonstrated on MNIST/CIFAR-10; quantifies the differential privacy needed to mitigate.
papers.venue: 2022 IEEE Symposium on Security and Privacy (SP)

papers.title: Towards Learning Universal Audio Representations
papers.abstract: The ability to learn universal audio representations that can solve diverse speech, music, and environment tasks can spur many applications that require general sound content understanding. In this work, we introduce a holistic audio representation evaluation suite (HARES) spanning 12 downstream tasks across audio domains and provide a thorough empirical study of recent sound representation learning systems on that benchmark. We discover that previous sound event classification or speech models do not generalize outside of their domains. We observe that more robust audio representations can be learned with the SimCLR objective; however, the model’s transferability depends heavily on the m…
papers.summary: Introduces HARES, a 12-task benchmark spanning speech, music, and environmental audio, and finds prior domain-specific models generalize poorly. Shows SimCLR-style objectives help but depend on architecture, and proposes a normalizer-free Slowfast NFNet reaching state-of-the-art across all audio domains.
papers.venue: ICASSP 2022 - 2022 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)

papers.title: The prospects and opportunities of protein structure prediction with AI
papers.summary: A Comment from DeepMind reflecting on AlphaFold's CASP14 breakthrough in protein structure prediction, discussing its impact across biology, the promising research directions it opens, and the limitations and challenges that remain.

papers.title: Normalizing flows for atomic solids
papers.abstract: Abstract We present a machine-learning approach, based on normalizing flows, for modelling atomic solids. Our model transforms an analytically tractable base distribution into the target solid without requiring ground-truth samples for training. We report Helmholtz free energy estimates for cubic and hexagonal ice modelled as monatomic water as well as for a truncated and shifted Lennard-Jones system, and find them to be in excellent agreement with literature values and with estimates from established baseline methods. We further investigate structural properties and show that the model samples are nearly indistinguishable from the ones obtained with molecular dynamics. Our results thus d…
papers.summary: Uses normalizing flows to model atomic solids, transforming a tractable base distribution into the target crystal without ground-truth samples. Yields Helmholtz free-energy estimates for ice and a Lennard-Jones system matching literature and molecular-dynamics baselines, with samples nearly indistinguishable from MD.

papers.title: Machine learning and health need better values
papers.abstract: Health care is a human process that generates data from human lives, as well as the care they receive. Machine learning has worked in health to bring new technology into this sociotechnical environment, using data to support a vision of healthier living for everyone. Interdisciplinary fields of research like machine learning for health bring different values and judgements together, requiring that those value choices be deliberate and measured. More than just abstract ideas, our values are the basis upon which we choose our research topics, set up research collaborations, execute our research methodologies, make assessments of scientific and technical correctness, proceed to product devel…
papers.summary: A perspective arguing that machine learning for health must make its value choices explicit, since values shape research topics, methods, deployment, and policy. Highlights areas needing attention to support healthier lives while minimizing harm.

papers.title: Deep learning, reinforcement learning, and world models
papers.abstract: Deep learning (DL) and reinforcement learning (RL) methods seem to be a part of indispensable factors to achieve human-level or super-human AI systems. On the other hand, both DL and RL have strong connections with our brain functions and with neuroscientific findings. In this review, we summarize talks and discussions in the "Deep Learning and Reinforcement Learning" session of the symposium, International Symposium on Artificial Intelligence and Brain Science. In this session, we discussed whether we can achieve comprehensive understanding of human intelligence based on the recent advances of deep learning and reinforcement learning algorithms. Speakers contributed to provide talks abou…
papers.summary: A symposium review summarizing recent deep learning, reinforcement learning, and world-model research and its connections to neuroscience, discussing which of these technologies might be key to reaching human-level intelligence.

papers.title: Clinical Target Volume Automatic Segmentation Based on Lymph Node Stations for Lung Cancer with Bulky Lump Lymph Nodes
papers.abstract: Abstract The lack of standardized delineation of lymph node station in lung cancer radiotherapy leads to nonstandard clinical target volume (CTV) contouring, especially in patients with bulky lump gross target volume lymph nodes (GTVnd). Therefore, this study defines lymph node region boundaries in radiotherapy for lung cancer and automatically contours lymph node stations based on the International Association for the Study of Lung Cancer (IASLC) lymph node map. Computed tomography (CT) scans of 200 patients with small cell lung cancer are collected. The lymph node zone boundaries are defined based on the IASLC lymph node map, with adjustments to meet radiotherapy requirements. Contours…
papers.summary: Defines lung-cancer lymph-node-station boundaries from the IASLC map and trains DiUNet to automatically delineate clinical target volumes station-by-station, incorporating bulky gross-tumor lymph nodes. Reports improved accuracy, consistency, and efficiency for CTV contouring in radiotherapy planning.

papers.title: Symmetry-Based Representations for Artificial and Biological General Intelligence
papers.abstract: Biological intelligence is remarkable in its ability to produce complex behavior in many diverse situations through data efficient, generalizable, and transferable skill acquisition. It is believed that learning "good" sensory representations is important for enabling this, however there is little agreement as to what a good representation should look like. In this review article we are going to argue that symmetry transformations are a fundamental principle that can guide our search for what makes a good representation. The idea that there exist transformations (symmetries) that affect some aspects of the system but not others, and their relationship to conserved quantities has become ce…
papers.summary: A review arguing that symmetry transformations are a unifying principle for what makes a 'good' representation, drawing parallels across physics, machine learning, and neuroscience, where symmetry-aware methods yield more data-efficient and generalizable learning.

papers.title: Measuring Goodhart’s law
papers.abstract: Goodhart’s law famously says: “When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure.” Although originally from economics, it’s something we have to grapple with at OpenAI when figuring out how to optimize objectives that are difficult or costly to measure.
papers.summary: Examines reward-model overoptimization: optimizing a learned proxy objective eventually diverges from the true goal, an instance of Goodhart's law. Measures how this gap behaves, informing how to optimize objectives that are costly or impossible to measure directly.

papers.title: Hierarchical text-conditional image generation with CLIP latents
papers.summary: DALL-E 2: a two-stage text-to-image model that first uses a prior to produce a CLIP image embedding from a caption, then a diffusion decoder to generate the image. Improves diversity and enables image variations and language-guided editing.

papers.title: Training a Helpful and Harmless Assistant with Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback
papers.abstract: We apply preference modeling and reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) to finetune language models to act as helpful and harmless assistants. We find this alignment training improves performance on almost all NLP evaluations, and is fully compatible with training for specialized skills such as python coding and summarization. We explore an iterated online mode of training, where preference models and RL policies are updated on a weekly cadence with fresh human feedback data, efficiently improving our datasets and models. Finally, we investigate the robustness of RLHF training, and identify a roughly linear relation between the RL reward and the square root of the KL divergence…
papers.summary: Applies preference modeling and RLHF to fine-tune language models into helpful, harmless assistants. Alignment improves nearly all NLP evaluations and is compatible with skills like coding; introduces iterated online training and finds reward scales with the square root of policy-initialization KL divergence.

papers.title: Can neurogenesis act as a neural regularizer?
papers.abstract: Abstract New neurons are continuously generated in the subgranular zone of the dentate gyrus throughout adulthood. These new neurons gradually integrate into hippocampal circuits, forming new naïve synapses. Viewed from this perspective, these new neurons may represent a significant source of ‘wiring’ noise in hippocampal networks. In machine learning, such noise injection is commonly used as a regularization technique. Regularization techniques help prevent overfitting training data, and allow models to generalize learning to new, unseen data. Using a computational modeling approach, here we ask whether a neurogenesis-like process similarly acts as a regularizer, facilitating generalizat…
papers.summary: Uses computational modeling to test whether adult hippocampal neurogenesis acts like a regularizer. Modeling neurogenesis as periodic weight reinitialization in a CNN trained on CIFAR-10 improves generalization, matching or exceeding dropout, weight decay, and neural noise.

papers.title: Exploration in deep reinforcement learning: A survey
papers.summary: A survey of exploration methods in deep reinforcement learning, organizing the main approaches and intrinsic-motivation techniques and discussing open challenges in balancing exploration and exploitation in high-dimensional environments.

papers.title: Marginalized Operators for Off-policy Reinforcement Learning
papers.abstract: In this work, we propose marginalized operators, a new class of off-policy evaluation operators for reinforcement learning. Marginalized operators strictly generalize generic multi-step operators, such as Retrace, as special cases. Marginalized operators also suggest a form of sample-based estimates with potential variance reduction, compared to sample-based estimates of the original multi-step operators. We show that the estimates for marginalized operators can be computed in a scalable way, which also generalizes prior results on marginalized importance sampling as special cases. Finally, we empirically demonstrate that marginalized operators provide performance gains to off-policy eval…
papers.summary: Introduces marginalized operators, a class of off-policy evaluation operators that strictly generalize multi-step operators like Retrace and admit scalable, variance-reduced sample estimates. Empirically improves off-policy evaluation and downstream policy optimization.

papers.title: The Challenge of Value Alignment
papers.abstract: Abstract This chapter addresses the question of how to align artificial intelligence (AI) systems with human values and situates it within a wider body of thought regarding technology and value. Far from existing in a vacuum, there has long been an interest in the ability of technology to ‘lock-in’ different value systems. There has also been considerable thought about how to align technologies with specific social values, including through participatory design processes. In this chapter, we look more closely at the question of AI alignment and suggest that the power and autonomy of AI systems gives rise to opportunities and challenges in the domain of value that have not been encountered…
papers.summary: A book chapter on aligning AI with human values, situating it within longstanding thought on technology 'locking in' values. Argues AI's power and autonomy raise novel challenges and calls for 'social value alignment' to the plurality of values held across groups.

papers.title: Quantifying the effects of environment and population diversity in multi-agent reinforcement learning
papers.abstract: Abstract Generalization is a major challenge for multi-agent reinforcement learning. How well does an agent perform when placed in novel environments and in interactions with new co-players? In this paper, we investigate and quantify the relationship between generalization and diversity in the multi-agent domain. Across the range of multi-agent environments considered here, procedurally generating training levels significantly improves agent performance on held-out levels. However, agent performance on the specific levels used in training sometimes declines as a result. To better understand the effects of co-player variation, our experiments introduce a new environment-agnostic measure of…
papers.summary: Quantifies how environment and co-player diversity affect generalization in multi-agent RL. Procedurally generated training levels improve held-out performance (sometimes hurting training levels); a new behavioral-diversity measure shows larger populations and intrinsic motivation increase diversity, strengthening agents in some cases.

papers.title: Sequential changepoint detection in neural networks with checkpoints
papers.summary: Proposes online changepoint detection with simultaneous model learning for highly parametrized models like deep nets, using sequential generalized likelihood-ratio tests over checkpoints with a bounded Type I error. Improves continual learning with unknown task boundaries over Bayesian baselines.

papers.title: Restoring and attributing ancient texts using deep neural networks
papers.abstract: . However, over the centuries, many inscriptions have been damaged to the point of illegibility, transported far from their original location and their date of writing is steeped in uncertainty. Here we present Ithaca, a deep neural network for the textual restoration, geographical attribution and chronological attribution of ancient Greek inscriptions. Ithaca is designed to assist and expand the historian's workflow. The architecture of Ithaca focuses on collaboration, decision support and interpretability. While Ithaca alone achieves 62% accuracy when restoring damaged texts, the use of Ithaca by historians improved their accuracy from 25% to 72%, confirming the synergistic effect of th…
papers.summary: Ithaca, a deep network for restoring, geographically attributing, and dating damaged ancient Greek inscriptions. Alone it restores text at 62% accuracy, but historians using it improve from 25% to 72%; it dates inscriptions within 30 years and aids historical debates.

papers.title: Training language models to follow instructions with human feedback
papers.abstract: Making language models bigger does not inherently make them better at following a user's intent. For example, large language models can generate outputs that are untruthful, toxic, or simply not helpful to the user. In other words, these models are not aligned with their users. In this paper, we show an avenue for aligning language models with user intent on a wide range of tasks by fine-tuning with human feedback. Starting with a set of labeler-written prompts and prompts submitted through the OpenAI API, we collect a dataset of labeler demonstrations of the desired model behavior, which we use to fine-tune GPT-3 using supervised learning. We then collect a dataset of rankings of model o…
papers.summary: InstructGPT: fine-tunes GPT-3 with labeler demonstrations and RLHF to follow user intent. A 1.3B InstructGPT is preferred to the 175B GPT-3 despite 100x fewer parameters, with improved truthfulness and reduced toxicity and minimal regressions on public NLP datasets.

papers.title: A research agenda for assessing the economic impacts of code generation models
papers.summary: Outlines a research agenda for measuring the economic impacts of code-generation models such as Codex, identifying key open questions and methodological approaches for studying effects on developer productivity, labor, and the broader software economy.

papers.title: In-Context Learning and Induction Heads
papers.abstract: "Induction heads"are attention heads that implement a simple algorithm to complete token sequences like [A][B] ... [A] ->[B]. In this work, we present preliminary and indirect evidence for a hypothesis that induction heads might constitute the mechanism for the majority of all"in-context learning"in large transformer models (i.e. decreasing loss at increasing token indices). We find that induction heads develop at precisely the same point as a sudden sharp increase in in-context learning ability, visible as a bump in the training loss. We present six complementary lines of evidence, arguing that induction heads may be the mechanistic source of general in-context learning in transformer mo…
papers.summary: Presents evidence that induction heads, attention heads completing patterns like [A][B]...[A]->[B], are the primary mechanism of in-context learning in transformers. They emerge at a sharp phase change coinciding with an in-context-learning bump in training loss, supported by six lines of evidence.

papers.title: Structure of the decoy module of human glycoprotein 2 and uromodulin and its interaction with bacterial adhesin FimH
papers.abstract: Glycoprotein 2 (GP2) and uromodulin (UMOD) filaments protect against gastrointestinal and urinary tract infections by acting as decoys for bacterial fimbrial lectin FimH. By combining AlphaFold2 predictions with X-ray crystallography and cryo-EM, we show that these proteins contain a bipartite decoy module whose new fold presents the high-mannose glycan recognized by FimH. The structure rationalizes UMOD mutations associated with kidney diseases and visualizes a key epitope implicated in cast nephropathy.
papers.summary: Combines AlphaFold2 predictions with X-ray crystallography and cryo-EM to solve the bipartite decoy-module structure of human GP2 and uromodulin, revealing how its fold presents the high-mannose glycan recognized by bacterial adhesin FimH and rationalizing disease-associated mutations.

papers.title: Meta-control of social learning strategies
papers.abstract: Social learning, copying other's behavior without actual experience, offers a cost-effective means of knowledge acquisition. However, it raises the fundamental question of which individuals have reliable information: successful individuals versus the majority. The former and the latter are known respectively as success-based and conformist social learning strategies. We show here that while the success-based strategy fully exploits the benign environment of low uncertainly, it fails in uncertain environments. On the other hand, the conformist strategy can effectively mitigate this adverse effect. Based on these findings, we hypothesized that meta-control of individual and social learning…
papers.summary: Computational study comparing success-based and conformist social-learning strategies. Success-based learning excels under low uncertainty but fails under high uncertainty, while conformity mitigates this; meta-control arbitrating individual and social learning gives sample-efficient learning in volatile, uncertain environments.

papers.title: Retrieval-Augmented Reinforcement Learning
papers.abstract: Most deep reinforcement learning (RL) algorithms distill experience into parametric behavior policies or value functions via gradient updates. While effective, this approach has several disadvantages: (1) it is computationally expensive, (2) it can take many updates to integrate experiences into the parametric model, (3) experiences that are not fully integrated do not appropriately influence the agent's behavior, and (4) behavior is limited by the capacity of the model. In this paper we explore an alternative paradigm in which we train a network to map a dataset of past experiences to optimal behavior. Specifically, we augment an RL agent with a retrieval process (parameterized as a neur…
papers.summary: Augments RL agents with a learned retrieval process that reads a dataset of past experiences, letting behavior condition on the whole dataset rather than the current state alone. Added to offline DQN and online R2D2, it reduces task interference and speeds Atari learning.

papers.title: Magnetic control of tokamak plasmas through deep reinforcement learning
papers.abstract: Abstract Nuclear fusion using magnetic confinement, in particular in the tokamak configuration, is a promising path towards sustainable energy. A core challenge is to shape and maintain a high-temperature plasma within the tokamak vessel. This requires high-dimensional, high-frequency, closed-loop control using magnetic actuator coils, further complicated by the diverse requirements across a wide range of plasma configurations. In this work, we introduce a previously undescribed architecture for tokamak magnetic controller design that autonomously learns to command the full set of control coils. This architecture meets control objectives specified at a high level, at the same time satisfy…
papers.summary: Demonstrates deep reinforcement learning for tokamak plasma control: a single agent learns to command all control coils to achieve high-level shape and position targets. Validated on the TCV tokamak across diverse configurations, including elongated, negative-triangularity, snowflake, and simultaneous 'droplet' plasmas.

papers.title: Predictability and Surprise in Large Generative Models
papers.abstract: Large-scale pre-training has recently emerged as a technique for creating capable, general-purpose, generative models such as GPT-3, Megatron-Turing NLG, Gopher, and many others. In this paper, we highlight a counterintuitive property of such models and discuss the policy implications of this property. Namely, these generative models have a paradoxical combination of predictable loss on a broad training distribution (as embodied in their ”scaling laws”), and unpredictable specific capabilities, inputs, and outputs. We believe that the high-level predictability and appearance of useful capabilities drives rapid development of such models, while the unpredictable qualities make it difficult…
papers.summary: Argues large generative models combine predictable aggregate loss (scaling laws) with unpredictable specific capabilities and outputs, a combination that drives rapid deployment yet complicates anticipating harms. Discusses policy implications and proposes interventions for the AI community.

papers.title: The Brain-Computer Metaphor Debate Is Useless: A Matter of Semantics
papers.abstract: It is commonly assumed that usage of the word “computer” in the brain sciences reflects a metaphor. However, there is no single definition of the word “computer” in use. In fact, based on the usage of the word “computer” in computer science, a computer is merely some physical machinery that can in theory compute any computable function. According to this definition the brain is literally a computer; there is no metaphor. But, this deviates from how the word “computer” is used in other academic disciplines. According to the definition used outside of computer science, “computers” are human-made devices that engage in sequential processing of inputs to produce outputs. According to this def…
papers.summary: A perspective arguing the brain-computer metaphor debate is merely a semantic disagreement: under computer-science definitions the brain literally is a computer, while under everyday definitions it is not. Recommends researchers simply state which definition they use.

papers.title: Tackling Climate Change with Machine Learning
papers.abstract: Climate change is one of the greatest challenges facing humanity, and we, as machine learning experts, may wonder how we can help. Here we describe how machine learning can be a powerful tool in reducing greenhouse gas emissions and helping society adapt to a changing climate. From smart grids to disaster management, we identify high impact problems where existing gaps can be filled by machine learning, in collaboration with other fields. Our recommendations encompass exciting research questions as well as promising business opportunities. We call on the machine learning community to join the global effort against climate change.
papers.summary: A wide-ranging survey identifying high-impact opportunities for machine learning to mitigate and adapt to climate change, from smart grids to disaster management, pairing research questions with applications and calling the ML community to action.

papers.title: Unified Scaling Laws for Routed Language Models
papers.abstract: The performance of a language model has been shown to be effectively modeled as a power-law in its parameter count. Here we study the scaling behaviors of Routing Networks: architectures that conditionally use only a subset of their parameters while processing an input. For these models, parameter count and computational requirement form two independent axes along which an increase leads to better performance. In this work we derive and justify scaling laws defined on these two variables which generalize those known for standard language models and describe the performance of a wide range of routing architectures trained via three different techniques. Afterwards we provide two applicatio…
papers.summary: Derives scaling laws for routed (sparse mixture-of-experts) language models, treating parameter count and compute as independent axes. Validated across three routing techniques and five orders of magnitude, it introduces an Effective Parameter Count that unifies routed and dense model scaling.

papers.title: Solving (some) formal math olympiad problems
papers.abstract: We built a neural theorem prover for Lean that learned to solve a variety of challenging high-school olympiad problems, including problems from the AMC12 and AIME competitions, as well as two problems adapted from the IMO.
papers.summary: A neural theorem prover for Lean trained to solve formal mathematics, including high-school olympiad problems from AMC12 and AIME and two problems adapted from the IMO, advancing automated formal reasoning.

papers.title: Sebastian Dietz’s Contribution to the Discussion of ‘Gaussian Differential Privacy’ by Dong et al.
papers.summary: A brief invited discussion contribution commenting on Dong et al.'s 'Gaussian Differential Privacy', part of the journal's published discussion of that work on the f-differential-privacy and Gaussian-DP framework for quantifying privacy guarantees.
papers.venue: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society Series B (Statistical Methodology)

papers.title: Scaling Gaussian Process Optimization by Evaluating a Few Unique Candidates Multiple Times
papers.abstract: Computing a Gaussian process (GP) posterior has a computational cost cubical in the number of historical points. A reformulation of the same GP posterior highlights that this complexity mainly depends on how many \emph{unique} historical points are considered. This can have important implication in active learning settings, where the set of historical points is constructed sequentially by the learner. We show that sequential black-box optimization based on GPs (GP-Opt) can be made efficient by sticking to a candidate solution for multiple evaluation steps and switch only when necessary. Limiting the number of switches also limits the number of unique points in the history of the GP. Thus,…
papers.summary: Shows Gaussian-process black-box optimization can be made cheaper by reusing a candidate point across multiple evaluations, limiting unique history points so posteriors compute efficiently. Modified GP-UCB and GP-EI variants retain no-regret guarantees while cutting runtime and memory, helping high-switch-cost settings.

papers.title: Aligning language models to follow instructions
papers.summary: OpenAI's InstructGPT: fine-tunes GPT-3 with reinforcement learning from human feedback to better follow user intent. Human raters prefer the 1.3B InstructGPT over the 175B GPT-3, with gains in truthfulness and reductions in toxic generations.

papers.title: Text and code embeddings by contrastive pre-training
papers.summary: OpenAI's contrastive pre-training (cpt-text, cpt-code) trains embedding models on large unlabeled text and code using a contrastive objective over paired/neighboring data, producing strong embeddings for linear-probe classification, semantic search, and code search across multiple retrieval benchmarks.

papers.title: Reimagining chess with AlphaZero
papers.abstract: AI is driving the next evolution of chess, giving players a glimpse into the game's future.
papers.summary: Uses AlphaZero self-play to study nine chess variants with altered rules (e.g., no-castling, torpedo), assessing how rule changes affect game balance, piece values, and playing style — turning a game engine into a tool for exploring chess's design space.

papers.title: Spurious normativity enhances learning of compliance and enforcement behavior in artificial agents
papers.abstract: How do societies learn and maintain social norms? Here we use multiagent reinforcement learning to investigate the learning dynamics of enforcement and compliance behaviors. Artificial agents populate a foraging environment and need to learn to avoid a poisonous berry. Agents learn to avoid eating poisonous berries better when doing so is taboo, meaning the behavior is punished by other agents. The taboo helps overcome a credit assignment problem in discovering delayed health effects. Critically, introducing an additional taboo, which results in punishment for eating a harmless berry, further improves overall returns. This "silly rule" counterintuitively has a positive effect because it g…
papers.summary: Multi-agent RL study of social norms: agents learn to avoid a poisonous berry better when violations are punished (a taboo), which eases credit assignment for delayed harm. Adding a 'silly rule' punishing a harmless act further improves returns by giving extra enforcement practice.
papers.venue: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences

papers.title: Properties and Comparison of Some Kriging Sub-model Aggregation Methods
papers.summary: Examines and compares the statistical properties of several methods for aggregating local Kriging (Gaussian-process) sub-models fit on data subsets, a route to scaling GP regression to large geostatistical datasets.

papers.title: Protein structure predictions to atomic accuracy with AlphaFold
papers.summary: Describes AlphaFold's deep-learning system for predicting protein 3D structure directly from sequence at near-experimental atomic accuracy, recapping the CASP14 breakthrough and the model's design and discussing its impact on structural biology.

papers.title: AlignSDF: Pose-Aligned Signed Distance Fields for Hand-Object Reconstruction
papers.summary: Reconstructs hand and object meshes from a single image by predicting signed distance fields in pose-normalized coordinate frames: it first estimates hand and object poses, then aligns SDF queries to those poses, disentangling pose from shape and improving joint hand-object reconstruction.

papers.title: Transformer Grammars: Augmenting Transformer Language Models with Syntactic Inductive Biases at Scale
papers.abstract: Abstract We introduce Transformer Grammars (TGs), a novel class of Transformer language models that combine (i) the expressive power, scalability, and strong performance of Transformers and (ii) recursive syntactic compositions, which here are implemented through a special attention mask and deterministic transformation of the linearized tree. We find that TGs outperform various strong baselines on sentence-level language modeling perplexity, as well as on multiple syntax-sensitive language modeling evaluation metrics. Additionally, we find that the recursive syntactic composition bottleneck which represents each sentence as a single vector harms perplexity on document-level language mode…
papers.summary: Transformer Grammars inject recursive syntactic composition into Transformers via a special attention mask over linearized parse trees. They improve sentence-level perplexity and syntax-sensitive metrics, but the single-vector syntactic bottleneck hurts document-level modeling, suggesting a separate long-range memory is needed.
papers.venue: Transactions of the Association for Computational Linguistics

papers.title: 3D-Beacons: decreasing the gap between protein sequences and structures through a federated network of protein structure data resources
papers.abstract: While scientists can often infer the biological function of proteins from their 3-dimensional quaternary structures, the gap between the number of known protein sequences and their experimentally determined structures keeps increasing. A potential solution to this problem is presented by ever more sophisticated computational protein modeling approaches. While often powerful on their own, most methods have strengths and weaknesses. Therefore, it benefits researchers to examine models from various model providers and perform comparative analysis to identify what models can best address their specific use cases. To make data from a large array of model providers more easily accessible to the…
papers.summary: 3D-Beacons is a collaborative federated network providing unified access to experimental and predicted protein structures from many providers (including the PDB), letting researchers collate coordinates and metadata and compare models across sources for their use cases.

papers.title: What Makes Fiat–Shamir zkSNARKs (Updatable SRS) Simulation Extractable?
papers.summary: Cryptography paper analyzing when Fiat–Shamir-transformed zk-SNARKs with an updatable structured reference string achieve simulation extractability, giving conditions and proofs for this stronger security property.

papers.title: Relational Memory-Augmented Language Models
papers.abstract: Abstract We present a memory-augmented approach to condition an autoregressive language model on a knowledge graph. We represent the graph as a collection of relation triples and retrieve relevant relations for a given context to improve text generation. Experiments on WikiText-103, WMT19, and enwik8 English datasets demonstrate that our approach produces a better language model in terms of perplexity and bits per character. We also show that relational memory improves coherence, is complementary to token-based memory, and enables causal interventions. Our model provides a simple yet effective way to combine an autoregressive language model and a knowledge graph for more coherent and logi…
papers.summary: Augments an autoregressive language model with a memory of knowledge-graph relation triples, retrieving relevant relations for the current context. Improves perplexity/bits-per-character on WikiText-103, WMT19, and enwik8, boosts coherence, complements token-based memory, and enables causal interventions.
papers.venue: Transactions of the Association for Computational Linguistics

papers.title: Morphology-Preserving Autoregressive 3D Generative Modelling of the Brain
papers.summary: Presents an autoregressive generative model for synthesizing 3D brain images that preserves morphological structure, aimed at producing realistic, anatomically faithful volumetric brain scans.

papers.title: Control Prefixes for Parameter-Efficient Text Generation
papers.abstract: Prefix-tuning is a parameter-efficient and powerful technique for adapting a pre-trained language model to a downstream application. However, it uses the same dataset-level tuned set of parameters for all examples in the dataset. We extend the framework with a dynamic method, Control Prefixes, which allows for the effective inclusion of input-dependent information, thereby demonstrating how prefix-tuning can be used for controlled text generation tasks. The method incorporates attribute-level learnable representations into different layers of a pre-trained Transformer, enabling the generated text to be guided in a particular direction. We provide a systematic evaluation of the technique a…
papers.summary: Extends prefix-tuning with Control Prefixes: input-dependent, attribute-level learnable representations inserted across Transformer layers for controllable generation. Using 0.1–2% extra parameters, it can beat full fine-tuning, reaching state-of-the-art on data-to-text datasets including WebNLG.

papers.title: Counting Vampires: From Univariate Sumcheck to Updatable ZK-SNARK
papers.summary: Cryptography paper constructing an efficient updatable zero-knowledge SNARK from a univariate sumcheck argument, contributing techniques for proofs in the updatable structured-reference-string setting.

papers.title: Latent Space Smoothing for Individually Fair Representations
papers.summary: Proposes learning individually fair representations by smoothing in the latent space of a generative model, enabling provable certification that similar individuals (under a learned similarity) receive similar representations.

papers.title: Object Discovery and Representation Networks
papers.summary: Odin is a self-supervised learning method that discovers image structure on its own, coupling an object-discovery network producing unsupervised segmentations with a representation network. It reaches state-of-the-art transfer for COCO detection/segmentation, PASCAL/Cityscapes segmentation, and DAVIS video segmentation.

papers.title: Vision-Language Pretraining: Current Trends and the Future
papers.abstract: In the last few years, there has been an increased interest in building multimodal (vision-language) models that are pretrained on larger but noisier datasets where the two modalities (e.g., image and text) loosely correspond to each other (e.g., Lu et al., 2019; Radford et al., 2021). Given a task (such as visual question answering), these models are then often fine-tuned on task-specific supervised datasets. (e.g., Lu et al., 2019; Chen et al.,2020; Tan and Bansal, 2019; Li et al., 2020a,b). In addition to the larger pretraining datasets, the transformer architecture (Vaswani et al., 2017) and in particular self-attention applied to two modalities are responsible for the impressive perf…
papers.summary: ACL tutorial surveying vision-language pretraining: image-text datasets and benchmarks, transformer-based model families with their strengths and weaknesses, and the limits of purely statistical learning — motivating alternatives such as causal representation learning.

papers.title: Uncertainty for Safe Utilization of Machine Learning in Medical Imaging
papers.summary: A workshop proceedings collecting methods for uncertainty quantification and reliability in machine learning for medical imaging, aimed at calibrated, trustworthy predictions for safe clinical deployment.

papers.title: Publishing fast and slow: A path toward generalizability in psychology and AI
papers.abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) shares many generalizability challenges with psychology. But the fields publish differently. AI publishes fast, through rapid preprint sharing and conference publications. Psychology publishes more slowly, but creates integrative reviews and meta-analyses. We discuss the complementary advantages of each strategy, and suggest that incorporating both types of strategies could lead to more generalizable research in both fields.
papers.summary: Compares publishing cultures: AI publishes rapidly via preprints and conferences, while psychology publishes slowly through integrative reviews and meta-analyses. Argues combining both approaches could yield more generalizable research across the two fields.

papers.title: What is the simplest model that can account for high-fidelity imitation?
papers.abstract: What inductive biases must be incorporated into multi-agent artificial intelligence models to get them to capture high-fidelity imitation? We think very little is needed. In the right environments, both instrumental- and ritual-stance imitation can emerge from generic learning mechanisms operating on non-deliberative decision architectures. In this view, imitation emerges from trial-and-error learning and does not require explicit deliberation.
papers.summary: Commentary arguing high-fidelity imitation in multi-agent AI needs few inductive biases: in suitable environments, both instrumental- and ritual-stance imitation can emerge from generic trial-and-error learning on non-deliberative architectures, without explicit deliberation.

papers.title: Continual Reinforcement Learning with Multi-Timescale Successor Features
papers.summary: Proposes computing successor features over multiple timescales (discount factors) to support continual reinforcement learning, aiming to improve transfer and adaptation as tasks change over time.

papers.title: Reward-Punishment Symmetric Universal Intelligence
papers.summary: Extends the Legg-Hutter universal-intelligence framework to include punishments, arguing an agent's intelligence can be negative. Shows that under certain Kolmogorov-complexity symmetries of the universal Turing machine the measure is symmetric about zero, so reward-ignoring agents score zero.

papers.title: Learning agents that acquire representations of social groups
papers.abstract: Humans are learning agents that acquire social group representations from experience. Here, we discuss how to construct artificial agents capable of this feat. One approach, based on deep reinforcement learning, allows the necessary representations to self-organize. This minimizes the need for hand-engineering, improving robustness and scalability. It also enables "virtual neuroscience" research on the learned representations.
papers.summary: Commentary arguing that deep reinforcement learning agents can self-organize representations of social groups from experience, minimizing hand-engineering to improve robustness and scalability while enabling 'virtual neuroscience' study of the learned representations.

papers.title: Analysis of Aromatic Substances in Different Flavored Cigarette Paper by Using Headspace-Gas Chromatography-Ion Mobility Spec-Trometry (Hs-Gc-Ims)
papers.summary: Analytical-chemistry study applying headspace gas chromatography–ion mobility spectrometry (HS-GC-IMS) to profile aromatic compounds across differently flavored cigarette papers.

papers.title: Utility of Equivariant Message Passing in Cortical Mesh Segmentation
papers.summary: Investigates whether equivariant message-passing graph neural networks improve segmentation of cortical surface meshes, assessing the benefit of geometric equivariance for parcellating brain-surface data.

papers.title: Spatially-embedded Recurrent Neural Networks: Bridging common structural and functional findings in neuroscience, including small-worldness, functional clustering in space and mixed selectivity
papers.summary: Spatially embedded RNNs place units in a physical space and penalize long-range communication during training. Under this constraint the networks spontaneously develop neuroscience-like properties — small-world topology, spatial functional clustering, and mixed selectivity — linking structural and functional brain findings.

papers.title: Analysis of Volatile Substances in Different Flavored Cigarette Paper by Using Headspace-Gas Chromatography-Ion Mobility Spectrometry (HS-GC-IMS) Combined with Chemometrics
papers.summary: Analytical-chemistry study using headspace gas chromatography–ion mobility spectrometry (HS-GC-IMS) with chemometric methods to profile and distinguish volatile compounds across differently flavored cigarette papers.

papers.title: A smile is all you need: predicting limiting activity coefficients from SMILES with natural language processing
papers.abstract: SPT is a natural language processing model that predicts limiting activity coefficients from SMILES. High accuracy is achieved by pre-training the model on millions of synthetic data points and fine-tuning the model on limited experimental data.
papers.summary: SPT, a natural-language-processing model, predicts limiting activity coefficients of mixtures directly from SMILES strings. Pre-training on millions of synthetic data points then fine-tuning on limited experimental data yields high accuracy for this thermodynamic property.

papers.title: Hierarchical Transformers Are More Efficient Language Models
papers.abstract: Transformer models yield impressive results on many NLP and sequence modeling tasks. Remarkably, Transformers can handle long sequences, which allows them to produce long coherent outputs: entire paragraphs produced by GPT-3 or well-structured images produced by DALL-E. These large language models are impressive but also very inefficient and costly, which limits their applications and accessibility. We postulate that having an explicit hierarchical architecture is the key to Transformers that efficiently handle long sequences. To verify this claim, we first study different ways to downsample and upsample activations in Transformers so as to make them hierarchical. We use the best performi…
papers.summary: Introduces Hourglass, a hierarchical Transformer that downsamples then upsamples activations to handle long sequences efficiently. It matches baseline Transformer quality at lower compute, sets state-of-the-art on ImageNet32 autoregressive generation, and improves enwik8 language-modeling efficiency.
papers.venue: Findings of the Association for Computational Linguistics: NAACL 2022

papers.title: WebGPT: Improving the factual accuracy of language models through web browsing
papers.abstract: We’ve fine-tuned GPT-3 to more accurately answer open-ended questions using a text-based web browser.
