Our core contribution is a unified conceptual and mathematical framework for the study of algorithms in this regime. This analysis reveals that for naive approaches, the possibility of erroneous value overestimation leads to a difficult-to-satisfy requirement: in order to guarantee that we select a policy which is near-optimal, we may need the dataset to be informative of the value of every policy. To avoid this, algorithms can follow the pessimism principle, which states that we should choose the policy which acts optimally in the worst possible world. We show why pessimistic algorithms…
papers.summary: Develops a unified framework for worst-case guarantees in offline (fixed-dataset) policy optimization, showing naive methods risk value overestimation and that pessimistic algorithms, acting optimally in the worst plausible world, perform well without data informative of every policy. Validated on gridworld and MinAtar.

papers.title: Domesticating the techno-racial project
papers.summary: A Nature Machine Intelligence commentary examining how race and racialization are embedded in and reproduced by technological and AI systems, engaging critical scholarship on the 'techno-racial project' and the social stakes of how it becomes normalized.

papers.title: Minimal Assumptions Refinement for Realizable Specifications
papers.abstract: A challenge that has gathered much attention in recent years is automated synthesis of correct-by-construction software systems from declarative specifications. The specification language is typically a subset of linear temporal logic called generalized reactivity of rank 1, for which there exists an efficient synthesis algorithm. Specifications in this language model the system as the interaction between an environment and a controller, the former satisfying a set of assumptions and the latter a set of guarantees. In order for a solution to exist, a sufficient set of assumptions implying the guarantees must be provided. The assumptions must be as general as possible and small enough to b…
papers.summary: Presents a method to refine environment assumptions in GR(1) reactive synthesis, computing assumptions general enough to imply the guarantees yet minimal and intelligible, so engineers can assess their consistency with the real deployment environment.

papers.title: Measurement in AI Policy: Opportunities and Challenges
papers.abstract: As artificial intelligence increasingly influences our world, it becomes crucial to assess its technical progress and societal impact. This paper surveys problems and opportunities in the measurement of AI systems and their impact, based on a workshop held at Stanford University in the fall of 2019. We identify six summary challenges inherent to measuring the progress and impact of AI, and summarize over 40 presentations and associated discussions from the workshop. We hope this can inspire research agendas in this crucial area.
papers.summary: Surveys problems and opportunities in measuring AI systems and their societal impact, distilling a 2019 Stanford workshop's 40+ presentations into six core measurement challenges to guide research agendas in AI policy.

papers.title: Declarative abstractions for tensor program partitioning
papers.abstract: The size of state-of-the-art machine learning models is continuously growing; for instance GPT-3, a recent language model trained by OpenAI, contains 175B parameters. Due to memory limitations and scalability constraints hardware acceleration for such models relies on configuring them as systems of accelerator devices (such as GPUs, or TPUs, or even simple compute cores with fast local memory) with custom interconnect networks. This setting poses a challenge for software: there is an increasing need for flexible ways to distribute these multi-dimensional array programs (tensor programs) across systems of accelerator devices.
papers.summary: Introduces declarative abstractions for partitioning large tensor (multi-dimensional array) programs across systems of accelerator devices, addressing the growing need to distribute models like the 175B-parameter GPT-3 under memory and scalability constraints.

papers.title: Generative language modeling for automated theorem proving
papers.abstract: We explore the application of transformer-based language models to automated theorem proving. This work is motivated by the possibility that a major limitation of automated theorem provers compared to humans -- the generation of original mathematical terms -- might be addressable via generation from language models. We present an automated prover and proof assistant, GPT-f, for the Metamath formalization language, and analyze its performance. GPT-f found new short proofs that were accepted into the main Metamath library, which is to our knowledge, the first time a deep-learning based system has contributed proofs that were adopted by a formal mathematics community.
papers.summary: GPT-f applies transformer language models to automated theorem proving in the Metamath formalization, targeting the generation of original mathematical terms; it discovered new shorter proofs that were accepted into the main Metamath library.

papers.title: Learning to summarize with human feedback
papers.abstract: We’ve applied reinforcement learning from human feedback to train language models that are better at summarization.
papers.summary: Applies RLHF to abstractive summarization: human preference comparisons train a reward model, then a policy is optimized against it, producing summaries humans prefer over reference summaries and far larger supervised models on Reddit TL;DR and CNN/DM.

papers.title: Keynote Lecture: Building Knowledge For AI AgentsWith Reinforcement Learning
papers.abstract: Reinforcement learning allows autonomous agents to learn how to act in a stochastic, unknown environment, with which they can interact. Deep reinforcement learning, in particular, has achieved great success in well-defined application domains, such as Go or chess, in which an agent has to learn how to act and there is a clear success criterion. In this talk, I will focus on the potential role of reinforcement learning as a tool for building knowledge representations in AI agents whose goal is to perform continual learning. I will examine a key concept in reinforcement learning, the value function, and discuss its generalization to support various forms of predictive knowledge. I will also…
papers.summary: A keynote arguing for reinforcement learning as a tool for building predictive knowledge in continually-learning agents, generalizing the value function, using temporally-extended actions, and leveraging intents and affordances to tame the complexity of learning knowledge representations.

papers.title: Artificial Intelligence, Values, and Alignment
papers.abstract: Abstract This paper looks at philosophical questions that arise in the context of AI alignment. It defends three propositions. First, normative and technical aspects of the AI alignment problem are interrelated, creating space for productive engagement between people working in both domains. Second, it is important to be clear about the goal of alignment. There are significant differences between AI that aligns with instructions, intentions, revealed preferences, ideal preferences, interests and values. A principle-based approach to AI alignment, which combines these elements in a systematic way, has considerable advantages in this context. Third, the central challenge for theorists is no…
papers.summary: A philosophical treatment of AI alignment arguing its normative and technical aspects are interrelated, distinguishing alignment targets (instructions, intentions, revealed/ideal preferences, interests, values), and advocating fair principles that earn reflective endorsement despite widespread moral disagreement.

papers.title: Variational Information Bottleneck for Semi-Supervised Classification
papers.abstract: In this paper, we consider an information bottleneck (IB) framework for semi-supervised classification with several families of priors on latent space representation. We apply a variational decomposition of mutual information terms of IB. Using this decomposition we perform an analysis of several regularizers and practically demonstrate an impact of different components of variational model on the classification accuracy. We propose a new formulation of semi-supervised IB with hand crafted and learnable priors and link it to the previous methods such as semi-supervised versions of VAE (M1 + M2), AAE, CatGAN, etc. We show that the resulting model allows better understand the role of variou…
papers.summary: Develops a variational information-bottleneck framework for semi-supervised classification with hand-crafted and learnable priors, unifying prior methods (semi-supervised VAE, AAE, CatGAN) and analyzing how regularizer components affect accuracy on MNIST under varying label budgets.

papers.title: The Advantage Regret-Matching Actor-Critic
papers.abstract: Regret minimization has played a key role in online learning, equilibrium computation in games, and reinforcement learning (RL). In this paper, we describe a general model-free RL method for no-regret learning based on repeated reconsideration of past behavior. We propose a model-free RL algorithm, the AdvantageRegret-Matching Actor-Critic (ARMAC): rather than saving past state-action data, ARMAC saves a buffer of past policies, replaying through them to reconstruct hindsight assessments of past behavior. These retrospective value estimates are used to predict conditional advantages which, combined with regret matching, produces a new policy. In particular, ARMAC learns from sampled traje…
papers.summary: ARMAC is a model-free no-regret RL algorithm that stores past policies and replays them to reconstruct hindsight advantage estimates; combined with regret matching it approaches Nash equilibria in partially-observable zero-sum games, including betting-abstracted no-limit Texas Hold'em.

papers.title: Derivative-free & order-robust optimisation
papers.abstract: In this paper, we formalise order-robust optimisation as an instance of online learning minimising simple regret, and propose VROOM, a zeroth order optimisation algorithm capable of achieving vanishing regret in non-stationary environments, while recovering favorable rates under stochastic reward-generating processes. Our results are the first to target simple regret definitions in adversarial scenarios unveiling a challenge that has been rarely considered in prior work.
papers.summary: Formalizes order-robust optimization as online learning minimizing simple regret and proposes VROOM, a zeroth-order (derivative-free) algorithm achieving vanishing regret in non-stationary environments while recovering favorable rates under stochastic reward processes.
papers.venue: HAL (Le Centre pour la Communication Scientifique Directe)

papers.title: Non-linear changes in modelled terrestrial ecosystems subjected to perturbations
papers.abstract: Perturbed ecosystems may undergo rapid and non-linear changes, resulting in 'regime shifts' to an entirely different ecological state. The need to understand the extent, nature, magnitude and reversibility of these changes is urgent given the profound effects that humans are having on the natural world. General ecosystem models, which simulate the dynamics of ecosystems based on a mechanistic representation of ecological processes, provide one novel way to project ecosystem changes across all scales and trophic levels, and to forecast impact thresholds beyond which irreversible changes may occur. We model ecosystem changes in four terrestrial biomes subjected to human removal of plant bio…
papers.summary: Uses a mechanistic general ecosystem model across four terrestrial biomes to show that removing over ~80% of plant biomass commonly triggers irreversible, non-linear regime shifts, especially at higher trophic levels and in less productive ecosystems.

papers.title: The NodeHopper: Enabling Low Latency Ranking with Constraints via a Fast Dual Solver
papers.abstract: Modern recommender systems need to deal with multiple objectives like balancing user engagement with recommending diverse and fresh content. An appealing way to optimally trade these off is by imposing constraints on the ranking according to which items are presented to a user. This results in a constrained ranking optimization problem that can be solved as a linear program (LP). However, off-the-shelf LP solvers are unable to meet the severe latency constraints in systems that serve live traffic. To address this challenge, we exploit the structure of the dual optimization problem to develop a fast solver. We analyze theoretical properties of our solver and show experimentally that it is…
papers.summary: Exploits the dual structure of the constrained-ranking linear program to build NodeHopper, a fast solver that meets recommender serving latency limits, solving constrained ranking an order of magnitude faster than off-the-shelf LP solvers.

papers.title: Machine learning enables completely automatic tuning of a quantum device faster than human experts
papers.abstract: Variability is a problem for the scalability of semiconductor quantum devices. The parameter space is large, and the operating range is small. Our statistical tuning algorithm searches for specific electron transport features in gate-defined quantum dot devices with a gate voltage space of up to eight dimensions. Starting from the full range of each gate voltage, our machine learning algorithm can tune each device to optimal performance in a median time of under 70 minutes. This performance surpassed our best human benchmark (although both human and machine performance can be improved). The algorithm is approximately 180 times faster than an automated random search of the parameter space,…
papers.summary: A statistical machine-learning algorithm automatically tunes gate-defined quantum-dot devices across up to eight gate-voltage dimensions, reaching optimal performance in a median under 70 minutes, surpassing expert humans and roughly 180x faster than automated random search.

papers.title: Fast reinforcement learning with generalized policy updates
papers.abstract: The combination of reinforcement learning with deep learning is a promising approach to tackle important sequential decision-making problems that are currently intractable. One obstacle to overcome is the amount of data needed by learning systems of this type. In this article, we propose to address this issue through a divide-and-conquer approach. We argue that complex decision problems can be naturally decomposed into multiple tasks that unfold in sequence or in parallel. By associating each task with a reward function, this problem decomposition can be seamlessly accommodated within the standard reinforcement-learning formalism. The specific way we do so is through a generalization of t…
papers.summary: Generalizes policy improvement and evaluation across tasks: when a new task's reward is well-approximated as a linear combination of solved tasks' rewards, RL reduces to linear regression, substantially cutting the data needed to solve new problems.
papers.venue: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences

papers.title: NCNet: Neighbourhood Consensus Networks for Estimating Image Correspondences
papers.abstract: We address the problem of finding reliable dense correspondences between a pair of images. This is a challenging task due to strong appearance differences between the corresponding scene elements and ambiguities generated by repetitive patterns. The contributions of this work are threefold. First, inspired by the classic idea of disambiguating feature matches using semi-local constraints, we develop an end-to-end trainable convolutional neural network architecture that identifies sets of spatially consistent matches by analyzing neighbourhood consensus patterns in the 4D space of all possible correspondences between a pair of images without the need for a global geometric model. Second, w…
papers.summary: NCNet is an end-to-end CNN that finds reliable dense image correspondences by analyzing neighbourhood-consensus patterns in the 4D space of all possible matches; trainable from weak supervision, it achieves state-of-the-art on PF, TSS, InLoc, and HPatches.
papers.venue: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence

papers.title: Catch & Carry
papers.abstract: We address the longstanding challenge of producing flexible, realistic humanoid character controllers that can perform diverse whole-body tasks involving object interactions. This challenge is central to a variety of fields, from graphics and animation to robotics and motor neuroscience. Our physics-based environment uses realistic actuation and first-person perception - including touch sensors and egocentric vision - with a view to producing active-sensing behaviors (e.g. gaze direction), transferability to real robots, and comparisons to the biology. We develop an integrated neural-network based approach consisting of a motor primitive module, human demonstrations, and an instructed rei…
papers.summary: Trains physics-based humanoid controllers for whole-body object-interaction tasks like box carrying and ball catching, using egocentric vision and touch sensing, a motor-primitive module, human demonstrations, and instructed RL with curricula; controllers run in real time.

papers.title: Ecological theory predicts ecosystem stressor interactions in freshwater ecosystems, but highlights the strengths and weaknesses of the additive null model
papers.abstract: Abstract Understanding and predicting how multiple co-occurring environmental stressors combine to affect biodiversity and ecosystem services is an on-going grand challenge for ecology. So far progress has been made through accumulating large numbers of smaller-scale individual studies that are then investigated by meta-analyses to look for general patterns. In particular there has been an interest in checking for so-called ecological surprises where stressors interact in a synergistic manner. Recent reviews suggest that such synergisms do not dominate, but few other generalities have emerged. This lack of general prediction and understanding may be due in part to a dearth of ecological t…
papers.summary: Compares classical food-web model predictions against a 546-interaction freshwater dataset, finding additive stressor interactions are most frequent (versus meta-analyses' antagonistic classifications) and that interaction-classification statistical tests are highly sensitive to sampling variation.

papers.title: dm_control: Software and tasks for continuous control
papers.abstract: The dm_control software package is a collection of Python libraries and task suites for reinforcement learning agents in an articulated-body simulation. Infrastructure includes a wrapper for the MuJoCo physics engine and libraries for procedural model manipulation and task authoring. Task suites include the Control Suite, a set of standardized tasks intended to serve as performance benchmarks, a locomotion framework and task families, and a set of manipulation tasks with a robot arm and snap-together bricks. An adjunct tech report and interactive tutorial are also provided.
papers.summary: Releases dm_control, Python libraries and task suites for continuous-control RL built on the MuJoCo physics engine, including the standardized Control Suite benchmark, a locomotion framework, and robot-arm manipulation tasks with snap-together bricks.

papers.title: A quick look at impredicativity
papers.abstract: Type inference for parametric polymorphism is wildly successful, but has always suffered from an embarrassing flaw: polymorphic types are themselves not first class. We present Quick Look, a practical, implemented, and deployable design for impredicative type inference. To demonstrate our claims, we have modified GHC, a production-quality Haskell compiler, to support impredicativity. The changes required are modest, localised, and are fully compatible with GHC's myriad other type system extensions.
papers.summary: Presents Quick Look, a practical, implemented design for impredicative type inference, integrated into the GHC Haskell compiler with modest, localized changes that stay compatible with its other type-system extensions, making polymorphic types more first-class.
papers.venue: Proceedings of the ACM on Programming Languages

papers.title: Para que serve o subteste de Aritmética do Teste de Desempenho Escolar
papers.abstract: As dificuldades de aprendizagem da matematica (DAM) se associam a menor renda, empregabilidade e adaptacao psicossocial, entretanto, a identificacao de criancas com DAM e dificultada pela ausencia de marcadores cognitivos e biologicos confiaveis. Um dos criterios diagnosticos do transtorno especifico de aprendizagem da matematica ou discalculia e o desempenho em aritmetica situado abaixo de um ponto de corte em teste padronizado de desempenho. Um dos principais instrumentos psicometricos disponiveis no Brasil para esse tipo de diagnostico e o Teste de Desempenho Escolar (TDE). No presente artigo, nos analisamos alguns aspectos da validade psicometrica do subteste Aritmetica do TDE, 1a edi…
papers.summary: Evaluates the psychometric validity, construct, dimensionality, internal consistency, and criterion validity, of the Arithmetic subtest of Brazil's School Achievement Test (TDE) in 2,226 children, finding satisfactory reliability and hierarchically ordered item difficulty for screening math-learning difficulties.

papers.title: Non-parametric modelling of temporal and spatial counts data from RNA-seq experiments
papers.abstract: A bstract Motivation The negative binomial distribution has been shown to be a good model for counts data from both bulk and single-cell RNA-sequencing (RNA-seq). Gaussian process (GP) regression provides a useful non-parametric approach for modeling temporal or spatial changes in gene expression. However, currently available GP regression methods that implement negative binomial likelihood models do not scale to the increasingly large datasets being produced by single-cell and spatial transcriptomics. Results The GPcounts package implements GP regression methods for modelling counts data using a negative binomial likelihood function. Computational efficiency is achieved through the use o…
papers.summary: GPcounts implements scalable Gaussian-process regression with a negative-binomial (or zero-inflated) likelihood via variational inference for RNA-seq counts, better detecting over-dispersed temporal and spatial expression changes than Gaussian- or Poisson-based methods on single-cell and spatial data.

papers.title: Learning Compositional Neural Programs for Continuous Control
papers.abstract: We propose a novel solution to challenging sparse-reward, continuous control problems that require hierarchical planning at multiple levels of abstraction. Our solution, dubbed AlphaNPI-X, involves three separate stages of learning. First, we use off-policy reinforcement learning algorithms with experience replay to learn a set of atomic goal-conditioned policies, which can be easily repurposed for many tasks. Second, we learn self-models describing the effect of the atomic policies on the environment. Third, the self-models are harnessed to learn recursive compositional programs with multiple levels of abstraction. The key insight is that the self-models enable planning by imagination, o…
papers.summary: AlphaNPI-X solves sparse-reward continuous control in three stages: off-policy RL learns atomic goal-conditioned policies, self-models learn their effects, and those models enable planning-by-imagination to learn recursive compositional neural programs, solving tasks like stacking multiple blocks.

papers.title: Fast active learning for pure exploration in reinforcement learning
papers.abstract: Realistic environments often provide agents with very limited feedback. When the environment is initially unknown, the feedback, in the beginning, can be completely absent, and the agents may first choose to devote all their effort on <i>exploring efficiently</i>. The exploration remains a challenge while it has been addressed with many hand-tuned heuristics with different levels of generality on one side, and a few theoretically backed exploration strategies on the other. Many of them are incarnated by <i>intrinsic motivation</i> and in particular <i>explorations bonuses</i>. A common rule of thumb for exploration bonuses is to use 1/ √ n bonus that is…
papers.summary: For reward-free pure exploration in RL, shows exploration bonuses scaling as 1/n (rather than 1/sqrt(n)) yield faster learning rates, improving horizon dependence in regret bounds and tightening best-policy-identification sample complexity via improved stopping-time analysis.
papers.venue: INRIA a CCSD electronic archive server

papers.title: Tighter risk certificates for neural networks
papers.abstract: This paper presents an empirical study regarding training probabilistic neural networks using training objectives derived from PAC-Bayes bounds. In the context of probabilistic neural networks, the output of training is a probability distribution over network weights. We present two training objectives, used here for the first time in connection with training neural networks. These two training objectives are derived from tight PAC-Bayes bounds. We also re-implement a previously used training objective based on a classical PAC-Bayes bound, to compare the properties of the predictors learned using the different training objectives. We compute risk certificates for the learnt predictors, ba…
papers.summary: Trains probabilistic neural networks with objectives derived from tight PAC-Bayes bounds and computes non-vacuous risk certificates on MNIST and CIFAR-10 far tighter than prior work, suggesting these methods support model selection and self-certified learning.

papers.title: Negotiating team formation using deep reinforcement learning
papers.summary: A protocol-agnostic, experience-driven deep-RL framework trains self-interested agents to negotiate and form teams in non-spatial and spatial environments; agents beat hand-crafted bots, reach outcomes consistent with cooperative game theory's fair solutions, and reveal how physical location shapes negotiation.

papers.title: Topic Modeling in Embedding Spaces
papers.abstract: Topic modeling analyzes documents to learn meaningful patterns of words. However, existing topic models fail to learn interpretable topics when working with large and heavy-tailed vocabularies. To this end, we develop the embedded topic model (etm), a generative model of documents that marries traditional topic models with word embeddings. More specifically, the etm models each word with a categorical distribution whose natural parameter is the inner product between the word’s embedding and an embedding of its assigned topic. To fit the etm, we develop an efficient amortized variational inference algorithm. The etm discovers interpretable topics even with large vocabularies that include r…
papers.summary: The embedded topic model (ETM) marries topic models with word embeddings, modeling each word via a categorical distribution parameterized by the dot product of word and topic embeddings; fit with amortized variational inference, it yields interpretable topics and beats LDA on large vocabularies.
papers.venue: Transactions of the Association for Computational Linguistics

papers.title: Deep Reinforcement Learning for Tactile Robotics: Learning to Type on a Braille Keyboard
papers.abstract: Artificial touch would seem well-suited for Reinforcement Learning (RL), since both paradigms rely on interaction with an environment. Here we propose a new environment and set of tasks to encourage development of tactile reinforcement learning: learning to type on a braille keyboard. Four tasks are proposed, progressing in difficulty from arrow to alphabet keys and from discrete to continuous actions. A simulated counterpart is also constructed by sampling tactile data from the physical environment. Using state-of-the-art deep RL algorithms, we show that all of these tasks can be successfully learnt in simulation, and 3 out of 4 tasks can be learned on the real robot. A lack of sample ef…
papers.summary: Introduces a tactile-robotics RL benchmark, learning to type on a braille keyboard across four tasks of increasing difficulty, and shows deep-RL agents trained purely on tactile images succeed in simulation and on three of four real-robot tasks.

papers.title: Predicting the long-term stability of compact multiplanet systems
papers.abstract: Significance Observations of planets beyond our solar system (exoplanets) yield uncertain orbital parameters. Particularly in compact multiplanet systems, a significant fraction of observationally inferred orbital configurations can lead to planetary collisions on timescales that are short compared with the age of the system. Rejection of these unphysical solutions can thus sharpen our view of exoplanetary orbital architectures. Long-term stability determination is currently performed through direct orbital integrations. However, this approach is computationally prohibitive for application to the full exoplanet sample. By speeding up this process by up to five orders of magnitude, we enab…
papers.summary: Trains a machine-learning model to predict the long-term orbital stability of compact multiplanet exoplanet systems up to five orders of magnitude faster than direct N-body integration, enabling rejection of unphysical orbital configurations and population-wide stability analysis.
papers.venue: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences

papers.title: The Monte Carlo Transformer: a stochastic self-attention model for\n sequence prediction
papers.abstract:
This paper introduces the Sequential Monte Carlo Transformer, an original
approach that naturally captures the observations distribution in a transformer
architecture. The keys, queries, values and attention vectors of the network
are considered as the unobserved stochastic states of its hidden structure.
This generative model is such that at each time step the received observation
is a random function of its past states in a given attention window. In this
general state-space setting, we use Sequential Monte Carlo methods to
approximate the posterior distributions of the states given the observations,
and to estimate the gradient of the log-likelihood. We hence propose a
generat…
papers.summary: The Sequential Monte Carlo Transformer recasts a transformer's keys, queries, values, and attention as latent stochastic states in a state-space model, using Sequential Monte Carlo to approximate state posteriors and log-likelihood gradients, yielding predictive distributions instead of point estimates.

papers.title: Deep Reinforcement Learning and Its Neuroscientific Implications
papers.summary: Neuron review arguing deep RL deserves neuroscientists' attention: it surveys recent agents and draws implications for reward-based learning and dopamine (including distributional RL), representation learning, model-based control, memory, and meta-learning, proposing deep RL as a framework linking learning, representation, and decision-making in the brain.

papers.title: Revisiting Fundamentals of Experience Replay
papers.abstract: Experience replay is central to off-policy algorithms in deep reinforcement learning (RL), but there remain significant gaps in our understanding. We therefore present a systematic and extensive analysis of experience replay in Q-learning methods, focusing on two fundamental properties: the replay capacity and the ratio of learning updates to experience collected (replay ratio). Our additive and ablative studies upend conventional wisdom around experience replay -- greater capacity is found to substantially increase the performance of certain algorithms, while leaving others unaffected. Counterintuitively we show that theoretically ungrounded, uncorrected n-step returns are uniquely benef…
papers.summary: Systematically dissects experience replay in deep Q-learning along replay capacity and replay ratio. Larger buffers substantially help some algorithms while leaving others unaffected, and theoretically uncorrected n-step returns prove uniquely able to exploit the added memory, upending conventional wisdom about off-policy replay.

papers.title: Structured Policy Iteration for Linear Quadratic Regulator
papers.abstract: Linear quadratic regulator (LQR) is one of the most popular frameworks to tackle continuous Markov decision process tasks. With its fundamental theory and tractable optimal policy, LQR has been revisited and analyzed in recent years, in terms of reinforcement learning scenarios such as the model-free or model-based setting. In this paper, we introduce the \textit{Structured Policy Iteration} (S-PI) for LQR, a method capable of deriving a structured linear policy. Such a structured policy with (block) sparsity or low-rank can have significant advantages over the standard LQR policy: more interpretable, memory-efficient, and well-suited for the distributed setting. In order to derive such a…
papers.summary: Structured Policy Iteration (S-PI) solves a regularized LQR formulation to produce sparse or low-rank linear policies that are more interpretable, memory-efficient, and suited to distributed control. Convergence is proven in both known-model and model-free smoothed-gradient settings, with a weight parameter trading performance against structure.

papers.title: A Provably Efficient Sample Collection Strategy for Reinforcement Learning
papers.abstract: One of the challenges in online reinforcement learning (RL) is that the agent needs to trade off the exploration of the environment and the exploitation of the samples to optimize its behavior. Whether we optimize for regret, sample complexity, state-space coverage or model estimation, we need to strike a different exploration-exploitation trade-off. In this paper, we propose to tackle the exploration-exploitation problem following a decoupled approach composed of: 1) An "objective-specific" algorithm that (adaptively) prescribes how many samples to collect at which states, as if it has access to a generative model (i.e., a simulator of the environment); 2) An "objective-agnostic" sample…
papers.summary: Decouples online RL exploration into an objective-specific module that prescribes how many samples to collect at each state-action pair and an objective-agnostic strategy that collects them in Õ(BD + D^{3/2}S²A) steps, improving guarantees for model estimation, sparse-reward discovery, and reward-free exploration in communicating MDPs.

papers.title: Decolonial AI: Decolonial Theory as Sociotechnical Foresight in Artificial Intelligence
papers.abstract: Abstract This paper explores the important role of critical science, and in particular of post-colonial and decolonial theories, in understanding and shaping the ongoing advances in artificial intelligence. Artificial intelligence (AI) is viewed as amongst the technological advances that will reshape modern societies and their relations. While the design and deployment of systems that continually adapt holds the promise of far-reaching positive change, they simultaneously pose significant risks, especially to already vulnerable peoples. Values and power are central to this discussion. Decolonial theories use historical hindsight to explain patterns of power that shape our intellectual, po…
papers.summary: Argues that post-colonial and decolonial theory offer useful tools for anticipating and mitigating AI's harms to vulnerable populations. Proposes three tactics, a critical technical practice of AI, reverse tutelage, and renewed affective/political community, to align development with ethical principles.

papers.title: XTREME: A Massively Multilingual Multi-task Benchmark for Evaluating Cross-lingual Generalisation
papers.abstract: Much recent progress in applications of machine learning models to NLP has been driven by benchmarks that evaluate models across a wide variety of tasks. However, these broad-coverage benchmarks have been mostly limited to English, and despite an increasing interest in multilingual models, a benchmark that enables the comprehensive evaluation of such methods on a diverse range of languages and tasks is still missing. To this end, we introduce the Cross-lingual TRansfer Evaluation of Multilingual Encoders XTREME benchmark, a multi-task benchmark for evaluating the cross-lingual generalization capabilities of multilingual representations across 40 languages and 9 tasks. We demonstrate that…
papers.summary: A multilingual benchmark spanning 40 languages and 9 tasks for evaluating cross-lingual transfer of multilingual encoders. Models near human performance in English show a sizable cross-lingual gap, especially on syntactic and sentence-retrieval tasks, motivating better transfer methods.

papers.title: Data-Efficient Image Recognition with Contrastive Predictive Coding
papers.summary: An improved Contrastive Predictive Coding pipeline for self-supervised image representation. The learned features enable highly data-efficient recognition, matching strong supervised baselines with far fewer labels, and achieve competitive ImageNet linear-probe classification.

papers.title: Agent57: Outperforming the Atari Human Benchmark
papers.summary: The first deep RL agent to exceed the human baseline on all 57 Atari games. Building on Never Give Up, it uses a family of policies spanning exploration to exploitation, separate intrinsic/extrinsic value heads, and an adaptive meta-controller that selects behavior per episode.

papers.title: Rigging the Lottery: Making All Tickets Winners
papers.abstract: Many applications require sparse neural networks due to space or inference time restrictions. There is a large body of work on training dense networks to yield sparse networks for inference, but this limits the size of the largest trainable sparse model to that of the largest trainable dense model. In this paper we introduce a method to train sparse neural networks with a fixed parameter count and a fixed computational cost throughout training, without sacrificing accuracy relative to existing dense-to-sparse training methods. Our method updates the topology of the sparse network during training by using parameter magnitudes and infrequent gradient calculations. We show that this approach…
papers.summary: RigL trains sparse networks at a fixed parameter count and FLOP budget throughout training, periodically updating connectivity using weight magnitudes and occasional dense gradients. It matches or beats dense-to-sparse methods at lower compute on ResNet-50, MobileNets, and RNNs.

papers.title: Learning to Simulate Complex Physics with Graph Networks
papers.summary: Graph Network-based Simulators (GNS) represent physical systems as particles in a graph and learn message-passing dynamics to predict per-particle motion. A single architecture accurately simulates fluids, rigid solids, and deformable materials, generalizing well beyond its training conditions.

papers.title: Learning with Good Feature Representations in Bandits and in RL with a Generative Model
papers.summary: Studies linear bandits and RL with a generative model when features only approximately realize value functions. Proves algorithms whose suboptimality scales with the feature-misspecification error times the square root of the feature dimension, and shows via matching lower bounds that this dimension-dependent amplification is unavoidable.

papers.title: Stabilizing Transformers for Reinforcement Learning
papers.abstract: Owing to their ability to both effectively integrate information over long time horizons and scale to massive amounts of data, self-attention architectures have recently shown breakthrough success in natural language processing (NLP), achieving state-of-the-art results in domains such as language modeling and machine translation. Harnessing the transformer's ability to process long time horizons of information could provide a similar performance boost in partially observable reinforcement learning (RL) domains, but the large-scale transformers used in NLP have yet to be successfully applied to the RL setting. In this work we demonstrate that the standard transformer architecture is diffic…
papers.summary: Shows standard Transformers are hard to optimize under RL objectives and proposes the Gated Transformer-XL (GTrXL), with reordered layer normalization and gating layers. It trains stably, surpasses LSTMs on memory-intensive tasks, and sets state-of-the-art results on the DMLab-30 suite.

papers.title: PolyGen: An Autoregressive Generative Model of 3D Meshes
papers.abstract: Polygon meshes are an efficient representation of 3D geometry, and are of central importance in computer graphics, robotics and games development. Existing learning-based approaches have avoided the challenges of working with 3D meshes, instead using alternative object representations that are more compatible with neural architectures and training approaches. We present an approach which models the mesh directly, predicting mesh vertices and faces sequentially using a Transformer-based architecture. Our model can condition on a range of inputs, including object classes, voxels, and images, and because the model is probabilistic it can produce samples that capture uncertainty in ambiguous…
papers.summary: Models 3D polygon meshes directly with a Transformer, autoregressively predicting vertices then faces, and can condition on classes, voxels, or images. Being probabilistic, it captures uncertainty in ambiguous cases, produces high-quality meshes, and sets log-likelihood benchmarks for mesh modeling.

papers.title: Bootstrap Latent-Predictive Representations for Multitask Reinforcement Learning
papers.abstract: Learning a good representation is an essential component for deep reinforcement learning (RL). Representation learning is especially important in multitask and partially observable settings where building a representation of the unknown environment is crucial to solve the tasks. Here we introduce Prediction of Bootstrap Latents (PBL), a simple and flexible self-supervised representation learning algorithm for multitask deep RL. PBL builds on multistep predictive representations of future observations, and focuses on capturing structured information about environment dynamics. Specifically, PBL trains its representation by predicting latent embeddings of future observations. These latent e…
papers.summary: Prediction of Bootstrap Latents (PBL), a self-supervised representation method for multitask deep RL that predicts latent embeddings of future observations, which are themselves trained to be predictive of the representation. This bootstrapping improves over state-of-the-art agents on DMLab-30 and Atari-57.

papers.title: MetaFun: Meta-Learning with Iterative Functional Updates
papers.abstract: We develop a functional encoder-decoder approach to supervised meta-learning, where labeled data is encoded into an infinite-dimensional functional representation rather than a finite-dimensional one. Furthermore, rather than directly producing the representation, we learn a neural update rule resembling functional gradient descent which iteratively improves the representation. The final representation is used to condition the decoder to make predictions on unlabeled data. Our approach is the first to demonstrates the success of encoder-decoder style meta-learning methods like conditional neural processes on large-scale few-shot classification benchmarks such as miniImageNet and tieredIma…
papers.summary: A functional encoder-decoder approach to supervised meta-learning that encodes labeled data into an infinite-dimensional functional representation, then refines it via learned iterative updates resembling functional gradient descent. Achieves state-of-the-art few-shot classification on miniImageNet and tieredImageNet.
papers.venue: Oxford University Research Archive (ORA) (University of Oxford)

papers.title: Training Neural Networks for and by Interpolation
papers.abstract: In modern supervised learning, many deep neural networks are able to interpolate the data: the empirical loss can be driven to near zero on all samples simultaneously. In this work, we explicitly exploit this interpolation property for the design of a new optimization algorithm for deep learning, which we term Adaptive Learning-rates for Interpolation with Gradients (ALI-G). ALI-G retains the two main advantages of Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD), which are (i) a low computational cost per iteration and (ii) good generalization performance in practice. At each iteration, ALI-G exploits the interpolation property to compute an adaptive learning-rate in closed form. In addition, ALI-G cli…
papers.summary: Introduces ALI-G, an optimizer that exploits the interpolation regime (near-zero training loss) to set an adaptive learning rate in closed form, clipped at a tunable maximum. It eliminates learning-rate decay schedules while matching SGD's per-step cost and generalization across diverse architectures.
papers.venue: Oxford University Research Archive (ORA) (University of Oxford)

papers.title: Normalizing Flows on Tori and Spheres
papers.abstract: Normalizing flows are a powerful tool for building expressive distributions in high dimensions. So far, most of the literature has concentrated on learning flows on Euclidean spaces. Some problems however, such as those involving angles, are defined on spaces with more complex geometries, such as tori or spheres. In this paper, we propose and compare expressive and numerically stable flows on such spaces. Our flows are built recursively on the dimension of the space, starting from flows on circles, closed intervals or spheres.
papers.summary: Constructs expressive, numerically stable normalizing flows on non-Euclidean spaces such as tori and spheres, built recursively over dimensions from flows on circles, closed intervals, and spheres—targeting distributions over angle-like variables that Euclidean flows handle poorly.

papers.title: Linear bandits with Stochastic Delayed Feedback
papers.abstract: Stochastic linear bandits are a natural and well-studied model for structured exploration/exploitation problems and are widely used in applications such as online marketing and recommendation. One of the main challenges faced by practitioners hoping to apply existing algorithms is that usually the feedback is randomly delayed and delays are only partially observable. For example, while a purchase is usually observable some time after the display, the decision of not buying is never explicitly sent to the system. In other words, the learner only observes delayed positive events. We formalize this problem as a novel stochastic delayed linear bandit and propose ${\tt OTFLinUCB}$ and ${\tt OT…
papers.summary: Formalizes linear bandits with stochastically delayed, censored feedback, where only positive events (e.g., purchases) are ever observed and only after random delays. OTFLinUCB and OTFLinTS integrate information as it arrives, achieve optimal Õ(d√T) regret, and are validated on simulated and real data.

papers.title: Invariant Causal Prediction for Block MDPs
papers.summary: Adapts invariant causal prediction to block MDPs, learning state abstractions from causal feature sets that stay invariant across environments. Provides generalization-error bounds for transfer to unseen environments and empirically improves generalization over standard representation-learning baselines in imitation and RL settings.

papers.title: Improving the Gating Mechanism of Recurrent Neural Networks
papers.abstract: Gating mechanisms are widely used in neural network models, where they allow gradients to backpropagate more easily through depth or time. However, their saturation property introduces problems of its own. For example, in recurrent models these gates need to have outputs near 1 to propagate information over long time-delays, which requires them to operate in their saturation regime and hinders gradient-based learning of the gate mechanism. We address this problem by deriving two synergistic modifications to the standard gating mechanism that are easy to implement, introduce no additional hyperparameters, and improve learnability of the gates when they are close to saturation. We show how…
papers.summary: Derives two simple, hyperparameter-free modifications to RNN gates that keep them learnable near saturation, where standard gates stall. The improved gating robustly outperforms alternatives like chrono initialization and Ordered Neurons across memorization, sequential image classification, language modeling, and RL tasks involving long-term dependencies.

papers.title: Interference and Generalization in Temporal Difference Learning
papers.abstract: We study the link between generalization and interference in temporal-difference (TD) learning. Interference is defined as the inner product of two different gradients, representing their alignment. This quantity emerges as being of interest from a variety of observations about neural networks, parameter sharing and the dynamics of learning. We find that TD easily leads to low-interference, under-generalizing parameters, while the effect seems reversed in supervised learning. We hypothesize that the cause can be traced back to the interplay between the dynamics of interference and bootstrapping. This is supported empirically by several observations: the negative relationship between the g…
papers.summary: Analyzes the link between gradient interference and generalization in temporal-difference learning: TD tends toward low-interference, under-generalizing solutions, the reverse of supervised learning. Evidence traces this to bootstrapping's interplay with interference, including contrasts between TD(0), TD(λ), and Monte-Carlo regression targets.

papers.title: OPtions as REsponses: Grounding behavioural hierarchies in multi-agent reinforcement learning
papers.summary: Studies generalization to unseen opponents in multi-agent games via two new games with concealed information and non-transitive rewards, where standard deep RL explores strategy space poorly. A hierarchical agent grounded in game-theoretic structure—strategic responses on top, primitive actions below—generalizes where conventional baselines fail entirely.

papers.title: From Chaos to Order: Symmetry and Conservation Laws in Game Dynamics
papers.summary: Presents mechanism-design tools for building games with predictable multi-agent learning dynamics: large network games mixing cooperation and competition exhibit conservation laws under Follow-the-Regularized-Leader. With enough linearly independent conservation laws, dynamics grow predictable and chaotic behavior becomes formally impossible in some cases.

papers.title: A simpler approach to accelerated optimization: iterative averaging meets optimism
papers.summary: Achieves Nesterov-style acceleration by simply running optimistic online learning algorithms and querying gradients at the running average of iterates. This recovers optimal accelerated rates for strongly-convex and convex composite objectives with deterministic or stochastic oracles, extends to variance reduction, and yields tuning-free universal algorithms.

papers.title: Divide, Conquer, and Combine: a New Inference Strategy for Probabilistic Programs with Stochastic Support
papers.abstract: Universal probabilistic programming systems (PPSs) provide a powerful framework for specifying rich probabilistic models. They further attempt to automate the process of drawing inferences from these models, but doing this successfully is severely hampered by the wide range of non--standard models they can express. As a result, although one can specify complex models in a universal PPS, the provided inference engines often fall far short of what is required. In particular, we show that they produce surprisingly unsatisfactory performance for models where the support varies between executions, often doing no better than importance sampling from the prior. To address this, we introduce a ne…
papers.summary: Shows universal probabilistic-programming inference engines degrade badly on models whose support varies between executions, often matching mere importance sampling from the prior. The Divide, Conquer, and Combine framework handles such stochastic-support programs as an automated, generic inference engine, with substantial gains on three examples.

papers.title: Gamification of Pure Exploration for Linear Bandits
papers.abstract: Virtual conference
papers.summary: Casts fixed-confidence best-arm identification in linear bandits as a zero-sum game between learner and nature, tracking the optimal sampling allocation with saddle-point and online-learning techniques. The resulting algorithms are asymptotically optimal while remaining computationally practical.

papers.title: Taylor Expansion Policy Optimization
papers.abstract: International audience
papers.summary: Develops a Taylor-expansion view of policy optimization in which trust-region methods like TRPO arise as first-order expansions of off-policy corrections, connecting them to off-policy evaluation operators such as Retrace. Incorporating higher-order expansion terms into existing distributed RL agents improves performance.
papers.venue: HAL (Le Centre pour la Communication Scientifique Directe)

papers.title: Improved sleeping bandits with stochastic action sets and adversarial rewards
papers.abstract: International audience
papers.summary: For sleeping bandits with stochastic action availability and adversarial rewards, gives an efficient EXP3-inspired algorithm achieving O(√T) regret under independent arm availabilities—where prior polynomial-time methods managed only O(T^{2/3})—plus an efficient O(√(2^K T)) algorithm for arbitrary unknown availability distributions.
papers.venue: HAL (Le Centre pour la Communication Scientifique Directe)

papers.title: Fast computation of Nash Equilibria in Imperfect Information Games
papers.summary: Mirror Ascent against an Improved Opponent (MAIO) computes Nash equilibria in two-player zero-sum games, including sequential imperfect-information games: each player takes mirror-ascent steps against an improved opponent (best response, greedy, or policy-gradient). Proves last-iterate convergence to Nash, with exponential rates under best responses.

papers.title: CoMic: Co-Training and Mimicry for Reusable Skills
papers.summary: CoMic studies large-scale motion-capture tracking for simulated humanoids and what makes motor skills reusable: co-training mimicry with complementary tasks produces a low-level skill module that transfers, speeding up learning of new whole-body control tasks from egocentric observations.

papers.title: Analyzing the effect of neural network architecture on training performance
papers.summary: Examines how neural-network architecture choices affect training performance. The indexed record from the ICML 2020 period provides no abstract, so the specific methodology and findings are not publicly documented.

papers.title: Non-Stationary Bandits with Intermediate Observations
papers.abstract: Online recommender systems often face long delays in receiving feedback, especially when optimizing for some long-term metrics. While mitigating the effects of delays in learning is well-understood in stationary environments, the problem becomes much more challenging when the environment changes. In fact, if the timescale of the change is comparable to the delay, it is impossible to learn about the environment, since the available observations are already obsolete. However, the arising issues can be addressed if intermediate signals are available without delay, such that given those signals, the long-term behavior of the system is stationary. To model this situation, we introduce the prob…
papers.summary: Studies non-stationary bandits with long feedback delays where undelayed intermediate observations make long-term outcomes conditionally stationary—as in recommenders optimizing long-term metrics. A computationally efficient UCRL-based algorithm attains sublinear regret and empirically learns in changing, delayed environments where existing methods fail.

papers.title: Influence Diagram Bandits
papers.summary: Models structured bandit problems as influence diagrams, unifying settings such as cascading and combinatorial bandits where rewards arise through latent variables. Thompson-sampling-style algorithms, including scalable variational variants, exploit the diagram structure for efficient exploration.

papers.title: Generative Pretraining From Pixels
papers.summary: Image GPT trains sequence Transformers autoregressively (or BERT-style) on raw pixel sequences with no 2D inductive biases. Despite low resolution, the learned representations achieve strong linear-probe accuracy on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet and, with fine-tuning, rival top self-supervised convolutional methods.

papers.title: Leveraging Procedural Generation to Benchmark Reinforcement Learning
papers.abstract: We introduce Procgen Benchmark, a suite of 16 procedurally generated game-like environments designed to benchmark both sample efficiency and generalization in reinforcement learning. We believe that the community will benefit from increased access to high quality training environments, and we provide detailed experimental protocols for using this benchmark. We empirically demonstrate that diverse environment distributions are essential to adequately train and evaluate RL agents, thereby motivating the extensive use of procedural content generation. We then use this benchmark to investigate the effects of scaling model size, finding that larger models significantly improve both sample effi…
papers.summary: Procgen Benchmark offers 16 procedurally generated game-like environments with experimental protocols for measuring sample efficiency and generalization in RL. Experiments show diverse environment distributions are essential for training and evaluating agents, and that larger models improve both sample efficiency and generalization.

papers.title: Generative Teaching Networks: Accelerating Neural Architecture Search by Learning to Generate Synthetic Training Data
papers.abstract: This paper investigates the intriguing question of whether we can create learning algorithms that automatically generate training data, learning environments, and curricula in order to help AI agents rapidly learn. We show that such algorithms are possible via Generative Teaching Networks (GTNs), a general approach that is, in theory, applicable to supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning, although our experiments only focus on the supervised case. GTNs are deep neural networks that generate data and/or training environments that a learner (e.g. a freshly initialized neural network) trains on for a few SGD steps before being tested on a target task. We then differentiate throu…
papers.summary: Generative Teaching Networks synthesize training data by meta-gradient differentiation through a learner's short training loop, so freshly initialized networks rapidly learn a target task. Applied to neural architecture search, GTN-generated data massively accelerates candidate evaluation and finds higher-performing architectures than the prior state of the art.

papers.title: Enhanced POET: Open-ended Reinforcement Learning through Unbounded Invention of Learning Challenges and their Solutions
papers.abstract: Creating open-ended algorithms, which generate their own never-ending stream of novel and appropriately challenging learning opportunities, could help to automate and accelerate progress in machine learning. A recent step in this direction is the Paired Open-Ended Trailblazer (POET), an algorithm that generates and solves its own challenges, and allows solutions to goal-switch between challenges to avoid local optima. However, the original POET was unable to demonstrate its full creative potential because of limitations of the algorithm itself and because of external issues including a limited problem space and lack of a universal progress measure. Importantly, both limitations pose imped…
papers.summary: Enhances the open-ended POET algorithm with a domain-general measure of how meaningfully novel generated challenges are and an efficient goal-switching heuristic, plus a richer environment space and a progress measure for open-endedness. Together these yield the most open-ended algorithmic demonstration to date.

papers.title: Estimating Q(s,s') with Deterministic Dynamics Gradients
papers.summary: Replaces Q(s,a) with a value function Q(s,s′) over state transitions, learning which next state is best and recovering the action that reaches it via dynamics gradients. The decomposition enables learning from action-free demonstrations and transfer across agents with different action spaces.

papers.title: Responsive Safety in Reinforcement Learning
papers.summary: Interprets Lagrangian constrained RL as integral-only control on constraint violations, explaining its characteristic oscillation and overshoot. Adding proportional and derivative terms—PID control of the Lagrange multiplier—damps these dynamics, sharply reducing safety-constraint violations with little cost to return on Safety Gym tasks.

papers.title: A Game Theoretic Perspective on Model-Based Reinforcement Learning
papers.summary: Recasts model-based RL as a Stackelberg game between a policy player maximizing reward under a learned model and a model player fitting the policy's real-world data. The resulting two-timescale PAL/MAL algorithms stabilize training, mitigate model exploitation, and deliver strong sample efficiency on continuous control.

papers.title: Conditional Augmentation for Generative Modeling
papers.summary: Applies aggressive data augmentation to generative-model training while conditioning the model on which transformation was applied, keeping the modeled distribution unbiased. This regularization lets large autoregressive image models train longer without overfitting, improving density estimation and sample quality (published as Distribution Augmentation, DistAug).

papers.title: Learning General-Purpose Controllers via Locally Communicating Sensorimotor Modules
papers.summary: Builds locomotion controllers from identical per-limb sensorimotor modules that coordinate only via learned local message passing, with no centralized controller. A single shared modular policy simultaneously controls many agent morphologies and generalizes zero-shot to unseen body plans (published as 'One Policy to Control Them All').

papers.title: The Monte Carlo Transformer
papers.abstract: This paper introduces the Sequential Monte Carlo Transformer, an original approach that naturally captures the observations distribution in a recurrent architecture. The keys, queries, values and attention vectors of the network are considered as the unobserved stochastic states of its hidden structure. This generative model is such that at each time step the received observation is a random function of these past states in a given attention window. In this general state-space setting, we use Sequential Monte Carlo methods to approximate the posterior distributions of the states given the observations, and then to estimate the gradient of the log-likelihood. We thus propose a generative m…
papers.summary: Recasts attention's keys, queries, values, and outputs as latent stochastic states in a state-space model. Uses Sequential Monte Carlo to approximate state posteriors given observations and to estimate log-likelihood gradients, yielding a Transformer that outputs predictive distributions rather than point estimates.
papers.venue: HAL (Le Centre pour la Communication Scientifique Directe)

papers.title: A Kernel-Based Approach to Non-Stationary Reinforcement Learning in Metric Spaces
papers.abstract: In this work, we propose KeRNS: an algorithm for episodic reinforcement learning in non-stationary Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) whose state-action set is endowed with a metric. Using a non-parametric model of the MDP built with time-dependent kernels, we prove a regret bound that scales with the covering dimension of the state-action space and the total variation of the MDP with time, which quantifies its level of non-stationarity. Our method generalizes previous approaches based on sliding windows and exponential discounting used to handle changing environments. We further propose a practical implementation of KeRNS, we analyze its regret and validate it experimentally.
papers.summary: KeRNS, an algorithm for episodic RL in non-stationary MDPs over metric spaces, builds a nonparametric model with time-dependent kernels. It achieves regret scaling with the state-action covering dimension and the environment's total variation over time, generalizing sliding-window and exponential-discounting approaches.

papers.title: Better Document-Level Machine Translation with Bayes’ Rule
papers.abstract: We show that Bayes’ rule provides an effective mechanism for creating document translation models that can be learned from only parallel sentences and monolingual documents a compelling benefit because parallel documents are not always available. In our formulation, the posterior probability of a candidate translation is the product of the unconditional (prior) probability of the candidate output document and the “reverse translation probability” of translating the candidate output back into the source language. Our proposed model uses a powerful autoregressive language model as the prior on target language documents, but it assumes that each sentence is translated independently from the…
papers.summary: Frames document translation via Bayes' rule, combining an autoregressive target-language document prior with sentence-level reverse-translation probabilities. This noisy-channel formulation learns from monolingual documents plus sentence-parallel data, induces cross-sentence dependencies at inference, and outperforms prior document-translation methods with left-to-right beam search.
papers.venue: Transactions of the Association for Computational Linguistics

papers.title: Adaptive Mechanism Design: Learning to Promote Cooperation
papers.abstract: In the future, artificial learning agents are likely to become increasingly widespread in our society. They will interact with both other learning agents and humans in a variety of complex settings including social dilemmas. We consider the problem of how an external agent can promote cooperation between artificial learners by distributing additional rewards and punishments based on observing the learners' actions. We propose a rule for automatically learning how to create right incentives by considering the players' anticipated parameter updates. Using this learning rule leads to cooperation with high social welfare in matrix games in which the agents would otherwise learn to defect with…
papers.summary: An external planning agent learns to distribute extra rewards and punishments to foster cooperation among independent learners in social dilemmas, by anticipating their parameter updates. It induces high-welfare cooperation that, in some games, persists even after intervention stops.

papers.title: Pitfalls of Learning a Reward Function Online
papers.abstract: In some agent designs like inverse reinforcement learning an agent needs to learn its own reward function. Learning the reward function and optimising for it are typically two different processes, usually performed at different stages. We consider a continual (``one life'') learning approach where the agent both learns the reward function and optimises for it at the same time. We show that this comes with a number of pitfalls, such as deliberately manipulating the learning process in one direction, refusing to learn, ``learning'' facts already known to the agent, and making decisions that are strictly dominated (for all relevant reward functions). We formally introduce two desirable prope…
papers.summary: Analyzes agents that learn and optimize a reward function simultaneously, exposing failure modes like manipulating the learning process, refusing to learn, and strictly dominated choices. Formalizes 'unriggability' and 'uninfluenceability' as safeguards, proving uninfluenceable reward-learning processes are automatically unriggable.

papers.title: Automatic Curriculum Learning For Deep RL: A Short Survey
papers.abstract: Automatic Curriculum Learning (ACL) has become a cornerstone of recent successes in Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL). These methods shape the learning trajectories of agents by challenging them with tasks adapted to their capacities. In recent years, they have been used to improve sample efficiency and asymptotic performance, to organize exploration, to encourage generalization or to solve sparse reward problems, among others. To do so, ACL mechanisms can act on many aspects of learning problems. They can optimize domain randomization for Sim2Real transfer, organize task presentations in multi-task robotic settings, order sequences of opponents in multi-agent scenarios, etc. The ambition…
papers.summary: A concise survey of Automatic Curriculum Learning for deep RL, organizing methods that adapt task difficulty to an agent's evolving competence. Covers uses for sample efficiency, exploration, generalization, sparse rewards, sim2real domain randomization, and multi-agent curricula.

papers.title: Learning to Combine Top-Down and Bottom-Up Signals in Recurrent Neural Networks with Attention over Modules
papers.abstract: Robust perception relies on both bottom-up and top-down signals. Bottom-up signals consist of what's directly observed through sensation. Top-down signals consist of beliefs and expectations based on past experience and short-term memory, such as how the phrase `peanut butter and~...' will be completed. The optimal combination of bottom-up and top-down information remains an open question, but the manner of combination must be dynamic and both context and task dependent. To effectively utilize the wealth of potential top-down information available, and to prevent the cacophony of intermixed signals in a bidirectional architecture, mechanisms are needed to restrict information flow. We exp…
papers.summary: A modular recurrent architecture in which bottom-up sensory signals and top-down expectations are combined dynamically via attention, with modularity restricting information flow. This bidirectional flow improves language modeling, sequential image classification, video prediction, and RL, notably boosting robustness to distractions and noise.

papers.title: Sampling from a $k$-DPP without looking at all items
papers.abstract: Determinantal point processes (DPPs) are a useful probabilistic model for selecting a small diverse subset out of a large collection of items, with applications in summarization, stochastic optimization, active learning and more. Given a kernel function and a subset size $k$, our goal is to sample $k$ out of $n$ items with probability proportional to the determinant of the kernel matrix induced by the subset (a.k.a. $k$-DPP). Existing $k$-DPP sampling algorithms require an expensive preprocessing step which involves multiple passes over all $n$ items, making it infeasible for large datasets. A naïve heuristic addressing this problem is to uniformly subsample a fraction of the data and per…
papers.summary: An algorithm for sampling a size-k determinantal point process that avoids preprocessing all n items. It adaptively builds a uniform sub-sample sufficient to draw exactly from the true k-DPP, achieving orders-of-magnitude speedups by observing only a small fraction of the data.

papers.title: Scalable Deep Generative Modeling for Sparse Graphs
papers.abstract: Learning graph generative models is a challenging task for deep learning and has wide applicability to a range of domains like chemistry, biology and social science. However current deep neural methods suffer from limited scalability: for a graph with $n$ nodes and $m$ edges, existing deep neural methods require $Ω(n^2)$ complexity by building up the adjacency matrix. On the other hand, many real world graphs are actually sparse in the sense that $m\ll n^2$. Based on this, we develop a novel autoregressive model, named BiGG, that utilizes this sparsity to avoid generating the full adjacency matrix, and importantly reduces the graph generation time complexity to $O((n + m)\log n)$. Further…
papers.summary: BiGG, an autoregressive generative model for sparse graphs that avoids materializing the full adjacency matrix, cutting generation cost to O((n+m) log n) with O(log n) training parallelism. It scales to far larger graphs than prior deep autoregressive models while improving generation quality.

papers.title: What can I do here? A Theory of Affordances in Reinforcement Learning
papers.abstract: Reinforcement learning algorithms usually assume that all actions are always available to an agent. However, both people and animals understand the general link between the features of their environment and the actions that are feasible. Gibson (1977) coined the term "affordances" to describe the fact that certain states enable an agent to do certain actions, in the context of embodied agents. In this paper, we develop a theory of affordances for agents who learn and plan in Markov Decision Processes. Affordances play a dual role in this case. On one hand, they allow faster planning, by reducing the number of actions available in any given situation. On the other hand, they facilitate mor…
papers.summary: Develops a theory of affordances, the state-dependent set of feasible actions, for agents in MDPs. Affordances speed planning by pruning actions and enable more sample-efficient, generalizable transition-model learning under function approximation, supported by theory, examples, and a method to learn them.

papers.title: On the Generalization Benefit of Noise in Stochastic Gradient Descent
papers.abstract: It has long been argued that minibatch stochastic gradient descent can generalize better than large batch gradient descent in deep neural networks. However recent papers have questioned this claim, arguing that this effect is simply a consequence of suboptimal hyperparameter tuning or insufficient compute budgets when the batch size is large. In this paper, we perform carefully designed experiments and rigorous hyperparameter sweeps on a range of popular models, which verify that small or moderately large batch sizes can substantially outperform very large batches on the test set. This occurs even when both models are trained for the same number of iterations and large batches achieve sma…
papers.summary: Through careful experiments and rigorous hyperparameter sweeps, confirms that small to moderate batch sizes generalize better than very large batches even at equal iterations and lower training loss. Attributes the benefit to gradient noise and analyzes optimal learning-rate schedules via an SDE perspective.

papers.title: The influence of microsatellite polymorphisms in sex steroid receptor genes ESR1, ESR2 and AR on sex differences in brain structure
papers.abstract: The androgen receptor (AR), oestrogen receptor alpha (ESR1) and oestrogen receptor beta (ESR2) play essential roles in mediating the effect of sex hormones on sex differences in the brain. Using Voxel-based morphometry (VBM) and gene sizing in two independent samples (discovery n ​= ​173, replication ​= ​61), we determine the common and unique influences on brain sex differences in grey (GM) and white matter (WM) volume between repeat lengths (n) of microsatellite polymorphisms AR(CAG)n, ESR1(TA)n and ESR2(CA)n. In the hypothalamus, temporal lobes, anterior cingulate cortex, posterior insula and prefrontal cortex, we find increased GM volume with increasing AR(CAG)n across sexes, decreasi…
papers.summary: Using voxel-based morphometry and gene sizing across two cohorts, links repeat-length polymorphisms in the AR, ESR1, and ESR2 sex-steroid receptor genes to grey- and white-matter volume differences in regions including hypothalamus and prefrontal cortex, mapping their shared and unique neuroanatomical correlates.

papers.title: Scaling MAP-Elites to deep neuroevolution
papers.abstract: Quality-Diversity (QD) algorithms, and MAP-Elites (ME) in particular, have proven very useful for a broad range of applications including enabling real robots to recover quickly from joint damage, solving strongly deceptive maze tasks or evolving robot morphologies to discover new gaits. However, present implementations of ME and other QD algorithms seem to be limited to low-dimensional controllers with far fewer parameters than modern deep neural network models. In this paper, we propose to leverage the efficiency of Evolution Strategies (ES) to scale MAP-Elites to high-dimensional controllers parameterized by large neural networks. We design and evaluate a new hybrid algorithm called MA…
papers.summary: Combines MAP-Elites quality-diversity search with Evolution Strategies to scale to high-dimensional neural-network controllers. The hybrid ME-ES enables post-damage recovery on a hard control task where standard MAP-Elites fails, and explores as well as state-of-the-art methods under strongly deceptive rewards.

papers.title: Replay bursts coincide with activation of the default mode and parietal alpha network
papers.abstract: Abstract Our brains at rest spontaneously replay recently acquired information, but how this process is orchestrated to avoid interference with ongoing cognition is an open question. We investigated whether replay coincided with spontaneous patterns of whole brain activity. We found, in two separate datasets, that replay sequences were packaged into transient bursts occurring selectively during activation of the default mode network (DMN) and parietal alpha network. These networks were characterized by widespread synchronized oscillations coupled to increases in ripple band power, mechanisms that coordinate information flow between disparate cortical areas. Our data show a tight correspon…
papers.summary: Using neuroimaging across two datasets, finds that spontaneous memory-replay sequences cluster into transient bursts occurring selectively during activation of the default mode and parietal alpha networks. Suggests these networks coordinate replay via synchronized oscillations and ripple-band power while minimizing interference with ongoing cognition.

papers.title: Stochastic bandits with arm-dependent delays
papers.abstract: Significant work has been recently dedicated to the stochastic delayed bandit setting because of its relevance in applications. The applicability of existing algorithms is however restricted by the fact that strong assumptions are often made on the delay distributions, such as full observability, restrictive shape constraints, or uniformity over arms. In this work, we weaken them significantly and only assume that there is a bound on the tail of the delay. In particular, we cover the important case where the delay distributions vary across arms, and the case where the delays are heavy-tailed. Addressing these difficulties, we propose a simple but efficient UCB-based algorithm called the P…
