papers.abstract: Grasping objects under uncertainty remains an open problem in robotics research. This uncertainty is often due to noisy or partial observations of the object pose or shape. To enable a robot to react appropriately to unforeseen effects, it is crucial that it continuously takes sensor feedback into account. While visual feedback is important for inferring a grasp pose and reaching for an object, contact feedback offers valuable information during manipulation and grasp acquisition. In this paper, we use model-free deep reinforcement learning to synthesize control policies that exploit contact sensing to generate robust grasping under uncertainty. We demonstrate our approach on a multi-fing…
papers.summary: Uses model-free deep RL to learn multi-fingered grasping policies that exploit contact-force sensing. Contact feedback substantially improves grasp robustness under object-pose uncertainty and for complex shapes, compared with vision-only policies.

papers.title: A Practical Approach to Insertion with Variable Socket Position Using Deep Reinforcement Learning
papers.abstract: Insertion is a challenging haptic and visual control problem with significant practical value for manufacturing. Existing approaches in the model-based robotics community can be highly effective when task geometry is known, but are complex and cumbersome to implement, and must be tailored to each individual problem by a qualified engineer. Within the learning community there is a long history of insertion research, but existing approaches are either too sample-inefficient to run on real robots, or assume access to high-level object features, e.g. socket pose. In this paper we show that relatively minor modifications to an off-the-shelf Deep-RL algorithm (DDPG), combined with a small numbe…
papers.summary: Shows minor modifications to DDPG plus a few human demonstrations let a real robot learn peg- and clip-insertion tasks with variable socket position in under 10 minutes, with no modeling, simulation, reward shaping, or pose input beyond raw images.

papers.title: T157. Neurocomputational Plasticity of the Self-Other Distinction
papers.summary: A computational-neuroscience conference presentation examining the neural and computational mechanisms underlying the self-other distinction and how that distinction can be reshaped (plasticity).

papers.title: MuseNet
papers.abstract: We’ve created MuseNet, a deep neural network that can generate 4-minute musical compositions with 10 different instruments, and can combine styles from country to Mozart to the Beatles. MuseNet was not explicitly programmed with our understanding of music, but instead discovered patterns of harmony, rhythm, and style by learning to predict the next token in hundreds of thousands of MIDI files. MuseNet uses the same general-purpose unsupervised technology as GPT-2, a large-scale transformer model trained to predict the next token in a sequence, whether audio or text.
papers.summary: MuseNet generates 4-minute, 10-instrument musical compositions spanning styles from country to Mozart, using the same GPT-2-style transformer trained to predict the next token over hundreds of thousands of MIDI files, discovering harmony, rhythm, and style without hand-coded music theory.

papers.title: Generative modeling with sparse transformers
papers.abstract: We’ve developed the Sparse Transformer, a deep neural network which sets new records at predicting what comes next in a sequence—whether text, images, or sound. It uses an algorithmic improvement of the attention mechanism to extract patterns from sequences 30x longer than possible previously.
papers.summary: Introduces the Sparse Transformer, which factorizes the attention mechanism to cut its compute and memory cost, enabling modeling of sequences roughly 30x longer and setting new records on autoregressive generation of text, images, and audio.

papers.title: From GAN to WGAN
papers.abstract: This paper explains the math behind a generative adversarial network (GAN) model and why it is hard to be trained. Wasserstein GAN is intended to improve GANs' training by adopting a smooth metric for measuring the distance between two probability distributions.
papers.summary: A tutorial deriving the math behind GANs and explaining why their training is unstable, then showing how Wasserstein GAN replaces the objective with the smoother Wasserstein distance to improve training stability.

papers.title: Reinforcement Learning, Fast and Slow
papers.abstract: Recent AI research has given rise to powerful techniques for deep reinforcement learning. In their combination of representation learning with reward-driven behavior, deep reinforcement learning would appear to have inherent interest for psychology and neuroscience. One reservation has been that deep reinforcement learning procedures demand large amounts of training data, suggesting that these algorithms may differ fundamentally from those underlying human learning. While this concern applies to the initial wave of deep RL techniques, subsequent AI work has established methods that allow deep RL systems to learn more quickly and efficiently. Two particularly interesting and promising tech…
papers.summary: A review arguing that sample-inefficiency criticisms of deep RL are answered by episodic-memory and meta-learning methods, which enable fast learning atop slow learning, and discussing the implications of these mechanisms for psychology and neuroscience.

papers.title: Low Bit-rate Speech Coding with VQ-VAE and a WaveNet Decoder
papers.abstract: In order to efficiently transmit and store speech signals, speech codecs create a minimally redundant representation of the input signal which is then decoded at the receiver with the best possible perceptual quality. In this work we demonstrate that a neural network architecture based on VQ-VAE with a WaveNet decoder can be used to perform very low bit-rate speech coding with high reconstruction quality. A prosody-transparent and speaker-independent model trained on the LibriSpeech corpus coding audio at 1.6 kbps exhibits perceptual quality which is around halfway between the MELP codec at 2.4 kbps and AMR-WB codec at 23.05 kbps. In addition, when training on high-quality recorded speech…
papers.summary: Demonstrates very low bit-rate (1.6 kbps) speech coding with a VQ-VAE and a WaveNet decoder. A speaker-independent model on LibriSpeech reaches quality between MELP at 2.4 kbps and AMR-WB at 23 kbps, approaching AMR-WB when trained on the test speaker.

papers.title: OpenAI Five defeats Dota 2 world champions
papers.abstract: OpenAI Five is the first AI to beat the world champions in an esports game, having won two back-to-back games versus the world champion Dota 2 team, OG, at Finals this weekend. Both OpenAI Five and DeepMind’s AlphaStar had previously beaten good pros privately but lost their live pro matches, making this also the first time an AI has beaten esports pros on livestream.
papers.summary: OpenAI Five became the first AI to defeat reigning world champions (team OG) at Dota 2, winning two livestreamed games. It was trained via large-scale self-play deep RL on the complex, partially observable, long-horizon multiplayer game.

papers.title: The value of what’s to come: neural mechanisms coupling prediction error and reward anticipation
papers.abstract: Having something to look forward to is a keystone of well-being. Anticipation of a future reward, like an upcoming vacation, can often be more gratifying than the very experience itself. Theories of anticipation have described how it induces behaviors ranging from beneficial information-seeking through to harmful addiction. However, it remains unclear how neural systems compute an attractive value from anticipation, instead of from the reward itself. To address this gap, we administered a decision-making task to human participants that allowed us to analyze brain activity during receipt of information predictive of future pleasant outcomes. Using a computational model of anticipatory valu…
papers.summary: Combines an fMRI decision task with a computational model of anticipatory value, showing that anticipating pleasant outcomes is computed via vmPFC (tracking anticipation value), dopaminergic midbrain (responding to anticipation-enhancing information), and sustained hippocampal activity coupling the two.

papers.title: Food Webs: Insights from a General Ecosystem Model
papers.abstract: Abstract Food webs have been intensively studied throughout modern ecology, using empirical evidence, statistics and modelling tools to search for consistent patterns, underlying commonalities, and variations between food webs and ecosystems. However, with few exceptions, the modelling approaches have not been based on the emergent properties of complex simulated ecosystems. For the same reason, there have also been few studies of ‘sampling the model’, in which different levels of sampling effort are imposed in a controlled simulated environment to explore the effects of varying sampling intensity in order to relate those back to empirical observations. Here, we introduce the Madingley Mo…
papers.summary: Applies the Madingley general ecosystem model, built from first-principle interactions between individual organisms, to study food webs. Aggregating and sub-sampling its emergent networks reproduces properties of empirical food webs and reveals how sampling effort shapes observed structure.

papers.title: Implementation of a Digitally Enabled Care Pathway (Part 2): Qualitative Analysis of Experiences of Health Care Professionals
papers.abstract: BACKGROUND: One reason for the introduction of digital technologies into health care has been to try to improve safety and patient outcomes by providing real-time access to patient data and enhancing communication among health care professionals. However, the adoption of such technologies into clinical pathways has been less examined, and the impacts on users and the broader health system are poorly understood. We sought to address this by studying the impacts of introducing a digitally enabled care pathway for patients with acute kidney injury (AKI) at a tertiary referral hospital in the United Kingdom. A dedicated clinical response team-comprising existing nephrology and patient-at-risk…
papers.summary: Qualitative interviews with 19 clinicians evaluating Streams, a mobile app delivering real-time acute-kidney-injury alerts, found the digitally enabled care pathway improved access to patient data and reshaped interprofessional working practices and relationships.

papers.title: Rapid advances in auto-segmentation of organs at risk and target volumes in head and neck cancer
papers.summary: A commentary reviewing recent rapid progress in automated segmentation of organs-at-risk and target volumes for head and neck cancer radiotherapy planning, where machine-learning auto-segmentation methods are advancing quickly.

papers.title: Implicit generation and generalization methods for energy-based models
papers.abstract: We’ve made progress towards stable and scalable training of energy-based models (EBMs) resulting in better sample quality and generalization ability than existing models. Generation in EBMs spends more compute to continually refine its answers and doing so can generate samples competitive with GANs at low temperatures, while also having mode coverage guarantees of likelihood-based models. We hope these findings stimulate further research into this promising class of models.
papers.summary: Reports progress on stable, scalable training of energy-based models, where generation iteratively refines samples using more compute. The approach yields sample quality competitive with GANs at low temperatures while retaining the mode coverage of likelihood-based models.

papers.title: A Generic Human–Machine Annotation Framework Based on Dynamic Cooperative Learning
papers.abstract: The task of obtaining meaningful annotations is a tedious work, incurring considerable costs and time consumption. Dynamic active learning and cooperative learning are recently proposed approaches to reduce human effort of annotating data with subjective phenomena. In this paper, we introduce a novel generic annotation framework, with the aim to achieve the optimal tradeoff between label reliability and cost reduction by making efficient use of human and machine work force. To this end, we use dropout to assess model uncertainty and thereby to decide which instances can be automatically labeled by the machine and which ones require human inspection. In addition, we propose an early stoppi…
papers.summary: Proposes a generic human-machine annotation framework using dropout-based uncertainty to route easy instances to automatic labeling and ambiguous ones to humans, plus an inter-rater-agreement stopping rule. On non-native English prosody it cut human annotations 74% with comparable accuracy.

papers.title: The U-net and its impact to medical imaging (Conference Presentation)
papers.abstract: The U-net has become the predominant choice when facing any medical image segmentation task. This is due to its high performance in many different medical domains. In this talk, I will introduce the U-net, and I will present three projects from DeepMind Health Research that use the U-net to address different challenges. The first project, a collaboration with University College London Hospital, deals with the challenging task of the precise segmentation of radiosensitive head and neck anatomy in CT scans, an essential input for radiotherapy planning. The second project, together with Moorfields Eye Hospital, developed a system that analyses 3D OCT (optical coherence tomography) eye scans…
papers.summary: A talk introducing the U-net and three DeepMind Health applications: head-and-neck CT segmentation for radiotherapy, expert-level referral from 3D OCT eye scans, and a U-net/conditional-VAE that produces multiple calibrated segmentation hypotheses for ambiguous medical images.

papers.title: Exploring mobile working in healthcare: Clinical perspectives on transitioning to a mobile first culture of work
papers.summary: A qualitative study of clinicians' perspectives on shifting to a mobile-first culture of work in healthcare, exploring the practical opportunities and challenges of adopting mobile working practices in clinical settings.

papers.title: Introducing Activation Atlases
papers.abstract: We’ve created activation atlases (in collaboration with Google researchers), a new technique for visualizing what interactions between neurons can represent. As AI systems are deployed in increasingly sensitive contexts, having a better understanding of their internal decision-making processes will let us identify weaknesses and investigate failures.
papers.summary: Introduces activation atlases (with Google), an interpretability technique that aggregates feature visualizations over many dataset examples to map what combinations of neurons in vision models represent, revealing global structure in learned features and exposing failure modes.

papers.title: Backpropagation through time and the brain
papers.abstract: It has long been speculated that the backpropagation-of-error algorithm (backprop) may be a model of how the brain learns. Backpropagation-through-time (BPTT) is the canonical temporal-analogue to backprop used to assign credit in recurrent neural networks in machine learning, but there's even less conviction about whether BPTT has anything to do with the brain. Even in machine learning the use of BPTT in classic neural network architectures has proven insufficient for some challenging temporal credit assignment (TCA) problems that we know the brain is capable of solving. Nonetheless, recent work in machine learning has made progress in solving difficult TCA problems by employing novel me…
papers.summary: A review assessing backpropagation-through-time as a model of temporal credit assignment in the brain, arguing that recent memory- and attention-based architectures, developed in reference to BPTT, strengthen its role as a normative guide for biological credit assignment.

papers.title: Activation Atlas
papers.abstract: By using feature inversion to visualize millions of activations from an image classification network, we create an explorable activation atlas of features the network has learned and what concepts it typically represents.
papers.summary: Builds an explorable activation atlas by using feature inversion to visualize millions of activations from an image-classification network, revealing the features and concepts the model has learned and how they combine.

papers.title: Neural MMO: A massively multiagent game environment
papers.abstract: We’re releasing a Neural MMO, a massively multiagent game environment for reinforcement learning agents. Our platform supports a large, variable number of agents within a persistent and open-ended task. The inclusion of many agents and species leads to better exploration, divergent niche formation, and greater overall competence.
papers.summary: Releases a massively multiagent game environment for RL supporting large, variable numbers of agents in a persistent, open-ended world. Training many agents together yields better exploration, divergent niche formation, and higher overall competence.

papers.title: The Termination Critic
papers.abstract: In this work, we consider the problem of autonomously discovering behavioral abstractions, or options, for reinforcement learning agents. We propose an algorithm that focuses on the termination condition, as opposed to -- as is common -- the policy. The termination condition is usually trained to optimize a control objective: an option ought to terminate if another has better value. We offer a different, information-theoretic perspective, and propose that terminations should focus instead on the compressibility of the option's encoding -- arguably a key reason for using abstractions. To achieve this algorithmically, we leverage the classical options framework, and learn the option transit…
papers.summary: Proposes discovering RL options by optimizing their termination conditions from an information-theoretic view, favoring options whose encodings are compressible. Learns an option transition model as a critic to derive termination gradients, yielding meaningful, useful options.

papers.title: Revisiting CFR+ and Alternating Updates
papers.abstract: The CFR+ algorithm for solving imperfect information games is a variant of the popular CFR algorithm, with faster empirical performance on a range of problems. It was introduced with a theoretical upper bound on solution error, but subsequent work showed an error in one step of the proof. We provide updated proofs to recover the original bound.
papers.summary: Re-examines CFR+, a faster variant of counterfactual regret minimization for solving imperfect-information games. After prior work found a flaw in its convergence proof, provides corrected proofs that recover the original error bound and clarifies alternating-update behavior.

papers.title: Strategic behavior and learning in all-pay auctions: an empirical study using crowdsourced data
papers.summary: Models crowdsourcing contests as all-pay auctions and runs Mechanical Turk experiments comparing human bidding to game-theoretic equilibrium and online-learning algorithms. Finds bimodal effort, a tendency to overbid, and that humans behave as weak learners.

papers.title: AI safety needs social scientists
papers.abstract: We’ve written a paper arguing that long-term AI safety research needs social scientists to ensure AI alignment algorithms succeed when actual humans are involved. Properly aligning advanced AI systems with human values requires resolving many uncertainties related to the psychology of human rationality, emotion, and biases. The aim of this paper is to spark further collaboration between machine learning and social science researchers, and we plan to hire social scientists to work on this full time at OpenAI.
papers.summary: Argues that aligning advanced AI with human values, e.g. via debate or amplification that query humans, requires resolving open questions about human rationality, emotion, and bias, and calls for ML researchers to collaborate with social scientists on these experiments.

papers.title: Piano Genie
papers.abstract: We present Piano Genie, an intelligent controller which allows non-musicians to improvise on the piano. With Piano Genie, a user performs on a simple interface with eight buttons, and their performance is decoded into the space of plausible piano music in real time. To learn a suitable mapping procedure for this problem, we train recurrent neural network autoencoders with discrete bottlenecks: an encoder learns an appropriate sequence of buttons corresponding to a piano piece, and a decoder learns to map this sequence back to the original piece. During performance, we substitute a user's input for the encoder output, and play the decoder's prediction each time the user presses a button. T…
papers.summary: Presents an interface letting non-musicians improvise piano: eight buttons are decoded in real time into plausible performances. Trains RNN autoencoders with discrete bottlenecks so button presses map to musically coherent note sequences, with constraints improving intuitiveness.

papers.title: Analyzing biological and artificial neural networks: challenges with opportunities for synergy?
papers.abstract: Deep neural networks (DNNs) transform stimuli across multiple processing stages to produce representations that can be used to solve complex tasks, such as object recognition in images. However, a full understanding of how they achieve this remains elusive. The complexity of biological neural networks substantially exceeds the complexity of DNNs, making it even more challenging to understand the representations they learn. Thus, both machine learning and computational neuroscience are faced with a shared challenge: how can we analyze their representations in order to understand how they solve complex tasks? We review how data-analysis concepts and techniques developed by computational neu…
papers.summary: Reviews how representation-analysis techniques from computational neuroscience can illuminate deep network representations, and conversely how DNN-analysis methods aid neuroscience. Argues for synergy, including using DNNs as in silico model systems to generate hypotheses about biological computation.

papers.title: Better language models and their implications
papers.abstract: We’ve trained a large-scale unsupervised language model which generates coherent paragraphs of text, achieves state-of-the-art performance on many language modeling benchmarks, and performs rudimentary reading comprehension, machine translation, question answering, and summarization—all without task-specific training.
papers.summary: Announces GPT-2, a large transformer language model trained on WebText that generates coherent long-form text and performs reading comprehension, translation, QA, and summarization zero-shot, reaching SOTA on several language-modeling benchmarks; notes misuse concerns motivating a staged release.

papers.title: From Pixels to Percepts: Highly Robust Edge Perception and Contour Following Using Deep Learning and an Optical Biomimetic Tactile Sensor
papers.abstract: Deep learning has the potential to have same the impact on robot touch as it has had on robot vision. Optical tactile sensors act as a bridge between the subjects by allowing techniques from vision to be applied to touch. In this letter, we apply deep learning to an optical biomimetic tactile sensor, the TacTip, which images an array of papillae (pins) inside its sensing surface analogous to structures within human skin. Our main result is that the application of a deep convolutional neural network can give reliable edge perception and, thus a robust policy for planning contact points to move around object contours. Robustness is demonstrated over several irregular and compliant objects w…
papers.summary: Applies a deep convolutional network to images from the TacTip optical biomimetic tactile sensor, achieving robust edge perception and a policy for following object contours. A model trained only on tapping a disk generalizes to irregular, compliant objects via tapping and sliding.

papers.title: Action Recognition Happens Very Quickly in the Human Brain
papers.abstract: “What is that person doing?” Without even thinking about it, your brain is processing what you see and using it to recognize other people’s actions, like running and walking. Recognizing actions comes easily to humans, but it is an extremely challenging problem for computers to solve. In particular, it is difficult for computers to recognize the same actions from different viewpoints, like front or side views, or against different backgrounds. As a result, scientists and engineers have not been able to develop computer algorithms that can recognize actions as well as humans can. Our research goal is to better understand how the human brain recognizes actions so we can replicate those func…
papers.summary: A young-audience account of neuroscience research showing the human brain recognizes others' actions very rapidly and across viewpoints, a task computers struggle with; the work aims to understand these brain mechanisms to inform action-recognition algorithms.

papers.title: Distilling Policy Distillation
papers.abstract: The transfer of knowledge from one policy to another is an important tool in Deep Reinforcement Learning. This process, referred to as distillation, has been used to great success, for example, by enhancing the optimisation of agents, leading to stronger performance faster, on harder domains [26, 32, 5, 8]. Despite the widespread use and conceptual simplicity of distillation, many different formulations are used in practice, and the subtle variations between them can often drastically change the performance and the resulting objective that is being optimised. In this work, we rigorously explore the entire landscape of policy distillation, comparing the motivations and strengths of each va…
papers.summary: Systematically analyzes the many formulations of policy distillation in deep RL, showing subtle variants change the optimized objective and performance. Recommends three techniques by task and proposes an expected-entropy-regularized distillation that speeds learning while guaranteeing convergence.

papers.title: Computational limitations in robust classification and win-win results
papers.abstract: We continue the study of statistical/computational tradeoffs in learning robust classifiers, following the recent work of Bubeck, Lee, Price and Razenshteyn who showed examples of classification tasks where (a) an efficient robust classifier exists, in the small-perturbation regime; (b) a non-robust classifier can be learned efficiently; but (c) it is computationally hard to learn a robust classifier, assuming the hardness of factoring large numbers. The question of whether a robust classifier for their task exists in the large perturbation regime seems related to important open questions in computational number theory. In this work, we extend their work in three directions. First, we dem…
papers.summary: Extends results on statistical-computational tradeoffs in adversarially robust learning, constructing classification tasks where efficient robust learning is provably hard, even when computationally unbounded robust classifiers exist, based on average-case hardness, one-way functions, and learning-parity-with-noise.

papers.title: Adaptive long-term control of biological neural networks with Deep Reinforcement Learning
papers.summary: Uses deep reinforcement learning to control living neuronal cultures grown on microelectrode arrays, clamping their response strengths to target levels over long durations despite non-stationary drift. Learned controllers beat random and linear LSPI baselines in 27 of 29 networks.

papers.title: Feature Control as Intrinsic Motivation for Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning
papers.abstract: One of the main concerns of deep reinforcement learning (DRL) is the data inefficiency problem, which stems both from an inability to fully utilize data acquired and from naive exploration strategies. In order to alleviate these problems, we propose a DRL algorithm that aims to improve data efficiency via both the utilization of unrewarded experiences and the exploration strategy by combining ideas from unsupervised auxiliary tasks, intrinsic motivation, and hierarchical reinforcement learning (HRL). Our method is based on a simple HRL architecture with a metacontroller and a subcontroller. The subcontroller is intrinsically motivated by the metacontroller to learn to control aspects of t…
papers.summary: Combines hierarchical RL with intrinsic motivation by treating pixel- and feature-control auxiliary tasks as reusable skills a subcontroller learns under a metacontroller's direction. Improves data efficiency and exploration on Atari, notably on the hard-exploration game Montezuma's Revenge.
papers.venue: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems

papers.title: Psychological Artificial Intelligence Service, Tess: Delivering On-demand Support to Patients and Their Caregivers: Technical Report
papers.abstract: This technical report highlights how one mental health chatbot, or psychological artificial intelligence service named Tess, has been customized to deliver on-demand support for caregiving professionals, patients, and family caregivers at a non-profit organization. This low-cost, user friendly, and highly customizable service allows emotional support to be scaled to thousands of people at a single time. The following report describes the phased approach to implementing Tess in order to reach staff, caregivers, and patients across Canada and the United States.
papers.summary: A technical report describing how the mental-health chatbot Tess was customized to deliver scalable, on-demand emotional support to caregivers, patients, and families at a non-profit, outlining a phased deployment across Canada and the United States.

papers.title: Degenerate Feedback Loops in Recommender Systems
papers.abstract: Machine learning is used extensively in recommender systems deployed in products. The decisions made by these systems can influence user beliefs and preferences which in turn affect the feedback the learning system receives - thus creating a feedback loop. This phenomenon can give rise to the so-called "echo chambers" or "filter bubbles" that have user and societal implications. In this paper, we provide a novel theoretical analysis that examines both the role of user dynamics and the behavior of recommender systems, disentangling the echo chamber from the filter bubble effect. In addition, we offer practical solutions to slow down system degeneracy. Our study contributes toward understan…
papers.summary: Provides a theoretical analysis of feedback loops in recommender systems, disentangling 'echo chamber' from 'filter bubble' effects by separating user dynamics from system behavior, and proposes practical methods to slow the resulting degeneration of recommendations.

papers.title: Ranking soccer teams on the basis of their current strength: A comparison of maximum likelihood approaches
papers.abstract: We present 10 different strength-based statistical models that we use to model soccer match outcomes with the aim of producing a new ranking. The models are of four main types: Thurstone–Mosteller, Bradley–Terry, independent Poisson and bivariate Poisson, and their common aspect is that the parameters are estimated via weighted maximum likelihood, the weights being a match importance factor and a time depreciation factor giving less weight to matches that are played a long time ago. Since our goal is to build a ranking reflecting the teams’ current strengths, we compare the 10 models on the basis of their predictive performance via the Rank Probability Score at the level of both domestic…
papers.summary: Compares ten strength-based statistical models, including Thurstone-Mosteller, Bradley-Terry, and independent/bivariate Poisson, fit by weighted maximum likelihood with match-importance and time-decay weights to rank soccer teams. Bivariate and independent Poisson models give the best predictive Rank Probability Score.

papers.title: Toward high-performance, memory-efficient, and fast reinforcement learning—Lessons from decision neuroscience
papers.abstract: Recent insights from decision neuroscience raise hope for the development of intelligent brain-inspired solutions to robot learning in real dynamic environments full of noise and unpredictability.
papers.summary: A perspective arguing that insights from decision neuroscience, on how brains learn efficiently and robustly, can inspire reinforcement-learning solutions for robots operating in noisy, unpredictable real-world environments.

papers.title: Optimistic optimization of a Brownian
papers.abstract: We address the problem of optimizing a Brownian motion. We consider a (random) realization $W$ of a Brownian motion with input space in $[0,1]$. Given $W$, our goal is to return an $ε$-approximation of its maximum using the smallest possible number of function evaluations, the sample complexity of the algorithm. We provide an algorithm with sample complexity of order $\log^2(1/ε)$. This improves over previous results of Al-Mharmah and Calvin (1996) and Calvin et al. (2017) which provided only polynomial rates. Our algorithm is adaptive---each query depends on previous values---and is an instance of the optimism-in-the-face-of-uncertainty principle.
papers.summary: Studies optimizing a single realization of Brownian motion on [0,1], returning an epsilon-approximate maximum with the fewest function evaluations. An adaptive algorithm using the optimism-in-the-face-of-uncertainty principle attains sample complexity of order log^2(1/epsilon), improving prior polynomial rates.

papers.title: Activity in perceptual classification networks as a basis for human subjective time perception
papers.abstract: Despite being a fundamental dimension of experience, how the human brain generates the perception of time remains unknown. Here, we provide a novel explanation for how human time perception might be accomplished, based on non-temporal perceptual classification processes. To demonstrate this proposal, we build an artificial neural system centred on a feed-forward image classification network, functionally similar to human visual processing. In this system, input videos of natural scenes drive changes in network activation, and accumulation of salient changes in activation are used to estimate duration. Estimates produced by this system match human reports made about the same videos, replic…
papers.summary: Proposes that human duration perception arises from accumulating salient changes in the activations of perceptual classification networks. A system built on a feed-forward image classifier estimates the duration of natural-scene videos and reproduces key human timing biases.

papers.title: Credit Assignment Techniques in Stochastic Computation Graphs
papers.abstract: Stochastic computation graphs (SCGs) provide a formalism to represent structured optimization problems arising in artificial intelligence, including supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning. Previous work has shown that an unbiased estimator of the gradient of the expected loss of SCGs can be derived from a single principle. However, this estimator often has high variance and requires a full model evaluation per data point, making this algorithm costly in large graphs. In this work, we address these problems by generalizing concepts from the reinforcement learning literature. We introduce the concepts of value functions, baselines and critics for arbitrary SCGs, and show how t…
papers.summary: Generalizes value functions, baselines, and critics from reinforcement learning to arbitrary stochastic computation graphs, deriving lower-variance gradient estimators from partial model evaluations. Unifies recent advances in probabilistic inference and RL under a common credit-assignment framework.

papers.title: nbgrader: A Tool for Creating and Grading Assignments in the Jupyter Notebook
papers.abstract: nbgrader is a flexible tool for creating and grading assignments in the Jupyter Notebook nbgrader allows instructors to create a single, master copy of an assignment, including tests and canonical solutions. From the master copy, a student version is generated without the solutions, thus obviating the need to maintain two separate versions. nbgrader also automatically grades submitted assignments by executing the notebooks and storing the results of the tests in a database. After auto-grading, instructors can manually grade free responses and provide partial credit using the formgrader Jupyter Notebook extension. Finally, instructors can use nbgrader to leave personalized feedback for eac…
papers.summary: Describes nbgrader, an open-source tool for authoring and grading Jupyter Notebook assignments: instructors maintain one master copy with solutions and tests, auto-generate student versions, auto-grade by executing notebooks, and add manual feedback.

papers.title: Reconciling deep learning with symbolic artificial intelligence: representing objects and relations
papers.abstract: In the history of the quest for human-level artificial intelligence, a number of rival paradigms have vied for supremacy. Symbolic artificial intelligence was dominant for much of the 20th century, but currently a connectionist paradigm is in the ascendant, namely machine learning with deep neural networks. However, both paradigms have strengths and weaknesses, and a significant challenge for the field today is to effect a reconciliation. A central tenet of the symbolic paradigm is that intelligence results from the manipulation of abstract compositional representations whose elements stand for objects and relations. If this is correct, then a key objective for deep learning is to develop…
papers.summary: Reviews efforts to reconcile connectionist deep learning with symbolic AI's compositional representations of objects and relations, highlighting recent architectures that learn to discover objects and relational structure from raw data for downstream processing.

papers.title: Analogues of mental simulation and imagination in deep learning
papers.abstract: Mental simulation — the capacity to imagine what will or what could be — is a salient feature of human cognition, playing a key role in a wide range of cognitive abilities. In artificial intelligence, the last few years have seen the development of methods which are analogous to mental models and mental simulation. This paper outlines recent methods in deep learning for constructing such models from data and learning to use them via reinforcement learning, and compares such approaches to human mental simulation. Model-based methods in deep learning can serve as powerful tools for building and scaling cognitive models. However, a number of challenges remain in matching the capacity of huma…
papers.summary: Reviews deep-learning methods analogous to human mental simulation, learning world models from data and using them via reinforcement learning, and compares them with human imagination. Notes remaining gaps in efficiency, compositionality, generalization, and creativity.

papers.title: Task representations in neural networks trained to perform many cognitive tasks
papers.summary: Trains a single recurrent network on 20 cognitive tasks and finds that units self-organize into clusters functionally specialized for distinct cognitive processes. Introduces fractional task variance to quantify single-unit task selectivity and shows tasks share compositional representations.

papers.title: Average Age of Information With Hybrid ARQ Under a Resource Constraint
papers.abstract: Scheduling the transmission of status updates over an error-prone communication channel is studied in order to minimize the long-term average age of information at the destination under a constraint on the average number of transmissions at the source node. After each transmission, the source receives an instantaneous ACK/NACK feedback, and decides on the next update without prior knowledge on the success of future transmissions. The optimal scheduling policy is first studied under different feedback mechanisms when the channel statistics are known; in particular, the standard automatic repeat request (ARQ) and hybrid ARQ (HARQ) protocols are considered. The structural results are derived…
papers.summary: Schedules status updates over an error-prone channel to minimize long-term average age of information under a transmission budget, deriving the optimal policy for ARQ and structural results for HARQ, plus an average-cost reinforcement-learning algorithm for unknown channel statistics.

papers.title: Unsupervised Recurrent Neural Network Grammars
papers.abstract: Yoon Kim, Alexander Rush, Lei Yu, Adhiguna Kuncoro, Chris Dyer, Gábor Melis. Proceedings of the 2019 Conference of the North American Chapter of the Association for Computational Linguistics: Human Language Technologies, Volume 1 (Long and Short Papers). 2019.
papers.summary: Extends recurrent neural network grammars to the unsupervised setting, learning latent constituency structure jointly with language modeling via amortized variational inference using a CRF parser as inference network. Achieves strong perplexity and competitive unsupervised parsing without annotated trees.

papers.title: Confidence modulates exploration and exploitation in value-based learning
papers.abstract: Uncertainty is ubiquitous in cognitive processing. In this study, we aim to investigate the ability agents possess to track and report the noise inherent in their mental operations, often in the form of confidence judgments. Here, we argue that humans can use uncertainty inherent in their representations of value beliefs to arbitrate between exploration and exploitation. Such uncertainty is reflected in explicit confidence judgments. Using a novel variant of a multi-armed bandit paradigm, we studied how beliefs were formed and how uncertainty in the encoding of these value beliefs (belief confidence) evolved over time. We found that people used uncertainty to arbitrate between exploration…
papers.summary: Human multi-armed bandit experiments show confidence in value beliefs arbitrates exploration versus exploitation: people explore more when belief confidence is low. Belief confidence also drives choice confidence, and individuals with greater metacognitive insight track environmental uncertainty better.

papers.title: GhostVLAD for Set-Based Face Recognition
papers.summary: Aggregates multiple face images of a subject into one compact template descriptor using a NetVLAD extension with 'ghost' clusters that absorb low-quality faces, automatically down-weighting them. Sets state-of-the-art set-based face recognition on the IJB-B benchmark.

papers.title: Restoring ancient text using deep learning: a case study on Greek epigraphy
papers.abstract: Yannis Assael, Thea Sommerschield, Jonathan Prag. Proceedings of the 2019 Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing and the 9th International Joint Conference on Natural Language Processing (EMNLP-IJCNLP). 2019.
papers.summary: Pythia, a sequence-to-sequence network, restores missing characters in damaged ancient Greek inscriptions, achieving roughly 30% character error rate versus 57% for human epigraphists; its ranked restoration hypotheses are intended as a search-space-narrowing aid for historians.

papers.title: Learning to Discover, Ground and Use Words with Segmental Neural Language Models
papers.abstract: We propose a segmental neural language model that combines the generalization power of neural networks with the ability to discover word-like units that are latent in unsegmented character sequences. In contrast to previous segmentation models that treat word segmentation as an isolated task, our model unifies word discovery, learning how words fit together to form sentences, and, by conditioning the model on visual context, how words’ meanings ground in representations of nonlinguistic modalities. Experiments show that the unconditional model learns predictive distributions better than character LSTM models, discovers words competitively with nonparametric Bayesian word segmentation mode…
papers.summary: A segmental neural language model jointly discovers word-like units in unsegmented character streams, learns how words compose into sentences, and grounds word meanings in visual context. It out-predicts character LSTMs, segments competitively with Bayesian models, and improves further when visually conditioned.

papers.title: Exact sampling of determinantal point processes with sublinear time preprocessing
papers.abstract: International audience
papers.summary: Proposes an exact determinantal point process sampler whose one-time preprocessing costs n*poly(k) — sublinear in the kernel matrix size — and whose per-sample cost is poly(k), independent of n, making diversity-inducing subset sampling practical for very large datasets.
papers.venue: HAL (Le Centre pour la Communication Scientifique Directe)

papers.title: A Causal Bayesian Networks Viewpoint on Fairness
papers.summary: Interprets dataset unfairness as unfair causal paths in the causal Bayesian network of the data-generating mechanism, revisiting the COMPAS risk-assessment debate. Argues fairness evaluation must consider unfairness patterns in training data, and shows CBNs help measure unfairness and design fair models.
papers.venue: IFIP advances in information and communication technology

papers.title: Probabilistic Successor Representations with Kalman Temporal Differences
papers.abstract: The effectiveness of Reinforcement Learning (RL) depends on an animal's ability to assign credit for rewards to the appropriate preceding stimuli. One aspect of understanding the neural underpinnings of this process involves understanding what sorts of stimulus representations support generalisation. The Successor Representation (SR), which enforces generalisation over states that predict similar outcomes, has become an increasingly popular model in this space of inquiries. Another dimension of credit assignment involves understanding how animals handle uncertainty about learned associations, using probabilistic methods such as Kalman Temporal Differences (KTD). Combining these approaches…
papers.summary: Combines Kalman temporal differences with the successor representation to learn a distribution over the SR, capturing uncertainty and covariances between long-term predictions. KTD-SR reproduces human-like partial transition revaluation without replay, unlike standard TD-SR, linking predictive and probabilistic accounts of animal learning.

papers.title: Text Genre and Training Data Size in Human-like Parsing
papers.abstract: John Hale, Adhiguna Kuncoro, Keith Hall, Chris Dyer, Jonathan Brennan. Proceedings of the 2019 Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing and the 9th International Joint Conference on Natural Language Processing (EMNLP-IJCNLP). 2019.
papers.summary: Relates a neural phrase-structure parser's internal states to EEG recorded as people listened to the same literary text. More training data yields a better cognitive model only when training examples match the text's genre, consistent with humans adapting syntactic expectations to genre.

papers.title: Reinforcement Learning Agents acquire Flocking and Symbiotic Behaviour in Simulated Ecosystems
papers.abstract: In nature, group behaviours such as flocking as well as cross-species symbiotic partnerships are observed in vastly different forms and circumstances. We hypothesize that such strategies can arise in response to generic predator-prey pressures in a spatial environment with range-limited sensation and action. We evaluate whether these forms of coordination can emerge by independent multi-agent reinforcement learning in simple multiple-species ecosystems. In contrast to prior work, we avoid hand-crafted shaping rewards, specific actions, or dynamics that would directly encourage coordination across agents. Instead we test whether coordination emerges as a consequence of adaptation without e…
papers.summary: Independent multi-agent RL in simulated three-level predator-prey ecosystems — a 3D physics engine with tens of agents and 2D grid worlds with thousands — produces emergent flocking and cross-species symbiotic coordination without any hand-crafted shaping rewards encouraging it.

papers.title: Compact Deep Aggregation for Set Retrieval
papers.summary: Addresses retrieving images containing a query set of identities: per-face embeddings are aggregated into one compact fixed-length descriptor by a trained SetNet with GhostVLAD-style pooling, enabling efficient large-scale set retrieval; introduces the Celebrity Together dataset for evaluation.

papers.title: Exploiting structure of uncertainty for efficient matroid semi-bandits
papers.abstract: International audience
papers.summary: Makes structure-exploiting combinatorial semi-bandit algorithms computationally tractable by reducing arm selection to submodular maximization. For matroid constraints, tailored approximation routines give the first efficient algorithms that use reward structure to improve regret, tightening the best efficient gap-free bound by a factor sqrt(m)/log m.
papers.venue: HAL (Le Centre pour la Communication Scientifique Directe)

papers.title: A Kantian Cognitive Architecture
papers.summary: Formalizes Kant's account of cognition as a computational architecture: making sense of sensory input means constructing a unified model of objects, properties, and causal laws satisfying Kantian synthetic-unity constraints, realized through logic-program synthesis.

papers.title: WikiCREM: A Large Unsupervised Corpus for Coreference Resolution
papers.abstract: Vid Kocijan, Oana-Maria Camburu, Ana-Maria Cretu, Yordan Yordanov, Phil Blunsom, Thomas Lukasiewicz. Proceedings of the 2019 Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing and the 9th International Joint Conference on Natural Language Processing (EMNLP-IJCNLP). 2019.
papers.summary: Constructs WikiCREM, a large unsupervised corpus of pronoun-disambiguation instances generated by masking repeated personal names in Wikipedia sentences. Language models fine-tuned on it improve coreference resolution across benchmarks such as GAP, DPR, and Winograd-style tasks without task-specific annotation.

papers.title: Deep Learning Under Scrutiny: Performance Against Health Care Professionals in Detecting Diseases from Medical Imaging - Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
papers.summary: Systematic review and meta-analysis comparing deep learning with health-care professionals at detecting diseases from medical imaging. Pooled estimates show roughly equivalent sensitivity and specificity, but very few studies used external validation or head-to-head clinician comparisons, and reporting quality was generally poor.

papers.title: Feasibility of Automated Deep Learning Design for Medical Image Classification by Healthcare Professionals with Limited Coding Experience
papers.summary: A feasibility study in which health-care professionals without coding experience used automated deep learning (such as Google Cloud AutoML) to train medical image classifiers across several open imaging datasets, assessing whether non-experts can build models with clinically useful diagnostic performance.

papers.title: Codes, functions, and causes: A critique of Brette's conceptual analysis of coding
papers.abstract: Brette argues that coding as a concept is inappropriate for explanations of neurocognitive phenomena. Here, we argue that Brette's conceptual analysis mischaracterizes the structure of causal claims in coding and other forms of analysis-by-decomposition. We argue that analyses of this form are permissible and conceptually coherent and offer essential tools for building and developing models of neurocognitive systems like the brain.
papers.summary: A commentary rebutting Brette's argument that 'coding' is an inappropriate concept for neuroscience. Contends his analysis mischaracterizes the causal structure of coding claims, and that analysis-by-decomposition remains coherent and essential for modeling neurocognitive systems.

papers.title: Compositional Neural Representations in the Hippocampal Formation and Prefrontal Cortex Underlie Visual Construction and Planning
papers.summary: An fMRI study showing humans form hierarchical, compositional representations when constructing novel visual objects from building blocks and relations. The hippocampal-entorhinal system and medial prefrontal cortex encode factorized relational knowledge that generalizes to new stimuli.

papers.title: On two ways to use determinantal point processes for Monte Carlo integration -- Long version
papers.abstract: International audience
papers.summary: Studies replacing i.i.d. samples in Monte Carlo integration with determinantal point processes (repulsive distributions) to obtain faster-than-1/N variance. Revisits and generalizes the Bardenet-Hardy and Ermakov-Zolotukhin DPP estimators to continuous settings and provides sampling algorithms.
papers.venue: HAL (Le Centre pour la Communication Scientifique Directe)

papers.title: How AI training scales
papers.abstract: We’ve discovered that the gradient noise scale, a simple statistical metric, predicts the parallelizability of neural network training on a wide range of tasks. Since complex tasks tend to have noisier gradients, increasingly large batch sizes are likely to become useful in the future, removing one potential limit to further growth of AI systems. More broadly, these results show that neural network training need not be considered a mysterious art, but can be rigorized and systematized.
papers.summary: Shows the gradient noise scale, a simple statistic, predicts the largest useful data-parallel batch size across supervised and RL tasks: complex tasks with noisier gradients tolerate bigger batches. Offers an empirical model that systematizes compute/time tradeoffs in large-batch training.

papers.title: Quantifying generalization in reinforcement learning
papers.abstract: We’re releasing CoinRun, a training environment which provides a metric for an agent’s ability to transfer its experience to novel situations and has already helped clarify a longstanding puzzle in reinforcement learning. CoinRun strikes a desirable balance in complexity: the environment is simpler than traditional platformer games like Sonic the Hedgehog but still poses a worthy generalization challenge for state of the art algorithms.
papers.summary: Introduces CoinRun, a procedurally generated platformer that quantifies generalization in RL. Agents overfit to surprisingly large training sets, while deeper convolutional architectures and supervised-learning-style regularization — L2, dropout, data augmentation, batch norm — improve transfer to unseen levels.

papers.title: Spinning Up in Deep RL
papers.abstract: We’re releasing Spinning Up in Deep RL, an educational resource designed to let anyone learn to become a skilled practitioner in deep reinforcement learning. Spinning Up consists of crystal-clear examples of RL code, educational exercises, documentation, and tutorials.
papers.summary: Educational resource for deep reinforcement learning: clear, minimal implementations of core algorithms (VPG, TRPO, PPO, DDPG, TD3, SAC) plus tutorials, exercises, and curated paper lists designed to take newcomers to practitioner level.

papers.title: Learning concepts with energy functions
papers.abstract: We’ve developed an energy-based model that can quickly learn to identify and generate instances of concepts, such as near, above, between, closest, and furthest, expressed as sets of 2d points. Our model learns these concepts after only five demonstrations. We also show cross-domain transfer: we use concepts learned in a 2d particle environment to solve tasks on a 3-dimensional physics-based robot.
papers.summary: An energy-based model learns spatial concepts such as near, above, between, and furthest from about five demonstrations of 2D point sets, supporting both recognition and generation of concept instances, and transfers concepts learned in 2D to a physics-based 3D robot.

papers.title: Plan online, learn offline: Efficient learning and exploration via model-based control
papers.summary: POLO couples online trajectory optimization with an offline-learned value function ensemble: the value function extends the effective planning horizon, ensemble disagreement supplies exploration bonuses, and local planning stabilizes value learning, enabling rapid acquisition of simulated humanoid control without demonstrations.

papers.title: Reinforcement learning with prediction-based rewards
papers.abstract: We’ve developed Random Network Distillation (RND), a prediction-based method for encouraging reinforcement learning agents to explore their environments through curiosity, which for the first time exceeds average human performance on Montezuma’s Revenge.
papers.summary: Random Network Distillation rewards an RL agent with its error at predicting a fixed, randomly initialized network's features for each state, a cheap curiosity bonus for hard-exploration games. Combined with PPO, it exceeded average human performance on Montezuma's Revenge for the first time.

papers.title: Learning complex goals with iterated amplification
papers.abstract: We’re proposing an AI safety technique called iterated amplification that lets us specify complicated behaviors and goals that are beyond human scale, by demonstrating how to decompose a task into simpler sub-tasks, rather than by providing labeled data or a reward function. Although this idea is in its very early stages and we have only completed experiments on simple toy algorithmic domains, we’ve decided to present it in its preliminary state because we think it could prove to be a scalable approach to AI safety.
papers.summary: Proposes iterated amplification for AI safety: behaviors and goals beyond human scale are specified by decomposing tasks into simpler subtasks a human aided by AI assistants can handle, instead of labels or reward functions. Early experiments cover only toy algorithmic domains.

papers.title: FFJORD: Free-form continuous dynamics for scalable reversible generative models
papers.abstract: A promising class of generative models maps points from a simple distribution to a complex distribution through an invertible neural network. Likelihood-based training of these models requires restricting their architectures to allow cheap computation of Jacobian determinants. Alternatively, the Jacobian trace can be used if the transformation is specified by an ordinary differential equation. In this paper, we use Hutchinson's trace estimator to give a scalable unbiased estimate of the log-density. The result is a continuous-time invertible generative model with unbiased density estimation and one-pass sampling, while allowing unrestricted neural network architectures. We demonstrate our…
papers.summary: FFJORD builds continuous-time reversible generative models by estimating log-densities with Hutchinson's trace estimator on ODE-defined transformations, lifting normalizing flows' architectural restrictions. Achieves state-of-the-art exact-likelihood results on density estimation, image generation, and variational inference with one-pass sampling.

papers.title: The International 2018: Results
papers.abstract: OpenAI Five lost two games against top Dota 2 players at The International in Vancouver this week, maintaining a good chance of winning for the first 20–35 minutes of both games.
papers.summary: OpenAI Five lost both exhibition games against professional Dota 2 teams at The International 2018, though it maintained a good chance of winning through the first 20-35 minutes of each match — a public stress test of large-scale self-play RL.

papers.title: Large-scale study of curiosity-driven learning
papers.abstract: Reinforcement learning algorithms rely on carefully engineering environment rewards that are extrinsic to the agent. However, annotating each environment with hand-designed, dense rewards is not scalable, motivating the need for developing reward functions that are intrinsic to the agent. Curiosity is a type of intrinsic reward function which uses prediction error as reward signal. In this paper: (a) We perform the first large-scale study of purely curiosity-driven learning, i.e. without any extrinsic rewards, across 54 standard benchmark environments, including the Atari game suite. Our results show surprisingly good performance, and a high degree of alignment between the intrinsi…
papers.summary: First large-scale study of purely curiosity-driven RL, with no extrinsic rewards, across 54 environments: prediction-error objectives align surprisingly well with hand-designed game rewards. Random features often suffice but learned features generalize better, and stochastic 'noisy-TV' dynamics expose the approach's limits.

papers.title: OpenAI Five Benchmark: Results
papers.abstract: Yesterday, OpenAI Five won a best-of-three against a team of 99.95th percentile Dota players: Blitz, Cap, Fogged, Merlini, and MoonMeander—four of whom have played Dota professionally—in front of a live audience and 100,000 concurrent livestream viewers.
papers.summary: OpenAI Five won a best-of-three against a team of 99.95th-percentile Dota 2 players, four of them former professionals, before a live audience — a milestone for large-scale self-play reinforcement learning ahead of its appearance at The International.

papers.title: Learning dexterity
papers.abstract: We’ve trained a human-like robot hand to manipulate physical objects with unprecedented dexterity.
papers.summary: Dactyl trains a five-fingered Shadow Hand to reorient a block using large-scale PPO entirely in simulation, with extensive domain randomization of physics and visuals enabling vision-based dexterous manipulation to transfer to the physical robot without real-world training.

papers.title: Variational option discovery algorithms
papers.summary: Casts unsupervised skill discovery as variational autoencoding between latent contexts and trajectories, unifying objectives like VIC and DIAYN. Proposes VALOR, which decodes contexts from whole trajectories and grows the number of contexts through a curriculum, learning diverse behaviors without task rewards.

papers.title: Glow: Better reversible generative models
papers.abstract: We introduce Glow, a reversible generative model which uses invertible 1x1 convolutions. It extends previous work on reversible generative models and simplifies the architecture. Our model can generate realistic high resolution images, supports efficient sampling, and discovers features that can be used to manipulate attributes of data. We’re releasing code for the model and an online visualization tool so people can explore and build on these results.
papers.summary: Glow is a flow-based generative model built on invertible 1x1 convolutions and actnorm, simplifying reversible architectures. It generates realistic high-resolution faces, supports exact-likelihood training with efficient sampling, and exposes latent directions for semantic attribute manipulation.

papers.title: Learning Montezuma’s Revenge from a single demonstration
papers.abstract: We’ve trained an agent to achieve a high score of 74,500 on Montezuma’s Revenge from a single human demonstration, better than any previously published result. Our algorithm is simple: the agent plays a sequence of games starting from carefully chosen states from the demonstration, and learns from them by optimizing the game score using PPO, the same reinforcement learning algorithm that underpins OpenAI Five.
papers.summary: Reaches a then-record 74,500 points on Montezuma's Revenge from one human demonstration by restarting PPO episodes from progressively earlier states along the demo, sidestepping the game's notoriously hard exploration problem.

papers.title: OpenAI Five
papers.abstract: Our team of five neural networks, OpenAI Five, has started to defeat amateur human teams at Dota 2.
papers.summary: Five LSTM-based agents trained with massively scaled PPO self-play—about 180 years of Dota 2 per day—begin defeating amateur human teams at 5v5, with coordinated teamwork emerging from a shared reward weighting rather than explicit communication.

papers.title: Retro Contest: Results
papers.abstract: The first run of our Retro Contest—exploring the development of algorithms that can generalize from previous experience—is now complete.
papers.summary: Reports results of the Retro Contest on RL generalization to held-out Sonic the Hedgehog levels: top entries were tuned variants of the provided PPO and Rainbow baselines, improving transfer scores but remaining well below human performance.

papers.title: Learning policy representations in multiagent systems
papers.summary: Learns embeddings of agent policies from interaction episodes via a generative imitation objective combined with a contrastive triplet loss; the representations support agent classification and improve returns when conditioning policies in RoboSumo and ParticleWorld multi-agent environments.

papers.title: GamePad: A learning environment for theorem proving
papers.abstract: In this paper, we introduce a system called GamePad that can be used to explore the application of machine learning methods to theorem proving in the Coq proof assistant. Interactive theorem provers such as Coq enable users to construct machine-checkable proofs in a step-by-step manner. Hence, they provide an opportunity to explore theorem proving with human supervision. We use GamePad to synthesize proofs for a simple algebraic rewrite problem and train baseline models for a formalization of the Feit-Thompson theorem. We address position evaluation (i.e., predict the number of proof steps left) and tactic prediction (i.e., predict the next proof step) tasks, which arise naturally in tact…
papers.summary: GamePad exposes the Coq proof assistant as an ML environment for interactive theorem proving, defining position-evaluation (proof steps remaining) and tactic-prediction tasks, with baselines on an algebraic rewrite problem and the Feit-Thompson formalization.

papers.title: Gym Retro
papers.abstract: We’re releasing the full version of Gym Retro, a platform for reinforcement learning research on games. This brings our publicly-released game count from around 70 Atari games and 30 Sega games to over 1,000 games across a variety of backing emulators. We’re also releasing the tool we use to add new games to the platform.
papers.summary: Releases the full Gym Retro platform, expanding available RL environments from roughly 100 Atari and Sega titles to over 1,000 games across multiple emulators, plus an integration tool for adding new games—aimed at studying generalization between similar tasks.

papers.title: AI and compute
papers.abstract: We’re releasing an analysis showing that since 2012, the amount of compute used in the largest AI training runs has been increasing exponentially with a 3.4-month doubling time (by comparison, Moore’s Law had a 2-year doubling period)[^footnote-correction]. Since 2012, this metric has grown by more than 300,000x (a 2-year doubling period would yield only a 7x increase). Improvements in compute have been a key component of AI progress, so as long as this trend continues, it’s worth preparing for the implications of systems far outside today’s capabilities.
papers.summary: Analysis showing compute used in the largest AI training runs has grown exponentially since 2012 with a 3.4-month doubling time—over 300,000x total—arguing this trend warrants preparing for systems far beyond today's capabilities.

papers.title: AI safety via debate
papers.abstract: To make AI systems broadly useful for challenging real-world tasks, we need them to learn complex human goals and preferences. One approach to specifying complex goals asks humans to judge during training which agent behaviors are safe and useful, but this approach can fail if the task is too complicated for a human to directly judge. To help address this concern, we propose training agents via self play on a zero sum debate game. Given a question or proposed action, two agents take turns making short statements up to a limit, then a human judges which of the agents gave the most true, useful information. In an analogy to complexity theory, debate with optimal play can answer any question…
papers.summary: Proposes aligning agents on tasks too complex for humans to judge directly by training them via self-play on a zero-sum debate game judged by humans. Optimal debate can answer PSPACE questions, and a sparse-pixel MNIST judge's accuracy rises from 59.4% to 88.9%.

papers.title: Evolved Policy Gradients
papers.abstract: We propose a metalearning approach for learning gradient-based reinforcement learning (RL) algorithms. The idea is to evolve a differentiable loss function, such that an agent, which optimizes its policy to minimize this loss, will achieve high rewards. The loss is parametrized via temporal convolutions over the agent's experience. Because this loss is highly flexible in its ability to take into account the agent's history, it enables fast task learning. Empirical results show that our evolved policy gradient algorithm (EPG) achieves faster learning on several randomized environments compared to an off-the-shelf policy gradient method. We also demonstrate that EPG's learned loss can gener…
papers.summary: Evolves a differentiable RL loss function, parameterized by temporal convolutions over the agent's experience, such that policies minimizing it achieve high reward. EPG learns faster than standard policy gradients on randomized environments and generalizes to out-of-distribution test tasks.

papers.title: Gotta Learn Fast: A new benchmark for generalization in RL
papers.summary: Benchmark measuring RL generalization via held-out Sonic the Hedgehog levels: agents train on one level set and are scored on fast adaptation to unseen levels. Rainbow, PPO, and joint-training baselines show transfer from prior experience helps, but humans adapt far faster.

papers.title: Retro Contest
papers.abstract: We’re launching a transfer learning contest that measures a reinforcement learning algorithm’s ability to generalize from previous experience.
papers.summary: Launches a transfer-learning contest measuring how well RL algorithms generalize from training levels of Sonic the Hedgehog to held-out test levels with limited test-time experience, rewarding agents that exploit prior experience rather than learning each task from scratch.

papers.title: Variance reduction for policy gradient with action-dependent factorized baselines
papers.summary: Derives action-dependent baselines for policy gradients with factorized action spaces: each action dimension's baseline conditions on the other dimensions' actions, cutting gradient variance without bias and improving sample efficiency on high-dimensional continuous control and multi-agent tasks.

papers.title: Improving GANs using optimal transport
papers.summary: OT-GAN trains generative models by minimizing a mini-batch energy distance—an optimal-transport (Sinkhorn-based) divergence computed in an adversarially learned feature space—yielding more stable GAN training and strong image sample quality on benchmarks like CIFAR-10.

papers.title: On first-order meta-learning algorithms
papers.summary: Analyzes meta-learning with only first-order gradients, covering first-order MAML and introducing Reptile. A Taylor-series analysis shows both maximize within-task gradient inner products, and they match full MAML on Omniglot and Mini-ImageNet few-shot classification at lower compute cost.

papers.title: Reptile: A scalable meta-learning algorithm
papers.abstract: We’ve developed a simple meta-learning algorithm called Reptile which works by repeatedly sampling a task, performing stochastic gradient descent on it, and updating the initial parameters towards the final parameters learned on that task. Reptile is the application of the Shortest Descent algorithm to the meta-learning setting, and is mathematically similar to first-order MAML (which is a version of the well-known MAML algorithm) that only needs black-box access to an optimizer such as SGD or Adam, with similar computational efficiency and performance.
papers.summary: Reptile meta-learns an initialization by repeatedly sampling a task, running SGD on it, and nudging the initial parameters toward the task-adapted weights. It is mathematically close to first-order MAML but needs only black-box optimizer access, with similar efficiency and few-shot performance.

papers.title: Some considerations on learning to explore via meta-reinforcement learning
papers.summary: Reformulates meta-RL objectives so exploratory actions taken during meta-training receive proper credit, yielding E-MAML and E-RL2 variants that explicitly incentivize exploration and outperform the original algorithms at fast adaptation on Krazy World gridworlds and mazes.

papers.title: Multi-Goal Reinforcement Learning: Challenging robotics environments and request for research
papers.summary: Specifies eight goal-conditioned, sparse-reward robotics environments spanning Fetch manipulation and ShadowHand dexterity tasks, reports DDPG plus Hindsight Experience Replay baselines, and poses open research directions for multi-goal reinforcement learning.

papers.title: Ingredients for robotics research
papers.abstract: We’re releasing eight simulated robotics environments and a Baselines implementation of Hindsight Experience Replay, all developed for our research over the past year. We’ve used these environments to train models which work on physical robots. We’re also releasing a set of requests for robotics research.
papers.summary: Releases eight simulated goal-based robotics environments (Fetch arm and ShadowHand) plus a Baselines implementation of Hindsight Experience Replay, validated by policies that transfer to physical robots, alongside a set of open requests for robotics research.

papers.title: Preparing for malicious uses of AI
papers.abstract: We’ve co-authored a paper that forecasts how malicious actors could misuse AI technology, and potential ways we can prevent and mitigate these threats. This paper is the outcome of almost a year of sustained work with our colleagues at the Future of Humanity Institute, the Centre for the Study of Existential Risk, the Center for a New American Security, the Electronic Frontier Foundation, and others.
papers.summary: Joint report with FHI, CSER, CNAS, EFF and others forecasting how malicious actors could misuse AI across digital, physical, and political security domains, and recommending interventions for researchers and policymakers to prevent and mitigate these threats.

papers.title: Interpretable machine learning through teaching
papers.abstract: We’ve designed a method that encourages AIs to teach each other with examples that also make sense to humans. Our approach automatically selects the most informative examples to teach a concept—for instance, the best images to describe the concept of dogs—and experimentally we found our approach to be effective at teaching both AIs
papers.summary: Method for selecting maximally informative teaching examples that stay human-interpretable: training teacher and student networks iteratively rather than jointly prevents them from converging on opaque codes, producing example sets that effectively teach concepts to both AIs and humans.

papers.title: Discovering types for entity disambiguation
papers.abstract: We’ve built a system for automatically figuring out which object is meant by a word by having a neural network decide if the word belongs to each of about 100 automatically-discovered “types” (non-exclusive categories).
papers.summary: Resolves which object a word refers to by having a neural network predict membership in roughly 100 automatically discovered, non-exclusive types; optimizing the type system over a knowledge base lets type predictions alone drive high entity-disambiguation accuracy.

papers.title: Requests for Research 2.0
papers.abstract: We’re releasing a new batch of seven unsolved problems which have come up in the course of our research at OpenAI.
papers.summary: Second batch of seven unsolved problems drawn from OpenAI's research, spanning multi-agent self-play, transfer in RL, and deep learning fundamentals, each framed as a concrete, difficulty-rated project for researchers entering the field.

papers.title: Scaling Kubernetes to 2,500 nodes
papers.summary: Engineering lessons from scaling a single Kubernetes cluster to 2,500 nodes for deep learning workloads, covering etcd storage bottlenecks, API-server tuning, Docker image distribution, and networking issues encountered along the way.

papers.title: Block-sparse GPU kernels
papers.abstract: We’re releasing highly-optimized GPU kernels for an underexplored class of neural network architectures: networks with block-sparse weights. Depending on the chosen sparsity, these kernels can run orders of magnitude faster than cuBLAS or cuSPARSE. We’ve used them to attain state-of-the-art results in text sentiment analysis and generative modeling of text and images.
papers.summary: Releases GPU kernels optimized for block-sparse weight matrices that run orders of magnitude faster than cuBLAS/cuSPARSE at high sparsity, enabling wider or deeper sparse networks that achieved state-of-the-art results in text sentiment analysis and generative text/image modeling.

papers.title: Learning sparse neural networks through L₀ regularization
papers.summary: Regularizes networks toward exact zeros with a differentiable L0 penalty based on the hard-concrete distribution, a smoothed discrete relaxation enabling gradient-based optimization of expected sparsity; networks prune themselves during training, cutting computation while maintaining or improving generalization.

papers.title: Interpretable and pedagogical examples
papers.summary: Studies when machine teaching yields examples humans can understand: jointly optimizing teacher and student networks produces opaque coding schemes, while iterative training—student first, then teacher—recovers intuitive teaching strategies whose examples also work for teaching humans.

papers.title: Learning a hierarchy
papers.abstract: We’ve developed a hierarchical reinforcement learning algorithm that learns high-level actions useful for solving a range of tasks, allowing fast solving of tasks requiring thousands of timesteps. Our algorithm, when applied to a set of navigation problems, discovers a set of high-level actions for walking and crawling in different directions, which enables the agent to master new navigation tasks quickly.
papers.summary: Meta-learning shared hierarchies (MLSH) learns reusable sub-policies shared across tasks with a per-task master policy switching among them; on navigation problems it discovers walking and crawling primitives that let agents quickly master new tasks spanning thousands of timesteps.

papers.title: Generalizing from simulation
