papers.abstract: Our latest robotics techniques allow robot controllers, trained entirely in simulation and deployed on physical robots, to react to unplanned changes in the environment as they solve simple tasks. That is, we’ve used these techniques to build closed-loop systems rather than open-loop ones as before.
papers.summary: Robot controllers trained entirely in simulation with domain randomization transfer to physical robots as closed-loop systems, reacting to unplanned changes in the environment during simple manipulation tasks rather than replaying fixed open-loop trajectories.

papers.title: Sim-to-real transfer of robotic control with dynamics randomization
papers.summary: Randomizing simulator dynamics—mass, friction, latencies, and other physical parameters—during training yields a recurrent pushing policy that transfers directly to a physical Fetch arm without real-world fine-tuning, compensating for sim-to-real dynamics mismatch.

papers.title: Asymmetric actor critic for image-based robot learning
papers.abstract: Deep reinforcement learning (RL) has proven a powerful technique in many sequential decision making domains. However, Robotics poses many challenges for RL, most notably training on a physical system can be expensive and dangerous, which has sparked significant interest in learning control policies using a physics simulator. While several recent works have shown promising results in transferring policies trained in simulation to the real world, they often do not fully utilize the advantage of working with a simulator. In this work, we exploit the full state observability in the simulator to train better policies which take as input only partial observations (RGBD images). We do this by em…
papers.summary: Trains sim-to-real visuomotor policies with an asymmetric actor-critic: the critic exploits the simulator's full state while the actor sees only RGBD images. Combined with domain randomization, this enables real-robot picking, pushing, and block-moving without any real-world training data.

papers.title: Domain randomization and generative models for robotic grasping
papers.abstract: Deep learning-based robotic grasping has made significant progress thanks to algorithmic improvements and increased data availability. However, state-of-the-art models are often trained on as few as hundreds or thousands of unique object instances, and as a result generalization can be a challenge. In this work, we explore a novel data generation pipeline for training a deep neural network to perform grasp planning that applies the idea of domain randomization to object synthesis. We generate millions of unique, unrealistic procedurally generated objects, and train a deep neural network to perform grasp planning on these objects. Since the distribution of successful grasps for a given obj…
papers.summary: Trains an autoregressive grasp-planning network on millions of procedurally generated, unrealistic objects, modeling the multimodal distribution over grasps. Despite never seeing realistic objects in training, it reaches over 90% grasp success on unseen realistic objects in simulation and 80% on a real robot.

papers.title: Meta-learning for wrestling
papers.abstract: We show that for the task of simulated robot wrestling, a meta-learning agent can learn to quickly defeat a stronger non-meta-learning agent, and also show that the meta-learning agent can adapt to physical malfunction.
papers.summary: In simulated robot wrestling, a meta-learning agent learns to adapt quickly mid-competition, defeating a stronger non-meta-learning opponent and coping with physical malfunction — evidence that fast adaptation pays off in competitive multi-agent settings.

papers.title: Competitive self-play
papers.abstract: We’ve found that self-play allows simulated AIs to discover physical skills like tackling, ducking, faking, kicking, catching, and diving for the ball, without explicitly designing an environment with these skills in mind. Self-play ensures that the environment is always the right difficulty for an AI to improve. Taken alongside our Dota 2 self-play results, we have increasing confidence that self-play will be a core part of powerful AI systems in the future.
papers.summary: Simulated humanoid agents trained via competitive self-play discover physical skills like tackling, ducking, faking, kicking, catching, and diving without these being designed into the environment; self-play keeps the task at the right difficulty for continual improvement.

papers.title: Nonlinear computation in deep linear networks
papers.summary: Float32 arithmetic is itself nonlinear near its smallest representable values, so networks built purely from linear layers can compute nonlinear functions. Training with evolution strategies — backprop cannot exploit the effect — pushes a deep linear network past the accuracy ceiling of any truly linear MNIST classifier.

papers.title: Learning to model other minds
papers.abstract: We’re releasing an algorithm which accounts for the fact that other agents are learning too, and discovers self-interested yet collaborative strategies like tit-for-tat in the iterated prisoner’s dilemma. This algorithm, Learning with Opponent-Learning Awareness (LOLA), is a small step towards agents that model other minds.
papers.summary: Releases LOLA (Learning with Opponent-Learning Awareness), an algorithm whose agents account for the fact that other agents are learning too rather than treating them as static, discovering self-interested yet collaborative strategies like tit-for-tat in the iterated prisoner's dilemma.

papers.title: Learning with opponent-learning awareness
papers.summary: LOLA agents differentiate through opponents' anticipated learning updates instead of treating them as a fixed part of the environment. This opponent shaping yields tit-for-tat and cooperation in the iterated prisoner's dilemma, where naive learners defect, and convergence to Nash in iterated matching pennies.

papers.title: OpenAI Baselines: ACKTR & A2C
papers.abstract: We’re releasing two new OpenAI Baselines implementations: ACKTR and A2C. A2C is a synchronous, deterministic variant of Asynchronous Advantage Actor Critic (A3C) which we’ve found gives equal performance. ACKTR is a more sample-efficient reinforcement learning algorithm than TRPO and A2C, and requires only slightly more computation than A2C per update.
papers.summary: Releases Baselines implementations of A2C — a synchronous variant of A3C with equal performance — and ACKTR, which uses Kronecker-factored approximate natural-gradient updates to deliver better sample efficiency than TRPO and A2C at only slightly higher per-update compute.

papers.title: More on Dota 2
papers.abstract: Our Dota 2 result shows that self-play can catapult the performance of machine learning systems from far below human level to superhuman, given sufficient compute. In the span of a month, our system went from barely matching a high-ranked player to beating the top pros and has continued to improve since then. Supervised deep learning systems can only be as good as their training datasets, but in self-play systems, the available data improves automatically as the agent gets better.
papers.summary: Recounts how the 1v1 Dota 2 bot improved from barely matching a high-ranked human to beating top professionals within a month of self-play, arguing that self-play's automatically improving data distribution can carry systems from sub-human to superhuman given enough compute.

papers.title: Dota 2
papers.abstract: We’ve created a bot which beats the world’s top professionals at 1v1 matches of Dota 2 under standard tournament rules. The bot learned the game from scratch by self-play, and does not use imitation learning or tree search. This is a step towards building AI systems which accomplish well-defined goals in messy, complicated situations involving real humans.
papers.summary: A bot trained from scratch purely via self-play — no imitation learning or tree search — defeats the world's top professional players at 1v1 Dota 2 under standard tournament rules, a step toward AI pursuing well-defined goals in messy situations involving humans.

papers.title: Gathering human feedback
papers.abstract: RL-Teacher is an open-source implementation of our interface to train AIs via occasional human feedback rather than hand-crafted reward functions. The underlying technique was developed as a step towards safe AI systems, but also applies to reinforcement learning problems with rewards that are hard to specify.
papers.summary: Open-sources RL-Teacher, an implementation of the interface for training agents from occasional human feedback instead of hand-crafted reward functions, developed as a step toward safe AI and applicable to RL problems where rewards are hard to specify.

papers.title: Better exploration with parameter noise
papers.abstract: We’ve found that adding adaptive noise to the parameters of reinforcement learning algorithms frequently boosts performance. This exploration method is simple to implement and very rarely decreases performance, so it’s worth trying on any problem.
papers.summary: Adds adaptive noise to an RL agent's network parameters rather than its action outputs, giving more consistent exploration. The technique frequently boosts performance across algorithms, very rarely hurts, and is simple enough to try on any problem.

papers.title: Proximal Policy Optimization
papers.abstract: We’re releasing a new class of reinforcement learning algorithms, Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), which perform comparably or better than state-of-the-art approaches while being much simpler to implement and tune. PPO has become the default reinforcement learning algorithm at OpenAI because of its ease of use and good performance.
papers.summary: Introduces Proximal Policy Optimization, policy-gradient algorithms built on a clipped surrogate objective that match or beat state-of-the-art methods while being much simpler to implement and tune; PPO became OpenAI's default RL algorithm.

papers.title: Robust adversarial inputs
papers.abstract: We’ve created images that reliably fool neural network classifiers when viewed from varied scales and perspectives. This challenges a claim from last week that self-driving cars would be hard to trick maliciously since they capture images from multiple scales, angles, perspectives, and the like.
papers.summary: Creates adversarial images that reliably fool classifiers across varied scales, angles, and perspectives, countering the claim that systems like self-driving cars are hard to attack because they capture images from multiple viewpoints.

papers.title: Hindsight Experience Replay
papers.summary: Hindsight Experience Replay relabels failed episodes with the goals the agent actually achieved, making sparse binary-reward, goal-conditioned tasks learnable without reward shaping; combined with DDPG it learns robotic pushing, sliding, and pick-and-place that standard replay cannot.

papers.title: Teacher–student curriculum learning
papers.abstract: We propose Teacher-Student Curriculum Learning (TSCL), a framework for automatic curriculum learning, where the Student tries to learn a complex task, and the Teacher automatically chooses subtasks from a given set for the Student to train on. We describe a family of Teacher algorithms that rely on the intuition that the Student should practice more those tasks on which it makes the fastest progress, i.e., where the slope of the learning curve is highest. In addition, the Teacher algorithms address the problem of forgetting by also choosing tasks where the Student's performance is getting worse. We demonstrate that TSCL matches or surpasses the results of carefully hand-crafted curricula…
papers.summary: In Teacher-Student Curriculum Learning, a Teacher selects subtasks where the Student is improving fastest — or regressing, to combat forgetting. TSCL matches hand-crafted curricula on LSTM decimal addition and solves a Minecraft maze unsolvable by direct training, an order of magnitude faster than uniform sampling.

papers.title: Faster physics in Python
papers.abstract: We’re open-sourcing a high-performance Python library for robotic simulation using the MuJoCo engine, developed over our past year of robotics research.
papers.summary: Open-sources mujoco-py, a high-performance Python library for robotic simulation with the MuJoCo engine, built during a year of robotics research to speed up large-scale simulated experiments.

papers.title: Learning to cooperate, compete, and communicate
papers.abstract: Multiagent environments where agents compete for resources are stepping stones on the path to AGI. Multiagent environments have two useful properties: first, there is a natural curriculum—the difficulty of the environment is determined by the skill of your competitors (and if you’re competing against clones of yourself, the environment exactly matches your skill level). Second, a multiagent environment has no stable equilibrium: no matter how smart an agent is, there’s always pressure to get smarter. These environments have a very different feel from traditional environments, and it’ll take a lot more research before we become good at them.
papers.summary: Introduces MADDPG, extending actor-critic RL with centralized critics and decentralized actors so agents learn in mixed cooperative-competitive environments. Argues multi-agent settings supply a natural curriculum — difficulty tracks opponents' skill — and no stable equilibrium, so there is always pressure to get smarter.

papers.title: UCB exploration via Q-ensembles
papers.summary: Adapts bandit-style upper-confidence-bound exploration to deep RL: an ensemble of Q-functions supplies an uncertainty estimate, and acting optimistically on the ensemble's confidence bound yields significant gains on the Atari benchmark.

papers.title: OpenAI Baselines: DQN
papers.abstract: We’re open-sourcing OpenAI Baselines, our internal effort to reproduce reinforcement learning algorithms with performance on par with published results. We’ll release the algorithms over upcoming months; today’s release includes DQN and three of its variants.
papers.summary: Launches OpenAI Baselines, open-source reference implementations of RL algorithms tuned to match published results; the first release covers DQN and three of its variants, giving the community reliable baselines to build on and compare against.

papers.title: Robots that learn
papers.abstract: We’ve created a robotics system, trained entirely in simulation and deployed on a physical robot, which can learn a new task after seeing it done once.
papers.summary: Demonstrates one-shot imitation on physical hardware: a robotics system trained entirely in simulation is deployed on a real robot and learns to perform a new task after seeing it demonstrated just once.

papers.title: Roboschool
papers.abstract: We are releasing Roboschool: open-source software for robot simulation, integrated with OpenAI Gym.
papers.summary: Open-sources Roboschool, robot simulation environments integrated with OpenAI Gym and built on the Bullet physics engine, removing the need for a MuJoCo license and adding new control tasks, including multiplayer environments where agents train against each other.

papers.title: Equivalence between policy gradients and soft Q-learning
papers.abstract: Two of the leading approaches for model-free reinforcement learning are policy gradient methods and $Q$-learning methods. $Q$-learning methods can be effective and sample-efficient when they work, however, it is not well-understood why they work, since empirically, the $Q$-values they estimate are very inaccurate. A partial explanation may be that $Q$-learning methods are secretly implementing policy gradient updates: we show that there is a precise equivalence between $Q$-learning and policy gradient methods in the setting of entropy-regularized reinforcement learning, that "soft" (entropy-regularized) $Q$-learning is exactly equivalent to a policy gradient method. We also point out a co…
papers.summary: Proves entropy-regularized 'soft' Q-learning is exactly equivalent to policy gradient methods — a possible explanation for why Q-learning works despite inaccurate Q-value estimates — and builds a Q-learning variant matching A3C's learning dynamics without a target network or epsilon-greedy schedule, performing on par on Atari.

papers.title: Stochastic Neural Networks for hierarchical reinforcement learning
papers.summary: Pre-trains a large span of skills with stochastic neural networks guided by a single proxy reward plus an information-theoretic regularizer, then trains a high-level policy that switches among the frozen skills, markedly improving exploration on sparse-reward, long-horizon downstream tasks.

papers.title: Unsupervised sentiment neuron
papers.abstract: We’ve developed an unsupervised system which learns an excellent representation of sentiment, despite being trained only to predict the next character in the text of Amazon reviews.
papers.summary: A character-level LSTM trained only to predict the next character of Amazon reviews learns a representation containing a single 'sentiment neuron' that tracks review sentiment, enabling excellent sentiment classification from very few labeled examples.

papers.title: Spam detection in the physical world
papers.abstract: We’ve created the world’s first Spam-detecting AI trained entirely in simulation and deployed on a physical robot.
papers.summary: April Fools' joke post announcing 'the world's first Spam-detecting AI trained entirely in simulation and deployed on a physical robot' — a parody of the lab's sim-to-real robotics work in which the robot detects canned Spam rather than email spam.

papers.title: Evolution strategies as a scalable alternative to reinforcement learning
papers.abstract: We explore the use of Evolution Strategies (ES), a class of black box optimization algorithms, as an alternative to popular MDP-based RL techniques such as Q-learning and Policy Gradients. Experiments on MuJoCo and Atari show that ES is a viable solution strategy that scales extremely well with the number of CPUs available: By using a novel communication strategy based on common random numbers, our ES implementation only needs to communicate scalars, making it possible to scale to over a thousand parallel workers. This allows us to solve 3D humanoid walking in 10 minutes and obtain competitive results on most Atari games after one hour of training. In addition, we highlight several advant…
papers.summary: Shows Evolution Strategies, a black-box optimizer, rivals MDP-based RL (Q-learning, policy gradients) on MuJoCo and Atari while scaling almost linearly with CPUs: a common-random-numbers scheme communicates only scalars, letting 1,000+ workers solve 3D humanoid walking in 10 minutes.

papers.title: One-shot imitation learning
papers.summary: A meta-learned policy conditions on a single demonstration of a task plus the current state, using soft attention over the demonstration, and performs new instances of that task. Trained across many block-stacking tasks, it generalizes to unseen stacking configurations from one demo.

papers.title: Learning to communicate
papers.abstract: In this post we’ll outline new OpenAI research in which agents develop their own language.
papers.summary: Outlines research in which RL agents in a simple 2D world invent their own grounded language: discrete symbols whose meanings emerge from cooperative goal-driven tasks rather than from training on human text.

papers.title: Emergence of grounded compositional language in multi-agent populations
papers.abstract: By capturing statistical patterns in large corpora, machine learning has enabled significant advances in natural language processing, including in machine translation, question answering, and sentiment analysis. However, for agents to intelligently interact with humans, simply capturing the statistical patterns is insufficient. In this paper we investigate if, and how, grounded compositional language can emerge as a means to achieve goals in multi-agent populations. Towards this end, we propose a multi-agent learning environment and learning methods that bring about emergence of a basic compositional language. This language is represented as streams of abstract discrete symbols uttered by…
papers.summary: In a proposed multi-agent environment, a basic compositional language of abstract discrete symbols — with a defined vocabulary and syntax — emerges because communicating helps agents achieve their goals; when the symbol channel is unavailable, non-verbal strategies like pointing and guiding emerge instead.

papers.title: Prediction and control with temporal segment models
papers.summary: Learns dynamics as deep generative models (convolutional autoregressive models and VAEs) over whole temporal segments of states and actions rather than single timesteps, giving stable long-horizon predictions for stochastic systems and enabling fully differentiable trajectory optimization and model-based policy optimization.

papers.title: Third-person imitation learning
papers.summary: Trains agents to imitate demonstrations recorded from a third-person viewpoint rather than the agent's own perspective. A GAN-style domain-confusion objective extracts viewpoint-independent features, enabling imitation without costly first-person demonstrations in simple simulated control tasks.

papers.title: Adversarial attacks on neural network policies
papers.abstract: Machine learning classifiers are known to be vulnerable to inputs maliciously constructed by adversaries to force misclassification. Such adversarial examples have been extensively studied in the context of computer vision applications. In this work, we show adversarial attacks are also effective when targeting neural network policies in reinforcement learning. Specifically, we show existing adversarial example crafting techniques can be used to significantly degrade test-time performance of trained policies. Our threat model considers adversaries capable of introducing small perturbations to the raw input of the policy. We characterize the degree of vulnerability across tasks and trainin…
papers.summary: Demonstrates that adversarial-example attacks transfer to deep RL: small, human-imperceptible perturbations of a policy's raw inputs substantially degrade trained agents' performance regardless of task or training algorithm, in both white-box and black-box threat models.

papers.title: PixelCNN++: Improving the PixelCNN with discretized logistic mixture likelihood and other modifications
papers.summary: Improves PixelCNN with a discretized logistic mixture likelihood, conditioning on whole pixels rather than sub-pixel channels, multi-scale downsampling with shortcut connections, and dropout regularization, reaching state-of-the-art log-likelihood on CIFAR-10; implementation open-sourced.

papers.title: Universe
papers.abstract: We’re releasing Universe, a software platform for measuring and training an AI’s general intelligence across the world’s supply of games, websites and other applications.
papers.summary: Releases a platform exposing games, websites, and other applications as RL environments through a VNC remote desktop, so agents act with the same pixels, keyboard, and mouse interface as humans — aiming to measure and train general problem-solving across thousands of tasks.

papers.title: #Exploration: A study of count-based exploration for deep reinforcement learning
papers.abstract: Count-based exploration algorithms are known to perform near-optimally when used in conjunction with tabular reinforcement learning (RL) methods for solving small discrete Markov decision processes (MDPs). It is generally thought that count-based methods cannot be applied in high-dimensional state spaces, since most states will only occur once. Recent deep RL exploration strategies are able to deal with high-dimensional continuous state spaces through complex heuristics, often relying on optimism in the face of uncertainty or intrinsic motivation. In this work, we describe a surprising finding: a simple generalization of the classic count-based approach can reach near state-of-the-art per…
papers.summary: Generalizes classic count-based exploration to high-dimensional spaces by hashing states and granting reward bonuses from hash-table counts. With appropriately granular, task-relevant hash codes, this simple strategy nears state-of-the-art performance on continuous-control benchmarks and Atari games.

papers.title: On the quantitative analysis of decoder-based generative models
papers.summary: Uses annealed importance sampling, validated by bidirectional Monte Carlo, to accurately measure log-likelihoods of decoder-based generative models like VAEs, GANs, and GMMNs. Finds IWAE bounds substantially underestimate true likelihoods, and GANs miss important modes of the data distribution rather than memorizing training data.

papers.title: A connection between generative adversarial networks, inverse reinforcement learning, and energy-based models
papers.abstract: Generative adversarial networks (GANs) are a recently proposed class of generative models in which a generator is trained to optimize a cost function that is being simultaneously learned by a discriminator. While the idea of learning cost functions is relatively new to the field of generative modeling, learning costs has long been studied in control and reinforcement learning (RL) domains, typically for imitation learning from demonstrations. In these fields, learning cost function underlying observed behavior is known as inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) or inverse optimal control. While at first the connection between cost learning in RL and cost learning in generative modeling may a…
papers.summary: Shows a sample-based algorithm for maximum-entropy inverse reinforcement learning is mathematically equivalent to a GAN whose discriminator receives the generator's density, and interprets GAN training as fitting energy-based models, unifying cost learning across the three fields.

papers.title: RL²: Fast reinforcement learning via slow reinforcement learning
papers.summary: Meta-RL: an RNN trained with conventional 'slow' RL across many tasks — receiving observations, actions, rewards, and termination flags, with state persisting across episodes — itself implements a fast RL algorithm, rivaling near-optimal bandit and tabular methods and scaling to visual maze navigation.

papers.title: Variational lossy autoencoder
papers.summary: Combines VAEs with autoregressive PixelCNN decoders and autoregressive flow priors, using a bits-back coding argument to explain when latent codes go unused and to control which information (e.g., global structure) the lossy code captures; state-of-the-art density estimation on MNIST, OMNIGLOT, and Caltech-101 Silhouettes.

papers.title: Extensions and limitations of the neural GPU
papers.abstract: The Neural GPU is a recent model that can learn algorithms such as multi-digit binary addition and binary multiplication in a way that generalizes to inputs of arbitrary length. We show that there are two simple ways of improving the performance of the Neural GPU: by carefully designing a curriculum, and by increasing model size. The latter requires a memory efficient implementation, as a naive implementation of the Neural GPU is memory intensive. We find that these techniques increase the set of algorithmic problems that can be solved by the Neural GPU: we have been able to learn to perform all the arithmetic operations (and generalize to arbitrarily long numbers) when the arguments are…
papers.summary: Shows curriculum design and increased model size let the Neural GPU learn decimal arithmetic that generalizes to arbitrarily long numbers; failure analysis still finds errors on highly symmetric, atypical inputs and only imperfect evaluation of multi-operand expressions requiring operator precedence.

papers.title: Semi-supervised knowledge transfer for deep learning from private training data
papers.summary: PATE: teachers trained on disjoint partitions of sensitive data label public examples via noisy vote aggregation, and a student model learns from those labels. Provides differential-privacy guarantees independent of the underlying learner, with strong privacy-utility trade-offs on MNIST and SVHN.

papers.title: Transfer from simulation to real world through learning deep inverse dynamics model
papers.summary: Sim-to-real transfer for robot control: a policy trained in simulation proposes desired next states, while an inverse dynamics model learned from real-world experience selects actions to reach them, executing simulated skills on a physical robot arm without retraining the policy.

papers.title: Infrastructure for deep learning
papers.abstract: Deep learning is an empirical science, and the quality of a group’s infrastructure is a multiplier on progress. Fortunately, today’s open-source ecosystem makes it possible for anyone to build great deep learning infrastructure.
papers.summary: Describes OpenAI's deep-learning infrastructure — TensorFlow experiments scaled from single-machine prototypes to large distributed runs on Kubernetes over AWS and Azure — and open-sources kubernetes-ec2-autoscaler for elastic, batch-oriented cluster autoscaling.

papers.title: Concrete AI safety problems
papers.abstract: We (along with researchers from Berkeley and Stanford) are co-authors on today’s paper led by Google Brain researchers, Concrete Problems in AI Safety. The paper explores many research problems around ensuring that modern machine learning systems operate as intended.
papers.summary: Announcement of the Google Brain-led 'Concrete Problems in AI Safety' paper, co-authored with OpenAI, Berkeley, and Stanford, framing five practical research problems: avoiding negative side effects, reward hacking, scalable oversight, safe exploration, and robustness to distributional shift.

papers.title: Generative models
papers.abstract: This post describes four projects that share a common theme of enhancing or using generative models, a branch of unsupervised learning techniques in machine learning. In addition to describing our work, this post will tell you a bit more about generative models: what they are, why they are important, and where they might be going.
papers.summary: Explainer accompanying four OpenAI projects on generative models: describes what GANs, VAEs, and autoregressive models are, why learning to generate data is a route to understanding its structure, and near-term directions for unsupervised learning.

papers.title: Adversarial training methods for semi-supervised text classification
papers.abstract: Adversarial training provides a means of regularizing supervised learning algorithms while virtual adversarial training is able to extend supervised learning algorithms to the semi-supervised setting. However, both methods require making small perturbations to numerous entries of the input vector, which is inappropriate for sparse high-dimensional inputs such as one-hot word representations. We extend adversarial and virtual adversarial training to the text domain by applying perturbations to the word embeddings in a recurrent neural network rather than to the original input itself. The proposed method achieves state of the art results on multiple benchmark semi-supervised and purely supe…
papers.summary: Adapts adversarial and virtual adversarial training to text by perturbing word embeddings of recurrent networks instead of sparse one-hot inputs, setting state of the art on semi-supervised and supervised text-classification benchmarks while improving embedding quality and reducing overfitting.

papers.title: OpenAI Gym Beta
papers.abstract: We’re releasing the public beta of OpenAI Gym, a toolkit for developing and comparing reinforcement learning (RL) algorithms. It consists of a growing suite of environments (from simulated robots to Atari games), and a site for comparing and reproducing results.
papers.summary: Public beta of OpenAI Gym: a toolkit giving RL research a common environment interface — spanning Atari, classic control, and simulated robotics — plus a growing benchmark suite and website for comparing and reproducing algorithm results.

papers.title: Weight normalization: A simple reparameterization to accelerate training of deep neural networks
papers.summary: Reparameterizes each weight vector as a learned scalar magnitude times a unit direction, conditioning optimization much like batch normalization but without minibatch dependence or added noise; speeds convergence across supervised image recognition, VAE generative modeling, and deep reinforcement learning.
